text:
Wow - CNN’s front page is the investigation “Inside North Korea’s effort to infiltrate US companies.”

4 months ago covered this same threat in The Pragmatic Engineer with exactly this AI filter (which was about an applicant pretending to be a Polish person applying to 6-person security startup Vidoc Security Lab - and the startup getting suspicious and Dawid Moczadło asking him to put his hand in front of his face which he refused to do!)

If you’re hiring full remote as a tech company, you NEED to expect North Korea to try and infiltrate your recruitment process - and this is not just for US companies, but also Wester European ones.

The investigation back in March in The Pragmatic Engineer  where a 6-person US/Polish startup (Vidoc Security) almost got infiltrated: https://lnkd.in/eGU4vc3S

The CNN investigation today that shows how US residents help launder paychecks, and how this operation is far more organized and spread out than one would expect: https://lnkd.in/ecJvqpSU

The scale of this is bigger than I imagined… who would have thought that a single nation would go to such lengths to “exploit” remote work to fund their regime?

So, how are ways you can counter this threat? Vidoc Security introduced a mandatory in-person final interview as the final round. Other options include far more thorough background checks, more structured onboarding… but I can’t really see anything working as efficient as an in-person meeting (because the scheme involves fake photos and identities!)

text:
This number (20 million+ users using GH Copilot) is wild because… most estimates put the # of all professional software developers in the world somewhere between 20-30 million.

GitHub Copilot either is getting ~100% adoption (unlikely) or it’s expanding the dev pool (likely!) or this number could include many people who are no -developers and not really “using” GH Copilot (could be - Microsoft doesn’t make it clear what qualifies as a user, but it’s safe to assume it’s a paid account!)

I’m assuming this number means the number of accounts that are paid for this plugin - and companies don’t like to pay for something their staff won’t use.

So we could see more people doing what accounts for professional software development with these LLM-powered coding tools! (So much for what many non-developers said recently: how AI tools would be reducing the need for software engineers? Which was nonsense to start with for anyone who built software and also used these tools…)

Post from GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke. Full post: https://lnkd.in/eFmyqTKu

In The Pragmatic Engineer 2025 survey, we saw GitHub Copilot as the most mentioned AI tool - with about 2.5x as many mentions as Cursor, and half of devs saying they use it: https://lnkd.in/eB-ziSYi (a massive percentage, but it would still mean at most ~10M developer users globally based on this survey split)

See my deepdive with Thomas about the past, present and future of GitHub in this recent The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast episode: https://lnkd.in/eaaNZxDD

One of the most surprising learning for me is that LLMs are NOT a good fit for generating + maintaining SDKs (from eg a Java SDK, generating the Rust one) due to their non-deterministic nature. And because maintaining an SDK (and keeping all SDKs up-to-date with one another) is just as important as building the initial one.

But, LLMs can be a good fit to help build the automated tooling that generates the SDKs from a spec.

There are many use cases where deterministic code generation beats non-deterministic code generation (done by LLMs). SDKs are just one such example! The full deepdive:

text:
An amusing use of LLMs at a more traditional company, shared with me by a PM:

“A project with ~50 people got stuck. There are too many JIRA tickets, no clear specification, and anytime one team tries to make progress, the others shoot it down.

So a dev built an LLM to try and break the deadlock:

• Fed all JIRA tickets to the LLM. Built a basic RAG with vector DB

• Had it generate questions about the project, about topics not covered by the tickets

• Had the LLM attempt to answer the same questions

• Generated a report of what areas are not specified 

• Tried to use this to stop teams rejecting suggestions “because this is not well specified”

So, did the LLM solution help break the deadlock? The PM told me:

“No. We’re still stuck. But it was good fun to build it and an excuse to play around with vector databases!”

From all I understood (and the PM also confirmed this) the project suffers from no clear project lead, and teams looking out for their own interest and no one taking the initiative to step in as lead (“not my job”)

AI/LLMs didn’t solve for this. But it didn’t make things worse, and was an excuse to experiment with it.

My take is: good on the dev working there who took initiative (not that common at such places!) and got hands-on experience with LLMs, doing a cool experiment! And this experiment took about 2-3 days, not more - a pretty good investment to try to move the project forward, plus get real LLM experience!

text:
How it started: "AI vibe coding tools will replace devs!"

How it's going, today:

"To vibe code successfully, securely and responsibly, do this:

- Provide it with a detailed spec
- Break down tasks into as small ones as possible
- Separate dev and prod environments
- Use version control, and create "backup points" to every single operation the agent does (and be prepared to "roll back" when needed)
- Run security checks on every change the agent does (assume it does insecure stuff by default)
- Do NOT give access to the agent to prod
- Never trust the agent; verify every step it takes"

What I'm waiting for to be added, soon:

- Ensure a human reviews all critical code paths changed
- Use feature flags when shipping new stuff or even bugfixes
- Add monitoring and alerting to detect any suspicious changes in prod

Here is Replit (a leading vibe coding tool) adding dev/prod environments after it turned out the AI can (and will!) delete the database in prod in secret, without telling the user (full post in the screenshot: https://lnkd.in/eNySrvt7)

This happened with humans decades back: and so we invented dev vs prod separation, and it has been the norm across mature organizations since about ~20 years ago. About ~10 years ago more companies moved to production tenancies (which I'm sure some vibe coding platforms will eventually also get into)

In many ways, we are reinventing what software engineers already know... amusing to see.

Us software engineers won't be going anywhere: and I am more convinced that a software engineer using an AI coding tool will be 100x as powerful/responsible/effective than a non-engineer using one (and not being able to validate when this non-deterministic agent goes off rails!)

text:
What are the odds that lightning strikes twice: Datadog's and Heroku's biggest-ever outage was caused by the same thing, two years apart!!

Here is what I discovered when analyzing the largest-ever Heroku outage in June 2025 (Heroku was down for 23 hours):

This outage was caused by:
- Ubuntu 22.04 doing an automatic update
- This update updated systemd
- Because of the update, systemd was restarted. The restart cleared IP routes
- And so all machines with the  restarted systemd process lost networking
- ... and the restart happened at the same time for most Heroku machines!

In March 2023, Datadog's biggest-ever outage (Datadog was down for 2 days and it cost the company $5M in lost revenue):
- Ubuntu 22.04 doing an automatic update
- This update updated systemd
- Because of the update, systemd was restarted. The restart cleared IP routes
- And so all machines with the  restarted systemd process lost networking
- ... and the restart happened at the same time for all Datadog machines!

How does this happen??

Also, both Heroku's and Datadog's handling of the outage leaves a lot to be desired. Datadog published no postmortem for two months (despite their CEO claiming in public they did so) so I published the postmortem on their behalf back in 2023: https://lnkd.in/end9Ayhd

And Heroku did not communicate publicly anything for the first 8 hours (!!) into the outage, despite the service being fully down. Sure, Heroku's own status page went down with this update, but it took then 8 hours to even post an update to X, which was not down.

More in today's The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive in what happened, and what we can learn from Heroku (and how running infra at scale is hard): 

https://lnkd.in/e6Bv83ZP

text:
Surprised to hear so little about this: Section 174 is (mostly) reversed!! This is a BIG deal:

From 2023, US companies were no longer allowed to deduct developer salaries as an expense. Instead, they were mandated to amortize it over 5 years (for US developers) or 15 years (foreign developers). This made no sense: as it started to treat software development similar to e.g. hardware (like servers). But the big difference: if you buy a server for $1,000 in year 1, you can probably sell it for $800 in year 2 (so amortizing it over 5 years makes sense). 

For software: this is not true! If you spend $1,000 on software development costs in year 1... that same software might be worth $0 in year 2 (e.g. it's been thrown away / rewritten/doesn't work as expected etc).

This tax change plagued US tech businesses and handcuffed the US tech industry. The US was the only country in the world where developer salaries could not be expensed in the same tax year as they were made (except for oil and gas companies in the US that somehow lobbied to be exempt from this change)

This nonsenible tax regulation - that was passed by the first Trump administration in 2017 - has finally been reversed by the second Trump administration's "Big, Beautiful Bill." But not fully: foreign software development costs still need to be amortized for 15 years.

As an outcome of the change: US tech companies are disincentivized to hire abroad (all R&E expenses - basically, anything software - mandated to be amortized over 15 years)

Expect US companies to hire more in the US, and less outside. Whether this is good or bad: it depends where you're based!

More in this week's bonus The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/eVhVPTjg

And here's an amusing (and hilarious!) story about hiring at Google:

Steve (11:13 in the episode https://lnkd.in/e9b3-8WF): “When I was at Google, I was on their hiring committee, which is a “double blind”. They don't see the candidates, they don't know the interviewers. They're just reading feedback packets, and the interviewers don't bias each other.

One day they did an experiment with us, because we were the ones that ultimately decided to make that [hiring] decision about, the thumbs up, thumbs down type thing.

Google has a separate committee that looks at all the feedback. The recruiters did an exercise with us where they presented a bunch of packets. They just said, these were just a bunch of candidates, we're going to go and do the process on them.

Went through and we evaluated 'em all and decided we were going to not hire 60% of them. You figured this one out yet?”

Gergely: “No, not yet.”

Steve: “We were reviewing our own packets, so we voted not to hire 60% of ourselves. And it was a very sobering realization! The next week or two was the best time to apply to Google. We were just like ‘come on through’. I mean, it was nuts!”

text:
As a dev, I find it's a lot less energy to STAY in "the zone" - vs context switch while I'm waiting on something, then get back to work.

When using an AI tool, I cannot "stay in the zone" while waiting on the tool. And so I often get... distracted. By attempting to "save time!!"

The irony of "it takes longer to do stuff thanks to this tool that should speed you up."

When I know I have time to spend *elsewhere than work* then... well, it's just too easy to get distracted?

I wonder if NEEDING to code "in the zone (without distractions)" is perhaps a feature, not a bug?

text:
Why did Cognition buy Windsurf? One theory: Devin (their flagship product) is not popular with developers, but Windsurf is.

From the The Pragmatic Engineer 2025 survey with 3,000 responses (mostly from experienced software engineers) we see that Devin got surprisingly little mentions across developers who listed tools they use for work. In all fairness, why *would* developers embrace a tools that is an "AI software engineer" and marketed as a tool that replaces developers, and all marketing messages speak to PMs/EMs/founders? On the landing page, the tagline is "Crush your backlog with your personal AI engineering team."

Windsurf, meanwhile has respectable traction: it's used by likely a similar number of devs like the excellent Zed editor.

To live up to billions in valuation, Cognition needs to have traction across developers. So my guess is that Cognition needed this purchase badly - and things lined up luckily for them because after Google "gutted" Windsurf (hired away all founders and the cream of their team to Google), Windsurf badly wanted to sell to a company that can provide stability and a path to growth.

See the full survey results and more analysis on the Cognition acquisition in this free The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive: https://lnkd.in/eB-ziSYi

PS: to be clear, I am not saying that Devin itself might not be popular... but it's probably not popular *with developers*. See also how vibe-coding tools that have serious traction like Lovable (crossed $40M in ARR in ~5 months), Replit (crossed $100M in ARR) or Bolt (crossed $40M ARR) are popular... but with a group that seems to not be experienced software engineers (the target group for this survey). It's with some other group, we can say with very high certainty.

text:
Cannot believe the Wall Street Journal writes *complete* nonsense about AI as "digital employees."

Here they write that an AI agent that has "company logins and works alongside human staff" is a "digital employee."

What??

So using the same logic, back in 2015, when Uber had ~2,000 developers, they then also had 5,000+ "digital employees," right? 5,000+ microservices which all had "company logins" (credentials / API keys!) and "worked alongside human staff" (you could invoke them to do work, they cost money to operate etc).

Wait until the WSJ learns about CI/CD and the logins and credentials those services have and the "work" that they do on behalf of humans.

It's tragic that mainstream media gets this so wrong.

Thanks to Laura Tacho who shared this article with me, as an example of why the burden is on us, engineers, to have to explain to non-technical people around us why even the historically most reliable mainstream publications like the WSJ are wrong, and what is really happening. How AI agents are pretty much the same as what we've had before with e.g. services / microservices in how we use them. Sure, they have more capabilities, but they remain services we spin up, pay for resources used, and then spin down or put as idle. Heck, most of them run on the cloud or on internal infra, just like microservices!

And wait until you hear how you can "talk" with some microservices using Slack webhooks.

Here's the article that gets it all wrong: https://lnkd.in/evzt9gNg 

A very good example of what happens when a journalist tries to oversimplify something they don't understand. And why as developers we should not believe headlines - and keep two feet on the ground.

text:
"Fundamental" truths about software:

1. Code is liability

2. The more code you have, the more bugs you tend to have

3. The more complex a system, the more important architecture becomes

4. Writing maintainable code is a lot more effort than just getting it to work

5. The speed of typing out code has never ever been the bottleneck for software development (not since keyboards became widespread from the 60s or 70s)

None of the above changes with AI tools generating code.

So if AI tools can generate code faster (and more of it with less effort), ask yourself: what will happen?

(These thoughts came to me during my hour-long with Laura Tacho - CTO at DX, and one of the best people to talk about developer productivity and how AI changes this, and what are things that will not change. This will be a podcast episode in The Pragmatic Engineer in about 2 weeks' time. I can heavily recommend following Laura for interesting ideas on software, and me for posts like this that come during researching topics like "what are things that won't change, even with AI coding tools")

posted_at.relative: 0 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Starting my new washing machine in 2025 after I loaded the clothes: 5-10 seconds. Doing the same in 1990: ~0 seconds. How did we regress so bad?

I got a new washing machine and find myself frustrated with how long it takes to start the damn thing.

I load in the clothes and throw in the cleaning capsule, then:
- Turn on the machine: 2-3 second delay (booting, I guess?)
- Plays the "turning on tune" for ~2 seconds
- I can now select the program
- The thing doesn't default to any program (and doesn't remember the last selected!) so I turn the knob to the 6th position (the program I also use): another 2-3 seconds, or 5 when I turn over it
- Start it!
- (The machine then does a bunch of checks and starts 3-5 seconds later: but this delay is perfectly fine and I don't care about it: I walk away by this time, and assume it either works or it will beep if it has some issues)


Comparing it with our machine in 1990:

- The mechanical switch is on the last used program
- Hit start!

How did technology regress appliances this bad? And why did developers not do basic things like:

- Prioritize the UI booting up so people can select programs rapidly (with a delay of perhaps 200-300ms, not seconds!)
- Remember the last used program

I have heard of some models displaying language like "Optimizing with AI..." and then still not selecting the last used program.

These machines have 100x more software in them the ones in 90s, and yet most of this software feels like bloat and waste. At the very least it doesn't covey anything useful to the end user, and the UX/usability of the 90s washing machines feels superior to modern ones of today.

What happened - and is the same happening across other industries?

(My washing machine is an LG; drier is a Samsung. Both have this strangely long delay when turning on!)

text:
What a wild story: a software engineer based in India successfully got hired at 10+ Silicon Valley AI startups... to "scam" them by taking a paycheck but not doing the work (until he got fired)

He did this for 3 years before being caught... all thanks to a founder being pissed enough to publicly call him out.

It's the tech industry's version of "Catch Me if You Can." I talked to several founders who hired him (and fired him later) or interviewed him.

Key details:

1. This engineer (Soham Parekh) had an impressive, but ultimately fabricated resume (claiming things like graduating at Georgia Tech that he never did)

2. He told companies that he will be receiving the O1-visa (which means he can work in the US with no sponsorship required) and is pending some administrative processing

3. He aced all interviews: coding, trial days, you name it. Founders speak highly of his tech skills.

4. A never-ending stream of made-up excuses on why his work is not ready or why he cannot do calls. These include claiming that a drone crashed on his house in the India-Pakistan conflict... when in reality he was 1,000 miles away!

More details in today's The Pragmatic Engineer investigation: https://lnkd.in/enBCaiQb

Still can't believe this story is real.

Lesson learned: DO NOT skip background checks! Startups that dodged hiring him did proper background checks / references.

text:
How do you avoid this scenario: a candidate aces the interview process. They then do terrible on the (remote) job. Turns out they were "overemployed" working at several other places.

This is not a fictional scenario: a dev with the name "Soham Parekh" did this with 10+ YC startups, working parallel at many of them the last year. Clearly a very smart person, he aces all kinds of interviews (coding, 'trial days' when pairing with the team for a few days etc). Then he got unusually unproductive on the job, making up excuses why he was behind getting stuff done. Well, it was because he was working several other jobs at once.

So, back to the question: as a founder or manager or lead hiring, what can you do to avoid this situation?

Open to any and all solutions for the hiring process, onboarding or day-to-day work that could help avoid this scenario. Hopefully they would not make the hiring process/working environment much more buerocratic to the majority who are truthful and do not work multiple jobs at once.

text:
The "Trimodal nature of software engineering compensation" made it to The Economist! Except you wouldn't really know because of how they did it:

A journalist from The Economist reached out saying they found my Trimodal Nature of Software Engineering salaries series very interesting, and they are working on a piece on tech compensation and have a few questions.

I usually don't respond to such requests because I have a bad experience with journalists: they often don't give credit to sources, and putting in a few hours to answer queries might result in a single line quoted (or not even.)

But this was The Economist so I made an exception. I wrote 7 pages of responses, linked to several of my articles, and asked them to link the original source if they would use something from my article.

And The Economist DID mention me!

What they did not mention is they took my article (in collaboration with Levels.fyi), redrew the chart and did not even bother with the backlink. And they slapped a "Chart: The Economist" label under the chart, which suggests original research by their team. But it's not: the original research (and the chart) comes from The Pragmatic Engineer, where I worked for ~2 months with Hakeem Shibly and the excellent Levels.fyi team (the most up-to-date "top tier" comp details available in an easily accessbile way)

One one end, it' validating that The Economist is confirming that this "trimodal" model (which I first published in 2021) is correct. But I'm disappointed that they could not link back to the original source, despite making it clear to them that this is what I would expect if they use my research.

The original The Pragmatic Engineer article: https://lnkd.in/eBmGD2CH (free article)

The piece from The Economist that redrew my image without a backlink: https://lnkd.in/eBRRSFwe (paywalled)

The upside is that now more people will see this data... even if they have no way to read the original source, if they only subscribe to The Economist (and perhaps this is a reason for the lack of backlinks?)

Anyway, I am reminded why I should keep to not respond to journalists, going forward. If you do original research and publish interesting data: this is a reminder that unfortunately even mainstream media like The Economist will strip credits from where data comes from, even when explicitly asked to provide links to the source.

Update: a few hours ago, The Economist at least added my name to the bottom of the chart as credits. The Business Editor of The Economist in the comments confirmed the "no linking to the source" policy stays for all their online articles.

In The Pragmatic Engineer I always have and always will link to sources. The difference between “legacy media” and “new media” I guess!

posted_at.relative: 1 month ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
People outside tech are starting to realize how quality software and typing/generating code fast are not correlated

From a post on X by Ben Landau-Taylor (at https://lnkd.in/egbXvGfB)

“Most of my programmer friends tell me that chatbots are a massive improvement and their work is now far far more efficient.

As a consumer I have noticed literally no change whatsoever in the quality or price of chatbot-assisted software.

What's going on here?”

Wait until the same people start to ask what it takes to build quality software.

Thing like… caring about quality (to the point of obsessing over it), having a clear view of what “great software” is, UX, architecture, rapid feedback, craftsmanship.

Code is the vehicle that we use to build programs - but great software is so much more than just code! Similarly to how novels are constructed using words - and yet a novel worth reading takes skill that goes well beyond being able to write thousands of grammatically correct sentences, one after the other.

A good thing about LLMs (and their ability to generate code blazing fast) is how the “hidden” parts about software engineering that are not about writing code will have to be much better understood and appreciated.

Time to re-learn why building and maintaining quality software has been hard, and will be hard?

text:
What an adventure: I travelled to Mongolia for the launch of The Software Engineer's Guidebook in Mongolian. 

I met the team at the tech startup that translated the book: called Nasha Tech, a company that is building the "Uber Eats of Mongolia" called TokTok, and they also work as a development agency for several Japanese and Korean companies.

And the launch event was at Mongolian startup incubator and workspace IT Park Mongolia where I met a bunch of startups: one building an AI virtual assistant, the other using AI for education, a comic/VR startup and a hardcore hardware startup building coffee machines, cocktail machines and drones!

Some impressions:

- Several devs I met and talked to use Cursor or GitHub Copilot (more mentions of Cursor than Copilot tho!)

- Lots of talk about "what will AI mean for us software developers?" Headlines about "AI will replace software developers" are circulating here and causing some anxiety (which headlines, to be clear, I think is nonsense and publications not understanding how AI coding tools work)

- Most companies I talked to are big on hiring interns: it helps that a technology university is right in front of IT Park Mongolia

- Devs are modest and hard-working.

- They translated the book because there is nothing about modern software engineering available in Mongolian language: the startup Nasha Tech is making the translation mandatory reading for all their devs, and they hope software engineers at other companies will also pick up a copy (at the signing event several engineers purchased extra copies for their company library)

Loving my time here so far! Thank you Nasha Tech, IT Park Mongolia, Batutsengel Davaa, Suuribaatar Sainjargal, Zolzaya Jargalsaikhan and everyone else who helped with the translation and the launch event!

text:
I got Mashable to change its false headline about a company's chatbots "revealed to be human employees" which was nonsense to begin with.

At first, fell for headlines about how Builder .ai had an AI that was actually 700 engineers pretending to be an AI... but only for a short while. Surely, this would be nonsense in a day and age of LLMs? So I did what is the sensible thing: reached out to software engineers who built Builder .ai's bot called Natasha to get the truth (and the tech stack) and verified with several people.

Turns out, there was no "devs pretending to be AI": it was an LLM being an LLM.

Also, engineers were telling me how these fake headlines were making a bad situation worse: not only had they lost their jobs (the company went bankrupt) but now being associated with the company meant that hiring managers were more likely to assume they “faked” AI with manual work - but they did none of this!

I wanted to set the record straight: yes, Builder seemingly did shady things in inflating revenue numbers, but its software engineers did real work, and were unlikely to assist in what might be accounting fraud.

After I published this article, on LinkedIn a few people said why they should believe my article over a reputable source like Mashable, which stated that the AI was humans in disguise?

Well, because they didn't talk to engineers or due their due diligence: Mashable simply took a LinkedIn influencer's made up post and treated as fact. I did the digging to separate fact from fiction.

A day after I published my article, Mashable *finally* corrected the record. It would have been nice if they did proper fact checking to start with!

The deepdive from The Pragmatic Engineer where I correct the record: https://lnkd.in/e6TZbPyi

Mashable's now corrected article: https://lnkd.in/ehRXnyqS

text:
Wow: Google experiencing the same lesson Cursor already learned. Sualeh A. (cofounder at Cursor) told me last week how “cold restarts” were one of their biggest pain points because of the thundering herd problem.

On Thursday, Google Cloud had an outage lasting 7 hours: and a good time of the downtime was due to the same thundering herd problem. From Google’s postmortem:

“Within some of our larger regions, such as us-central-1, as Service Control tasks restarted, it created a herd effect on the underlying infrastructure it depends on (i.e. that Spanner table), overloading the infrastructure. Service Control did not have the appropriate randomized exponential backoff implemented to avoid this. 

It took up to ~2h 40 mins to fully resolve in us-central-1 as we throttled task creation to minimize the impact on the underlying infrastructure and routed traffic to multi-regional databases to reduce the load.”

Google’s action item to improve here:

“We will audit and ensure our systems employ randomized exponential backoff.”

This is a reminder that building large systems is not easy, but operating them remains hard - and also that the challenges the 50-person Cursor engineering team faces can sometimes be similar to what the much larger Google Cloud engineering team does. (And this is true for many other small and large engineering teams)

Deepdive to how Cursor is built: https://lnkd.in/e6XU_BmD

Postmortem for the Google Cloud outage: https://lnkd.in/eXnVdBPu

text:
Have you heard the headline "startup backed by Microsoft fakes AI with 700 developers?" Well, it's fake! How could it EVER be real?

I talked with (now former) engineers at now-bankrupt startup Builder.ai to find out what really happened... and I also tried to "design" this system that would let 700 devs pretend to be AI: https://lnkd.in/eBR6SwwT Turns out it would be quite the engineering challenge to have hundreds of devs "fake" an AI in a time when we have LLMs are our fingertips!

Spoiler: nothing out of the ordinary in the tech stack side of things. Devs were NOT "pretending to be AI." Yes, there is what seems to be potentially accounting fraud: but the AI stack worked, and was built like you'd expect for any other AI platform.

This is a good reminder of the importance of checking sources, and to be extra sceptical about social media posts. This also applies to me because last week The Pragmatic Engineer was among those which reported the incorrect claim. This is why I consider it important to recognise the error, and to go get the full story by talking with people who worked at Builder.ai

If your team is looking to hire engineers with experience building real AI systems, the Builder.ai alumni group is likely a great source of such hires. It’s sad to see a startup implode in the AI space over fraud allegations, and good luck to engineers who worked at this startup in finding their next role!

Read exactly what happened at Builder.ai here: https://lnkd.in/eBR6SwwT

text:
Kent Beck is a legend in software engineering: and after coding for 52 years, he's never had more fun than now, he told me. Why? Because AI agents brought back the joy of creating software without the stuff that he's started to hate about coding for so long.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eyjGuANt 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/edFBaPGQ 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/e-PZVTAY 

Brought to you by:

• Sonar — Code quality and code security for ALL code https://lnkd.in/eGWgj-is 
•⁠ Statsig ⁠ — ⁠ The unified platform for flags, analytics, experiments, and more https://lnkd.in/eQkXqjMZ
• Augment Code — AI coding assistant that pro engineering teams love https://lnkd.in/ddCkDrxF

Two of my takeaways from this chat with Kent:

𝟭. 𝗞𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗿𝗲-𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗴𝗶𝘇𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗸𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗜 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘀𝘁𝘂𝗳𝗳. Kent has been coding for 52 years, and the last decade, he’s gotten a lot more tired of all of it: learning yet another new language or framework, or debugging the issues when using the latest framework.

What he loves about these AI agents (and AI coding tools) is how he doesn’t need to know exactly all the details: he can now be a lot more ambitious in his projects.

Currently, Kent is building a server in Smalltalk (that he’s been wanting to do for many years) and a Language Server Protocol (LSP) for Smalltalk

𝟮. 𝗙𝗮𝗰𝗲𝗯𝗼𝗼𝗸 𝘄𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗲 𝗻𝗼 𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁 𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟭𝟭, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝘂𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝗞𝗲𝗻𝘁, 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗮𝘆. Kent joined Facebook in 2011, and was taken aback by the lack of testing and how everyone pushed code to production without automated testing.

What he came to realize – and appreciate! – was how Facebook had several things balancing this out:

• Devs took responsibility for their code very seriously
• Nothing at Facebook was “someone else’s problem:” devs would fix bugs when they saw them, regardless of whose commit caused it
• Feature flags were heavily used for risky code
• Facebook did staged rollouts to smaller markets like New Zealand

To this date, Facebook ships code to production in a unique way. We covered more in the deepdive Shipping to Production at https://lnkd.in/dsQzhQ7S

text:
Have you heard of this tax change called "Section 174" that could be contributing to tech layoffs by US companies? If you are subscribed to The Pragmatic Engineer, you probably did a year ago, when the impact was first felt.

For some reason, this topic never got much discussion... until an article in Quartz went viral last week, covering the same topic, though with a lot more dramatic headline.

My social media feeds are now full of people writing how no one has talked about this until now.

I beg to differ: there's been a bunch of talk, and a year back I struggled to understand the self-inflicted wound the US is doing to its tech sector by not allowing expensing developer salaries in the same year.

It's also amusing how it took a year and a half for mainstream media to discover the same topic that's been out in the open for so long.

Here's the deepdive on what Section 174 changes are, and why they are so devastating to many US tech businesses - as published in January 2024, in The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/eH6Jzaqc

I am not sure this tax change is to blame for the layoffs, though: my view is that most cuts are likely because of the end of zero interest rates in 2022, as I cover in https://lnkd.in/eqTg2Rus and https://lnkd.in/eG2z_7hi

In some ways, it's amusing that as a small tech publication, I still get to surface trends months or years before it reaches "mainstream" news. The benefit of talking to software engineers and founders all the time, I guess!

(Here's a longer list of cases when The Pragmatic Engineer shares trends months or even years before mainstream news reports on the same: https://lnkd.in/eGfcFusk )

text:
Something amusing I heard recently: a Fortune 500, non-tech company is NOT allowing devs to purchase one of the popular AI coding tools (e.g Cursor, Windsurf, GH Copilot, Augment Code etc).

Instead: they are building their own, internally.

The official reasoning: IP, security, compliance.

The unofficial reason (that no one will say, of course): getting headcount for a team; playing around with LLMs, and a sure and trusted path to get promoted by shipping this project and demonstrating company-wide impact.

It's amusing because now this company has subpar coding tools compared to their competition that allows devs to get tools built by startups specializing in dev tooling - and all of this for a higher price in the end!

And we can see how this will play out already: the project ships, gets some usage, people get promoted, then in 6-18 months the company will finally sign an enterprise contract for a dedicated AI coding tool that works better, more reliably and has far superior developer experience.

We've seen this in the past with e.g. "private cloud", Big Data solutions, CRMs or even chat tools ("Slack would not scale for our use case" - then four years after building the internal chat tool that was subpar in features and reliability, the company finally moved over to Slack and turned out Slack 'scaled' just fine for tens of thousands of people).

Looks like this time is different - but some things remain the same!

posted_at.relative: 1 month ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I am getting SO tired of these posts from influencers:

“We literally cloned an N billion-dollar company in 20 minutes with {vibe coding tool}. This changes the game forever.”

No, you didn’t “clone” a billion-dollar business. You created a landing page similar to it. That’s all.

Take Docusign, one example a recent post uses. Docusign generates $3B in revenue per year, $1B of that is profit (!!)

There are hundreds of “Docusign clones” and some very good ones! A few examples from a quick search: PandaDoc, SignNow, SignRequest, Signeasy, eSignly, YouSign, DocSend, Document .so, DottedSign, CocoSign, Signaturely. Plus, Office 365, Google Workspace, Box and Dropbox all have e-signature products included in their existing offering at no additional cost to existing customers.

All of these e-signature businesses took at least months but probably years to build - and yet none of them can get close to Docusign in revenue or market share… because they have not cracked enterprise workflows or enterprise sales like Docusign has. Docusign is sometimes ridiculoued for having thousands of staff: but they are doing something extremely well when their annual profits alone are probably more than the combined valuation of their competitors! And whatever Docusign is doing, it doesn’t seem like it can be “cloned” despite many trying to do so.

For what it’s worth: some of these hyperbolic posts going around on social media can be undisclosed ads (I’m getting lots of DMs by startups offering to pay for seemingly organic posts to advertise recent launches which I ignore. It’s also why I have an ethics starement: https://lnkd.in/eG4G6svW ) But some of these posts are genuinely or deliberately naive and seem to be written with the goal to get widely shared.

If it sounds too good to be true it almost always is. Don’t believe the hype of how successful businesses can be “cloned” by some tool in minutes/hours/weeks/months.

But sure, a landing page can now be cloned with an AI agent a bit faster than it took before. I still can’t name a “clone” business that became successful by pure cloning tho.

text:
Why and how did Microsoft create VS Code? The actual story is amusing because creating VS Code was seen as the least important decision across 3 bold changes. As Scott Guthrie (who heads up Cloud + AI at Microsoft) recalls below, creating a Community version of Visual Studio and open sourcing .NET were both seen as potentially much more important decisions... and yet, VS Code turned out to be perhaps the biggest one of the three.

Watch or listen to the full episode where we go through the history of Microsoft all the way to today, and looking at what's next:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eWqAGi2y 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eUrYniEr 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eipgrez7 

Brought to you by:

• Statsig ⁠ — ⁠ The unified platform for flags, analytics, experiments, and more. https://lnkd.in/eQkXqjMZ
• Sinch — Connect with customers at every step of their journey. https://lnkd.in/eVZTnNTZ
• Modal — The cloud platform for building AI applications. https://lnkd.in/ePGPk2py

A takeaway from this episode:

𝗠𝗶𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗱𝗲𝘃 𝘁𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗱𝗲𝘃 𝘁𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗗𝗡𝗔. The first product Microsoft released was a BASIC interpreter in 1975. Over the decades, the company kept building not just platforms (Windows, Azure) or applications (Office, Teams) but developer tools (languages like Visual Basic, C#, TypeScript, and tools like Visual Studio or VS Code.)

I have the feeling that out of all of Big Tech, Microsoft still “gets” developers the most. Looking back at the history of the company helps to understand why this is.

Read more interesting takeaways here: https://lnkd.in/esw2D8yu

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What does it take to land a job as an AI Engineer—and thrive in the role?

Janvi Kalra works at OpenAI in this role: and she transitioned from software engineer to AI Engineer at her previous company, Coda. Before joining OpenAI, she interviewed with 46 different AI startups (!!) and so has an unusually broad and deep overview of how interviews work, how companies compare, and practical mental models to think about your career.

Brought to you by:

• Statsig ⁠ — ⁠ The unified platform for flags, analytics, experiments, and more. https://lnkd.in/eQkXqjMZ

• Sinch⁠ — Connect with customers at every step of their journey. https://lnkd.in/eVZTnNTZ 

• Cortex — Your Portal to Engineering Excellence. http://cortex.io/pragmatic 

Watch or listen to the full episode:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eT8nkXpe 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/edDaMr4X 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/e-E68sP7 

Two of my takeaways from talking with Janvi:

𝟭. 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗶𝗰𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗮 𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝘄𝗮𝘆 𝘁𝗼 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗼 𝗔𝗜 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴. Janvi wanted to move onto Canva’s first AI Engineering team, but she was politely rejected at first. Instead of giving up: she started to teach herself how to build apps using LLMs. She went to hackathons, educated herself online, and built apps on the side.

A few months later, she was already one of the experts at work on how to work with LLMs. So the next time Canva’s AI team was expanding, the team was thrilled to have her on! My two cents: it’s much easier to transfer internally, so if you are hoping to get into AI, see if you can do something similar to what Janvi did.

𝟮. 𝗔𝗜 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲. Janvi got a broad look at the AI Engineering job market, thanks to interviewing with 46 different companies (!) There doesn’t seem to be an “AI Engineer interview” format. Instead, interviews are a mix of:

• Leetcode-style algorithmic interviews

• System design interviews

• Project-based takehomes where you need to build an app

• Domain deepdives into LLM specifics

This means that the best way to prepare for these interviews is to prepare for software engineering interviews: build products using LLMs on the side, and familiarize yourself with common AI Engineering concepts (some covered here: https://lnkd.in/ekrPx8GV)

More takeaways and Janvi's thinking in Big Tech vs startup upsides: https://lnkd.in/eMTWS7wu

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Something I hear very little talk about:

How AI coding tools are so much LESS useful when used on existing, large codebases at work (with custom frameworks, conventions, coding style etc)

... compared to doing greenfield work or side projects

So common for me to hear: "yeah I love it on my side projects, but at work it's 'meh'"

I'm getting some of these details talking with devs at the likes of eg Google, Meta, Microsoft: the companies building some of the best AI coding tools out there! And yet, for their existing codebases, the usefulness is much smaller (vs greenfield). Most of the usage I hear is for autocomplete (and not chat or agentic stuff).

As a specific example: a large tech company (thousands of devs) got a Cursor license for all devs to use. They checked in a few months later, and ~half the devs stopped using it. Just wasn't as useful *inside the company* And this is a "top" tech company!

And yes, surely there are workarounds - from better project structures, to better using e.g. cursor .md or copilot-instructions .md files. I also heard of eg legacy Java projects with CQRS/Event Sourcing framework work fine with eg Copilot. But still, most stories I hear as positive ones are almost all greenfield ones and small ones - at least today.

Of course, tooling should get better over time for larger projects as well: but the ground reality of AI coding tools usage/usefulness is a far cry from the "vibe coding success stories" that are more common to see amplified.

Screenshot from yesterday's The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive at https://lnkd.in/eERZgPpx

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Why would you want to hire *more* interns/junior developers than before? GitHub is doing just this and I asked Thomas Dohmke about this last week in Seattle:

These days, loots of companies are hiring less assuming AI might be at the level of a junior dev and are pausing new grad hiring. GitHub, meanwhile, is increasing this. Thomas told me that companies thinking because of AI they should pause hiring young folks have it backwards.

He told me that if you're not hiring young people who are open-minded about new ideas (including GenAI) then you risk getting stuck where you are, innovation drops.

Plus, young folks (interns/new grads) often improve team dynamics greatly.

And there's also the thing about giving back to the industry that few talk about, but Thomas did:

In college, you learn a lot but don't get to get involved in large codebases/legacy code/tech debt.

By doing more internships: more students see the reality of the industry... and also see the value in using tools like GitHub. In some ways this indirectly means more (eventual) GitHub advocates and customers.

(Plus it's how you invest in the next generation!)

Great to hear this thinking. Food for thought that if GitHub is hiring more interns (when they created Copilot and are heavy users of it) - then perhaps they see something benefitial to this approach that companies pausing investing in young people are missing!

(A full podcast to come with Thomas - this was probably two minutes worth of discussion from our 90-minute long conversation)

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I don't think most people appreciate both the nuance and the importance of this UI that Microsoft is shipping with their new coding agent

It works *on behalf of* a dev. This has lots of implications!

Implications such as:

1. It's NOT autonomous and doesn't pretend to be

2. You can imagine reporting coming real soon about how much work a dev is prompting vs assigning the agent / correcting it

3. Data gathered on "successful" and "unsuccessful" delegations 

Before you ask: OF COURSE stats will eventually make their way somehow into performance / promotion discussions. In the future, with this tooling, in calibration sessions I imagine EMs would say things like:

"Did good work but almost all of it was prompting"

"Consistently caught bugs from the AI before it was shipped, for example these PRs"

"Uses little AI but consistently ships faster than peers"

Still, I like what GitHub/Microsoft do here so much better than any other tool I've seen. It feels honest that you can try to delegate stuff to an agent, clear that it's tracked/visible and that we don't know what will work and what will not.

I also DO like how an agent's "work" is now attributed to the dev kicking it off. As it should be!!

In the end, the dev still 100% responsible for shipping code. If they ship a regression: that was the developer's decision. AI doesn't "decide" anything - it's a tool!

(The screenshot is from Microsoft's BUILD keynote - and this functionality is live in Visual Studio 2022 Preview)

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Every 10-20 years, a breakthrough technology promises non-developers to finally create software without needing to hire programmers.

So far, every such technology resulted in the need for more devs… expert in this NEW technology (or knowing how to fix it up). 

Every. Time.

Of course, there’s always a chance of *this time* being different.

But the surest way to have more career stability + more options + be more in-demand so far in all cases has been become an expert in *the new thing*.

Like integrating + working with LLMs. Here are 7 devs that already did and are now “AI engineers” after a few months (they are software engineers who can build whatever with LLMs:) https://lnkd.in/ea5ju-cs

There seems to be little to no downside of doing this as an engineer and much upside.

And there WILL be an explosion of demand in professionals to take over maintaining “vibe coded” software that works, generates money but would need improvements (eg cost less, add more features without breaking existing stuff; get audited for compliance, support enterprise features etc)

Source of screenshot: Gary Bernhardt on X: https://lnkd.in/eEn5WH4z

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Kubernetes is the second-largest open-source project in the world. What does it do—and why is it so widely adopted?

Kat Cosgrove has led several Kubernetes releases and joined The Pragmatic Engineer podcast to share the history of Kubernetes, how it's built, when it is a good (or not so good) use case, and how you can contribute to it (and why it's a good thing to do for professional and career growth).

Brought to you by:

•⁠ WorkOS — The modern identity platform for B2B SaaS https://workos.com

•⁠ Modal — The cloud platform for building AI applications https://lnkd.in/ePGPk2py

•⁠ Cortex — Your Portal to Engineering Excellence http://cortex.io/pragmatic

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/e7HH8u8P 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eurN3Cyy 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eK7cbCr9

• Summary and transcript: https://lnkd.in/e9xnqqTp 

Three of my takeaways from this great conversation:

𝟭. 𝗞𝘂𝗯𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗲𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗽𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗲-𝘀𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗲 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀. Google originally built an in-house tool to manage the tens of thousands (then hundreds of thousands and later millions) of machines: this internal tool is called Borg. The roots of Kubernetes come from Borg: but Google has since donated the project to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) – and today, Kubernetes is the second largest open source project, after Linux. We previously did a deepdive on How Linux is built, and touched on Google’s SRE roots in What is Reliability Engineering?

𝟮. 𝗞𝘂𝗯𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗲𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝘄𝗲𝗹𝗹-𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗿𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘇𝗲𝗱 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗻 𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁. The structure of the project and all processes are well documented. The project has around 150-200 maintainers, has a few dozen SIGs (Special Interest Groups) and releases run on a 14-16 week cycle.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 “𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱” 𝗮𝗻𝗱 “𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗱𝗼𝘄” 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗽𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗰𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗼𝗻𝗲, 𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘇𝗲𝗱 𝗯𝘆 𝗞𝘂𝗯𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗲𝘁𝗲𝘀. The Release Team within Kubernetes owns releases, and the Release Team has about 20-30 people participating in each release. More than of the members on the Release Team are “shadows” who get to learn on the job how a release is done – and, hopefully, in a release or two, become leads themselves!

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
“AI Engineering is just software engineering with AI models thrown into the stack” - Anton Bacaj in the book AI Engineering by Chip Huyen
 
Having talked to many "AI Engineers": I have to agree! The common trait they all share is their title usually was along the lines of "software engineer" until recently - and then they started to work with LLMs and AI models, integrating into software products. Here are stories from 7 such software engineers turned "AI engineers:" https://lnkd.in/ea5ju-cs

So if you are a software engineer: you're practically almost an AI engineer! Just pick up this toolset (LLMs) and use it.

And if you are a founder or manager looking to hire AI engineers... your team probably already has several of them. Just look closer! (I also encourage you to check out the story about Ryan Cogswell - whose company hired an "AI agency" for expertise... but the agency offered such a complicated and expensive AI solution to be built over 6+ months that Ryan just taught himself how to work with models and built the whole thing in 2 months, at a fraction of the cost)

To hear more about AI engineering from the book's author, Chip Huyen: here's a recent podcast episode we did: https://lnkd.in/eJWzaf3q

text:
Seen the graph on the left posted lots of times with the narration "It's the end of software developer jobs" before? So have I. Those posting it speculate that AI is causing a depressing decline in developer postings.

So let's look at the companies that are using AI the most: Big Tech (like Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Meta, Apple) "top tech" companies (eg Pinterest, Coinbase, GitLab etc) and VC-funded scaleups and startups (OpenAI, Anthropic, Grafana etc). If the narrarive of AI tools spreading reduce jobs were true: these are the companies adopting these tools the most! So job postings would be down in this group: as they have integrated AI into their developer workflow and products more than any other company has!

BUT: they are not! 

This second group is hiring more than they have in 2 years's time - since AI has gone mainstream (ChatGPT was released in Nov 2022, and it crossed 100M weekly users in Nov 2023. Hiring sped up at these companies right around this time!)

So something doesn't add up. 

Worth asking: what is this? And why are the companies using AI the most the ones increasing tech hiring the most (including software engineer and AI engineer hiring - these two are not that different btw!)

Data source for first chart: FRED https://lnkd.in/epht9MZS

Data source for second chart: TrueUp at https://lnkd.in/e9j8Xcra. Browse the 236,000+ jobs at these companies listed at https://trueup.io/jobs

And see my analysis on what really caused this decline from 2022: the end of zero interest rates in The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/eqTg2Rus (free article)

As closing: it's clear that the current tech jobs market is much tougher than the years before. For new grads, it might even be the toughest job market ins 20 years (since the Dotcom Bust). And the massive decline in hiring since 2022, coupled with tech sector layoffs, and (now) stricter performance management all make getting a new job a lot more cutthroat. But the job market started to get tougher even before AI went mainstream: and while it probably contributes some, it's unclear to me if AI is the one and main reason for all of this. (I plan to look more at this strange market. If you have tips for data sources or angles, feel free to comment or send a direct message!)

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Will tools like Windsurf result in fewer software engineers? "It feels like it's people hating software engineers who say this" says Windsurf cofounder and CEO Varun Mohan

In today's The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast episode, we go into the engineering challenges (and tradeoffs!) of building an AI-powered IDE like Windsurf and how Windsurf has changed how the Windsurf dev team (and non-developers!) write software.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eSzptNW6 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eSaTSxG9 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/e354J9Mu 

Brought to you by:

• CodeRabbit⁠⁠ — Cut code review time and bugs in half. Use the code PRAGMATIC to get one month free at https://www.coderabbit.ai/

• Modal⁠ — The cloud platform for building AI applications Get started at https://lnkd.in/ePGPk2py

Three of my takeaways:

𝟭. 𝗔𝗜-𝗽𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗜𝗗𝗘𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 “𝗳𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗹𝗲𝘀𝘀” 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗰𝗲 𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗹𝗼𝗮𝗱. I asked Varun how using Windsurf changed the workload and output of engineers — especially given how most of the team have been software engineers well before LLM coding assistants were a thing. A few of Varun’s observations:

• Engineers are more “fearless” in jumping into unfamiliar parts of the codebase — when, in the past, they would have waited to talk to people more familar with the code. 

• Devs increasingly first turn to AI for help, before pinging someone else (and thus interrupting that person)

• Mental fatigue is down, thanks to tedious tasks can be handed off to prompts or AI agents

Varun stressed that he doesn’t see tools like Windsurf eliminating the need for skilled engineers: it simply changes the nature of the work, and can increase potential output.

𝟮. 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗩𝗦 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 “𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁” 𝘄𝗮𝘆 𝗺𝗲𝗮𝗻𝘀 𝗱𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮 𝗹𝗼𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗶𝗻𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸. While VS Code is open source and can be forked: VS Code Marketplace and lots of proprietary extensions. For example, when forking VS Code, the fork is not allowed to use extensions like Python language servers, remote SSH, and dev containers. The Windsurf team had to build custom extensions from scratch — which took a bunch of time, and users probably did not even notice the difference!

However, if Windsurf had not done this, and had broken the license of these extensions, they could have found themselves in legal hot water. So forking VS Code “properly” is not as simple as most devs would normally expect.

𝟯. 𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗲 𝘀𝗲𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝗻-𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲 “𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲?” 𝗠𝗮𝘆𝗯𝗲. One of the very surprising stories was how Windsurf’s partnership lead (a non-developer) created a quoting tool by prompting Windsurf. This tool replaced a bespoke, stateless tool that the company paid for.

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
One interesting outcome of building AI applications/products (those on top of LLMs): o11y (observability) becomes SO important! 

You need to monitor, monitor, monitor; alert, alert alert!!

This is because LLMs are nondeterministic: so you can forget automated testing.

This is a BIG change compared to "traditional" software engineering, where investing a lot into automated testing often meant you didn't need as granular monitoring and alerting.

Amusingly, it validates companies like Meta which have never done much automated testing: and have built best-in-class monitoring, alerting and auto-rollback systems.

So companies building AI products will do well to take inspiration from Facebook - and from other startups that are big on monitoring and alerting!

(This insight comes as I was talking with ChatGPT's Head of Engineering about how they think about reliability - OpenAI's engineering team monitor so many things with greater detail I usually see! A deepdive coming on engineering challenges they recently solved in The Pragmatic Engineer - sign up to get it in your inbox if you're not yet subscribed:  https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw)

And for more details on how Facebook's core product ships to production in this this deepdive: https://lnkd.in/dsQzhQ7S

text:
"Protecting" devs from the product/the business is harmful - for the company/team that does this! Via Ebi Atawodi (hands-down the best PM I worked with - and who did, and still does the exact opposite!)

At companies where engineering is "shielded" from product/the business, you hear things like:

"Product wants us to do this... it's ridiculous and makes no sense"
"The business told us to build this... let's do the bare minimum and then get back to refactoring the service"

At places where engineering is involved with product and the business, it's "we" instead of "them:"

"Our goal is to increase conversion from free to paid the next several months: and so we need to build these two experiments, and see if they will help"

"Having the old service slows us down: but we need to decide if addressing this first will help us as a business, or if shipping this new product can help grow the customer base, and as we roll it out and tweak it based on feedback, we get breathing space to and do this much-needed refactoring"

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What are traits of standout software engineers? Here are great observations from Ebi Atawodi - currently Director of Product Management at YouTube Studio, and a former product leader at Netflix and Uber.

I worked with Ebi for 4 years at Uber, and in this The Pragmatic Engineer podcast episode we go through how engineers, engineering managers and product managers can work better together.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eGf7CNEd 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/ekaFmE_u  

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eTRFMsWR 

Brought to you by:

• WorkOS — The modern identity platform for B2B SaaS https://workos.com/ 

• The Software Engineer’s Guidebook: Written by me (Gergely) – now out in audio form as well https://lnkd.in/dEkcvW2T 

----
Some of my takeaways:

𝟭. 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁-𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗱 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗱𝗼𝗻𝗲: 𝘀𝗼 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗼𝗻𝗲 — and if you are a PM or EM, help your engineers become one! One of the biggest gifts from Ebi, myself, and our engineering team was helping all of us become product-minded engineers. Ebi exposed us to how Product worked, made decisions, allocated headcount, and pitched new initiatives to be funded by the business. I wrote more on how to become a product-minded engineer.

𝟮. 𝗚𝗲𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗸𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝗯𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝗠/𝗣𝗠/𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿/𝗰𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗴𝘂𝗲. At work, roles are somewhat artificial. What is not artificial is the person behind the role. It made a massive difference when I got to know the “real Ebi” behind “Ebi, the product manager” — and the other way around, about “Gergely, the engineering manager.” It’s a lot easier to trust a partner when you know more about them than “just” their role at work.

𝟯. 𝗙𝗼𝗰𝘂𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗱𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗮𝘀 𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿 — 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁. As tempting as it is to “game the system” of performance reviews or promotions: both Ebi and I agree that you absolutely can game whatever system your company has in place to fast-track that next promotion, or try to get better performance reviews. But the people who do this, others around them notice — especially engineers! What speeds them up in the short term often slows them down in the long term. This is because more senior positions often need strong referrals: and someone who was visibly focused on their own advancement only can struggle to get these.

Do standout work, help others when you can, and be kind to others around you, and years later, other standout colleagues could well tap your shoulder, wanting to recruit you to the companies they are currently at. This is how Ebi often got recruited for increasingly senior roles.

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What is it like to write software for humanoid robots? Sandor Felber does just this: formerly Humanoid Robotics Researcher at MIT, and before that interning at Tesla (working on Optimus). Today, in a deepdive in The Pragmatic Engineer he explained:

First: before jumping to software, you need to understand the hardware characteristics of (humanoid) robots. Some of the biggest challenges in this field are related to electrical engineering, mechanical engineering and just "plain" physics (like heating issues)

The biggest software engineering ones are related to balance control, state estimation and real-time control. A lot of software solves for these problems.

In general, robotics is about solving *dynamic* problems, and not static ones. As an example (one far simpler than the real world!) is writing code that helps a "robot" (a dot below) climb a mountain that is moving (the running code solving for it visualized below)

To solve for this, you need to understand the maths behind the problem, and write an algorithm that uses gradient computation: at every step, re-computing the direction to the steepest point.

This is just a taste of what actual real-world development looks like: and Sandor shared how it's hard to separare software from hardware: as the two need to work together. So there's not really a "pure" software robotics engineer: just robotics engineers.

However, there are increasingly more software challenges that robotics engineers tackle - things like large-scale data processing, practical usage of AI for eg trajectory planning - so it could be an interesting area to venture for any software engineer.

The deepdive with a lot more details and the source code for this example: https://lnkd.in/e55nQUPi

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Here are a few things you probably did not know about Reddit's iOS and Android apps: they are ~2.5M lines of code each, with 500+ screens, and a total of 200 native mobile engineers work on the both of them (including a dedicated iOS and Android mobile platform team)

But a few years ago, things looked very different - and Reddit quietly rebuilt their native apps from 2021. Today's conversation goes through what happened and how, with  three engineers from Reddit’s mobile platform team who led this work: Lauren Darcey (Head of Mobile Platform), Brandon Kobilansky (iOS Platform Lead), and Eric Kuck (Principal Android Engineer)

---

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eaNX8Uxf 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eMwvEaYG 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eC69JYCF

Brought to you by:

• Graphite — The AI developer productivity platform https://gt.dev/pragmatic
• Sentry — Error and performance monitoring for developers. Get 150k errors (three months of Team Plan) for free at https://lnkd.in/e58sfumb

---

Three of my takeaways from this episode:

𝟭. 𝗣𝗼𝗼𝗿 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝘀𝗹𝗼𝘄 𝗱𝗼𝘄𝗻 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆 – 𝘀𝗼 𝗽𝗮𝘆 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻! One of the reasons Reddit started investing heavily in modernizing its mobile stack was that the “old stack” was slowing down developers. Reddit’s platform team got proof of this simply by asking native engineers about the biggest development-related challenges they face.

𝟮. 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘀 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸. Advice from Brandon for anyone looking to work on a platform team:

"You should try to work at a tech company for a year or two and actually see what happens after you ship a system — and then the assumptions change! You then have to figure out how to keep this thing going. You get a bunch of software design intuition because you have to like re-evaluate your assumptions for an incredibly long time. If you can do that, you're probably ready for platform stuff."

𝟯. 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗔𝗜 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗹 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘆 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝘁 “𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲” 𝘆𝗲𝘁 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗲. LLMs integrated into IDEs seem to be increasingly helpful with backend, fullstack, web and even cross-platform (React Native / Expo) projects. However, Reddit’s mobile team shared that they get a moderate boost from the Apple and Android Studio LLM additions.

Native mobile development is distinctively different from web, fullstack and backend coding – and it seems that these IDEs with AI functionality have not done much to optimize for the expereince of native mobile engineers. Over time, this will likely change – but it’s a reminder that there are differences between fullstack, backend and native mobile development. (I wrote a book reflecting on more of the challenges unique to native mobile titled Building Mobile Apps at Scale)

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
This is wild: 📈 Wes Winham Winler's company (Woven) specializes in helping tech companies screen inbound applications. That they found evidence of "AI fakers" at scale shows this is a *very* real problem that probably all full-remote tech companies need to consider.

Previously shared an example on how a 6-person startup split between Poland and the US nearly hired one of these "AI fakers" (who are competent engineers... but use fake identities and AI filters): https://lnkd.in/eGU4vc3S

Outside of mandating an in-person interview (e.g. as a final interview) it is hard to say how else it is possible to defend against this type of attack. There is growing suspicion this could be a state-funded attack (NK!), though there is not enough definite evidence of this, just lots of suspicion, a clear warning from Google's security team, and other pointers.

Here's Google's security team detailing how North Korea seems to be infiltrating US tech companies with remote tech workers, and why they do it (generate revenue, financial explitation and cororporate espionage): https://lnkd.in/e96uHp4K

That full-remote tech companies *might* need to defend against a state-funded attackers trying to pass interviews - well, this is new. But any responsible company hiring should figure out how to mitigate against this type of attack, that seems to be spreading.

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

reshared_post.text:
We just busted an international crime syndicate infiltrating US remote engineering jobs! 🕵️

Sharing what we found to help others stay safe.

300+ applications across dozens of company with ~30 fake candidate profiles (and counting)

- All to US/Canada remote roles
- Real software engineers show up to video interviews
- They have "camera problems" or are "traveling" as the first try
- If you force video, they reschedule and show up with a deep fake video
- Often incongruous accent and fishy story about how they ended up in the US

And they passed DOZENS of recruiter phone screens with very good tech recruiters at VC-backed US companies!

This appears to be the same behavior that Gergely Orosz recently highlighted  in the The Pragmatic Engineer 

After one of our customers noticed the pattern, we pieced it together the ring of candidates in retrospect, so we were able to see that these folks really do pass interviews.

Commonalities across the profiles:

- Similar resume formats
- Showed up on the same set of niche developer profile websites on a google search
- Often broken LinkedIn profile links

Hiring managers and recruiters are now counter-spies. It's getting wild!

Anyone else seeing this?

text:
What is it like to work at Amazon as a software engineer? David Anderson spent over 12 years at Amazon working closely with engineers on his teams: starting as an Engineering Manager (or, SDM in Amazon lingo) and eventually becoming a Director of Engineering. 

In this episode, he shares a candid look into Amazon’s engineering culture—from how promotions work to why teams often run like startups. Dave has retired from Amazon (and corporate life!) and writes Scarlet Ink by Dave Anderson - Big Tech Careers & Leadership - and so can also talk more freely.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eaNCy9xq 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eWi8YqJd 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eDAHZzC7 

Brought to you by:

WorkOS — The modern identity platform for B2B SaaS https://workos.com 
Modal⁠ — The cloud platform for building AI applications https://lnkd.in/ePGPk2py 
Vanta — Automate compliance and simplify security with Vanta http://vanta.com/pragmatic 

---

Three interesting takeaways I noted:

𝟭. 𝗕𝗼𝘁𝗵 𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮 𝗴𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗻. Teams get to decide how they build things and what tech stack they use – and they are also responsible for oncall. Pretty much all engineers at Amazon are oncall for their team’s services on a rotation.

𝟮. 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗻 𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝘀𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗼𝘂𝘀𝗹𝘆 – 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝘁 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀. For SEV-1 outages (the highest severity) is common for VPs of Engineering or even SVPs to join in on the incident call. There is usually then a separate call for managers and individual contributors (ICs). This is extremely rare at most other Big Tech companies – and it shows how much Amazon cares about customer experience and operational excellence.

𝟯. 𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻’𝘀 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝘂𝗹𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀 𝗶𝘁 𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗶𝗲𝗿 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗺𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗿 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝘂𝗽𝘀 – 𝘄𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗶𝘀 𝗿𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗕𝗶𝗴 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀. At Amazon, most engineering teams are already run like a startup, use AWS infra, and embrace the frugality value. Transitioning to a scrappy startup is usually much easier because of this – and it could be a reason why startups also like hiring from Amazon, and ex-Amazon engineers do well in smaller companies as well.

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
A surprising thing I keep hearing hiring managers recruiting software engineers: LinkedIn job postings are practically useless. Apparently, this is an open secret within this group.

Repeated complaints echoed by multiple engineering managers / Head of Engineering folks hiring:

1. Lots of applications feel like they are "fake": a profile that doesn't feel likes is a real person

2. 95%+ of applicants are unqualified. As in they clearly have not read the job advert and applied when they lack the skills or experience the job described (may this be location, or even discipline)

3. LinkedIn keeps agressively suggesting to post free jobs when companies advertise jobs outside of LinkedIn: but the catch is that after receiving 50 applicants, companies need to pay for the job to keep being shown to LinkedIn members. However, of those 50 inbound applicants (that usually arrive within 24 hours of posting) most are unqualified (see the second point!)

Right now, most startups I talk with are looking for other ways to reach qualified candidates than LI jobs. Referrals are the only sure way that work (even if it's not scaleable). Reaching out to people over LinkedIn (aka sourcing) also seems to work better - even though it's more expensive.

It's an odd job market in tech, with both solid engineers struggling to hear back from jobs they are qualified for, and companies struggling to find solid engineers for jobs they post.

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
John Ousterhout is a legend in software design - and so a great person to ask: "How will AI coding tools change software engineering and design?"

John is the author of A Philosophy of Software Design, taught software design at Stanford, (possibly the first and only software design class for university students) created the Tcl programming language - and currently is busy contributing to the Linux Kernel (!)

In this episode of The Pragmatic Engineer, John joins me to talk about why design still matters and how most teams struggle to get it right. We dive into his book A Philosophy of Software Design, unpack the difference between top-down and bottom-up approaches, and explore why some popular advice, like writing short methods or relying heavily on TDD, does not hold up, according to John.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eXJcY5qN 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/ehmw887M 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eDqykTaJ

Brought to you by our wonderful sponsors:

•⁠ CodeRabbit⁠⁠ — Cut code review time and bugs in half. Use the code PRAGMATIC to get one month free. https://www.coderabbit.ai/
•⁠ Modal⁠ — The cloud platform for building AI applications. Try it at https://lnkd.in/ePGPk2py

---

Three of my takeaways from talking with John:

𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗜 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲. Currently, AI coding tools and agents are akin to “tactical tornadoes” that code fast, fix issues fast… while creating new issues and adding tech debt. John doesn’t see the current tools being able to replace high-level design. And so software design could be more important than before – thanks to more code being written than before!

𝟮. 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺. How do you take a large system and divide it into smaller units that you can implement relatively independently?

John believes that the most important idea for all of computer science is just this – decomposition. If you can break up complicated problems into smaller parts: you can solve so many problems!

𝟯. 𝗧𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗗𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 (𝗧𝗗𝗗) 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀 𝗮𝗴𝗮𝗶𝗻𝘀𝘁 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻. John firmly believes that TDD is counter-productive because it forces thinking about the small details before thinking about the high-level design. This observation could explain why TDD has not gained much traction in the last decade or so!

... and a plenty more more food for thought in our discussion!
