posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Why did Meta build its own internal developer tooling instead of using industry-standard solutions like GitHub? And what do their systems "Sandcastle," "Landcastle", "Butterflybot" and "Phabricator" do?

Tomas Reimers, former Meta engineer and co-founder of Graphite, joins the show to talk about Meta's custom developer tools – many of which were years ahead of the industry.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eh6i3fm4 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/e2qSE2Xm
  
• Apple: https://lnkd.in/e_8wE2uC 

Brought to you by our wonderful sponsors:

• Swarmia — The engineering intelligence platform for modern software organizations. Start a free trial at https://lnkd.in/eii3Q6z8

• Sentry — Error and performance monitoring for developers. Get 3 months of the Team Plan for free at https://lnkd.in/e58sfumb

---

Three of my biggest takeaways from our conversation:

𝟭. “𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝘀” 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀 𝗮 𝗹𝗼𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀. However, it makes less sense when working on, e.g., open source projects. Perhaps this is a reason that GitHub has not added support for this workflow — even though it’s popular inside companies like Meta or Uber. We previously did a deepdive on Stacked Diffs (and why you should know about them).

𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗯𝗲 𝗮𝗻 𝗶𝗻𝗱𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘆-𝘄𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗽𝗼𝘀: at mid-sized and larger scaleups and tech companies. Tomas sees a lot of scaleups they work with the move from polyrepos (several repositories) to monorepos. Moving to a monorepo is still a lot of work and requires custom tooling: and this is why it was limited to the largest tech companies in the past. Interesting to hear about this change!

𝟯. 𝗔𝗜 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄𝘀. We’ll see more code churned out by AI: but an engineer needs to review it before it goes out. It’s a good question how we can stick to thorough code reviews when it is so tempting to just say, “Looks good to me (LGTM)” and have the seemingly correct code merged.

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Last month AI crawlers generated 90% of my site's traffic, forcing me to pay $90 in data penalties. It was the point I had enough:

As context, this was for my side project, TechPays (techpays.com) which sees about 1,000 visitors per day (human visitors that is!), and around 30-40GB in outbound data generated per month. This has been flat for a few years straight.

Until AI crawlers showed up a few months ago, that is! They started to generate more and more data, with seemingly senseless crawling (re-crawling the same pages daily, or even hourly, crawling empty pages etc). Last month, the site broke the 500GB outbound data barrier for the first time - which was the plan included in current plan on my hosting provider, Render. And which tier is more than sensible for this project. From then, I had to pay $30 per additional 100GB data: so it cost me $90 just last month. And there seemed no slowing down in data going up thanks to these crawlers (e.g. crawlers identifying themselves as Meta AI, ImagesiftBot, DotBot were all big offenders)

I didn't want to keep paying for AI crawler training - especially as data kept going up, with more and more AI crawlers blatantly ignoring robots.txt.

I tried solving the problem by blocking IPs of the worst offending bots at fist - and then decided to try out moving my nameservers to Cloudflare, proxying DNS records to resolve through their service (meaning the domain resolves to a Cloudflare IP, Cloudflare vets it decides to forward to my actual servers), and turning on AI crawler blocking (this one: https://lnkd.in/eKhCqKK5 )

It seems to have worked: my site's monthly outbound data back to ~40GB per month, down from 800GB (!!) There should be no impact on human visitors - save for potentially Cloudflare occasionally doing a bot check.

I have no affiliation with Cloudflare: and wanted to solve this problem the simplest and cheapest way possible. This solution is free (!!) and also comes with no lock-in: as I can always change nameservers to e.g. the ones of my domain provider, or another nameserver (I don't host my domains on Cloudflare just use their nameserver)

Cloudflare is clearly subsidizing their bandwidth costs and bot blocking functionality - and creating a lot of goodwill for devs, like myself. I just want this small side project to operate normally and not be subject to increasingly hostile AI crawler "attacks" for which I'm on the hook for paying for increased resource costs (e.g. bandwidth, CPU etc).

(As a bonus, I turned on Cloudflare's "AI Labyrinth" feature that uses AI-generated content to slow down, confuse, and waste the resources of AI Crawlers and other bots that don’t respect robots.txt limitations https://lnkd.in/eE4mkrch It's a kind of "payback!")

posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
A Shopify webshop using AI images scammed me and American Express refuses to do a chargeback. A cautionary story on how dropshipping scams work (and why you should probably stop purchasing from small webshops):

(This story is real - I'm posting this to warn about online shopping scams and to dispel the myth that using a credit card to shop will mean you are safe and you can always do a chargeback if scammed. I learned my lesson - but I'm not the first or last to fall for this more sophisticated scam that renders chargebacks useless.)

For a kids birthday, ordered a "hyper realistic robot bunny" that came as a recommendation. It's priced down from €50 to €25, and the website is for a local, Dutch company, the full website in Dutch.

I got the package, delivered from France. What I received was a perhaps €2 stuffed animal that is not a robot, is not realistic, and is nothing of what was advertised. (If you look closer, the image is clearly AI-generated! Look at the "graphics" on the box. I did not look closer.)

The website offered 30-day no questions asked returns though. And this is where the scam plays out:

1. The webshop initially wants to offer credits only (25% in credits, 50% in credits etc)

2. I stand my ground, stating I am entitled for a return.

3. They finally send me a return address in mainland China (in Hangzhou), stating I need to send the package back with tracking. This would cost €20-30 (roughly the value of the good!) - and the T&C did not indicate that there's any Chinese entity involved! As far as I was concerned, I purchased from a local, Dutch company, with a local address - which local address is even on the website itself.

4. I file a chargeback with my credit card provider, American Express, for fraud.

5. American Express refuses to file a chargeback, stating that a per the merchant's terms and conditions, they did not state exactly where the return address should be!

They tell me they understand I am disappointed, but advise me to next time read the T&C closer and email the merchant before purchasing to confirm the return address.

6. I report the site to Shopify for fraud... nothing happens (Shopify hosts the webshop, sends emails for eg orders, facilitates payments processing, and so on.)

This only cost me €50 (ordered 2 of these bunnies). But I've learned to forget how using your credit card to shop online protects you (it does not!) and learned to no longer trust independent webshops.

Back to ordering from Amazon, Bol - and to getting kids toys in the brick-and-mortar shops!

Btw the scam website is still live, posting it as education on how convincing they are these days: https://lnkd.in/eiH7qqz3

text:
AI crawlers are wrecking the open internet: and I’m now being hit for the bill for their training.

I have a side project I barely talk about called TechPays .com. It’s a site where devs can submit comp packages and browse ones from countries like the Netherlands, Germany, UK etc.

It has not seen much traffic - and traffic has been on a slow decline over the last year - on average around 1,000 visitors/day. Bandwidth usage of the site was ~100GB/month. (Just sharing this as context: it’s a helpful site, just one I did not have much time to spend on - and traffic shows how it’s a side project)

Until recently that is: when Meta’s AI crawler + other bots like Imagesiftbot have started to mindlessly crawl the site: pushed it to 700GB+ per month in traffic. The site is hosted on Render where 500GB/month included, above it’s $30 per 100GB. So this month I’m paying $90 just for the training of these LLMs.

The irony is how the bots - including Meta! - blatantly ignore the robots.txt on the site that tells them “please stay away” (here it is: https://lnkd.in/emYRFJbR )

I’m upset - and have had enough.

As the next step, I’ll try moving my domain’s DNS to Cloudflare who have a supposed solution to block these bots:

If it doesn’t work, I would either need to look for a host that allows terabytes of bandwidth on their plan. Or consider closing this side project: as 90% of traffic is bots, not humans.

This is how these AI crawlers kill the open internet: if my small site struggles with this problem of being forced to serve overwhelmingly bot traffic, then so will other sites.

Some sites will add Captchas to try and detect humans, some will move to Cloudflare (like me - to see if it works), and some will simply shut down. The open internet is getting less open and more walled thanks to this “AI crawler greed.”

[As always - I have no affiliation with Cloudflare. It’s the only solution that seems simple enough to try - that will hopefully block all the bot traffic.]

text:
How do you take a new product idea, and turn it into a successful product? Figma Slides started as a hackathon project a year and a half ago – and today it’s a full-on product, with more than 4.5M slide decks created by users. 

I talked with two founding engineers on this project, on how they built it: Jonathan Kaufman and Noah Finer shares details.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/e8FBKvkJ 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/ew94aUfz 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/ePPekKs5 

Brought to you by our wonderful sponsors:

• Graphite — The AI developer productivity platform https://gt.dev/pragmatic

• Sonar — Code quality and code security for ALL code https://lnkd.in/eGWgj-is 

• Chronosphere — The observability platform built for control https://lnkd.in/exbgnvch

Two interesting takeaways from this episode:

𝟭. 𝗙𝗶𝗴𝗺𝗮’𝘀 𝘄𝗲𝗯 𝗮𝗽𝗽 𝘂𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗮 𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗺𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗖++ 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲. It’s rare for web engineers to need to use C++ — but Figma is an exception, as the design tool built their own rendering engine. This means that frontend engineers need to get into the opinionated C++ codebase to make changes — though rewriting some parts of the code to TypeScript is currently on the way.

𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 “𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗖𝗿𝗶𝘁” 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗻 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 — 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘂𝗻𝗶𝗾𝘂𝗲! — 𝗼𝗻𝗲. Figma’s engineering reviews/discussions are called EngCrit. An engineer presents their plan or idea in a FigJam board, and other engineers join in to give their feedback. This process has parallels with the RFC or design doc process — but it feels a lot more lightweight. Plus, it dogfoods Figma’s own products!

Thanks a lot to Jonathan and Noah for offering a behind-the-scenes peek on the challenges and interesting learnings in building this new product!

text:
An encouraging story about how as a developer, you can teach AI engineering yourself: and probably should! And why hiring "AI agencies" is not a good idea:

Ryan Cogswell works at HR tech company, Data Solutions International. He joined the company 25 years ago, and was the first engineering hire. When AI tools came along, DSI decided to build a relatively simple first AI feature in their HR system that summarized comments for employee engagement purposes. 

Neither Ryan nor any of the other 4 devs had expertise in AI and LLMs, so the company contracted an external agency which offered a fixed, timeboxed offer to scope the project. Here’s how it went:

Month #1: the agency goes and builds stuff, and shares LLM outputs with DSI

Month #2: while continuing to iterate on desired outputs, devs at DSI ask the agency for access to how things work. They get access to scripts and notebooks

Month #3: the agency lays out the proposed architecture for the project.

And the proposed architecture was the one below. Very complicated, estimated 6-9 months to build (!!), and the quoted cost for *operating* it would have been more than the operational cost for the whole product!

So Ryan taught himself how to build LLM applications... and with a few colleagues, built the whole thing with a fraction of a time (two months!), and cost (on a proper budget!).

Neither Ryan, nor his colleagues touched LLMs before. But it's not exactly rocket science how to build on top of LLMs - especially with services like AWS Bedrock, APIs from AI companies, and so on.

If you're a developer and not yet taught yourself LLM basics: I hope this is good encouragement to do so.

And if you're a business owner thinking of hiring an external agency to help with GenAI: consider this actual story, and how engineers can pick up this pretty easily.

More real-world stories from seven software engineers who taught themselves to become "AI engineers" in today's The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/ea5ju-cs

posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Two strange thing I hear from devs on the job market an hiring managers hiring devs:

1. Experienced candidates on the job market tell me it's very hard to get interviews compared to a few years ago

2. Hiring managers tell me it's very hard to find qualified candidates compared to a few years ago! And far, far, far more effort....

By itself: neither is strange. But both happening the same time: this very much is!

There are a lot of reasons for both: tools that spit out AI-altered applications are one; a cooling tech job market with fewer openings but more qualified candidates; video/remote interviews carrying less signal thanks to AI tooling etc

We've had a few decades where doing great work on the job, but not being someone who goes out to test the waters & interview was usually a worse career strategy than doing "meh" work on the job, but interviewing frequently and job hopping often.

I wonder if this tide will turn.

Warm referrals ("I worked with X, they are amazing, we should see if they would be interested in this job we're recruiting for") seem like they will become a lot more relevant as inbound applications become far, far, far more noisy...

text:
What are "Growth Engineers?" They are software engineers focusing on running fast experiments to find quick wins that grows the business.

Alexey Komissarouk was both a growth engineer, and later the Head of Growth Engineering at MasterClass.

He shared a few interesting observations on how growth engineering differs from product engineering (which is what most software engineers do, who work on a product). Three interesting differences:

1. MarTech tools are VERY relevant to growth engineers! 

These are tools which promise marketing teams that they can do a lot of things that generate revenue – all without engineering involvement. Marketing teams buy these tools – from CRMs to A/B testing and email marketing tools – and then expect that it will “just” work. Growth engineers usually have to integrate these into the existing stack the company has.

2. Growth Engineering teams have a lot less stability than other dev teams. 

The theory of Growth Engineering sounds great: create experiments quickly; test if they work; keep the ones that do and throw the rest away. Except this means that Growth Engineers work a lot more in a flux, and rarely ever have long-running projects.

3. Working in a Growth team will most certainly make you a more rounded engineer. 

At mid-sized and larger companies, it can get hard to work closely with the business, and especially with marketing. On a Growth team, you are forced to do this.

Finally, some advice from Alexey on what makes a great growth engineer. Curisity is non-negotiable, according to him.

Read the deepdive on Growth Engineering in The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/eBp6zz2x

text:
Why did Linux win?

Linux is the most widespread OS globally: it’s the dominant choice for servers and embedded devices. It powers 4 billion+ Android devices and even runs inside a good chunk of iPhones (thanks to being the firmware on the 5G Qualcomm modems - as the embedded OS) (And its also a desktop OS that many different Linux distributions are built on top of - even if not the most widespread one in that category.) 

How did an open source project become this widespread - silently taking over the world without most people noticing?

Turns out, devs being selfish and solving their own problems works surprisingly well for the broader ecosystem.

From Greg Kroah-Hartman, Linux kernel maintainer for 25+ years and one of the 3 Linux Kernel Foundation Fellows (the other two are Linus Torvalds and Shuah Khan).

Full episode:

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/ez3QySat

posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
"Most people don’t understand how Linux deals with 4,000+ devs from 500+ companies a year with only email, git and no project managers."

When Greg Kroah-Hartman wrote this to me as an interesting topic to talk about around Linux development - I also did not understand! (Greg is one of the three Linux Foundation kernel fellows - the other two being Linus Torvalds and Shuah Khan). 

A project of this complexity *surely* needs some kind of project management - and dedicated roles for eg steering its direction, planning the roadmap, ensuring the right thing is being built?

Apparently, Linux is different - and unique in ways I’ve not seen any other large project operate.

The Linux kernel can afford to have no project managers, product managers or TPMs because these activities happen at companies and teams contributing to Linux (before) - or companies integrating the kernel into their products or Linux distributions (after).

He explains how the Linux kernel pulls this unlikely setup off (and why it’s unlikely any other project could replicate it - or want to aim to copy it)

The full episode:

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/ez3QySat

text:
Linux is the most widespread operating system, globally – but how is it built? Few people are better to answer this than Greg Kroah-Hartman: 

Greg has been a Linux kernel maintainer for 25 years, and one of the 3 Linux Kernel Foundation Fellows (the other two are Linus Torvalds and Shuah Khan). Greg manages the Linux kernel’s stable releases, and is a maintainer of multiple kernel subsystems.

We cover the inner workings of Linux kernel development, exploring everything from how changes get implemented to why its community-driven approach produces such reliable software. Greg shares insights about the kernel's unique trust model and makes a case for why engineers should contribute to open-source projects.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eySTgGmP 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/ez3QySat 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eszSqVwQ

• Vanta — Automate compliance and simplify security with Vanta. Get $1,000 off at http://vanta.com/pragmatic

Check out all of our current sponsor and their offerings: https://lnkd.in/eMi6CJiu
----

One of the most surprising things I learned about Linux: although 4,000 developers contribute to the project from 500 companies per year, and has around 800 kernel maintainers, the project is run with email, git... and that's about it! No dedicated project managers, no regular meetings across the group. It's a truly fascinating and unique model (and only applies to the Linux kernel project: not to Linux distributions.) We go into more detail about this topic with Greg in the podcast as well.

text:
What is "Submit Queue", "Local Dev Analytics", "DevPods?" Devs inside Uber use these tools every day: and Gautam Korlam built several of these during his 9-year tenure at Uber (!!)

Gautam was mobile engineer #9 at Uber, and the founding engineer on Uber's mobile platform team - and he's now the cofounder of Gitar (automating code maintenance with AI). If you are interested in modern dev tools and how Uber works from the inside, this episode is for you:

Three interesting parts of the conversation:

𝟭. 𝗦𝘂𝗯𝗺𝗶𝘁 𝗤𝘂𝗲𝘂𝗲: Uber bult a complex merge system to dela with the large number of commits, where each commit had to run long-running CI tests. It’s a problem that smaller and mid-sized companies don’t have, but Uber had: and so they scratched their own itch.

𝟮. 𝗟𝗼𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘁𝗶𝗰𝘀 (𝗟𝗗𝗔): years ago, Uber started to measure the experience that devs had. Like how long did a build take, locally? How much CPU is used? They used this data to improve internal tooling.

𝟯. 𝗔𝗜 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁: this is happening. “Vibe coding” leads to faster prototyping. Gautam believes junior engineers will thrive with AI tools because they will hit the ground running faster, and will be free of biases that hold back more experienced developers.

text:
Imagine interviewing a candidate who looks like a very strong coder. Almost extending an offer. But turns out, the candidate does not exist. This actually happened:

Security startup Vidoc Security Lab nearly hired a candidate who most likely used a high-quality AI filter. The reason they dodged this was because the background of the candidate did not add up in the final round of the interview, conducted by cofounder Klaudia Kloc. Learning from this near-miss, they added an in-person interview for their full-remote hiring loop.

And then, a few months later, cofounder Dawid Moczadło interviewed a candidate with a sloppy AI filter. He then confronted this person, asking him to do something seemingly simple: put his hand in front of his face.

The candidate refused.

The scary part: Vidoc Security is a tiny startup with 6 people in total. This organization feels like it's pretty organized, and could be coming from a state actor. The first fake candidate knocked on their door 2 years ago, when the startup was only 2 people (!!)

If you are hiring for full-remote, you probably need to take into account that fake candiades who are highly qualified coders might want to infintrate your company.

Today's The Pragmatic Engineer shares all details - including CVs and other investigation done by Vidoc - about this specific incident. Plus advice on what you might need to do to avoid this. For one: recording interviews is likely must-do, and verifying the identity of the person you interview before extending an offer could no longer be a "nice to have."

Read all details here: https://lnkd.in/eGU4vc3S

posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Hype cycle trends across VC-funded startups:

2000s: “Website for X”
2008-2015: “Mobile app for X”
2015- 2017: “Uber for X”
2018-2019: “Low code/no code for X”
2020-2022: “Blockchain for X”
2023-2024: “AI for X”
2025: “AI agents for X”

The last 1-2 months, I’m seeing an absolute flurry of “AI agent for X” startups funded, products launching and PR blitzes prepared.

As with every hype cycle, there will be lots of winners! Ones early to market, or building a practical solution to a pressing problem.

But many startups don’t realize their unique idea is… the most common one across the market! And how competition will be absolutely cutthroat. Building yet another generic AI agent product for “knowledge work” simply won’t stand out (it’s the most common pitch I hear these days.)

Good luck to every startup building a product around AI agents. Just plan for tough competition, and make it clear why you’re not just “yet another generic AI agent startup” from the get go.

posted_at.relative: 5 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Most engineering leaders and founders come from a backend engineering background. But not everyone: Balint Orosz has always been a "design-first" engineer.

Balint is my brother, and we're opposites in how I've always felt more pull for the backend, and he's been always building intuitive UIs. We go into what it means to start with the UI and design, and how he built Craft Docs - an app that won Apple's App of the Year award.

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eTYpWnbe 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eaPvT7c9
 
• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eqYr-t3g 

Brought to you by our wonderful sponsors:

• WorkOS — The modern identity platform for B2B SaaS https://workos.com

• Augment Code — AI coding assistant that pro engineering teams love https://lnkd.in/ddCkDrxF

• The Software Engineer’s Guidebook: Written by me (Gergely) – now out in audio form as well https://lnkd.in/dEkcvW2T

----

Three of my takeaways:

𝟭. 𝗗𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻-𝗳𝗼𝗰𝘂𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗼 𝗳𝗶𝘁 𝗶𝗻. Engineers who focus on backend and distributed systems can usually verbalize their impact clearer, and could see faster career growth – including getting into leadership positions. This creates a reinforcing cycle: as most engineering executives have a backend engineering background: they will recognize and reward backend contributions more.

𝟮. 𝗔 𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗻 𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗮𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗲𝗱, 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗲𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆. Craft has a total of 4 engineers building the respective apps for:
• iOS
• iPad
• MacOS
• VisionOS

They can do this, because it’s a single codebase! A single codebase also means that all features built on one platform immediately work on all others.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗼 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘀: 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗺𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗮 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵. Craft does not use Apple’s UI components: they don’t use SwiftUI or Autolayout – something that 95% or more of iOS apps all take advantage of.

Craft, instead, built their own components from scratch, and came up with their own layout and animations system. This is a lot more work at first: but it’s how Craft can do smooth animations that most apps are unable to do so – and it’s a reason engineers at Apple have asked the team “how are you able to do such a smooth animation on the navigation bar?” (The native iOS navigation bar cannot be animated like Craft does it) Craft can do all this thanks to building and maintaining their own components. It’s simpler to do than most would assume: in the episode, we look at actual code.

Tech compensation is trimodal: meaning a software eng can make 2-4x the compensation, depending on what company they work at.

Today, we validate this theory with more data points than ever: 20,000+, coming from Levels.fyi. We share data points with specific compensation numbers from the US, UK and India; median and 75th percentile values, and companies that offer the highest median compensation.

For example, in the US, for entry-level software engineers (0-2 YoE):
- Tier 1: $105K (50th percentile), $130K (75th percentile)
- Tier 2: $180K (50th percentile), $203K (75th percentile)
- Tier 3: $208K (50th percentile), $247K (75th percentile)

Thanks very much to Hakeem S., Zuhayeer Musa, Zaheer Mohiuddin and the Levels.fyi team for helping with this research.

text:
How useful is DORA and the "DORA 4?"? No better person to answer than the creator of this popular methodology, Nicole Forsgren:

DORA (short for DevOps Research and Assessment) is a framework used to measure and improve developer productivity and software delivery performance. It's widely discussed in the book Accelerate - which Nicole is a co-author of. DORA defines these four key metrics (the DORA 4):

• Deployment frequency
• Lead time for changes
• Change failure rate,
• Time to restore service

See the full episode where Nicole shares a lot more practical advice on how how to think about measuring, and how to measure developer productivity (including with AI tools becoming more popular):

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eTmtajFa 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eHNS__Uv

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 5 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Nicole Forsgren is the brains behind DORA, lead author of the SPACE framework, and the co-author of Accelerate. Hardly a better person to share how to think about, measure and improve developer productivity!

This is what we do in today's The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast episode.  Stream the full episode:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eTmtajFa 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eHNS__Uv 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/ezcUg7CY 

---
𝗕𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗯𝘆

• DX — An engineering intelligence platform designed by leading researchers. Learn more at https://lnkd.in/ejixzEsP

• Sentry — Error and performance monitoring for developers. Get 3 months of the Team Plan for free at https://lnkd.in/e8VS5YcA 
---

Three of my takeaways from this episode:

𝟭. 𝗠𝗲𝗮𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗼𝗳 𝗣𝗥𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗮 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗼𝗻

Measuring any single one “output” can be misused: and when devs know it is being measured, they will optimize for it.

However, measuring PRs is important: but to do it well, don’t look at it as an individual performance metric. Instead, use it to understand how well (or not well) systems across the team and company are working. For example, what systems are getting in the way of PRs taking long to merge?

𝟮. 𝗗𝗢𝗥𝗔 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝗣𝗔𝗖𝗘 𝗯𝗼𝘁𝗵 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗶𝗿 𝗼𝘄𝗻 𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

DORA is very well-defined in the metrics it measures. However, a massive limitation it has is how it only measures from commit to production.

SPACE can be used to measure the complete developer workflow – including e.g. planning, coding, and even post-release. In turn, it is a more vague framework where you need to put in more effort to make it useful for your envirnment.

𝟯. 𝗔𝗜 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝗱𝗼𝗻’𝘁 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗘𝘅 𝗳𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹𝘀

DORA and SPACE are still relevant. AI tools might be able to improve iteration speed, or make certain tasks more helpful. It’s really an open question how exactly this will play out. There’s a fair chance these tools significantly change how we do development. So if you’re a dev: experiment with them!

Check out the full episode (including the transcript and summary) here: https://lnkd.in/ez9fMCfh And thank you to Nicole for this outstanding conversation!

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 5 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
In May 2023, a live streaming world record was set with 32 million concurrent viewers watching the finale of the IPL cricket game. How was this system built?

Ashutosh Agrawal was the architect behind this system, and he walks us through how live streaming at scale works, how the system was built and tested, and other interesting learnings.

Watch or listen:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/dpfvrWjQ 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/dzq3ckCu 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/dHQ4WcU3 

---
Brought to you by our wonderful sponsors:

• WorkOS — The modern identity platform for B2B SaaS https://workos.com/
• CodeRabbit — Cut code review time and bugs in half https://www.coderabbit.ai/ (use the code PRAGMATIC to get one month free)
• Augment Code — AI coding assistant that pro engineering teams love https://lnkd.in/ddCkDrxF
---

Three of my biggest takeaways:

𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗹𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝘆 𝗹𝗼𝗴𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹. 

In the episode, Ashutosh explains how the live streaming system works, starting from the physical cameras on-site, through the production control room (PCR), streams being sliced-and-diced, and the HLS protocol (HTTP Live Streaming) used.

𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮 𝗟𝗢𝗧 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗼𝗳𝗳𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘆 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗹𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴! 

The tradeoffs between server load, latency, server resources vs client caching are hard decisions to make. Want to reduce the server load? Serve longer chunks to clients, resulting in fewer requests per minute, per client… at the expense of clients potentially lagging more behind. This is just one of many possible decisions to make.

𝟯. “𝗚𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗱𝗮𝘆” 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘂𝗰𝗵 𝗮 𝗻𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗹𝗼𝗮𝗱 𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗽𝘁. 

The team at Jio would simulate “game day” load months before the event. They did tell teams when the load test will start: but did not share anything else! Preparing for a “Game day” test is a lot of work, but it can pay off to find parts of the system that shutter under extreme load.

See more takeaways and a summary here: https://lnkd.in/dTKTRfiA 

Thanks Ashutosh for all these behind-the-scene details!

posted_at.relative: 5 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
A big surprise is how many paid subscribers for The Pragmatic Engineer come from Learnerbly: a platform to spend L&D budgets with a single click.

Closer to 1,000 subscribers come from this one platform. Which is honestly very surprising - and shows the difference it makes when something can be claimed and used by one click, instead of going though a more painful expensing process.

Their clever value proposal is this:

1. Most people at a company don't use their learning and development budget when it's painful to spend or invoice. Or they will buy one or two "large" spends just to not waste it (but pay for stuff they won't really use that much)

2. What if it was just a click to request a newsletter, book, course? From a catalog covering most professional development areas. People would more likely use it for stuff they need.

3. They offer a single invoice for a company, instead of hundreds/thousands of expense reports to approve (this is a major pain point for most L&D programs)

4. Vetted providers

5. Including not-yet-onboarded sources: this is SO clever. Learnerbly also fulfill requests from providers not yet onboarded - when it's an educational request or in-line with company L&D policy. This is how my newsletter got added! Abbout 30 people requested the newsletter in a span of a few weeks, and their team manually fulfilled it, initially. After this became too repetitive / inefficient: they reached out to see how we can integrate it. I built the integration so fulfillment is practically done in minutes with automation (thank you NodeJS, TypeScript and APIs) - and 💥

Their model seems to work pretty well!

This is them: https://www.learnerbly.com - and they also offer The Software Engineer's Guidebook (and so many other tech books, courses, newsletters etc)

(My only affiliation with them is how they offer my newsletter and book)

posted_at.relative: 5 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Where did TikTok’s software engineers leave to?

The past six months has been something of a Doomsday scenario-esque countdown for TikTok, as the start date of its ban in the US crept ever closer. In the event, TikTok did indeed go offline for a few hours on 19 January, before President Trump gave the social network a stay of execution lasting 75 days.

How has this uncertainty affected software engineers at the Chinese-owned social network? 

According to data shared exclusively with The Pragmatic Engineer by Live Data Technologies, which tracks real-time live job change data across more than 160M professionals, this is how:

It seems the uncertainty has motivated TikTok engineers to interview and quit when they get an offer. Still, I find it surprising that hardly any scaleups are popular destinations among TikTok leavers. To me, it indicates that people likely quit for better *liquid* total compensation packages; which may be why publicly traded companies are the most popular destination. It could also mean that public companies are hiring more.

---

Follow me for regular insights on how the software engineering industry is changing. Subscribe to The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw for more in-depth analysis of these.

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 6 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What is AI Engineering? No better person to talk about it than Chip Huyen, author of the O'Reilly book "AI Engineering" that is an excellent read.

Listen to or watch the whole episode:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eGAWT5kM 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/egZ6HWB3 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/eWEEZUTd 

--- 
Brought to you by:

🌟 Swarmia — The engineering intelligence platform for modern software organizations. https://lnkd.in/eii3Q6z8

🌟 Graphite — The AI developer productivity platform. https://gt.dev/pragmatic

🌟 Vanta — Automate compliance and simplify security with Vanta. http://vanta.com/pragmatic

----

3  takeaways from this episode:

1. “AI engineering” feels closer to software engineering than to ML engineering. 

The term itself is very new, and “AI engineering” evolved from ML engineering. A big difference is that thanks to LLMs being easy to use (both via APIs, and locally) “AI engineering” is much more about building a product first – and later on, getting around to tweaking the model itself.

2. To get good at AI engineering, focus on the basics. 

Understand what an LLM is (and how it works), how to evaluate them, how to use RAG, what finetuning is, and how to optimize inference. All of these techniques are foundational, and will remain important in a few years’ time as well.

3. Solving the problem is more important than using the latest AI tools. 

Amusingly, a lot of teams miss this part: and they build overcomplicated AI solutions that do practically nothing for the business.

Thank you, Chip, for this great chat. I can not recommend her new book, AI Engineering, enough either.

text:
The monthly views for The Pragmatic Engineer, and how it changed since starting the newsletter in Aug 2020.

Still surreal - because all issues are long, in-depth, take time to read and digest, and cover a topic that is anything but mainstream (software engineering at Big Tech and startups).

When I started the newsletter, I did it because I didn't find any online publication covering this area *in-depth*, on a regular basis, and from a practical point of view, relatable as a developer or engineering manager.

It just comes to shows that there's more interest for in-depth writing than I ever imagined would be! And that you never know if something unproven would be of interest, unless you give it a go.

Looking back, it was a lucky accident that I went full-time to start this newsletter back in 2020, instead of either doubling down to finish my book, The Software Engineer's Guidebook (which I did finish years later), or try to raise funding for startup to build platform engineering tooling.

As for "what worked?" I cannot point to a single thing. Every year, I experiment with new approaches - some that work (like the Industry Pulse column, and I keep doing it) and some that do not (like the Community Thread that got retired after a few issues). A summary of the changes I tried out, and some that stuck, over the years: https://lnkd.in/e8ARk4ay

posted_at.relative: 6 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
As a software engineer, it is inherently satisfying to see an open approach beat close approaches when it comes to tech innovations.

Linux is open: Windows is closed. Devs on the server side today overwhelmingly choose Linux as the preferred infrastructure component. It’s less lock-in, more customization and lower cost to do so.

Deepseek, Llama, Mistral are open : OpenAI, Gemini and others others are closed AI models. Devs building AI applications are likely to choose open models - assuming capabilities will be similar enough. The reasoning is the same: less lock-in, and likely to be lower cost.

Closed approaches winning almost always lead to monopolies.

Open approaches commoditize the technology: and *more* innovation happens a layer above. In the case of LLMs, the recently released and open Deepseek models being nearly as effective (sometimes more effective!) as OpenAI models - but costs a fraction to run, and can be self-hosted or modified - means it becomes cheaper to build and operate AI products.

We’ve seen the impact of an open operating system becoming as capable as a closed one with Linux vs Windows: which competition has been very good in improving closed operating systems like Windows, Windows Server, and even Mac, as well! I hope we will see a similar outcome and healthy competition with LLMs. If we do, everyone building AI products and features will win.

(Everyone, except vendors that depended on a closed approach to charge a premium for their models, where their market dominance depends on maintaining secrecy and competition not being able to reproduce their model performance)

text:
We're at a time when Google search increasingly feels like an "ads engine" serving advertisers, and not a "search engine" that helps people find what they need, quickly and efficiently.

Excited to do a deepdive with two startups disrupting Google: Perplexity and Kagi. They are two search engines I've replaced my Google search usage the last 12 months - and have not looked back.

Fun facts:

Kagi uses the Crystal programming language - an exotic choice!

Perplexity runs multiple models for *every* query. Under the hood they are running multiple models to provide the final answer. They can do this because they've optimized to make these custom models as small as possible. 

Kagi built half a dozen search indexes, like:
• Teclis: index of the non-commercial web
• Tinygem: index for trending news
• Small Web:index of personal blogs and websites
- Perplexity runs seveal LLMs under the hood on each search (including their own!)

Perplexity has already integrated the Deepseek R1 reasoning model in their search engine - in about a day after it was released. They are the first search engine to do this.

In a special collaboration, The Pragmatic Engineer paid subscribers get 12 months of Perplexity Pro, and 3 months of Kagi Ultimate for free (without the need to share payment details.) [Disclaimer: this is not an ad: I was not paid, nor do I receive any form of compensation. I reached out to both companies, asking if they can provide some kind of offer for readers, given the business model of both search engines is subscription-based to experience the full product.]

Read the deepdive in today's free issue The Pragmatic Engineer here: https://lnkd.in/ek8hHcFR

Signs that Audible operates like a cushy monopoly - and that it's "Day 2" at this part of Amazon:

- Has the highest take rate (75% of what customers pay for an audiobook is Audible's to keep. This is 2.5x the heavily criticized take rate of Apple and Google app stores with 30%, by the way)

- Has 10x longer approval times for new audiobooks than any of their competitors (biggest one being Spotify's Audiobooks offering)

And so The Software Engineer's Guidebook audiobook remains available everywhere - except on Audible! Not because I don't want to list the book on Audible: but because Audible cannot be bothered to approve it. In all fairness, as a publisher, it's a win for me, as it pushes people to get the book on platforms that take no more than 30% of a cut. (Even when it will be available on Audible: I will recommend to get it on this platform *only* if you cannot be bothered to use another platform which treats authors like myself more fairly, and does not keep three quarters of the money you pay for an audiobook for themselves.)

posted_at.relative: 6 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Why is observability so hard to do well - and so expensive, in general? What is "Observability 2.0" and is Open Telemetry any good?

In today's episode of The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast, we answer all of these with Charity Majors, co-author of the O'Reilly book "Observability Engineering," former engineer at Parse/Facebook, and cofounder and CTO at honeycomb.io.

Watch it here:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/d-Ey4MTs 

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/dNK9-4fu 

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/dQ6GEZti

• Summary and transcript: https://lnkd.in/d22ZAwXs 

Brought to you by our wonderful sponsors - check out their offerings:

• Sonar — Trust your developers – verify your AI-generated code. https://lnkd.in/dHWi3EAX 
• Vanta — Automate compliance and simplify security with Vanta http://vanta.com/pragmatic

----

Topics we cover in this episode:

• What is observability? Charity’s take
• What is “Observability 2.0?”
• Why Charity is a fan of platform teams
• Why DevOps is an overloaded term: and probably no longer relevant
• What is cardinality? And why does it impact the cost of observability so much?
• How OpenTelemetry solves for vendor lock-in
• Why Honeycomb wrote its own database
• Why having good observability should be a prerequisite to adding AI code or using AI agents
• And more!

--- 

My biggest takeaways:

1. The DevOps movement feels like it’s in its final days, having served its purpose.

2. Lots of people get dashboards wrong! Charity doesn’t think that static dashboards are helpful to engineering teams at all. In fact, misusing dashboards is one of the most common observability practices.

3. Observability will be especially important for AI use cases in these ways:

a) o11y for LLMs: to get data on how they behave and to be able to debug behaviors. This is relevant for teams building and operating AI models.

b) o11y for code generated by AI: the generated code should have the right amount of observability in place. Once the code is deployed to production, developers need to be able to get a sense of how the code is behaving there!

Wild how LLMs have made the business model of StackOverflow obsolete in two years - though, to be fair, there was an ongoing decline without a clear path on how to get people to ask questions (given the site was so aggressive in closing new questions)

The biggest winners seem to be the founders who sold the site for $1.8B in 2021 to private equity. And it's a reminder that private equity takes large risks in these purchases: and might lose a bunch of money on such an investment.

posted_at.relative: 6 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
As a paying user, I am SO fed up with AI chatbots. Here's a story on how these bots can chase away customers:

(The company name is irrelevant, and removed them. They are a vendor I've used for years, really like the service and there's a cool team behind them. They are also fixing this issue - while other companies probably don't know how bad their own AI chatbot experience is)

There's a useful service I've been using for a long time. A few months ago, I noticed they introduced a massive "chat icon" hovering on the bottom right of all pages, when you are logged in. But I didn't need support... until today. Today, I found a problem with this service related to my own account's billing. Something that looks like a potentially big deal. I tap the obvious place to get help: and an AI chatbot opens up.

1. The bot cannot answer my question, because it's not in any FAQ (it recites stuff that is not relevant ofc). It needs details or debugging from my account.

2. I ask it to connect me to a human (a common prompt these days to skip the automation)

3. I get a link to a Zendesk page

4. The Zendesk page no longer exists... likely thanks to this AI bot!

5. I'm now stuck. There's no way to contact the team (as far as I'm concerned!) I'm not interested in getting a response *now* (it's Sunday!) but having *any* way to send my problem so a human sees it. An AI bot that "replies within minutes" but cannot give me anything to work with is useless to me.

6. With no better ideas, I find the CEO on social media and send a DM. I also post a post, tagging the company, asking how I can contact humans, as a longtime paying customer

A few things happen:

- The post blows up (many people have this kind of poor AI agent experience, and can relate)

- The whole company is alerted, from the CEO through the CTO, to the Head of Customer Support... 

- My query is acknowledged

- The CEO and CTO admit they had no idea the chatbot gave this retired page as a contact... as it should not. The beauty of AI agents: they are non-deterministic!

- Turns out, there IS a way to contact humans: hidden behind a small icon on the site. It's noticeable, but barely: especially since adding the AI agent icon that dominates the page. The CTO is pretty surprised I didn't notice this. I truly did not, and trusted the AI agent telling me *THE* way to contact a human  is via a page that does not exist! (AI agents make things up with confidence!)

In the comments, people chimed it with so many similar frustrations from dozens of other vendors. It feels like AI support agents are everywhere: and it's hard to find positive experiences with them.

If you're a startup adding an AI agent to become your primary support channel: consider the damage it could do. And consider that GenAI can make things up, or confidently state incorrect details.

How will you know you are losing paying customers, unless they do what I do, and post it on social media, tagging your company?

text:
Google shipped the Gemini smart assistant to all paying Google Workspace Users. It cannot be turned off, and it continuously hallucinates.

Congratulations on a terrible product release, Google.

As a reminder, I am a customer paying for Google Workspace - because I use it for work. While I appreciate having the opportunity to test non-production ready features as opt-in if and when I have the time and energy to do so: I do not appreciate such net negative features being forced on me, with no way to turn it off.

And I am not joking when I say it cannot be turned off. It's impossible to turn off: as a Google Workspace admin, I've disabled all Gemini features and it's still there. I've turned off all "Smart" features in my Gmail and it's still there.

Some team at Google clearly had the KPI to get to 100% user penetration: auto opt-in combined with no way to turn off a broken feature is one way to do so.

I've been a Google Workspace customer and fan for many years, but this is a moment of this relationship souring.

posted_at.relative: 6 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
When you look at most CTOs and engineering executives at most tech companies: their engineering background is more often than not backend engineering.

Worth considering what this means esp if they lead a co where the main product *is* mobile, web - or to delight customers!

Flashback to a large company where a new CTO joined and they started to mandate unit tests written, code coverage tracked and code reviews to be done. (Backend teams were already doing this, for the most part - more frontend-heavy teams grudgingly went along with it: fewer of them were doing it before.)

Despite the hope and intent: none of this improved the UX of the mobile app that was the main product.

It's a reminder that while backend engineering principles are some of the cleanest and most logical: a different kind of engineering approach (and leadership!) could be more efficient for frontend and mobile-heavy products. Just ask frontend engineers, mobile developers, game developers and designers what the most important things are to build amazing user experiences.

I happen to be much more of a "backend-minded" engineer, and my brother Balint Orosz, founder of Craft Docs is more of a "mobile-minded" engineer. 

This reflection comes as I asked Balint about engineering approaches the Craft team focuses on. Our discussion was far more about design systems, smooth animations, color schemes, keeping things simple and performant, holding off onboarding to new UI frameworks when they don't offer UI/UX/performance benefits just added abstractions - and less about the "usual" topics. 

I wish there was more talk about *this* kind of engineering. To help with it: we'll do a podcast episode in The Pragmatic Engineer podcast on this topic with Balint.

----

Follow me for regular insights on how the software engineering industry is evolving. Subscribe to The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw for more in-depth analysis of these.

text:
GitHub was down, hard, for an hour.

As a company’s code repo is increasingly treated as infra: such an outage can mean that when a repo down —> no deploys possible (even to resolve outages!)

GitHub doesn’t make promises of 4 or 5 nines of reliability (which you might expect from critical infra!) - even though it can be tempting to assume that it does, thanks to its brand. (It does offer SLAs of 99.9% for enterprise customers, and pays customers credits, should it fails to meet those SLAs. More details at: https://lnkd.in/ey_JWD5U )

(The cause of the outage was a config change in GitHub’s load balancer that bounced all requests. It took GitHub an hour to detect and roll back the change. From GitHub’s incident summary: https://lnkd.in/eMdKEuQB)

Perhaps it's also time to accept that managed services - that are relatively cheap, as is GitHub! - will necessarily have downtime? Like GitHub, that charges ~$10-20/account/month. In cases when a team needs more uptime: perhaps it is time to invest more time/effort in mitigating these? A bit like how it's accepted your cloud provider could go down: so you have a backup. Either run cross-zone, cross-region, or even cross-cloud provider.

E.g. have a mirror git repo you use (on another service, or self-hosted) that you can also deploy to prod from, when needed.

A good addition from James Liu in the comments:

"Your self-hosted git installation, how good will the uptime be? GitLab Dedicated advertises 99.5% uptime and 4/8 hours to recovery. 99.5 is almost 2 days of downtime a year.

Better uptime self-hosted? Sure, it can be done, but it will cost you."

text:
Apparently, there is an "Amazon Podcasts." A listener messaged, asking me to add The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast. I did. 

He is now the only listener.

I'm frankly surprised that:

1. There *is* such a thing as Amazon Podcasts - and I hadn't heard of it until now!

2. Just how few people use it (the podcast passed 500,000 listens in December - no one asked for this to be added - until now!)

3. Podcasts need to be manually added - every single one! Many podcast platforms take public podcast feeds and pull them in. Not Amazon Podcasts: you need to create an account, claim a podcast, verify ownership, and then you can add your own podcast

This #3 limitation, combined with the low reach means that Amazon Podcasts will have lower podcast selection will mean even fewer people will use this product.

It's puzzling to see such an under-invested Amazon product. A good question if it will be around the long-term, or if Amazon will change things like automatically adding public podcasts. 

Here is the link to Amazon Podcasts: https://lnkd.in/eJTWJ_BE

And all other platforms you can listen to The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast: https://lnkd.in/e8-aFAtb

text:
Would you still pay $600/year for your email provider if some of your emails turned out to be not emails, but ads? LinkedIn, for some reason, thinks so.

Imagine if Google Workspace (basically, paid Gmail) showed that a new email arrived in your inbox with sender, image etc. But when you clicked on it, it would be an ad about a Google product, no way to reply, just tap links to sign up or learn more.

This is what LinkedIn is doing - except they charge *far* more for LinkedIn Premium than any email provider charges for their offering. And yet feel comfortable in tricking their own customers into thinking they got a new DM: when it’s just an ad that impersonates a LinkedIn employee!

I’ve cancelled LinkedIn Premium after closer to 5 years of being a paying member. LinkedIn really is losing their way. While you will no longer be able to DM me for free if we’re not connected (a big reason I paid for Premium!) you can reach me for free on other platforms that don’t pollute DMs with shady ads: https://lnkd.in/e3Xy-trJ

If anyone from the LinkedIn team reads this: it could be worth taking a long look in the mirror on how badly the product is degrading, and how far the current experience is from LinkedIn’s own #1 value “We put members first.”

It feels to me, you put paying members last, and the Ads team first.

reshared_post.text:
LinkedIn: what the hell? How is it ethical (or legal?) to use "messages" to push ads from fake profiles, and with no way to opt out? Even as a paying customer??

Recently, I started to see ads - disguised as messages - in my LinkedIn account. They do not come from a real person: they seem to be AI-generated (you cannot respond to them, and the "profile" does not exist - it cannot be clicked or responded to. For legit messages sent by a human you can reply and click through to their profile)

I happen to already be a paying LinkedIn member, paying around $600/year for keeping my messages open, and for the occasional InMail.

LinkedIn is truly losing its way, and trying to milk every last cent, integrity and ethics be damned.

Who in the LinkedIn product team thought this is acceptable, or that fake/AI profiles are on-brand for a supposedly trusted platform?

PS: I've now cancelled my LinkedIn Premium subscription. To message me on other platforms, for free, please see https://lnkd.in/eR2Dfg9f (I paid for LinkedIn Premium for anyone to be able to message me - but given how DMs can no longer be trusted to come from humans, I see no reason to encourage anyone to use LinkedIn DMs. Message me on email, Bluesky, X and Signal: all platforms where a DM means it is coming from a person, and a not bot built by the platform, trying to get more ad views.)

PPS: LinkedIn might be the only platform spamming paying customers more than free members. See the comment from Michael Malone below:

"Ever since I cancelled Premium, I have received far less spam. LinkedIn might be the only platform where you actually see more junk by paying for the service."

text:
I suspected bootstrapping will become a lot more popular: both because of VC investments drying up, and also because it's becoming easier to build software w/o funding. We now have data:

Did a deepdive 15 months ago on lessons from successful bootstrapped companies, founded by software engineers: https://lnkd.in/eksXfE3D

They are more pragmatic in technology choices; more conservative in hiring; and - at leas the ones I talked with! - tend to have far more focus and say no to distractions. text:
Anyone saying that GenAI could replace software engineers don't understand how software is created (and operated.)

Tool innovations have always make the process of building software faster, cheaper: GenAI and AI agents will also do this.

These are tools and efficiency gains: which bring efficiency to e.g. coding. They will likely bring more efficiency to other parts of the stack.

Which means that a team can do more (with the same people, or less) and that the barrier to build and ship working software gets lowered (once again - like it has many times before): making it more accessible to more people and businesses to build bespoke software, and turn it into a business (and eventually hire software professionals to grow and maintain their profitable software stack)

This thought comes as I'm writing down notes on how AI changes software engineering after reading Addy Osmani's excellent piece titled The 70% problem: Hard truths about AI-assisted coding: https://lnkd.in/e_Mi6Ueu

This sketch and more context to be published in The Pragmatic Engineer (you can get it in your inbox by signing up here: https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw )

posted_at.relative: 7 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
People who say AI agents will replace SaaS don’t understand that one pays for SaaS not b/c they are hard to BUILD: but b/c they are hard to build WELL and even harder to MAINTAIN

Why do you think developers still pay for the likes of Linear when we can build TODO apps in our sleep (+ we do this on interviews or takehome exercises)?

We can probably agree that a project management app like Linear is one step up in complexity from a TODO app. Software engineers could build a basic project management app in a few days at any startup or scaleup. So why don't we do so?

It’s because building WELL is incredibly hard. And even after having built it: maintaining it indefinitely - and adding new features - is a full-time job!

An imaginary AI agent that is at the level of a senior software engineer could build a pretty basic project management app - the same way any developer could do it today. But companies switching to AI-hacked ticketing apps (that need to be hosted and operated, mind you!) would make as much sense as building such an app for themselves, today.

Replace "ticketing app" with any other SaaS you use day-to-day, and it's a similar story.

(When you see someone saying this kind of stuff: ask yourself if they have an AI product to sell you, or to their investors!)

And on the topic of Linear, here's a podcast episode with their first EM Sabin Roman on how their dev team operates: https://lnkd.in/ew89pM4a

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 7 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
How do you ship projects successfully inside a larger tech company? Here are 3 practical tips from staff software engineer Sean Goedecke:

This is from the latest episode of The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast: which you can check out here:

YouTube: https://lnkd.in/e7MYNpe9 

Spotify: https://lnkd.in/e-bkf6Y6 

Web, transcript and other platforms: https://lnkd.in/ed8NnT2S

Brought to you by DX: an engineering intelligence platform designed by leading researchers. Learn more about them here: https://lnkd.in/ejixzEsP and Check out their unified framework for measuring developer productivity here: https://lnkd.in/enH8pQ76

1. Getting things done starts by being technical. 

Sean's original article got plenty of criticism because it talks so much about the “soft” parts of the tech lead role. 

Many readers assume that Sean implies that things like managing up are more important than being a good engineer. But this is not the case.
Being technical – and being able to build and ship solid code – is where “getting stuff done” starts. Being technical is necessary – but alone, it might not not sufficient to be seen as someone who gets things done in larger companies.

2. You can move mountains if you proactively build technical demos. 

If you can help product or design folks create prototypes they can use – or show: this is a great way to make yourself indispensable and get more visibility across your team or organization.

So, work on this skill! Build prototypes when you can on the side, pairing with, e.g. product folks or other people from the business.

3. As a tech lead: learn the “management lingo.” 

Engineering leadership and product management will oftentimes speak less directly at larger companies, especially in writing. To be an efficient tech lead, you need to both understand this language – and read between the lines. Speaking it “back” to managers will help you do so.

How do you do this? Spend time with managers, note the phrases they use, make note of ones that you’re unsure what they mean, and consider getting a mentor in the org: such as a PM or a TPM.

More takeaways and the full episode: https://lnkd.in/ed8NnT2S

text:
How the economics of software development changed over the decades: from Grady Booch (IBM Fellow & co-creator of UML), who saw all of this first-hand.

Back in the 1960s and 1970s: developers *had* to do a lot of thinking about coding - before coding! - because machine time was so expensive! And so design techniques to help with this were commonplace.

Fast forward to today: with LLMs, devs might not even need to think about *design*, but prompt an LLM to put a design together!

It's interesting to consider just how different the practice of "creating a program to do X" has changed over a few decades.

The below clip is one minute from our 90-minutes discussion in The Pragmatic Engineer podcast. 

Full episode (which is full of thoughtful observations like this clip)

text:
So few large companies realize how powerful it is to support employees who hit a rough patch in life - and what it does to culture.

I knew that NVIDIA was one of the very few Big Techs that never did mass layoffs - and CEO Jensen Huang made it clear to staff he will not do them (when all other Big Tech companies did it, despite those companies being at close to record profitability)

I am finishing the new book The NVIDIA Way by Tae Kim - and was pleasantly surprised to learn NVIDIA goes beyond “just” not doing mass layoffs to keep investors happy. The book brings several examples on helping employees during their toughest time - when many other large tech companies would either do nothing, and some might just consider managing out an employee struggling thanks to tough life events.

These things can add up. If you look at NVIDIA’s business trajectory and shockingly low attrition vs the industry (a quarter of industry average as per the book): they add up.

This book is the best tech/business book I’ve read this year. You can get it here: https://lnkd.in/eG6GxWJW

(I have no affiliation with the book. Just found it so good!)

text:
History doesn't repeat - but sometimes it rhymes.

In the 2010s, tiny dev teams built products with a massive impact. A social media company that raised $25M in total funding served 30M users - with only 13 developers. You might have heard of them: they are called Instagram.

In 2024, a social media company company that raised $23M in total funding is serving 25 million uses - with just 15 developers. They are called Bluesky.

In The Pragmatic Engineer, we did a deepdive with founding Bluesky engineers on how the platform is built, why they moved off from AWS to on-prem, and how they plan to make money (given the platform was designed to have no advertising: not now, not ever).

The deepdive on Bluesky: https://lnkd.in/eSgb79Mu

Source of the image is my talk What is Old is New Again - which attempts to predict what will happen in the tech industry, in the next year, and why: https://lnkd.in/e5rSHtZw (both a video and an article)

Neat to see efficient engineering teams punching above their weight is a thing in 2024 as well - even within the crowded social media space!

text:
If you're a mobile engineer or interested in iOS and Android, this podcast episode is as good as it gets: How Notion builds its iOS and Android apps. 

Two of Notion's founding mobile engineers Austin Louden an Karn Saheb help us dive deep into the productivity app used by tens of millions of users on mobile. Listen to it or watch it here:

Spotify: https://lnkd.in/e4kM-7bX 

YouTube: https://lnkd.in/eDgbYyuR 

Apple: https://lnkd.in/etHAB3G7 

Web & transcript: https://lnkd.in/epeQEQ3z

--
This episode is brought to you by DX, the engineering intelligence platform designed by leading researchers. Learn more about them here: https://lnkd.in/ejixzEsP (Coincidentally, DX have just launched a very interesting dev productivity framework called DX Core 4: https://lnkd.in/eDeKCWVr)

--

Three interesting takeaways from this conversation:

1. Notion’s native mobile team is surprisingly small!

Notion employs about 600 staff, but the mobile team is only 11 people – including iOS and Android. This team size is very small, especially considering how Notion serves more than 10M users on both iOS and Android. We’re likely talking about one engineer for every ~2M or more users!

2. Moving from web-based to native was an incremental process by necessity. 

Thanks to a small native team, and the need to keep the mobile apps up-to-date with the latest Notion features, the native mobile engineering team didn’t have the luxury of doing a large mobile app rewrite. Instead, they did the sensible thing of slowly migrating parts of the app. Today, most of Notion’s apps are fully native, save for the editor. The editor remains one of the most complex parts of Notion.

3. Notion releases their app on a weekly cadence. 

Native mobile apps need to go through an app review flow from Apple and Google, and so many native apps are released less frequently, to account for this overhead. Notion doing a weekly release cycle is impressive – and it can serve as inspiration for other native mobile teams. If Notion, with 10M+ native users and a small team, does this, other native teams can as well!

Thanks to the Notion team for sharing all these details!

text:
The difference between a platform having a 75% take rate (like Audible for non-exclusive audiobooks) versus a 30% take rate (like Apple has) is not double. Not triple. It's 7x!

Taking the example of an author or publisher generating $10,000 revenue from an audiobook (this was roughly the cost of creating The Software Engineer’s Guidebook audiobook):

At a 75% take rate, the platform (say, Amazon Audible) generates $30,000 in platform revenue.

At a 30% take rate, the platform (say, Apple) generates $4,285 in platform revenue.

That is a 7x difference.

No wonder Amazon is a fantastic business - and especially Audible!

Details in this post where I explain why my audiobook is available everywhere but on Audible: https://lnkd.in/dDrwUYWp

(This is also a good example why percentages don't always tell the full story. In this case, a 2.5x increase in take rate increases the platform's earnings 7x!)

reshared_post.text:
After months of researching Facebook / Meta, I published part 1 of a 65-page (17K word) deepdive into their engineering culture, revealing more details than I've seen anywhere.

As I talked with more than a dozen current and former Facebook engineers, managers and directors and here are 6 interesting things to know about Facebook's engineering culture, which are standout versus other Big Tech:

1. A lack of rigid processes. 

Facebook seems to have the least amount of processes or standardization across all of Big Tech. Don’t even try to compare it to Amazon’s engineering culture and the countless formal processes there. But even compared to companies like Google, Microsoft or Uber, Facebook’s processes are much looser. Most of this comes from the engineering-centric nature of the company and engineers disliking processes.

2. A quirky tech stack. 

Everything within Facebook is custom, much more so than at almost all of Big Tech. Google comes closest to a “non-standard tech stack”, but Facebook takes it a step further. This quirky tech stack is a good reason why they have — and need! — Bootcamp for onboarding.

3. Surprisingly little emphasis on testing or documentation. 

You’ll find shockingly little automated testing and documentation at Facebook, compared to the rest of Big Tech. Originally, this was a conscious decision in order to foster more collaboration and personal connection. Instead of reading documentation that is possibly stale, you have to look at the code, find out who wrote it — or modified it — and connect with them. This approach means building personal relationships, and possibly learning more than you could just from documentation.

4. A founder-engineer driven company. Facebook is now the only Big Tech firm whose founder is an engineer, and still is the CEO. Amazon was the other example of this until recently, but it’s not the case at Google, Apple, Netflix and others. There are good examples of smaller companies like CloudFlare, but they’re all younger than Facebook.

5. Engineers are product-oriented. 

They are expected — and are rewarded for — collaborating well with design, product and data science. Engineers tend to represent the product-minded engineer very closely. They do far more than just deliver on requirements. They frequently define requirements themselves and deliver on those they think will have the highest impact. This is true for the product organizations: Infra orgs are still very engineering oriented, focused on scaling, and engineering metrics.

6. Levels are hidden across the company. 

Every software engineer’s title is SWE, and every product manager is called Product Manager. For managers, you see Manager (but not if it’s M1 or M2), Director (but not if it’s D1 or D2) or VP. 

To read more about the engineering culture, see this article as published in The Pragmatic Engineer: 🔒 https://lnkd.in/eePCuTjt

---
Follow me for content on software engineering at Big Tech and startups.

#meta #facebook

text:
The audiobook for The Software Engineer's Guidebook is out in two days! It is launching first on Spotify. Here is where to get it, and why this launch:

You can get the audiobook here: https://lnkd.in/e8H3i73F or just search for "The Software Engineer's Guidebook" in Spotify.

On the "why."

When producing the audiobook, I did extensive research on platforms that can be used for publishing.

Audible is the very clear market leader, with ~65% market share in the US. However, Audible has monopolistic pricing practices in-place, meant to lock in publishers to keep their audiobooks Audible-exclusive. This is done via royalty rates:

When publishing an audiobook that is not exclusive to Audible (aka it can be bought elsewhere): Audible takes 75% of the revenue (!!), and the publisher gets 25%. (Compare this to Apple's much-criticized 30% take rate where they take 30% of the revenue, and publishers get 70%!)

BUT for audiobooks exclusive to Audible: Audible "only" takes 60%, and the publisher gets 40%. This is still double that of Apple's heavily criticized rate! Still, if you do the maths: thanks to Audible's dominant market share, it makes no *financial* sense to publish outside of Audible!

This doesn't sit well with me. Audible can get away with this thanks to being a mostly unchallenged monopoly. The only new and major challenger for audibooks is Spotify.

I dislike supporting monopolistic practices, and will launch the audiobook on platforms outside Audible, even if this means a worse deal for me, financially (it will!).

Still, the immediate reason for launching on Spotify first is more technical. It takes 2-6 weeks to list audibooks on most platforms (Audible taking 4-6 weeks). The Spotify Audibooks team helpfully reached out when they heard about my audibook, expedited this process to under a week, and hence the first platform to launch being them!

Audible will lag 3-5 weeks behind, and other platforms (like Apple, Google Play etc) will follow in about 2 weeks.

For now, pre-save the audiobook in Spotify here: https://lnkd.in/e8H3i73F

If you have no preference on where to listen: please consider Spotify or Apple or Google over Audible, as they cut a more than fair deal to publishers (Spotify: 0% take rate *for now*, Apple and Google: 30% versus 75% the take rate for Audible).

And thank you to the Spotify team for their help!

text:
This book (The Mythical Man Month) is an example of why books can be so important and influential even decades later. It's 50 years old, and still:

The book was written in 1975: in a very different time, when mainframes were what computers meant, machine time was far more expensive than programmer time, and there was no internet, nor ARPANET. The book is about software engineering approaches and observations valid at the time.

Amusingly, there are plenty of parts are still relevant today. Like how:

- Estimating projects is not much more successful

- The book advocating for prototyping as the first approach

- Advocating for shipping polished software and not a half-baked one (a trend that is making a comeback - just think to e.g. the recent deepdive with Linear we did in The Pragmatic Engineer Podcast at https://lnkd.in/ew89pM4a)

There are parts that are less relevant now, though. Like how:

- There's much less focus on program size optimisation

- How the lines of code per developer per month have easily increased 10-100x thanks to better languages and tools since the 70s

- How the "ideal developer team structure" looks quite different 50 years later.

I've been going through the book, chapter by chapter, reflecting what has changed. And most importantly: what has NOT changed, even after 50 years. Here's what I have so far: https://lnkd.in/d4gfw8wg

text:
Linear is a startup that punches well above its weight in the speed and quality of shipping, and supporting a large number of customers (10,000+ companies) with a small engineering team (25 devs).

I sat down with Linear's first engineering manager, Sabin Roman. Our discussion: https://lnkd.in/ew89pM4a

We covered: 

• No email. How Linear handles internal communications 

• Quality. The “goalie” program to address customer concerns and Linear’s zero bug policy 

• Full remote. How Linear keeps teams connected despite working entirely remotely 

• Getting stuff done. An in-depth, step-by-step walkthrough of a project at Linear  

• Creativity + QA.  Linear’s focus on quality and creativity over fast shipping  

• Career ladders. Titles at Linear, Sabin’s learnings from Uber

Brought to you thanks to our wonderful sponsors:

🌟 LaunchDarkly — a platform for high-velocity engineering teams to release, monitor, and optimize great software https://lnkd.in/eAjx6dic

🌟 Sevalla — Deploy anything from preview environments to Docker images https://lnkd.in/effpqesp

🌟 WorkOS — The modern identity platform for B2B SaaS https://workos.com/

Watch it on:

• YouTube: https://lnkd.in/ehb4wkGx

• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/eKrwfHnj

• Apple: https://lnkd.in/ezw4bRAa

One interesting takeaway I had:

Linear has a preference for creativity over process – when the goal is creativity! 

Linear is intentionally light on engineering processes when they build new products: their goal is to communicate using “principles” rather than “guidebooks.” The company manages to do this thanks to a mix of hiring engineers with strong product skills and still being a small enough company (60 people and 25 engineers at the time of writing). 

For more insights, read takeaways, or watch the full episode: https://lnkd.in/ew89pM4a
