text:
Startups hiring founding software engineers: what companies are they most likely to come from? The most common one happens to be: Google!

This data was shared with me by Live Data Technologies (and Jason Saltzman) on realtime job changes, for The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive on how to hire software engineers, to startups, away from Big Tech.

Some observations from that deepdive:

- Amazon is likely the Big Tech that has a culture resembling scrappy startups the most closely - and also happens to be a large company that is less challenging to hire away from

- When pitching to hire from Big Tech: consider pitching for impact.

- Offer candidates  path out of Big Tech bureaucracy by showing them how your startup is able to ship faster and make more decisions, while having fewer sign-off requirements for releasing new features, compared to the larger companies

And more suggestions, from hiring managers at startups. See more data and read the full deepdive here: https://lnkd.in/eizXhvCY

text:
I'm working on the audiobook version of The Software Engineer's Guidebook - and it will be out early December. What platform would you be interested in listening on?

I'm asking this question, as it turns out that Audible has a seemingly monoplistic pricing structure, designed to discourage releasing audibooks anywhere outside of Audible.

For audiobooks that agree to be exclusive to Audible: Amazon keeps 60% of sales, and the publisher is paid 40%. So a $10 audiobook nets $4 for the publisher.

For audiobooks that are sold outside of Audible: Amazon keeps 75% of sales, and the publisher gets 25%. So a $10 audiobook nets $2.50 for the publisher.

It's amusing that means that Amazon makes more on every audiobook sold (60-75% of the price) versus physical books sold (40% of the price).

I'll be aiming to optimize for reach of distribution over revenue with this book, so interested to hear what platform(s) you prefer to listen to audiobooks.

Please leave a comment, or fill out this form:https://lnkd.in/ecB4BD-n  (when filling out the form, you can be notified when the audibook is out) 

And here's me listening to the recording, while reading along in the book, checking for correctness and ensuring the audiobook comes out as smooth.

text:
15 years after graduating, I was back at Budapest University of Technology and Economics - doing a guest lecture titled "Software engineering in the real world."

This was a guest lecture for the Software Engineering course, for second year (sophomore) BSc students. (In Hungarian: this was probably my first and only Hungarian presentation about software engineering - which was amusing enough for myself, given I almost exclusively talk about this topic in English)

When I was a student, some of the most memorable lectures were guest lectures: former students coming back and telling stories about how stuff *actually* works outside of the classroom: when there are no study guides, no exams and no grades.

After graduating, I got quite the "reality check" about how techniques we studied at university were not used in the industry. The message of my lecture was to encourage students to understand how the industry works (even while being students) and to be a "sponge" during their first internships and jobs.

Of the ~150 students, only one fell visibly asleep: and this was a 2pm lecture, right after lunch. Based on my recollection on how many people were sleeping during boring lectures: I consider this presentation a smashing success!

text:
A good example how hyped technologies and solutions are oftentimes overused while their impact being *under measured.*

Because the business is so invested in the new technology being better (everyone says so!) they often set aside business pragmatic approaches used with any other new approach. Like following up with a survey on “how did you like this new AI-assisted feature, tell us more” or just measuring customer churn (increase or reduction) or customer spend changes (customers spending more or less).

Of course, there are businesses that treat any new product/technology with the same rigor: and they are also ones that are unlikely to ship generally useless features using hyped technology to production (but they will ship generally useful features using very boring technology!)

Just a personal opinion on AI hype..

AI is undoubtedly fascinating and has already proven to be a game-changer for boosting productivity in various scenarios. However, like any hype, it often gets overused. 

I recently had an intriguing experience with my energy provider's AI chatbot. Despite using all the key terms related to canceling my service, the bot kept giving me the same repetitive response. It failed to grasp my frustration, and after 10 minutes of going in circles, I switched my contract to a different provider.

It is a tool, not a silver bullet. You still need to provide value to customers to retain them.

text:
Where does the term "debugging" come from? Turns out, it could have had to do with an actual bug - at least for the first mention.

Here's the story behind it from today's The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive on the evolution of debugging:

As a note, faults were called “bugs” before this incident, but the serendipitous episode may have helped cement the term “debugging” in the lexicon. Several computer science papers from the 1950s mention “debugging” in passing, which suggests the word was in use and its meaning was common knowledge among professionals. It also appears in the 1963 manual of the first time-sharing operating system, the Compatible Time-Sharing System (CTSS.)

For more details on how debugging evolved from the 1940s - and what modern debugging tools look like - here's the overview: https://lnkd.in/exUm2tZD

text:
Shortly after I graduated from my CS program, I was a bit of upset at how almost all the technologies taught were so "last decade," and not popular in the industry.

For example, back in 2009 at university, I learned (and had to use) SOAP and CORBA (interop with Java). C as a programming language and some Assembly. 

But when graduating, these languages/approaches were already considered old-school, and definitely not cutting-edge. For my side projects: I taught myself to code using e.g. PHP. For my first job: I got lucky that on an electable class I learned a bit of ASP .NET: but I had to learn the ins-and-outs of C#, languages on my own. I felt "tricked" a bit, back then. Why would a CS program not teach modern languages and frameworks?

Now I realize there was a point to all this.

I was forced got to learn these "earlier" technologies: not just learn them, but use them! Once you do a little Assembly or C or C++, you have a pretty good idea of what needs to happen under the hood for higher-level languages like C#, PHP or others. "Compiled language" makes sense, as you already wrote bytecode that a high-level language compiles to.

It took me probably a decade to realize I'm grateful for having been pushed to learn old technology: because it helps pick up (and reason about) new technology easier. And in the tech industry, no new technology is created in a vacuum: there's always languages, frameworks, approaches and technologies that came before (and that new approaches aim to improve upon!)

text:
Assuming a startup *can* hire a software engineer from Big Tech: should they? Here are 5 reasons why a hire might not work out:

(As context, these come from talking with startup founders and hiring managers, who shared their experience on hiring from Big Tech - including cases when it did not work out.)

1. No real need for Big Tech expertise

Many startups don’t actually need Big Tech expertise.

2. Unfamiliar with “startup infra” and pace

In some Big Tech companies, engineers might have mostly been building on top of sophisticated, Big Tech-specific infrastructure, and be unfamiliar with common cloud infrastructures which many startups use.

3. No business model for paying Big Tech comp

If the business for a startup isn’t forecasted to earn $400K+ in revenue per employee, then paying the same kind of compensation as Big Tech isn’t sensible, nor practical.

4. Focus on speed, efficiency, and doing more with less

Most of Big Tech is used to doing more with lots of resources. Unless a startup is well-funded, the cultures of Big Tech hires might clash with a more scrappy culture.

5. To solve novel problems in scrappy ways

If the goal is to get from zero to one in a difficult problem space by using as few resources as possible, Big Tech probably isn’t the place to do it.

Of course, every situation is unique: this is merely a reminder that one person successful in one environment might not "automatically" become successful in a very different one!

For more examples on when hires might not work out - but also for situations when it's smart to consider hiring from Big Tech - read this week's The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive: https://lnkd.in/e39WcNdp

posted_at.relative: 9 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Why do software engineers leave Big Tech?

It’s easy to see why working at the one of the largest tech companies can be a final goal for some software engineers. These places offer unmatched brand recognition, the ability work at some of the largest-scale software deployed across the globe, and top-of-market compensation.

So why do people leave?

I talked to half a dozen tech professionals who departed, and collected these reasons:

1. Big Tech less stable than it was

Big Tech had few to no mass layoffs for years – but that’s all changed.

2. Professional growth in a startup environment

It’s hard to keep learning in some Big Tech environments, compared to at startups.

3. Closed career paths at Big Tech

It’s often more achievable to get to executive-level engineering positions at scaleups, than at global companies.

4. Forced out

The bigger the company, the more there’s politics and scope for workers to be victims of competing interests, personalities, and rivalries.

5. Scaleups get “too Big Tech”

Scaleups are nimble and move fast with few processes. Successful scaleups slow down and add more process.

6. Steep compensation drops

A falling stock price can make people consider leaving if it leads their compensation to also reduce. Also, when an initial equity grant vests out after 4 years.

Read the full deepdive with specific examples for all of these reasons in this free edition of The Pragmatic Engineer from last week: https://lnkd.in/eQC8QFed

You may not be familiar with Bending Spoons, but I guarantee you’ve encountered some of their products, like Evernote and Meetup. So how do they build software?

In today’s episode of The Pragmatic Engineer, I sat down with three key figures from the Italy-based startup: cofounder and CEO Luca Ferrari, CTO Francesco Mancone, and Evernote product lead Federico Simionato. Bending Spoons has been profitable from day one, and there's plenty we can learn from their unique culture, organizational structure, engineering processes, and hiring practices.

Listen to (or watch) the full episode here: 
https://lnkd.in/dREnGCm8

• DX: an engineering intelligence platform designed by leading researchers. Learn more: https://lnkd.in/d7GzMQpf

• ByteByteGo — Ace your next system design interview. Check it out at https://bytebytego.com

---

One of my favorite takeaways from this episode is how Bending Spoons doesn't follow conventional approaches: but do what works for them. For example, while similar companies would have oncall rotations: they try to run a setup where they do not need to have an engineer on the other side of a pager.

As always: avoid blindly copying practices that one company does. Still, it's helpful to understand what works for them, and why!

text:
How has the number of software engineers changed over the last year, the last five years: the last 25 years? I teamed up with Live Data Technologies to find out. Here's how:

As context, Live Data Technologies tracked 10.6M job changes for 2.9M unique individuals over the last 25 years. Their sample started with 725,000 individuals working in software engineering, and has grown by closer to 140% over this time period. (Thanks to Jason Saltzman for the details - and feel free to ping him with more questions!)

This growth is as close of an approximation I've seen in reflecting what actually happens on the software engineering job market.

And a surprising fact:

In 25 years, 2023 was the first time that the number of employed software engineers has *decreased.*

In positive news, we have data to show that since then, this number increased in 2024 - even if at a significantly slower pace than between 2011-2021.

See more data in today's The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive: The State of Software Engineering Jobs 2024, here:  https://lnkd.in/dzWeeFUK

posted_at.relative: 9 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
These comments are increasingly common here, on LinkedIn: comments that simply  summarize a post with ChatGPT (or a similar tool). 

What is the point of this - beyond generating useless noise?

(Beeen deleting such comments and blocking accounts doing it: but it keeps happening)

Update: read the insightful comment by Georg Zoeller below that explains the rationale (from the POV of who is doing LLM-generated comments) An excerpt:

“The point is to boost the engagement of the original poster. Linkedin teams are goaled on engagement and it’s measured in various ways including comments received and their  classification.  

By using an LLM to create unauthentic comments that seem positive and technical the linked ranking system is deceived to give the OG poster greater distribution, the team at LinkedIn gets engagement lift which will result in meeting their target metrics and the onto collateral is signal vs noise ratio which isn’t measurable and therefore not a concern for teams goaled on 6 month KPI

But you can’t just comment on your own stuff, like with google SEO you need to build a diverse portfolio of comments to not get weeded out by simplistic anti spam classifiers. Your specific profile will boost both comment engagement and visibility because of your reach. Just like SEO the ecosystem has long reverse engineered the ingredients.”

And from Annette G. also in the comments:

“I worked fighting user generated content abusers in comments before and what happens a lot is that those fake accounts are a part of a larger group of a bot network/farm that targets popular and trending content to drive traffic to some source. 

They write a comment that seems fairly innocent, often have other fake accounts upvote and react to it - this way the fake comment shoots up to the top and legit users see it as highly relevant because it was upvoted many times. The legit users then might then be inclined to check the fake profile out. And on the fake profile you could see links to some shady websites or whatever else the purpose of the given bot network is. What I've seen most often to drive traffic to some shady source.”

text:
The WordPress world is in distress after Automattic (the creator of WordPress) has launched a series of ever-escalating attacks on its chief competitor. How it's going:

As context, WordPress powers ~43% of all websites, and has a roughly 65% market share across content management systems (CMS). These are incredible numbers!

It's now been two weeks of increasingly escalating attacks by Automattic on its largest competitor, WP Engine (a managed WordPress hosting provider.) The latest move involved Automattic taking over WP Engine's largest WordPress plugin called Advanced Custom Fields (ACF): one with more than 2 million installs. This move itself is unprecedented, and is as close to the concept of theft as it comes in open source.

While developers in the WordPress world are increasingly tired of ongoing drama and escalation, my sense is that the harder Automattic is trying to paint WP Engine as a villain and a "cancer to WordPress," the more of these actions reflect back on Automattic.

Before these events, I had a generally favorable view of Automattic, and never heard of WP Engine. My view of Automattic has now severely degraded - to the point of associating "unethical" with Automattic thanks to the latest actions - and my sympathy for WP Engine has increased.

WordPress is here to stay and dominate websites and CMS solutions. Automattic is working very hard to demonstrate why WP Engine is the most drama-free and reliable the most stable managed hosting provider an enterprise can think of in the middle of such turmoil.

(As always, I have no affiliation with either of these parties, and have a slight bias towards companies investing in open source - as long as they play clean. Automattic is not doing this since it threw its gloves off.)

A timeline of events: https://lnkd.in/ejzmH_uY

My full analysis: https://lnkd.in/ej95cQmb

reshared_post.text:
As CEO, people often ask me if Twilio is returning to the office.

My answer is simple: Not while I’m here.

For me, it’s all about impact. If you focus on what you build rather than where you build it, you deliver more value for customers and bring together the best talent from around the world. 

That’s why we’ve adopted Open Work at Twilio—a blend of programs, policies, and resources designed to help our remote-first business and our employees thrive globally. 

Is it perfect? No. But we’re constantly listening, learning, and experimenting with new programs to keep us on the right track, and so far, we’re seeing great progress: 93% of Twilions report they collaborate effectively while remote. 

We cut out commutes and spend more time working on the stuff that matters or connecting with our families. And we’re intentional about creating flexibility so folks can get together in-office, with customers, or at an offsite.

The way we work changed for the better—for our employees and our customers.

Open Work is working.

So, no – we’re not calling employees back to the office. 

Link in comments to learn more about it.

text:
It's feedback like this that anyone spending months (or, in my case: years!) writing a book hopes for. That a pretty long book is still worth the read. It's not trivial that this will be the case, as an author!

The author's reality, in my case, is frequent doubt: not being sure if I struck the balance between readability and depth, if you lose readers midway, or if the book covers the topics they care about, and if in enough depth (but perhaps not too much: also see the problem with too much length).

This is a reason specialist editors exist like development editors (who help with the structure of the book and to "pace it"), copyeditors (who help make words flow better without changing the meaning) and beta readers (who, ideally, are "ruthless" in pointing out parts of the book that are could use editing) and the reality of book printing costs and length considerations (that motivated me to cut out less interesting chapters, an tagging them on as online-only reading, for those who really want to go into those not-so-core chapters)

Glad that it was helpful, Tomas!

text:
Very very interesting find: most devs using LLM tools for 6+ months see productivity increases, but most eng leaders where their teams use these tools do not see team-level increases. Why?

As context, this finding comes from this week's The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive where with Elin Nilsson we analyzed 216 responses from software engineers and engineering leaders on their usage of LLM tools for software engineering.

My theories on why this disreptancy:

1. Productivity translates to better work-life balance. 

People could well be more productive, but the gains are consumed in other tasks. If GenAI helps save a few minutes off a longer task, there’s more time for a walk, a coffee, go home earlier, etc.

2. GenAI mostly aids simpler work. 

GenAI helps with coding tasks in large organizations, but coding is often the smallest of all work! Alignment, meetings, and talking with others often takes up more time. GenAI won’t change this.

3. Maybe it's just hard to measure?

As an engineering leader: consider that GenAI can have a positive impact without the impact being measured! We have ample evidence via other studies (e.g. this peer reviewed one: https://lnkd.in/eCgXfyRw)  that GenAI tools are genuinely helpful for software engineers. And while most engineering leaders have the perception that these tools don’t make a big difference at the team level: I’d urge all engineering leaders to look closer before declaring that there really is no difference!

The full article where the image is from, with more findings: https://lnkd.in/eBkrxDJA

text:
Today I learned that Russia's military uses Telegram, an insecure and not E2E encrypted communications platform where Telegram (or any state Telegram collaborates with) can read (almost**) all messages.

Ukraine's military meanwhile uses... Signal. Which is an end-to-end encrypted messaging service that is secure, as Signal has no access to message content (thus any state wanting to access these messages has no 'simple' way like they can with Telegram).

No wonder Russia in panic?

Source is Politico: https://lnkd.in/dUU8UVX3

There are strong suspicions Russia has backdoors to access Telegram messages.

But this is the problem with backdoors: assuming only Russia had such a backdoor, this was convenient. But if one state can have a backdoor, what is stopping another (say, France?) to install one, and get access to data that Russia considers sensitive, yet they used an insecure medium (Telegram!) to communicate.

The amusing part is how it was largely unknown how Telegram offers no E2E encryption as default. The public considered Telegram as considered secure, when it was anything but.

**: Telegram 1:1 messages are unencrypted by default, unless starting a "secret chat" that both parties need to opt into. Consider that WhatsApp, iMessage and Signal encrypts 1:1 chats *by default*, making them immediately more secure than Telegram.

text:
An amusing turn of events: X (formerly: Twitter) sues advertisers for doing exactly what Elon Musk said they should do.

Ten months ago, Elon Musk told advertisers who were boycotting X to not advertise on his platform, emphasizing his point by swearing on stage. A video clip recorded at the DealBook Summit captured the billionaire’s outburst:

Elon Musk: “Don’t advertise.”

Reporter: “You don’t want them [the companies boycotting X with advertising] to advertise?”

Elon Musk: “No. If someone’s trying to blackmail me with advertising, blackmailing with money, go f**k yourself.”

[Reporter doesn’t know what to say]

Elon Musk: “GO F**K YOURSELF. Is that clear? I hope it is!”

[Audience awkwardly laughs]

(See the full video clip here: https://lnkd.in/eUckwXma )

Now in an amusing twist of fate, X is suing those same advertisers for not advertising on the platform! Specifically, the platform is taking legal action against:

- Mars (former X advertiser)
- CVS Health (former advertiser)
- Unilever (former X advertiser)
- The Global Alliance for Responsible Media (GARM, a nonprofit brand safety coalition)
- The World Federation of Advertisers (WFA)

The lawsuit is based on antitrust grounds, which refers to a monopoly using illegal means to maintain said monopoly.

It’s predicted this lawsuit will be thrown out of court for the simple reason that it’s beyond silly to claim it’s illegal for a company to not advertise on a platform which it has decided for commercial reasons is an unsafe place for its business. Since Musk purchased Twitter and renamed it X, he has welcomed back users previously banned for racism and other practices which respectable brands would run a mile from, and also slashed spending on content moderation and safety measures.

If the lawsuit goes ahead, the defendants could take the cheeky step of reminding the court of Musk’s own words, where he explicitly told advertisers to not advertise on X.

If this new lawsuit against the businesses whom Musk presumably wishes would spend money on X results in anything, it’s likely to be that even more advertisers will avoid X. But does the social media company need their money, anyway? Musk paid $44B for the site, is one of the wealthiest people on the planet, and could easily fund the operation out of his own pocket – assuming he treasures free speech as much as he insists.

(The above was an excerpt from this week's The Pulse in The Pragmatic Engineer. See the full issue here: https://lnkd.in/edCjNgAq)

text:
Scam developer conference alert: "We Love JS" and "Europe JS" are 99.99% that they are nonexistent conferences. Tied to the organizers of DevTernity. Do your due diligence:

Last year, I stumbled across an online-only tech conference called DevTernity that had an impressive speaker lineup with industry heavyweights. They also had a speaker called "Anna Boyko" who was introduced as a core contributor to Ethereum and a Staff Engineer at Coinbase. This got my interest: why would a core Ethereum contributor (a decentralized protocol!) decide to work at a centralized crypto exchange (Coinbase.)

Trying to find the answer to this question led me on an unlikely investigation that confirmed that Anna Boyko does not exist - and her profile image was AI generated - and so were a few women speakers on the previous editions of the conference, or conferences by the same organizer (claiming to be working at Coinbase or Meta)

More on DevTernity and the aftermath in my summary here: https://lnkd.in/ezvihsRG

Now, there are several new fake conferences appearing, seemingly loosely tied to the DevTernity organizers. The site "Dev .events" was allegedly built by some fake DevTernity organizers (more by The Verge on this here: https://lnkd.in/eZZm2Uzg)

This site is now listing the "We Love JS" conference series: but this conference is a confirmed scam. We know from speakers listed, who had no idea they are associated with this nonexistent event: https://lnkd.in/eAHRs53q

And judge for yourself if "Singapore JS" is a real conference, if the conference is supposed to take place on 13-15 September, but ticket sales are closing *today* on 1 August. Oh, wait: the Amsterdam JS and Paris JS events also all have tickets closing *today* (likely to create a sense of urgency.) Eg the Amsterdam event: https://lnkd.in/eQRfTtK2 So this "Europe JS" series looks to be as real as "We Love JS."

Please do your due diligence and assume that not only conference speakers can be fake, but whole conferences. Especially if they have any connection with DevTernity - the first and only tech conference I know of to having resorted to generating nonexistent speakers for years. List of these (likely fake) events on the suspicious Dev .Events page here: https://lnkd.in/eyvpj2Pt (where I would treat all events listed with heightened suspicion, going forward)

Sad that it is coming to this.

text:
Whoa: we're in the middle of what is likely the largest global software-inflicted outage: Airports, hospitals, pharmacy services,  flight operators, train services, TV broadcasters, supermarkets and more are down. What we know:

(The below photo is from Sydney airport where most screens are the Windows Blue Screen of Death, and travel plans are disrupted thanks to this outage.)

The outage has hit Windows machines that Crowdstrike for endpoint protection (antivirus, firewall, intrusion detection, encryption and application control.)

Crowdstrike is a cybersecurity company valued at $80B, and the market leader for Windows endpoint protection, with around 22% market share. So 1 out of 5 businesses operating Windows machines use them.

What seems to have happened is that Crowdstrike pushed an innocent-enough software update... to all Windows machines, globally, pretty much a the same time. Crowdstrike's software operates at the kernel level: and this update crashes Windows.

Normally, when buggy code is pushed to production: you'd "just" revert this change, and push the previous version (or code that works correctly,) and when clients get this patch: they are restored. But not in this case: because these machines are non-functional.

The fix - as advised by Crowdstrike - is manual and time consuming, and needs to be repeated for every single Windows machine impacted. The machine needs to be booted to safe mode, a file needs to be deleted, then the machine rebooted.

What is unexplainable about this outage is how (and why?) did Crodstrike do a global release of a software change with no staged rollout (aka a rollout with canarying)? This makes no  sense and no cybersecurity vendor with reasonable deployment practices would ever consider this. For all we know so far, this "global deploy" is more of a "YOLO deploy" (we covered approaches to ship to production in The Pragmatic Engineer, including YOLO deploys at https://lnkd.in/dsQzhQ7 ). YOLO deploys are fine when you don’t care much if a deploy goes wrong, and it’s easy enough to revert. A deploy that could take down the majority of your customers is not one to experiment with this approach with.

To me, it's incomprehensible how staged rollout could have been sidestepped: how it’s not an enforced process for all deploys, large or small. The fallout of this outage will surely be noticeable at the global GDP level - and it will be very bad news for Crowdstrike's business, looking ahead (who wants a security vendor that crashes 100% of Windows machines their software runs on, when it should keep them secure?)

Update: read my longer analysis and takeaways here: https://lnkd.in/evrxJxKF

(Image via Reddit: https://lnkd.in/ecfeP2Ys )

---
Follow me for insights on how the software engineering industry is evolving. Subscribe to The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw for more in-depth analysis.

text:
More than 200 developers shared their views on AI-assisted software development in a The Pragmatic Engineer survey. Findings on genuinely helpful use cases:

1. Massive help in completing specific projects

The most-cited positive experience is that AI tooling is a huge help for completing projects, such as converting existing bash scripts that only worked on Linux, to cross-platform Python3 scripts that also work on Windows

2. Better testing leading to better code quality

“We are seeing better use of test cases, better code quality, and better documentation. This is true both at the individual, and team-level.” – from a web developer with 7 YOE

3. Educational

Respondents shared many cases of this, and LLMs do indeed seem well suited to helping tech workers learn new technologies and frameworks faster, in a more interactive way. 

4. Easier to prototype and experiment

Building a prototype version of a product is much easier, share several respondents.

5. Better documentation = better communication

Examples mentioned included Boosting the quality of documentation, reviewing documents with AI tools, or finding relevant information with tools like Notion AI.

And there are a few more (see in the screenshot below.)

It's pretty clear that there a lot of areas where this tooling is already genuinely helpful. Of course, there are downsides (e.g. poor output, hallucinations, or devs over-trusting these tools.) But the upsides are hard to improve for anyone who does software engineering for a living.

(All these findings were from the report "AI Tooling for Software Engineers in 2024: Reality Check" from The Pragmatic Engineer. The full report is available for subscribers, here: https://lnkd.in/eQwYbtbx

---

text:
How Intuit is doing layoffs maddening and inexplainable. One sentence after Intuit's CEO claims to treat people let go with "the compassion and respect they deserve," he states that most people let go are actually low performers.

With a single sentence, Intuit calls the people losing their jobs “low performers” in full view of the public, in a message labeled “taking care of our people.” If that’s what taking care of people looks like at Intuit, then I fear for its current employees!

This fully public message is incredible for a few reasons:

1. It could harm affected folks’ employment prospects.

This is because most of them (more than half) have been publicly labeled “low performers” by their ex-employer. This means anyone considering hiring someone who just left Intuit can assume that there's a ~60% chance this person was a low performer, by Intuit standards. The 750 people let go for non-performance reasons likely have to live with this unfortunate public association as well!

2. What does Intuit’s CEO, Sasan Goodarzi gain from it? 

It makes Intuit’s leadership team look incompetent for having employed so many people who are apparently “not meeting expectations” (his words.) Shareholders could argue this was working against shareholders’ interest.

3. Let go for performance reasons, yet still offered generous severance?

Intuit claims 1,050 people (of the 1,800 let go) did not meet performance expectations, but it’s still paying them all very generous severance of 16 weeks pay, plus two weeks for every year served, plus bonuses paid. Actual low performers rarely get such a package.

Basically, the statement seems incoherent and baseless upon close inspection. By comparison, Meta let go of around 25% of staff in 6 months, but didn’t feel the need to say publicly that a single one of them was not meeting expectations.

Anyone who has conducted layoffs knows that the group affected contains a mix of low performers, and those in the wrong team or organization at the wrong time. Companies do not comment on who is who, so hiring managers give people the benefit of the doubt and the chance of a fresh start elsewhere. This clumsy statement by Intuit’s CEO strips everyone of the benefit of that doubt, tarring them all as incompetent. Who’s being incompetent here?

We previously did a deep dive on how to execute layoffs humanely in The Pragmatic Engineer (free article:  https://lnkd.in/e9NKSMP3 ). Intuit seems to have used several of the practices mentioned in the article, like generous severance, additional insurance, and job placement services. It makes the needless lack of compassion in the CEO’s email stick out even more.

--

This was an excerpt from today's The Pragmatic Engineer. Read the full analysis on these tone-deaf cuts at Intuit here: https://lnkd.in/eTrpnD3G

text:
How can we expect software engineering practices to change, looking ahead? Here is my full keynote from Craft Conference, from a month ago:

We've been seeing a lot of change in the software engineering industry. In the end of 2021, it was the hottest tech jobs market we've (probably) ever seen. And just a few months later, VC funding slowed down, companies did cuts, and there were no tech IPOs for 18 months. What happened, and why?

I've been analyzing these events for a few months, and summarized my findings on Craft Conference, in a 45 minute talk. Here is the full recording: https://lnkd.in/eQ7Rh3BW

And if you're interested in reading a summarized version, here is the article in today's The Pragmatic Engineer, at: https://lnkd.in/e5rSHtZw

Now, answering the question on how I expect software engineering to change, looking ahead, observations from my talk:

1. More push to choose “boring” technology

2. Monoliths becoming more popular (at least at early-stage)

3. “Fullstack” to be more popular (+Typescript)

4. More responsibilities “shifting left” onto developers

5. Less reinventing the wheel

Jump to this section in the video for my commentary on why these are likely to play out: https://lnkd.in/eMyCzbi5

What other changes would you expect to see, and why?

text:
Look at what is out: the new book, Leading Effective Engineering Teams by Addy Osmani. I got to read this book before it came out, and wrote the foreword:

The background on how this happened is I met Addy at the end of last year, when I was in the Bay Area. In-between him giving a tour of the Google offices, Addy shared that he's working on this book, which is a collection of many, many years of notes he's taken on helpful approaches.

Here is an excerpt of my foreword - which also summarized what to expect from the book:

"In the first part of this book, Addy walks through the "modern theory" of engineering management: research and mental model on this space that have stood the test of time for useful management practices - ones that work for tech teams. Many of these models are complementary, and some slightly contradict one another. In your own journey, you'll need to pick and choose the ones that are most helpful in your specific context - and you will likely find yourself changing philosophies as you change teams or companies.

The craft of leadership is far more practical than theoretical, though. The second part of his book is where Addy shares his practical approach to leading engineering teams, and this is the part where Addy shares a condensed history of a decade of leading tech teams. (...)

In addition to sharing his own, distilled experience, Addy allows us to peek at how effective managers at Google operate, as well as what effective leadership looks like in other environments like startups or large enterprises."

I recommend this book for those in tech lead, or engineering leadership positions, looking for mental models, or examples of approaches that have worked for others.

Congratulations on publishing, Addy!!

text:
In 2021, I tried to understand how tech compensation really works - at least in the Netherlands. I published "the trimodal nature of software engineering salaries" article. Time for an update:

Back then, based on ~100 data points (and my own experience as a hiring manager,) I suspected that there are three, distinctively different compensation bands for software engineers:

1. Local companies (Tier 1)

Most local businesses that benchmark to the numbers on most salary comparison sites. They usually offer close to – and sometimes slightly above – what government positions advertise for senior engineering roles.

2. Ambitious local companies (Tier 2)

Startups, scaleups, and more ambitious local companies that want to hire and retain the best people, locally.

3. Companies benchmarking across a region (Tier 3).

Big Tech companies, hedge funds and scaleups that hire across regions, frequently hire from abroad, and often compete with fellow Big Tech giants for talent.

Curiously, the third tier (Tier 3) seemed to be missing from most public compensation data sources.

Since publishing the model, I've both received lots of feedback suggesting it applies to the US, Canada, UK, Europe and many other countries. I've since then also collected far more data points, manually tagged companies by tier, and can finally confirm that yes, this model holds up when compared with actual data.

Here is the longer analysis (and the stats behind this graph below) in yesterday's The Pragmatic Engineer issue. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/e7FV4gBB

text:
In India The Software Engineer's Guidebook is published by the book publisher Shroff Publishers. Since the book has been out, it's been the consistent best seller for this publisher in the country!

Pinch me!

(This is especially true as Shroff Publishers & Distributors Pvt. Ltd. publish a lot of other tech books, including many O'Reilly titles). 

But given I self-published the book, why go with a publisher, for the same, English version of the book? A few reasons:

1. Amazon KDP (Kindle Direct Printing) does not do print-on-demand in India  

2. The feedback on print-on-demand services is mixed (low quality prints, for the most part)  

3. Printers in India work with different sizes than many other parts of the world. So the book needed to be reformatted and resized (e.g. the India version is more pages, as it's a smaller print! Plus it is a tiny bit longer: as it has dedicated intro for the India version that the international one does not!)

If I would have wanted to self-publish: it would have meant finding a local printer, reformatting the books, doing test prints, verifying quality. This is what a publisher does - and then some more, thanks to knowing the local market (e.g. helping set the price, given the printing and distribution costs, list it on other popular channels like Flipkart, and so on.)

Although I was hesitant to work with a publisher - as a self-published book! - this worked out very nicely with Schroff. And that's the story of how a self-published book is no longer self-published: at least in India!

(The full bestseller list is here: https://lnkd.in/evch3ncz. And more details on the book, and where to get it: https://lnkd.in/deSGVuYm )

text:
NVIDIA became the world's most valuable publicly traded company this week, surpassing Microsoft. Interesting enough something similar happened 24 years ago, in 2000:

This was a time when Cisco took over Microsoft, to become the world's most valuable company, as the time.

There are similarities between 2000 and 2024, including the sense of a potential bubble. The first year of this millennium turned out to be the peak of the “Dotcom Boom,” when massive amounts of investment flowed to internet companies. Cisco’s market cap soared, as businesses preparing for an “internet goldrush” stacked up on Cisco hardware to handle expected future networking demand.

At the end of 2000, we saw the “Dotcom bust” when many internet companies went bankrupt after burning through capital, and failing to raise a new round. Interestingly, the Dotcom Boom is thought to have been partially fuelled by low interest rates during 1998-1999, which caused an explosion of startup funding.

Today, there’s clearly an “AI boom” from which no other company benefits more than NVIDIA, which makes the processors for new AI tools. Some of NVIDIA’s biggest customers are tech companies like Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Tesla, which are setting up large AI clusters. Large and small AI startups are also investing raised capital in NVIDIAs’s AI chips.

So, are we at – or approaching – the peak of another boom, and could there be a rapid contraction in AI investment coming soon, and potentially a big “bust” after that, as in 2000?

It’s hard to predict, but we are seeing signs that investment in AI hardware is not providing a good return in terms of increased revenue. Just two months ago, I asked if we were at peak AI hype because in 2023, 10 times less revenue was generated than was spent on GPUs. And while it’s expected that hardware takes years to recoup its purchase price, a 10x imbalance looks significant, especially as GenAI startups must spend big on software engineering talent to build platforms and tools.

(This was an excerpt from this week's "The Pulse" issue from The Pragmatic Engineer. I wrote a longer analysis on how the future for NVIDIA could be different than that of Cisco, as well as covering other recent events in the software engineering industry are in this issue: https://lnkd.in/eWRsgYj3 )

text:
I would have never expected the EU to seriously consider Orwellian surveillance for all its residents using secure chat apps, but here we are. This bill goes to vote TOMORROW, and could null the right to privacy:

In the name of child safety, the EU would install backdoors for every formerly secure, end-to-end encrypted chat applications (like Signal, WhatsApp, and so on,) with the means for authorities to be able to listen in, anytime.

The problem? It's three-fold:

1. By adding a backdoor to these currently secure apps: we all know that attackers will be able to also spy in, with far more ease. There is no backdoor that is a "secure backdoor because only the government has the keys." This is a fundamental truth with secure systems. (Expect attackers to sooner, rather than later, get those keys, and use them, without notifying you, just like the government won't. This is the fundamental reason no secure communications platform installs backdoors, even for the government.)

2. Mass surveillance will be possible, without probable cause. Today it's a suspicion on one thing. Tomorrow, the government can extend it to practice monitoring against another thing. They already have the tools to do all this, after all!

3. This bill would not even be effective protecting children, while stripping away privacy for all EU residents! This assessment comes from Germany’s Federal Child Protection Association, who detail their problems with the bill here: https://lnkd.in/eEDjFt6Z

The Netherlands, Germany, Austria and Poland are voting against this unacceptable violation of privacy rights. Other countries have not yet said they will: and so the bill could easily pass.

If it does: it would be the end of messaging privacy, as we know it, in the EU. Which would be ironic, given how the EU has traditionally been one of the biggest protectors of individual privacy.

The vote happens tomorrow. If you live in the EU and don't want Orwellian surveillance, you can contact your EU representatives and tell them what you think, and how you expect them to represent fundamental European values like the right to privacy. Find representatives here: https://lnkd.in/e7-Ss9FT

Read more details here: https://lnkd.in/e8uEdtcp

text:
An app using a serverless backend went viral, and received a massive, $98K  bill after a few days of peak usage. What is there to learn from this?

Social media app Cara is learning an “expensive” lesson the hard way, after receiving a $98K bill serverless bill for a few days of high traffic, after their free app went viral.

Cara is an app built by volunteers which allows artists to share their portfolios, find jobs from studios, and stay in touch. It’s a nicely-built mobile and web app, and feels like Instagram / Twitter for artists. See a screenshot of the app below, or see their website here: https://cara.app/

The app launched in January 2023 and saw 3,000 signups the first 3 days, and 10,000 users in the first 3 months. Since then, growth was pretty predictable until a few days ago, when the app blew up, and became a top 5 most-downloaded social app in the US. Here’s what this meant for growth:

- 100,000 users: on 1 June (1.5 years from launch)
- 300,000 users: 2 days later (on 3 June)
- 500,000 users: 2 days later (on 5 June)

Apps built on a more “traditional” backend using virtual machines or SQL databases could struggle with this surge of usage. For Cara, this was not an issue, thanks to the serverless backend that was designed for scale. The issue is cost, though, as the bill for this growth spike needs to be paid to the serverless vendor, Vercel Functions. Vercel has just sent a $98,280 bill for the spike in usage to Cara: which is the bill for the last few days!

Most venture-backed social media startups would love to have Cara’s success. Unfortunately for Cara, the team doesn’t have this kind of funding. So on top of optimizing their serverless usage by caching where possible and reducing unnecessary calls, Cara likely needs to do one of the below:

- Generate more revenue to pay increased costs. Right now, the app only has a “buy me a coffee” donation option. Adding ads or introducing a membership with additional benefits would surely generate enough revenue to pay increased infrastructure costs, especially as they have more than 500,000 users.

- Throttle their own growth to limit costs, and keep the app smaller

- Move off serverless to control costs better. However, this would mean additional development work, and the team would have to make new infrastructure choices.

If you have a side project or a production app running serverless that does not generate revenue, let this be a reminder to set cost budgets, in order to avoid unexpectedly high bills. Good luck to the team behind Cara in navigating this unexpected popularity. It’s a problem many other apps would love to have!

(This was an expert from today's The Pulse in The Pragmatic Engineer. Read the full issue here: https://lnkd.in/eZaNpkmV )

text:
I have a couple of side projects running in production. One thing that is a HUGE difference in how I have no hesitation to touch them even after months of not seeing the code (and thus losing context:)

Tests.

I write them for all the business logic I add / modify.

Just did a refactor on a project I wrote 100% of the code of, and have not touched in several months. Confident that my changes are correct, I ran the tests, expecting all of them to pass. Instead, this happened (the below image:)

Wow!!

My *past self* just prevented my present self from pushing code to production that would have broken this project! Talk about me saving myself! High five to "earlier" me!!

I am convinced having these tests are better than anything else that I could have added to these side projects.

Coincidentally, side projects I am terrified to touch have no such tests, and I don't feel like understanding the whole codebase (my codebase!) from scratch, again.

If you are wondering whether it's worth writing tests: you decide if they are. I know that I don't write these tests for my current self. It's for my future self (or for any other person who will modify the code I wrote, and added descriptive enough tests for)

text:
Until now, if most developers could choose any laptop, the M-series Macbooks (M1, M2 or M3) were the ones with the best performance and battery life. Around 2020-2022, companies like Uber or Monzo replaced Intel-based developer laptops with M1 or M2 models for their massive performance gains.

Microsoft’s latest offering comes with a Qualcomm ​​Snapdragon® X Elite CPU, and the company showcased a side-by-side comparison which showed that image processing on the Surface Laptop is visibly faster than on Apple’s.

An unspoken downside of the Surface Laptop is that all software needs to be recompiled to ARM architecture for it to run. Most existing Windows laptops have been Intel or AMD processor-based, which are based on the x86, 64-bit architecture. Qualcomm’s Snapdragon is based on the custom 64-bit ARM architecture, similar to Apple’s M1, M2 and M3 processors, which are also based on this architecture.

The architecture change means that software needs to be re-compiled to ARM64 for it to run. When Apple introduced the M1 processor in 2020, lots of developer tools and utilities were not ARM64-compatible. Virtualization software like Docker, VMware, and Parallels, did not work, IDEs like Android Studio, Visual Studio Code, and PHpStorm had problems, and Adobe’s apps and games could not run. All had to be re-compiled for ARM64 and tested for compatibility, which took up to 2 years in some cases.

Still, as a developer, this competition in high-performance laptops makes me happy. I guess Apple will respond, and anyone using laptops for compute-heavy or memory-heavy work like compiling large codebases, editing images or videos, or just having hundreds of tabs open for browsing, will be a winner! 

(This was one of several topics from yesterday's The Pulse in The Pragmatic Engineer. Subscribers can read the full issue here: https://lnkd.in/eS8meaq2)

text:
Software engineers integrating LLM and AI building blocks into products is becoming very common within startups and scaleups, as Sam Julien also mentioned.

The last few months, I've talked to a scaleup that is launching "AI features" with no "AI engineers" behind this: "only" backed engineers who picked up the skills to integrate APIs and models into their backend. I know founders at AI companies with software engineering background who picked up the "AI know-how" as they went, and are building practical and useful applications.

One of these "software engineers turned founders" is Ross McNairn, who is building a legal tech startup called Wordsmith AI, where I am also an investor in (this is my first investment since the summer of 2022).

One common infrastructure challenge most AI startups have is building a "RAG pipeline" to provide cleaner prompts, and better LLM responses. Ross and the team at Wordsmith also did it: and Ross generously agreed to share their approach, together with a code-along tutorial in The Pragmatic Engineer.

Here are the details on building a RAG pipeline in a way that any software engineer can follow. If you've yet to get your hands dirty with LLMs and applied AI: this could be a good start: https://lnkd.in/efStNDAw

Use the source code in this GitHub repo to build and deploy your first RAG pipeline here: https://lnkd.in/eme4xUuj

And if you're a developer, I can only recommend dipping your toes in experimenting with LLM tools: may that be running a HuggingFace model on your machine, playing around with OpenAI APIs or following a tutorial to build LLM-enhanced functionality to better understand this technology (and its strengths and limitations!)

While it's clear that we'll see massive demand for specialized AI/ML/"AI productization" roles to scale LLM-powered solutions, tweak them, and make them cost efficient: I expect that prototyping and using basic LLM building blocks (like RAGs) will become a part of the backend engineering toolkit (see more in the thoughtful comment by Jacob Turpin on this topic)

[See the tweet in the screenshot from Sam Julien on X, here: https://lnkd.in/e62U4nRY]

text:
Since when does Microsoft execute *faster* than startups? Magic .dev was announced less than 3 months ago, to build whatever is after GitHub Copilot. GitHub released this "next" product this week:

It's called GitHub Copilot Workspace, and it's an AI-assisted workflow that feels to me will probably be much more powerful than Copilot - at least for a variety of features or small projects. For "CRUD" type work, smaller changes or tweaks, or even fixing bugs, I can see that workflow work pretty well. 

But this post is not about how well (or not well) Copilot Workspace would work. It's about the speed of execution at GitHub.

Conventional wisdom holds that startups have a massive advantage over large companies: speed. A team of ~10 developers can out-execute a company with hundreds or thousands of engineers: because that large company will take months to only decide on *what* to build.

And yet, we're seeing the opposite. Startups aiming to build solutions that work better than GitHub Copilot have not even launched in public, when the "next product to follow GitHub Copilot" is already launched, by the GitHub team.

Big Tech companies like Meta and Microsoft figured out how to move just as fast (or even faster) than startups. This is great news for them: but it means that startups can no longer just count on speed to compete with these tech giants.

See the original article analyzing the Magic .dev announcement, as published in The Pragmatic Engineer here: https://lnkd.in/eh4nBiNv

And here's a deepdive in how Meta built Threads in 6 months, from idea to launch, also unlikely fast for such a large company: https://lnkd.in/e2WqygQS

text:
Why does the Netherlands seemingly have so many successful startups, despite its relatively small size? Today (on "the King's birthday") I got a clue - kids here are encouraged to become entrepreneurs:

Today, is “the King’s day” in the Netherlands. It's a day when anyone can sell anything on the street, without needing a license. But there are some special rules.

Amsterdam’s biggest park (Vondelpark) ONLY allows kids (up to 16 years old) to sell stuff in the park up to 4pm. These children sell their toys, snacks they bought or made, and fun "services” like "try to smash the tomato," "throw an egg at a poster [with a disliked figure on it]" or "throw a plate of whipped cream at a kid" (pictured below.)

The Netherlands is where ASML (semiconductor supplier), Adyen (fintech, valued at $43B), Mollie (the "European Stripe," valued at $6.5B), MessageBird (the "European Twilio," valued at $3.8B), Booking .com is headquartered; and there are so many more examples. For a population of 17M people, the Netherlands seems to punch well above its weight in the number and impact with its startup ecosystem!

As I was walking through the park, seeing kids as young as 3 years old making their first sale: it got me wondering. Could one part of Dutch entrepreneurs being so successful was how early they start? If you get used to the idea of selling goods and services so young: it's less daunting to experiment selling goods and services when you’re older. 

And it’s not just the kids selling: but all the kids buying from each other, sometimes bargaining and trading. They see how it's other children coming up with these innovative "services," sometimes hustling to make a sale, and standing around all day to try sell most of their toys.

I don't know of too many countries that are as entrepreneurial as the Netherlands. Then again, I don't know any other major city that opens up the largest park only for children to become entrepreneurs for a day - and does it every year.

I have a feeling plenty of future startups will be started in 10-20 years by some of these little sellers!

text:
LEGO welcomed two new board members today. Here's me meeting with one of them this week: Ebi Atawodi! Huge congratulations!!

Ebi is hands-down the best product "getting things done" person I know and have worked with. At Uber, I was lucky enough for her to be our team's product manager.

We knocked it out of the water.

Ebi got the whole engineering team involved in product decisions, had regular 1:1s with several engineers on the team, and we very quickly became a team of "product-minded engineers." (Here's a longer post about product-minded engineers: with a lot of inspiration coming from this exact team! https://lnkd.in/et7nWBgW)

After Ebi's scope expanded (and she was no longer only working with our team): engineers on my team proposed projects to build (bringing a business plan to back their thinking) and we got those done. 

A memorable project was an annoyingly vague payments error message we kept seeing, but ignoring for years. An engineer estimated that allowing to fix the most common underlying errors with a tap (expired credit cards!) would bring in $1M/year in annual revenue. We made the fix, and it was a $20M/year incremental increase (!!). This project ended up being the second-most-impactful project (by revenue) the team shipped that year: and it all happened because engineers were encouraged, empowered and knew the tools on how to propose projects they thought were important, in a way that they were likely to get prioritized by the business.

If you'd like to get a sense of how Ebi thinks, this episode with her in Lenny's podcast is an excellent place to start: https://lnkd.in/eDBeyTsz

And see the press release from LEGO here, with the other new board member, Ilkka Paananen (cofounder and CEO of Supercell) also introduced: https://lnkd.in/ehRC7GYU. 

Can't wait to see the impact you'll make at LEGO, Ebi!!

text:
Huge news for anyone working in tech in the US: noncompetes will be banned: not just in California (like before), but nationwide. 

This is very, very relevant for anyone at Amazon (which is the Big Tech that has enforced noncompetes even for low-level engineering positions). But it's just as relevant at other companies that (outside California) added noncompetes to contracts.

Other countries should take notice. The FTC has correctly determined that noncompetes is bad for the economy: although undeniably good for businesses that want to keep wages lower, and enforce lower attrition.

If you read the ruling closer: there is an exception where noncompetes can remain for executives. The regulation defines as an executive as those making more than $151K/year AND being policy makers.

Many senior-and-above individual contributors will make more than this (especially in Big Tech). But they are not policymakers/execs! That's usually Director-and-above.

The regulation is expected to be in effect in a bit over 4 months' time. During this time, organizations can sue the FTC to get this reversed: and the US  National Chamber of Commerce has immediately announced they will do just this.

Still, there's now a very real chance that soon, noncompetes will be a thing of the past for almost all US workers. We've seen what happened in states that did this earlier: California is the hotbed of innovation and startups. It also has a ban on noncompetes. Coincidence?

The FTC doesn't seem to think so. Other countries (that still have noncompetes allowed) could well take notice.

The FTC ruling source: https://lnkd.in/dFeVcXwr

text:
Interesting reflection on "hypergrowth" (hiring a large number of people after raising a large funding round) by Tuomas Artman, who went though all of this at Uber:

(Tuomas is the cofounder and CTO at Linear. From him:)

"At Uber, I learned enough about hypergrowth in headcount that I never want to experience such a level of headcount growth again."

Here is the typical path he's seen for companies that find product-market fit, while raising capital aggressively:

1. Try and find product-market fit (PMF). Try out a bunch of stuff.

2. Found PMF. Hooray!

3. Scale, fast: partially by hiring.

4. Hire *lots* of people!

5. Firefighting, because you don't have much time to invest in infrastructure (need to keep up with the competition, and also onboard)

6. You (finally) get room to breathe! Usually a year or two after you hoped you would.

7. Rewrite it all.

8. Migrate and rebuild that will take years

So, back to hypergrowth:

"The big problem with hypergrowth in headcount is that after a while, barely any of your engineers work on the product. I noticed that a few years into hypergrowth, it felt like 70-75% of engineers worked on infrastructure, and no one was really working on the product. 

Having a large number of people work on infrastructure is one of the major things we at Linear want to make sure that we do not do. We want to keep software engineering as a product problem. Every single engineer should be focused on making things better for the customer, not having to worry about the infrastructure."

And hence the deliberately slow hiring pace at Linear!

The above was an excerpt from the story of Linear, published in The Pragmatic Engineer. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/eqxyrh4M

text:
There are truths that are widely known by insiders; not particularly a secret; and yet rarely ever written down. Here is one:

Hiring processes for CTOs, Head of Engineering, VPE or Sr VPE are all bespoke/different between companies. To the point of wildly different from how the company hires for any other non-executive role!

I’ve been talking with folks interviewing for CTO and Head of Engineering positions the past few months, and this is absolutely what they are seeing. The only shared characteristic is how these days processes are longer than before. Outside of that: there are processes that are 10+ interviews long (with more and more people brought in); ones heavy on references; ones where the board vetoes candidates / pushes candidates through and so on. There is no “standard” process at those levels.

Here’s this articulated in Will Larson’s latest book, The Engineering Executive’s Primer:

text:
This week, X (formerly Twitter) shipped the most amateur URL rewrite I've seen, and created a massive security attack vector. Here is what went down:

Twitter has been rebranded as “X,” but the twitter .com URL remains. In an attempt to fix this, starting on Monday (8 April,) X automatically changed anything in an URL with twitter ․com to x ․com. This would have been fine, if it would have only changed twitter ․com to x ․com, almost like a permanent rewrite. However, the change replaced all twitter ․com strings inside of any URL to x ․com, while still redirecting to the underlying URL. So, for example:

space-twitter ․com was displayed as space-x ․com (but directing, upon clicking, to space-twitter ․com)

Sounds pretty innocent so far, right? After all, SpaceX surely owns SpaceTwitter ․com as well, so that it’s hard to hack SpaceX .com.

But consider a malicious attacker wanting to hijack the Netflix ․com domain. All they now have to do is to register the domain NetfliTwitter ․com, and post a link to it! Users on Twitter will see Netflix ․com displayed, click, and the attacker can attempt to steal their Netflix passwords (in the screenshot below)

X just opened up a honeypot for phishing attacks by neglecting very basic security principles. This mistake suggests a couple of things:

1. The engineer(s) making this change are inexperienced. (I cannot imagine an experienced software engineer who wouldn’t consider security vulnerabilities with URL rewrites.) This is ok! The problem is how such a major change was most certainly not reviewed by a peer, an experienced engineer, or a security engineer.

2. Either X has no security team, or a security team never reviewed this change, or the security team is useless.

3. If high-profile changes like URL rewrites ship without concern for security practices, what kind of attention to detail can we expect for less visible changes? X has closer to 500 million monthly users. You would expect basic security hygene at such a company.

What is certain: this kind of miss would have been less likely to happen at “Twitter 1.0.” That version of the influential social media platform invested plenty in planning and security, meaning it’s likely this issue would have been caught and resolved before shipping.

To its credit, X reversed the change in one day, and by Tuesday (9 April) it was rolled back. The fact that such a widespread change was released with no feature flags and no staged rollouts to hundreds of millions of users, suggests the culture of shipping to production at X can be described as a “YOLO strategy”. I’ve covered the various strategies in shipping to production, and more resilient alternatives to YOLO here: https://lnkd.in/dsQzhQ7S

---
This was an expert from yesterday's Industry Pulse in The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/e2BzQCxZ. Follow me for similar updates on analysis of interesting software engineering-related events, or sign up to get it in your inbox, weekly: https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw

text:
The Pragmatic Engineer Job Board and Talent Collective was reasonably successful by all conventional metrics. And yet, after 2.5 years of operation, I am retiring it for good. Here's what happened:

Short version:

The tech jobs industry is changing, a lot. Job boards are becoming more and more useless/noisy. The "best candidates" are no longer signalling their availability. Looking ahead, platforms like LinkedIn Recruiter look to be winners, alongside specialized tech job firms. Business was ok, but on a stable decline. This move was kind of overdue on my end.

Longer version:

The trigger for all of this was how the vendor I was using to run the job board and talent collective - Pallet - retired the platform I used. They decided to pivot to a search firm approach. For everything I understand, this was absolutely the right decision from them.

Pallet started out as a job board. However, early 2022, the startup innovated with the concept of a "talent collective." 

The core insight Pallet had was how people trust "creators" – and how these "creators" already have engaged audiences.

I was personally reviewing every candidate application: and for the ones that I approved, I added a note on why this profile is interesting or standout – often adding details that I assumed some recruiters could miss (such as open source contributions, mentoring outside of work, or interesting and relevant extracurricular activities that I found encouraging to see.)

My job board had an (initial) advantage of lots of qualified candidates applying. My job board was not a standalone board: but it was interesting tech jobs listed at the bottom of my blog and newsletter. As such, companies initially received lots of qualified candidates.

Still, the majority of companies working with me never posted a single job! They went for the Talent Collective instead and contacted vetted, senior+ engineers there instead. About 80% of my customers were like this.

So why did Pallet decide to shut the board down? It was probably down to two broader trends:

#1: "Creators" stopped running their Pallet job boards and talent collectives. 

In early 2022, YouTubers and newsletter writers started to run their Pallet boards by the dozens. As I look back now, about 90% of the people I remembered having a Pallet stopped maintaining it and using it about a year ago.

#2: Interest in job boards/talent collectives declined, even as traffic displaying these offerings increased. 

Here are statistics on traffic that ended up on my job board (when people clicked through from my newsletters and blog): and how traffic across my newsletter and blog changed over the same time:

There are a few more reasons - including how automated AI application tools will, most certainly make job boards less useful, and others. Continue reading on my blog: https://lnkd.in/eZnhqRzu

This is the reality of experimenting with different things: some work, until they don't. Like now!

text:
WOW. Six months after release, The Software Engineer's Guidebook has sold more than 25,000 copies. This is far beyond what I hoped for - especially since it's a self-published book!

It's funny how things work out in the end. I *really* wanted this book to have a "proper" publisher and spent 6 months pitching to "traditional" tech book publishers and working with them. However, 2 our of the 3 publishers I originally pitched this exact book passed on it, and the third one pushed for changes that would have drastically altered the book in ways I disagreed with. I made the (at the time, very difficult and risky) call to break the contract with this publisher, and write the book in the original form I envisioned it - and the only way I could see it work. 

It's been weighing on me since: what if that publisher was right, and the concept would not work, and their well-meaning suggestions to "dumb down" the book would have been the way to go? I'm relieved that this was not the case, in the end.

The audiobook is now in the works, as well as translation to 7 languages (German, Korean, Japanese, Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, Hungarian and Mongolian).

Thank you to everyone who has picked up a copy of the book (or the e-book!) This is all thanks to you 🙌

More on the book here: https://lnkd.in/gq2722ZF

text:
Interesting how we like to compare tech debt and how it has many similarities with financial debt in characteristics. But one big difference on what it means to "default"

It's much harder & complicated to default on financial debt. With tech debt: you can "just" retire/delete the feature/codebase!

This is especially important in the sense of defaulting when something hasn't worked out. E.g. building a new feature/service, and there is no traction/fit. You can just remove it/delete it.

Defaulting on any kind of loan is more complex, and usually has longer-term implications. There are usually penalties for "serial defaulting" on several debts. But there's rarely any repercussions for "serial defaulting" on your own tech debt (e.g. building experiments in a hacky way, then deleting them when they don't take off). In fact: you could be learning by doing this, and getting faster and building "quick and dirty" prototypes.

This also means that (willingly) taking on tech debt is probably far less of a risky decision than taking on financial debt!

(Inspiration for this post is a guest post we're working on with Apurva Chitnis on what it means to be a founding engineer at a startup, and how tech debt at early-stage startups can be an actual accelerant! That article is coming out tomorrow in The Pragmatic Engineer.

Update: this is now out: https://lnkd.in/ePbE_qTW )

text:
I am seeing dozens of startups pop out of nowhere that are building along the lines of "our job AI job search tool automatically applies to hundreds of jobs for you." This will have a pretty significant impact on job searches:

First off: I get why these companies are doing it. There's tons of demand! It's hard enough to get a job, and paying $100-200 to save tens of hours of application sounds like a bargain. So it's good business to build them (at least for now)

But what does this mean for employers? It means we're about to see VERY noisy inbound applications, full with AI generated stuff. At first: this is easy enough to see. But it will get harder to catch later.

So what will companies do, who want legit applicants?

Either:

1. They buy tools that detect and filter out AI-generated responses.

Ironic how adding AI-powered tools to apply could also allow the very same company to sell a tool to detect applications they send off - but companies will more likely invest into such tools. (As a note: the EU will specifically ban companies to have AI tooling for job applications, unless it is reviewed and approved, given this usage will count as "high risk" as per the new AI regulation. The EU has a point - it doesn't exactly sound fair to have a potentially biased AI reject legit applications, no?)

2. Companies could post fewer positions publicly, or do so that they are out of reach of AI bots (e.g. moving them behind a login wall)

3. Referrals start to matter a LOT more!

You know what AI tools cannot fake? A referral from a current employee.

My bet is that #3 will be the biggest outcome of all this. Companies will invest more in referral programs. If an application doesn't come through a referral: it will end up in the "thousands of (probably mostly AI) applications."

This is one of many examples on how AI tooling going mainstream changes long-standing dynamics in an area. This AI tooling is great for vendors selling it; initially great for users paying for it; and a real headache for companies trying to hire thanks to noise it generates. Still, we will see this play out: so better prepare and respond ahead of time, instead of hoping it won't happen!

What is your take on how these AI application tools will change the hiring landscape?

(The below screenshot is from one of the several such new startups - I don't particularly want to advertise them, they are easy to find with a search about automated applications if you want to)

text:
So THIS is why GM said it will no longer support Apple CarPlay from 2026?! And build their own Android experience. Because they don't want Apple to take over all the car's screens - and Apple demands it does so!

This paragraph is from the US vs Apple lawsuit that the US Department of Justice made public today: 

"Like the smartphone market, infotainment systems are increasingly considered must-have capabilities in newer vehicles. After leveraging its smartphone dominance to car infotainment systems, Apple has told automakers that the next generation of Apple CarPlay will take over all of the screens, sensors, and gauges in a car, forcing users to experience driving as an iPhone-centric experience if they want to use any of the features provided by CarPlay. Here too, Apple leverages its iPhone user base to exert more power over its trading partners, including American carmakers, in future innovation. By applying the same playbook of restrictions to CarPlay, Apple further locks-in the power of the iPhone by preventing the development of other disintermediating technologies that interoperate with the phone but reside off device."

I am not sure which carmaker would agree to hand over full control to visualize and interact with all the car's sensors and controls to Apple? If Apple built this car: sure, I can see this. But without it: what happens when this system malfunctions, and causes an accident? Apple, I assume, will not take responsibility - they are not the car maker, after all!

The odd part is that Apple likely successfully pressured other car makers than GM to hand over full control on CarPlay - because those car makers don't want to lose out on customers with iPhones.

More details in the lawsuit, which is damning for Apple, and paints a monoplist using illegal tactics to reduce competition and raise prices for their customers: https://lnkd.in/eKKTrAZc

text:
What if "The world's first AI developer" is more of a marketing stunt that overpromises its capabilities - because it needs to?

I took a closer look at "Devin," which is a tool from Cognition Labs, marketed as the "world's first AI developer." In demos that look impressive, Devin builds websites, fixes a bug in a library, and can fix some GitHub issues, unassisted.

But the closer I looked, the more this looked like not an "AI dev" but a prototype of an "AI coding assistant."

In what was interesting, I expected that Cognition AI's webpage at least partially be built with this amazing "AI assistant." It can build webpages after all, right?

But even Cognition AI uses Webflow, Google Forms and other SaaS products to present this site, and collect signups.

If we can take away anything from how this startup utilizes their own "AI developer:" SaaS products don't have much to fear. Especially not Google Workspace or Webflow! I'd also argue developers can breathe out: there will be no "AI devs taking jobs:" because these are all AI assistants akin to GitHub Copilot, just with more grandiose marketing.

More of my thoughts on how these AI tools will evolve how we build software in today's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/ek5wX72Y

text:
Just signed the contract to have The Software Engineer's Guidebook translated in... Mongolian! This is a little unusual, for a few reasons:

In general, translating any tech book into another language is a somewhat risky investment that a publisher (the one translating the book) makes. The "main" language of software engineering is English: and if you're a developer, there's a fair chance you'll prefer to get the English version of an industry book.

At the same time, when the market is big enough - say, countries with 50M+ residents like Germany, Korea, Japan - it makes sense to translate these books, as they usually sell thousands of copies. Those kind of sales earn back the translation cost (usually a few thousand dollars/Euros equivalent) and the investment into creating a print copy, printing and distributing it.

For countries at or below 10M residents: it's rare to translate technical books. In a country of 10M, the number of people working in tech will be around 100-200,000; software developers will be a smaller part, and selling even 2,000 copies would mean that 1-2% of all people working in tech would need to buy one. That's a lot!

So it's because of this that a company offering to translate the book in Mongolian (a country with 3.3M residents) is very unusual. Even more unusual is how this is the only country where it's not a publisher, but a tech company, Nasha Tech that offered to do this work. Their goal is to advance the tech industry in Mongolia, and my sense is that - unlike a publisher would do so - they are not doing it in the hopes of generating a profit on this title.

Big up on this initiative, and I'm excited to see the book come out, hopefully later this year in Mongolian!

(The book is underway to be translated and published in Germany, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, Hungary, and under contract in China. Never thought there would be so much interest in it! In the meantime it's available in English as paperback and ebook here: https://lnkd.in/deSGVuYm )
