text:
What’s more important in a car, the software or the hardware? Electric vehicle company Fisker – founded in 2016 – has recently found out that there’s no right answer because they both matter equally:

Fisker recently released the Ocean model in the US, and popular tech reviewer and YouTuber Marques Brownlee reviewed the vehicle, summarizing it as “the worst car I’ve ever reviewed.” Poorly designed, buggy software and firmware were the reason for the harsh review, even though the car’s “hardware” is fine! Brownlee summed up his impression:

“You could give me this car, and I wouldn’t want to drive it. There are so many annoyances, especially with things like cameras, and the driver’s distance things beeping and blinking randomly for no reason. It just feels like the buggiest car I’ve ever tried.”

The video had more than 4 million views at time of publication, and caused a massive panic inside Fisker. We know this because the car dealer who lent the car to Brownlee recorded and shared a call he got from a panicked Fisker account representative, who said all senior management at Fisker were agitated by the review. In the conversation, we learn Fisker previously sent out a technician to “fix” the vehicle, but they had failed. They are now offering to send a more senior technician to do a firmware update. But even this software won’t fix everything. From the account executive:

“What we have now is this 2.0 software. It will fix a lot of problems, but it’s not the Holy Grail of fixing Fisker. It’s still got some holes in it, we still got some more software to redo, we’ve got a little bit more to revisit. But it will make the care markedly improved.”

This fiasco at Fisker is a good reminder that high-quality software is so important in premium products like electric cars. Fisker will have a hard time shedding the “buggy electric car” label, and has only itself to blame. It knowingly shipped cars for 8 months with poor software, and got unlucky that a well-known YouTuber reviewed one.

Manufacturing cars is no easy feat, but it feels to me like Fisker has treated software as a much smaller focus than the hardware itself. They failed to hire a strong developer team, or missed someone who could pull the handbrake and stop the car from shipping before the software was fixed to make it good quality.

The video review by MKHBD: https://lnkd.in/eBMZnHBU

----

This was an expert from The Pulse in today's The Pragmatic Engineer issue. Subscribers can read the full issue here: https://lnkd.in/efyPACCJ

text:
There’s a new (and better) PagerDuty aleternative in town thanks to incident.io adding paging (oncall) support.

This brings incident.io on-par with paging capabilities PagerDuty has… except incident.io has always focused on the full incident lifecycle - that only starts with paging! It’s why so many engineering teams use them, love them and no longer hack together custom tools and flows to track incidents, create postmortems and so on.

PagerDuty has been the go-to solution for oncall alerting:  it for years, the company did nothing *after* the oncall acknowledged the alert. So you still had to either build incident handling on top of PD (eg what Uber did!) or buy a second vendor (what incident.io used to be).

I’m an early investor in incident.io because it was clear that if PagerDuty won’t realize that paging is just the first step in incidents, they would be disrupted real soon. And I figured this team will probably disrupt them in ~5 years. But they did it in 3!

If you’re still using PagerDuty for paging, and a custom solution for incident handling… you might want to give them a go. Combined (incident handling + paging) they also charge less than PagerDuty does: just for paging!

Love to see this disruption. It’s a good reminder that if you don’t build what your customers need you for (looking at PagerDuty), someone else eventually will!

By the way, here are 20 more startups that are all good alternatives to PagerDuty - many of them offering more and better functionality, and most doing the same but at a better price point: https://lnkd.in/ert583VJ

text:
Happy Leap Day! This day can really mess up software systems. Some examples from today: 1. Card payments are down in Sweden's largest supermarket (ICA)

2. The biggest airline in Colombia (Avianca) printed tickets for today as 3/1 instead of 2/29

... and these are just two examples I heard of happening. 

Here are half a dozen more from today, in today’s The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/ezhka_V9 (including a recently released EA Sports rally game - WRC - unplayable, but only today!)

What other issues have you seen Leap Day cause in systems?

Note that some of these bugs might only happen in 8-12 months: e.g. for systems that expect a day to have 365 days (and have it hardcoded) things can get ugly on 31 December this year. Or billing systems that do annual repeat charges by adding a year... but 29 February 2025 is an invalid date!

Handling date and time edge cases are tricky - and while most of us are prepared for things like summer/winter time: Leap Day comes around only every 4 years. So it's easy to forget - until today!

text:
Too many such companies and products... and 90% will build the AI tool and declare success!

At the same time, there are companies like Linear (just one example) where there are surprisingly few customer support folks... because there are very few customer support tickets! Because the product is thoughtful, hard to get lost, and the product is built with the goal of customers not needing support!

When you hear about a company declare how they now handle millions of customer tickets with AI - maybe ask: why do they have so many? Why do customers feel they *need* to talk to a person to get answers? How come their product doesn't provide this?

Don't let AI tools make you lose focus of what matters. Straightforward products >> confusing ones with AI assistants.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
How does ChatGPT work? It might feel like magic, but it's far from it! Here's an engineer-to-engineer explanation from Evan Morikawa who heads up the Applied group in OpenAI (that includes ChatGPT):

When you ask ChatGPT a question, several steps happen:

1. Input. 

ChatGPT takes your text from the text input (simple, I know!)

2. Tokenization. 

It then chunks it into tokens. A token roughly maps to a couple of unicode characters. You can think of it as a word. (Note that tokenization doesn’t necessarily mean splitting text into words; tokens can be subsets of words as well.)

3. Create embeddings. 

An embedding is a multi-dimensional representation of a token. ChatGPT explicitly trains some of their models to explicitly allow the capture of semantic meanings and relationships between words or phrases, and embeddings help with this. The system then turns each token into a vector of numbers. These are called embeddings. 

4. Multiply embeddings by model weights. 

These embeddings by hundreds of billions of model weights (yes, really!) This is where most of the "magic" is: ChatGPT's weight matrix gives it the ability to work as well as it does. There's a lot of pertaining and fine-tuning for ChatGPT to produce this massive matrix. Note that this operation is an expensive one, compute-wise, and it's where specialized hardware like GPUs make a massive performance difference versus using e.g. CPUs.

5. Sample a prediction. 

At the end of this multiplication, the vector of numbers represents the probability of the next most likely token. The next most likely token are the next few characters that spit out of ChatGPT.

ChatGPT, in the end, is a probability machine that gets really, really good at always predicting the next best token/word, to the point that it feels like it understands what you ask. Once you know how it works, it's easier to understand its limitations (e.g. it won't be able to come up with things not in its training data - not without hallucination at least!) as well as how to get around these limitations (e.g. supplying additional context or training). 

It's also easier to understand why e.g. asking ChatGPT to summarize a text requires a LOT more compute than asking it a question (summarizing a text means a lot longer prompt, and the longer prompt means far more calculations; but asking a question "just" uses the existing weight matrix)

For more details and illustrations, see this week's free The Pragmatic Engineer deepdive on how ChatGPT works and scaling challenges (and hardware limitations) the team overcame: https://lnkd.in/eDb8d_T5

(Thanks, Evan, for all the details!)

text:
Happy to share that The Software Engineer's Guidebook is confirmed to be translated to Korean and Hungarian, and under contract for German, Japanese & traditional Chinese (Taiwan). 

When publishing the book, I considered whether I would translate it to Hungarian - because this is the country I'm from; where I grew up, studied and graduated. But at the time I figured: it would be a lot of work, and the "main language" for software engineering is English, after all. Why bother?

However, from the first week since the book was released, publishers from various countries reached out, asking to negotiate the rights. Several seemed to monitor Amazon for new releases and then evaluate whether the book could do well in their countries.

After negotiating with several of them - and getting to agreements - I also walked back on my thinking with Hungary. It would be weird to have the book come out in several languages, but not the one that I'm also a native of (and can validate the translation!) So for this country, I did the reaching out and convincing: Hungary is such a small market that few books are profitable at the publisher level.

The ETA for most translations is fall 2024, the in Hungarian is spring 2024. I'm talking with publishers in a few more countries, and perhaps more would be added.

Thank you to everyone who purchased a book early on - I sense it's what might have put the book on the radar for some publishers. And I'm both excited and curious how these translations will come out: I've never done anything like this! I'll share as they do, and what I learn about translating books, with a publisher.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The US did something no other country has considered: treating software development like hardware or property, forcing dev costs to be amortized over 5 years (domestic) or 15 years (foreign devs). This makes zero sense, and this is why:

When buying hardware or property: you have a one-off, large investment, and then you spend relatively little on it, while it generates its useful value. Like a server doing it over ~5 years, or an office building over ~20 years.

But say you want the code you wrote this year to generate useful value for 15 years (in the case of hiring a dev abroad, this is how long the development costs need to be amortized in the US, going forward). If you invest nothing more in that feature or code, it won't generate any value in a few years, when it stops working!

Software is not like a building that needs very little renovation, or hardware that needs little to no maintenance.

And this is why 99% of countries in the world do not mandate software development costs to be amortized over 5 years or more. Starting in 2023, the US is the only exception, with the change in Section 174.

Read more about this change, and why this will result in fewer software engineering jobs and more layoffs at US companies - and less innovation coming from the US, and also likely bad news for the global tech industry in this The Pragmatic Engineer article:  https://lnkd.in/eH6Jzaqc

(If you live in the US, and disagree with these tax changes, you have the option to contact your elected representatives who vote on such regulations.)

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The US has suddenly become one of the most unfavorable countries to start a software startup thanks to a tax change within "Section 174." Let me explain:

When you start a company, it's common to take a while to break even. Let's take a startup that has just reached this level: they employ 5 software engineers (paying $200,000 each) and generate $1,000,000 in revenue. How much corporate tax do they need to pay on profits?

For most countries: the answer is $0. This was also the case in the US, up to 2022. From 2023: the answer is that they generate $900,000 in profit the first year, taxed at $189,000.

All this is thanks to a new tax regulation that mandates that all software development labor cost MUST be treated as R&D. And all R&D cost MUST be amortized over 5 years (or 15 years for foreign activities). So employing software engineers can no longer be expensed in the same year, regardless if these engineers are maintaining existing software, fixing bugs, building internal tools not meant to sell to customers, and so on!

The outcome is already bad for companies with little cash reserves. There is less software engineer hiring; some companies are forcibly laying off devs. And there is now little to no incentive for foreign companies that get US VC investment to do what they have done until now - set up a Delaware corporation that owns the IP of the company.

US software companies, startups and even companies like Microsoft and Amazon are calling to the government to reverse this tax regulation, which has made the US the most hostile to software engineering innovation - putting a taxation burden no other country imposes when employing developers.

I talked with founders impacted and share a lot more details on this complicated issue that has a massive impact on all of the US tech sector (and the mainstream media, for some reason, has been sparse to report on it). Read it in this week's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/eH6Jzaqc

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I bought a new iPhone for a small fortune (upgrading my 2-year-old 13 Pro to a 15 Pro) and I've never been so disappointed in an upgrade. My new phone is the same as my old one:

Ok, there is one visible change: the USB-C charger which used to be a Lightning cable. Aside from that: performance is the same; memory is the same; the camera feels the same (the 13 Pro already had excellent cameras). If anything, my 13 Pro had a bit better built-in speaker with the bass!

Barclays has downgraded Apple stock for the first time since 2019 citing that they believe iPhone sales will continue to plummet thanks to new iPhones lacking features (and thus, reasons) to upgrade. Having upgraded and being disappointed with anything new (that used to be the case), I have to agree with them. This upgrade feels like money poorly spent.

I wonder if we've simply reached the point with smartphones that we did around the mid 2000s with PCs. In the 90s, you had to buy a new PC / processor / graphic card every 2 years to run the latest games, as hardware improved so dramatically. And since around 2010, 4-5 year-old laptops and PCs are still fine to run games or apps.

Smarphones used to add new features and capabilities (like biometric identification, strikingly better camera, far more battery life, bigger and better scenes) every year. But not the last few years.

I paid more for iPhone 15 Max than my Macbook Pro M1 (a 2-year-old laptop which I am using right now). The two devices have somewhat comparable hardware inside them (a 6-core CPU in the iPhone 15 Max, and 20 cores inside the M1). 

Unless we'd see new features on future phone models - like on-device AI capabilities that are useful - I can not see a reason most people would want to upgrade already powerful smartphones every few years. So I bet Apple and other device manufacturers will focus on bringing these features to *new* smartphones in the next year or two!

(The below screenshot from a December issue of The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/e-WkJRC2)

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
An interesting observation: most devs do *not* necessarily gravitate to work at profit centers within companies.

This is because many profit centers are generating revenue, but in return can feel “boring”… versus new projects that are greenfield and exciting. Some of those new projects have the opportunity to later become profit centers… but many will either be cost centers or later on, discontinued/merged in. But they provide lots of challenges before either of those outcomes manifest.

This is very similar to how companies that are incumbents and mostly focused on extracting more value/profit from existing products are usually a lot less exciting to work at: versus working at a startup that is actively experimenting on how to disrupt the status quo.

This is the case not just for devs, but folks like product managers, designers and other professions where creative experimentation and scaling up once hitting a “pull” is often more interesting than incrementally tweaking an existing product.

text:
Something interesting about Stripe’s engineering culture: helping (unblocking) other engineers is a big focus. Which sounds… smart? 

When a company goes beyond a certain size, being blocked by others is the most common time waster in my experience. And at companies that don’t have any incentives for engineers to help others outside their immediate team, things tend to slow down. 

Not only this: things usually become more siloed. When building your own service to unblock yourself is easier than to make a small change to another team’s one (because they keep ignoring your request): you get an engineering team that keeps reinventing the wheel, thanks to teams and engineers not particularly helping each other. Short-term gain, long-term pain!

This is how David Singleton describes their approach in The Pragmatic Engineer:

Full article with more details: https://lnkd.in/dPY3RwUa

text:
It's hard to believe it's *already* been two years - then again it's strange that it's *only* been two full years of The Pragmatic Engineer. Here are three things that changed with the newsletter, this year:

1. I go directly to the strongest sources for engineering deepdives. 

Previously, some details about tech companies and their engineering cultures came from former employees. So this year, I increasingly went directly to the most authoritative possible sources: CTOs, and current engineers, for fully accurate views of engineering cultures. With this approach, I brought you never-before-shared details about companies like Stripe, OpenAI (and ChatGPT), Meta (and its Threads team), Figma, Sourcegraph and Chronosphere. Read these here: https://lnkd.in/etA3C6UV

2. Renaming “The Scoop” as “The Pulse.”

One highly visible change this year was doing away with “The Scoop” title and rebranding Thursday’s series as “The Pulse.” ‘The Pulse’ just better represents the mission of these articles, which is to help you keep an “ear to the ground” on what’s happening across Big Tech and at startups – sectors which regularly affect the whole tech industry.

Names matter, and I feel “The Scoop” suggests a more gossipy, tabloid newspaper-style approach than what these articles actually deliver.

3. The “Industry Pulse”

This is a new column within The Pulse that rounds up recent events, with brief commentary. I experimented with this column in September and asked for feedback; almost all of you responded that you’d like it to stay. I also enjoy writing it, so it’s here to stay!

For a longer summary of how the newsletter changed; the most popular articles; and a recap of important events in tech during this year, see The Pragmatic Engineer 2023 review here: https://lnkd.in/eNDCb5QP

Thank you to everyone who has been, and continues to be a reader!

reshared_post.text:
I highly recommend getting yourself a mentor.

For a long time, I hesitated to actually try and find one. Not only am I not good at asking for help, but I felt embarrassed to ask the people on my radar.

But then it turned out *I* was mentoring someone and I didn't even know it! A friend and former team member asked me to keep having our 1-1s (which I always have with everyone on my teams) after I left the company and moved to a different country. Which was fine by me, I enjoyed the chats, I learned a lot from them. One day, this friend casually referred to himself as my "mentee", and I was taken aback. I had no idea!

This made me realise that being a mentor doesn't have to be a big imposition.  So I approached a friend whom I admire and asked for a recurring chat. It was very unstructured, and we didn't even manage to have many sessions, but the ones we did have were valuable to me.

With this new outlook on mentoring (and being mentored), I started thinking of whom I should ask next. I focused on people I admired, were approachable and had performed successfully in the challenges I saw myself facing in the near future. I quickly came to a name, that in hindsight sounded obvious. I asked him (again, timidly), and he quickly agreed.

There's much more structure, I have the simple "homework" of sending him the topics for our conversation beforehand, and we're very time-constrained so it tends to be quite focused (while still allowing for chatter - we are friends, after all). It has helped me immensely in the 1.5 year we've been doing it.

All of this to say: Gergely Orosz's suggestions are all excellent. But if you find yourself not having a mentor because of some (perhaps self-imposed) friction, try to unblock yourself by lowering the task. Ask a friend you admire for some of their time. Most people are happy to help.

text:
Lots of people use the “Jack of all trades, master of none” in a degrading manner. A reminder that the *full* saying is this:

“Jack of all trades, master of none,
Oftentimes better than master of one.”

It’s how the saying was meant to be used. Cutting off the second part leaves out the original observation and implies the opposite meaning than intended :)

The full saying is one I’ve observed with software engineers who are competent at several technologies and stacks (eg backend, frontend, native mobile, LLMs), versus someone who is very deep into language/technology/stack but unwilling to touch the others and needs to rely on another generalist or specialist to get stuff done across stacks.

This is not to say we shouldn’t encourage or celebrate people mastering things: like technologies, languages, stacks. We absolutely should, and there’s only upside in doing so. But I would caution looking down to those who are open and curious enough to dip their toes in unfamiliar territory (eg new technologies) and get things done (even if not perfectly at first - it’s the first step in eventually mastering things, after all!) versus not even trying.

- are you getting this done or waiting for someone to tell you what to do?
- underpromise and overdeliver.
- let people know what you did, because if no one knows, it didn't happen

text:
Software engineer Anna Boyko has an impressive profile and was a featured speaker at DevTernity conference on 7-8 Dec. So how come no one at Coinbase knew anything of her?

DevTernity advertises itself as the "#1 international software development conference" and has speakers like Scott Hanselman, Kelsey Hightower, Sam Newman - and, of course, Anna Boyko.

But I couldn't find ANYHING about Anna Boyko. Asked current and former Coinbase employees; checked Ethereum core. Nothing! Could she be made up? On such a prestigious conference? Her profile was featured on the site since January 2023 (more than 10 months). The conference was about to start. Surely, she couldn't possibly be fake?

But in the end, yes: Anna Boyko, one of the 3 confirmed women speakers at DevTernity doesn't exist. She was knowingly invented by the organisers (the conference has no call for papers).

But she wasn't the only fake speaker at the conference.

Alina Prokhoda, "Senior Engineer at WhatsApp, Microsoft MVP": she was also fake on the JDKon website scheduled in May 2024: ran by the same organizers.

Natalie Stadler, "Software Craftswoman at Coinbase," a featured speaker at DevTernity in 2021 and 2022: does not exist.

Julia Kirsina "Software Craftswoman, Tech Influencer @ Instagram:" a curious no-show speaker for years. Julia was listed as a speaker at DevTernity in 2021, 2022, 2023, but never delivered a talk. She lists herself as being a software engineer at Uber on her Xing ("LinkedIn for Germany") profile. But no such dev ever worked at Uber. An investigation from 404 Media suggests that a man is linked to this profile, details here: https://lnkd.in/eXKz2B2P

For 3 years, DevTernity listed fake women speakers: some obviously fake and one never showing up to talk. But why?

Possibly to address diversity concerns raised by high-profile speakers (who would have refused to accept to talk) and customers (who complained about the lack of diversity in the past).

Why invite women to speak when you can just make them up?

A new low in tech conferences.

Do your due diligence DevTernity, JDKon and anything organized by "Dev .Events" to confirm if speakers listed there exist. And - as sad it is to say - but check if speakers listed on lesser-known conference websites are actually real.

See more details on the facts here in my summary article here: https://lnkd.in/ezvihsRG

And if you would like to invite underrepresented folks in tech to speak to your conferences, there are plenty of them! Some resources:

- Post a CFP to Diversify Tech and reach 15,000 underrepresented folks for free: https://lnkd.in/eNrBt3sd

- Invite former guest from Scott Hanselman's podcast. Browse 920+ fascinating profiles: https://lnkd.in/e9T6iHhk 

- Reach out to people, do so respectfully, and offer to pay for their time! For a paid conference, this should be a given. You should hear back from most this way.

If you have other resources, please do share them.

text:
The story of how Amazon .com operated off a single server in 1997, and how Joshua Burgin accidentally took the whole site offline for several hours:

Joshua was one of the first 100 employees at the e-commerce giant back when it was a much more modest operation. Here is how he recalled this story  :

"My journey in backend development began with an unexpected stumble, not a carefully considered first step on a pre-planned career path. In 1997, when Amazon was a one-floor, sub-100 person startup trying to sell books online, I experienced what every engineer dreads.

On the Alpha box, we had an “online” and “offline” directory. To update code, we flipped a symlink (a symbolic link is a file whose purpose is to point to a file or directory) to swap between the code and HTML files contained in these directories. At the time, this approach was our best effort to deliver code on the nascent web. 

Amazon .com ran on top of a monolithic C binary named “Obidos,” named after the narrowest, swiftest section of the Amazon river, in Brazil. Obidos shouldered the entire load of a business which became an online retail behemoth. 

Here’s how the outage played out:

1. I pushed code from dev to staging using a script where everything worked fine.

2. I then half-manually pushed code from staging to production. I still used a script, but had to specify parameters.

3. I inadvertently specified the wrong parameters for this script. I set the source and destination Apache configuration as the same.

4. Rather than failing with an error, this encountered an existing bug in the DEC Unix “copy” (cp) command, where cp simply overwrote the source file with a zero-byte file.

5. After this zero-byte file was deployed to prod, the Apache web server processes slowly picked up the empty configuration file. And it lost pointers to all its resources!

6. Apache started to log like a maniac. The web server began diligently recording logs and errors it encountered.

7. Eventually, the excessive Apache logs exhausted the number of inodes you can have in a single directory

8. Which resulted in the servers becoming completely locked up!

9. To resolve this, we had to do a hard reboot. This involved literally pushing the power button button on the server!"

Read the full story with learnings, and how all this ties to the evolution of backend services in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/ebnySNd4

Thanks Joshua for sharing. What an experience!!

text:
It was unclear who or what could stop OpenAI from dominating and winning the AI race. The past 3 days, we've had our answer: OpenAI itself. Their board managed to implode the company from the inside.

On Friday, OpenAI's 6-person board fired their CEO Sam Altman (also a board member), and removed cofounder Greg Bockman from the board. Over the weekend, Sam Altman negotiated to potentially return to the company as CEO - following investors and employees demanding this - but OpenAI's now 4-person board hired a new interim CEO in a matter of hours from first contact to finalizing it: Emmett Shear, the cofounder of Twitch.

Today, Sam Altman announced he will be the CEO of Microsoft's newly created AI unit. The majority of the 700-person staff at OpenAI are most likely to follow him there this week.

From the leader in AI to a good question on what remains of the company, just in a few days.

If there's one takeaway from these events, it's that an inexperienced and immature board can cripple a company. OpenAI's board will be an example used to demonstrate this. So much value destroyed by a very small group of people who did not follow standard practices on how a board operates: like taking their time to debate key decisions; involving investors; and having a public communications plan ready for contentious decisions like removing a CEO, so they get ahead of public sentiment.

text:
How does OpenAI ship so fast that it's hard enough just to keep with new product releases? I managed to collect never-before-revealed details from inside the current team:

Evan Morikawa was one of the first engineers on the ChatGPT team; the first engineering manager here; and today he heads up about half of the 130-person Applied team. The Applied team builds - among others - ChatGPT.

Evan shared details on why he believes OpenAI (and ChatGPT) moves with velocity that is truly rare to observe. As we talked, a few things stood out to me:

1. Small and senior

It's striking just *how* fast small, senior teams can work. ChatGPT is a clear proof to this. The team only hired senior-and-above engineers until now.

2. In-person & colocation speeds things up

The ChatGPT team was in their own physical space, working in-person most of the time. Today, they spend 3 days in the San Francisco office (Mon-Wed), and the other two days are optionally work remote. In-person made not just experimentation rapid, but onboarding was very fast. The team grew from 6 to 150 people in less than a year: so this is a big deal!

3. High talent bar

OpenAI keeps a very high talent bar. Even though they are a startup, they have managed to create a brand that is well-known; they offer market (sometimes above!) compensation; and - most importantly - the opportunity to work on hard challenges, and ship those ideas in the hands of customers. 

While it might feel like OpenAI is “lucky” in how they have a well-known brand and the funds to attract standout people: don’t forget that just years back this wasn’t exactly like this. Sure, a well-known founding team helped: but OpenAI had to earn all this.

4. Start from scratch

When ChatGPT started, the whole team started from zero: fresh repo, fresh cluster, and no reuse of existing code (however tempting it was). It's so much easier to move faster when you have no baggage to carry!

For more details on how OpenAI operates - including surprising details on how they move fast while also prioritizing safety - see today's The Pragmatic Engineer article:

text:
The Software Engineer's Guidebook has been awarded the most prestigious "award" it can ever be eligible for: The Amazon "#1 Best Seller" badge. This means the book has been the #1 sold book in an Amazon market for days straight.

It is now day 3 that this book is consistently the most-sold in the Netherlands, and the badge is currently visible. This is surreal because I have mostly seen this badge for mainstream publications with an extremely wide appeal that have very heavy publisher backing: or for children's books around Christmas time! 

The "#1 Best Seller" badge is distinct from the "Best Seller" badge that all books that is #1 in their respective category gets. At any given time, there are 5,000+ books awarded the "Best Seller" badge (Amazon has about 16,000 book categories, and all larger ones have this badge), but only ONE book across the whole site the "#1 Best Seller" badge. In the Netherlands: it's mine, right now!

This book is not mainstream:it  is primarily targeted at software engineers and engineering managers (and those who want to dive into pretty in-depth topics for these groups). And not only does it not have publishing backing: in its current form, all traditional publishers either rejected it, or wanted to alter the style it was written in "to sell better."

This badge represents the highest possible "award" that this book could possibly get. You will hear of other books being on the New York Times Best Seller lists, or the Washington Post Best Seller list, and lists from other newspapers. But if a book is self-published, and only sold on Amazon: it will never make it to those lists: as those lists omit each and every sale on Amazon. A book needs to have been launched with a publisher to be even considered for those lists.

I cannot thank you all enough for taking a leap and ordering the book. It *is* my best writing yet - arguably "baked" years more than any of my newsletter issues. It is clear that most of you are buying this book ignoring any Amazon reviews: because there are (still!) none! (If you do finish the book, an honest review will be helpful for others, of course).

More on the book and you can look inside: https://lnkd.in/gq2722ZF

text:
A small downside of self-publishing on Amazon as an author: you cannot order advance copies - even when you try to!

I was the very first person to place a bulk order for The Software Engineer’s Guidebook on Saturday, a few seconds after the book silently went live on Amazon US, and before I told this fact to anyone.

Having snuck to the front of the line, I thought I’d be one of the first people to get my package. How wrong I was! I assume what happened is thanks to the major demand of orders coming in shortly after (thanks to all of you!) Amazon could have distributed my 15 printed copies to 15 individual orders, in the spirit of customer obsession: disappoint one person a bit to make 15 others happier. So a lot of you got the book even after ordering it well after I put in my order. I cannot complain, but I was a bit envious to see books arriving to lots of you, but not me :)

Well, today, I also finally got these copies! And it’s SO good to see the final book. (This is the first time I saw the finished book; beforehand it was only a proofing copy, and I made several modifications on it, and I hoped it would come out fine!)

text:
An untold story behind how The Software Engineer's Guidebook became one of the few tech books (perhaps the only one?) to be the #1 across *all* Amazon books in several countries. It's a story of repeated rejection.

In the fall of 2019, I decided that I would write this book: but I wanted to maximise its chance of success by going with the most prestigious publisher in the tech industry. It would have been my dream to be an author with them. So, I put together the strongest pitch I could.

And the publisher almost said yes! For two months, they debated internally. It was The Software Engineer's Guidebook, vs a few other similar books. Then they chose the other books. They were nice about it, but the rejection was basically, "we don't think yours is good enough."

I pitched to another publisher I liked. They passed almost immediately. I then went to my third choice, who offered me a contract, and I started writing.

However, this publisher had very strict style guidelines that they put in place to ensure a book would sell. My book was pushed to become a kind of schoolbook of "here is what you will learn in this chapter; here is what you have learned in this chapter." They kept pushing me to simplify what I wrote to make it beginner-friendly as it would sell better. The final straw was when they suggested I add "Alice and Bob" to make my point as all their recent best-selling books used this kind of simplified explanation. I tried to do this, but then looked at how my writing looked... and I hated it.

So I ended my contract and went back to writing how I originally envisioned the book. The structure that all three publishers rejected. I wanted to prove all of them wrong and also prove that a book can cover both the "hard" skills, and the "soft" skills for engineers, and not have Alice and Bob or other "language that makes books sell better".

I now wonder if the book would have become as good as it is without all these rejections. Every one of them fueled my motivation to better the book, and write what software engineers would want to read, badly: and not a format that has historically sold well (and thus was what publishers wanted to see).

This is my first rejection letter, below. It hurt. But in the end, the person writing it was right: I DID find a publisher that did the book justice. This publisher is myself, or as it's in the imprint: Pragmatic Engineer BV (my company).

I am so glad I was right to stick to my gut, in the end. And, of course: thank you every one of you who helped on this journey. The book is a success thanks to you buying it, and using it!

text:
The Software Engineer's Guidebook is out!! 413 pages, 27 chapters, 10 online bonus chapters... and 4 years of writing.

On 15 October 2019, I sent a pitch to a well-known book publisher, and wrote that I plan to write a book which:

"offers guidance on how to grow as an engineer at a tech company. It follows the structure of a typical career path for a software engineer, from starting as a junior, leveling up to an experienced engineer, all the way to being successful at the staff/principal/distinguished levels, making organization-wide impacts."

While awaiting their response, I got to writing it. The publisher ended up passing on the book – and so did a second one. A third publisher did offer a contract, but we parted ways a few months later when it became clear their rigid style guidelines would turn my book into something no longer in my voice. 

I knew exactly what I wanted to write, and how I wanted to write it. Going at it alone – by self-publishing – is the approach I’ve taken. I also promised myself to not publish the book until I was certain it was at the high quality bar I set for it.

Now, the book is finally ready! 

It’s been four years of writing, re-writing, and polishing the manuscript. Turns out, good things do take time. The book is called “The Software Engineer’s Guidebook.” It’s been my longest-running project to date – excluding raising children, that is! It’s also a project of which I’m very proud of, and that I hope you would find valuable.

You can take a look inside the book, and get it here: https://lnkd.in/deSGVuYm

(For now, the book is released in print. An ebook and audiobook will follow later - but I wanted to get the print version out first.)

text:
This is a neat response to the Stacked Diffs article I wrote, rightfully suggesting that merging to trunk *even when the review is not finished* should be fine in many cases.

There are certainly plenty of teams where this could work. There are also teams that do post-commit code reviews (especially teams that don't ship every change to production, but have build cuts).

In the end, it's down to your risk tolerance. If you are a small team, you shouldn't need to invest much (or any!) into more advanced tooling for problems you don't have! And pushing to trunk in most cases should be just fine! 

When you get larger and have more problems thanks to e.g. waiting on code reviews (that you decide should happen before merging) then stacked diffs can (and usually do!) become a lot more efficient.

But if it ain't broken: don't fix it! And don't add more complicated tooling than what you really need. Thanks for the great writeup Alex Jukes (and read it here: https://lnkd.in/e9pdkYRG )

text:
Imagine getting fired with no warning and your contract being very clear about what kind of severance you are entitled to expect in this case. But this severance not being paid. This is what the world's richest man is doing:

At Twitter, Elon Musk fired a few thousand people - 50% of the staff -  exactly a year ago. Everyone let go had very clear terms in their contract that specified how much severance they are owed.

And yet, a year later, those severance packages still not paid. Not because there is insufficient Money - Elon Musk is the world's richest man and paid $44B for Twitter - $26B out of his own money.

But because... why exactly?

Lawsuits are in progress, and in a span of years, the people owed severance - and joining in on the class-action lawsuits - should get their due.

But why are people celebrating a leader who ignores contracts, and doesn't pay severance and vendors, for the sake of it?

I used to have respect for Elon Musk for the things he made happen - electric cars, reusable rockets - but having seen how he treats people with unneeded and unnecessary cruelty makes me question why anyone *with other options* would want to work for the man, directly or indirectly.

This is what a billionaire bully looks like.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

reshared_post.text:
It's been a year today since the first wave of Twitter layoffs 🫡 and one year later, Twitter still hasn't paid the severance former employees were promised.
 
It may sound like a minor detail in the grand scheme of things but it's one of many that invalidates the narrative that it was "just another tech layoff", or the case of a new owner having to make hard decisions to save a company nobody asked him to buy (and he didn't want to buy either).

Instead, these layoffs, together with the week that preceded them, were engineered to bully thousands of people that the new owner despised and disparaged publicly in the months preceding the acquisition.
 
Why bring this up? Because there's a growing movement in the tech industry right now that claims that the end justifies the means. That kindness, respect and empathy are getting in the way of progress. And like everything else, this movement starts at the top, with the most visible figures in tech, who get pass after pass for their bullying because "electric cars" or "spaceships".
 
That's why this severance story matters. So many people still stretch themselves to excuse the bad behavior of tech leaders, but nobody can, in good faith, justify that not paying the severance promised to your former employees is going to make any difference to the “greater good”.

On a brighter note, in the months that followed the layoffs, I received countless messages of support from people across and beyond the industry. During my job search, many of my interview conversations would begin with words of empathy, with some people calling what happened "disgusting" or even "f***ed up".

The compassion I’ve encountered convinces me that most people in our industry do value and prioritize kindness, respect and empathy. But our collective voice will be heard only if these conversations happen in the open. That's why I'm speaking out today, as it is essential for our peers, especially those just starting in tech, to hear that message, and recognize the values that most of us stand for.

In India, The Software Engineer's Guidebook as paperback is now available for preorder! But why only in India, and when will the rest of the world follow? Some behind-the-scenes of self-publishing:

(If you are in India, here is the link to get it: https://lnkd.in/e2jxv-gT from my publisher Shroff Publishers & Distributors Pvt. Ltd.)

I'm self-publishing this book. When self-publishing, you need to figure out how to print and distribute books. Luckily, Amazon has a service called KDP (Kindle Direct Publishing) which is a print-on-demand service live in 12 countries: US, Canada, UK, Germany, Netherlands, Spain, France, Italy, Sweden, Poland Japan and Australia.

One major country it doesn't cover - but I've had lots of interest from - is India. To publish on Amazon India, I would need to either use a self-publishing service (such as Pothi), or partner with a publisher. I chose to do the second one, after talking with fellow author Alex Xu who has also self-published The Systems Design Interview, and ended up partnering with Shroff Publishers in India.

A downside of self-publishing with Amazon KDP is there is no option to set up preorders. But when going with a publisher, it is possible put this in place (which are fulfilled outside Amazon, until it will be listed on Amazon India as well). And this is what happened!

text:
What is happening at Okta? Okta is an identity provider, where security *should* be their #1 focus, and bread-and-butter. And yet, the company suffered the second, utterly embarrassing breach in two years:

The "utterly embarrassing" part on the two breaches is how in both cases, it was not Okta that disclosed the breach details: but third parties - like its customers - because Okta, for some reason, failed to share these.

Breach #1: In 2022, Okta suffered a security breach through its customer support function, and only acknowledged it after a third party disclosed the breach happening.

On 22 March 2022, a Twitter account shared evidence that suggested that Okta was compromised January 2022. The next day, Okta’s Chief Security officer acknowledged that Okta was, indeed, breached in January, confirming that it has taken Okta more than 2 months to investigate this incident - and not report anything on it until then! - writing:

“On March 22, 2022, nearly 24 hours ago, a number of screenshots were published online that were taken from a computer used by one of Okta’s third-party customer support engineers. The sharing of these screenshots is embarrassing for myself and the whole Okta team.”

In this report, the company estimated that up to 366 of its customers – companies – could have had their environments accessed by a threat actor, for a period of 5 days. A month later, in April, Okta concluded its investigation stating that 2 customers were compromised, and the threat actor had access for 25 minutes.

Worth asking: how was it possible that 2 months after the breach, Okta still had no precise understanding of how the threat played out? This offered no real reassurance to customers.

Breach #2: breached *again* through customer support, and not notifying customers for 3 weeks.

To make things worse, this time it was not Okta who detected the breach: but their own customers had alerted the identity management company of the breach. Okta then sat on this information, idle for weeks allowing more of its customers to be breached.

See the attached screenshot on the timeline, and how 1Password, Beyond Trust and Cloudflare (Okta customers) all detected the breach; mitigated it' notified Okta, and Okta did... nothing?

Why would any organization trust that Okta follows sensible security practices, following two breaches that showcase what feels like an inexplainably sloppy security culture? Breached the second time, through the same channel as before, and, again, not acting on the breach?

Screenshot from this week's The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/eXk-wBy7. Worth reading howw Cloudflare - an Okta customer - responded to the breach, mitigating it, and acting about 100x the speed as Okta's security team did: https://lnkd.in/eq7jqf8f

text:
This drawing is one of the most famous Big Tech comics, ever. Here's the story behind how Manu Cornet created this image in 2011, and then decided he should not publish it:

As context, Manu is an iconic cartoonist who created hundreds of cartoons during the 14 years he worked at Google and later when he was at Twitter. He just published a book with all his Twitter comics titled "Twittoons: One employee's cartoon chronicle of Twitter's accelerated descent." I read it, it's funny, and it's available here: https://twittoons.com. As I talked with Manu about this comic, I had to ask about the "Microsoft orgs with guns" image and how he came up with it. So he told me the full story:

"Initially, the punch line was meant to be Oracle. At the time, this company was suing Google over its use of Java, and I decided to make fun of how Oracle has a much larger legal department than engineering, so I drew the Oracle image.

To make a good punchline, you need a buildup. So to make this a good comic, I needed to draw other companies for a comparison. So I drew four more: Amazon, Google, Facebook, Apple.

I still needed one last one, to make it six and make it symmetric. Microsoft was, of course, the obvious missing one. So I researched the culture at Microsoft a little bit and came up with that — now famous — gun drawing with Microsoft.

After you’ve been working on an idea for a while, you completely lose track of whether it’s actually funny or not. After I finished it, I didn’t find it funny. I showed it to my partner at the time, and she didn’t find it funny either. I was very close to not releasing this cartoon, in the end! 

Then I just published it.

It’s ironic because this comic became probably my most popular comic, and the Microsoft drawing with the guns was the one that people found the most funny. It shows you how clueless I am about predicting what will work!"

See more of Manu's work and an how he visualized that time when engineers were told to print out all recent code code they wrote on paper (!!), and bring it for code review in today's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/ej4nHmNZ

#twitter #softwareengineering #comics

(As always, the links posted are not affiliates)

text:
The first thing I learned when working on payments systems is the concept of a ledger: all payments systems have this. So imagine my surprise reading in the FT that Revolut... might have not had this in 2021? Impossible.

A ledger is the very basis of any accounting system: it's a collection of debit and credit transactions. It allows to summarize these transactions (the sum of debits and credits) and you can then confidently tell what the balance of the account is (e.g. how much profit you are making, or how much loss).

Ledgers are so basic that they are part of accounting, everywhere. Implementations can vary - it can be as simple as a spreadsheet, and can go all the way to custom built systems. Any commercial accounting software has this, and when building an in-house payments system, you start by putting this in place.

The Financial Times later issued a separate correction that states:

"An article on 23 October 23 stated that Revolut used its own accountancy software. In fact the company developed its own accountancy tools which it complements with a third-party package."

So yes: Revolut didn't need to add a traditional ledger to custom tools, because the accounting tools that they have used already had them. The highlighted part of the article is inaccurate, and the correction comes later in the article. While Revolut is innovating in many areas, it has no intention (or option) to replace the concept of the ledger.

The full FT article titled "Will Revolut ever get a British banking licence?" is here: https://lnkd.in/eAHkafSf

It is worth paying attention to when (and if) Revolut will succeed in securing a UK banking license. 1 out of 8 people in the UK use Revolut - 8 million customers in the country alone! - and the UK has granted 36 banking licenses since 2013 for much smaller players. Surely it is just a matter of time that Revolut gets this license granted.

text:
You join a startup as a software engineer. Get promoted to director of engineering. Then you learn that the company is doing something fraudulent. What do you do? Hopefully, not this:

At cryptocurrency exchange FTX, an engineering director got direct evidence in September 2022 that there was almost certainly fraud happening: that Alameda Research (a separate hedge fund from FTX) had taken customer funds from FTX and lost them. Alameda Research should have never had access to FTX customer funds.

At this point, this engineering director had a choice to make: immediately stop assisting what looked like potential fraud, or to become an aid to them.

The smart thing would have been to talk immediately to a lawyer and get professional advice.

Instead: this engineering director talked to the founder; and decided to stay to "help clean up" the mess.

Two months later, FTX went bankrupt, and this person is facing up to 75 years in jail, having just pleaded guilty to six charges. (The actual sentence is likely to be much lighter thanks to a plea deal).

It's a good reminder that software engineers can be put in situations asked to assist with something illegal (or even carry out code changes to enable potentially fraudulent activities). And going along with them can get you in very tangible trouble.

It's cheaper to learn from someone else's mistake. Don't "stay around to try and clean up" what looks like fraud. Talk to a lawyer and get professional advice; consider being a whistleblower (here's a guide on it: https://lnkd.in/euJu4bp8), and refuse to assist to aid breaking laws.

Screenshot from yesterday's The Pragmatic Engineer. Full subscribers can read it here: https://lnkd.in/eMvNW7f3. Quote from the screenshot from Molly White at https://lnkd.in/e9gCcJQB

Update: in the comments, Andrew Row pointed out that this director of engineering took out a $3.7M loan from FTX days after he found out about the fraud. Could explain why he stayed on: and also an incredibly poor judgement (and likely to mean stricter sentencing for him) https://lnkd.in/ekwEximH

text:
I caught up with Linear's CTO Tuomas Artman, after they raised $35M at a time when very few startups can raise. I asked a lot of things, including how they use GenAI. Tuomas's answer surprised me:

He said:

"We've had a few projects that we’ve worked on, some of which are live in the product today, and some which we’re not yet happy with. We do believe that generative AI will quickly become table stakes that every app just needs to have in one way or another.

The current GenAI tooling moves so quickly that it feels like it’s wasteful to spend time on AI-driven functionality, right now. Six months from now you’re likely to be able to implement the same functionality in a fraction of the time, as tooling evolves. 

You won’t be seeing any 'write the description for my issue' AI any time soon in Linear. We try to keep you on top of the work that happens at your company, by summarizing and aggregating your data, not by expanding it needlessly."

While lots of startups are jumping on the GenAI bandwagon, I can't help but think that not rushing ahead - and waiting for tools to mature - is the right approach for startups where AI is not at the core of their business.

We talked about other things, like their tech stack changing, how they feel about so many businesses copying Linear's design, and how they raised funds at a time when it's challenging to do. Read the full discussion in today's The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/eB6DPVHC

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
We hear little about bootstrapped companies, despite bootstrapping being an effective way to get up and running. Why is this? Here are a few reasons - and five successful ones you probably have not heard about, until now:

Why is there so much media coverage about VC-funded companies, and publicly traded ones, but relatively little about bootsrapped ones?

1. By design

Many of these companies prefer to fly under the radar, and focus on building a sustainable, profitable business, and don’t seek a bigger public profile. In fact, as I was reaching out to feature bootstrapped companies, one bootstrapped company I hoped to interview flat out told me, “we're going to have to politely decline an interview at this point for focus reasons.” Good on them for knowing their priorities!

2. Lack of "drama" or newsworthiness

A well-run business simply isn’t very newsworthy because it’s inherently undramatic, and the media runs on stuff that is! When a company raises a large sum of cash, it’s newsworthy, and when it announces a major hiring drive, that also is. In contrast, there’s not much to cover in a bootstrapped business that didn’t raise funding or go on a hiring spree, and did no layoffs when the economy turned.

3. Not "buying" media coverage

A final reason is that the media landscape is full of stories which someone has got in the news by using money or influence. Companies can and do buy media coverage. You can hire PR agencies to pitch stories to media outlets, and this practice is so widespread that the line between genuine news and paid-for promotion is sometimes blurry. I’ve learnt this fact because I get so many press releases in my The Pragmatic Engineer inbox about funds raised, product launches, and so on. I ignore practically all of them.

4. Five successful and profitable bootsrapped companies, founded by software engineers, that you've (probably) not heard about, until now:

- Fern Creek Software founded in 2001 by M. Keith Warren: a boutique development agency

- Ticket Tailor founded in 2010 by Jonny White: an event ticketing platform

- Formspree founded in 2015 by former Apple engineer Rohit Datta and former Squarespace PM Cole Krumbholz: forms with no backend code

- Friendly Captcha founded in 2020 by former Lyft engineer Guido Zuidhof and Benedict Padberg: GDPR compliant bot protection

- Secta Labs founded in 2022 by former Facebook & Replit SWE Alex Kotliarskyi and Marko Jak: professional headshots with AI

Read about the founding story of all five startups; their growth path; and tech stack in this free issue of The Pragmatic Engineer here: https://lnkd.in/eksXfE3D

#bootsrapping #softwareengineering #startups

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
How does working at a startup, vs at a large company/Big Tech compare? Nothing beats first-hand experience, and Willem Spruijt alternated between these places for 15 years. His take:

As context, me and Willem worked at Uber for 4 years, on the same team most of this time (Rider Payments). Willem spent 6 years at Uber in total, before starting his latest startup, Rise Calendar (https://lnkd.in/eVsMFjGG)

Here are his observations on what is "the good" about each places:

Startups: learning.

"Startups are such amazing places to learn in! You usually have a small developer team, and for the most part, no infrastructure, security, data science, or observability teams – which are all commonplace in Big Tech. Engineers are expected to jump in where needed, even when you don’t have expertise.

So you are forced to learn about specific topics and immediately apply them in production. Just by doing this, you broaden your expertise incredibly fast in a wide range of topics."

Startups: impact.

"You can make a large impact and directly influence the direction of the company, and very clearly influence the success of the company, as well. Startups are very flat organizations, meaning you are often involved in strategic decisions.

An example of making a company-wide impact at Rise was during our Christmas hackathon, when an engineer built a Calendly-like feature for anyone to book a slot in your calendar. It worked so well that we launched the feature a month later. Going from idea, to adding, to building and shipping this to all customers, is something you rarely see in bigger companies."

Big Tech: specialization.

"Big Tech organizations have so many specialized teams! At Uber, they ranged from mobile feature teams (called program teams – here is some history about this split: https://lnkd.in/duxvwJeq) platform teams, networking teams, and infra ones."

Big Tech: financial stability and perks

"Speaking for software engineers, Big Tech pays well and almost always issues performance-based cash bonuses. Then there’s the equity granted, which is more stable and lower risk compared to startup equity.

Joining a Big Tech, and building up a “financial buffer” before going to a startup, is something I’ve seen plenty of engineers do. Things may always go south for a startup, so it’s good to have reserves!"

Read "the bad" and "the ugly" that Willem has to share in this week's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/egTJxGiB

Also, Willem's startup, Rise Calendar has publicly launched after 2.5 years of development. It's a very polished tool in my necessarily biased opinion: https://lnkd.in/eVsMFjGG (disclaimer: I'm an investor - it was a no brainer to be involved, as I knew Willem for so long before. More on my investments: https://lnkd.in/e2UuNH_m )

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
When I left fulltime employment 3 years ago, my plan was to finish a book on growing as a software engineer. It's taken longer than planned, but is coming together:

Below is the nearly finalized table of contents for The Software Engineer's Guidebook (https://lnkd.in/deSGVuYm ) which is the longest-running project I've worked on. It's ironic how writing a newsletter was what got in the way of finishing this book: but I finally have an end date in sight. The book will be out in the next 1-2 months, and before the end of the year.

Writing a book has several stages. Here are the ones I've already passed:
- Ideation phase
- Outline of the book
- Development editing (how the structure is built) 
- Weighed table of contents
- Placeholder cover design
- Landing page (so people can interested can leave their email)
- Writing of the draft (--> the largest of all!)
- Copyediting the draft

Currently, it's in this stage:
- Content review (thank you to all reviewers helping!)
- Final editing review

And next up, before it's ready:
- Print layout design
- Cover design, part 2
- Test printing
- Launch prep: e.g. update landing page; add potential bonus chapters (ones cut from the book in an effort to keep the length reasonable)
- Launch!

It's kind of reassuring how writing a book, and building a complex software project has lots of parallels. The biggest one being that things take longer than I planned: especially when having pride attached to the final outcome; or when it's stuff I've not done before.

One downside of the book taking this long to write: the rewriting! The world changes, and I want the book to reflect reality on launch, so I had to rewrite early drafts of chapters from 2-3 years ago. For example, I added in AI coding tools (a must-have in today's software engineer toolbelt); or tools that make engineering more productive like cloud development environments (CDE). In these cases, writing The Pragmatic Engineer has been helpful in keeping up with changes that feel like they are the start of long-term trends in the tech industry.

The most important thing is how the book is coming. I can't wait to get the remaining steps done, and launch the book!

#bookwriting

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Microsoft was the company to pioneer the Software Development Engineer in Test (SDET) role in the 90s, and then got rid of it in 2014. But why? I was there, and here is what I observed:

My first team at Microsoft was Skype for XBox One. On our team, for for every 2 developers (SDEs) we had 1 developer in test (SDET). Devs wrote production code; testers wrote automation and executed manual tests when necessary (and automated these).

What about unit tests? I'm glad you asked 😅 

This was an initial source of contention, and we had a lot of back-and-forth on it until we settled on a solution (detailed in https://lnkd.in/eFjW67Jd )

Early 2014, I moved to a new team, Skype for Web. This team was different to most other Skype (and Microsoft) teams in how we didn't ship once every few weeks: but daily, even multiple times per day.

This team consisted of 6 SDEs and 3 SDETs, on paper. In reality, we were 9 SDEs, thanks to our engineering manager and the test lead who quietly decided it made zero sense to have a dedicated test role, when we shipped new features every day. 

And it worked: we became much more productive by removing the SDET role from our team! 

SDETs still focused mainly on testing-related work, but also picked up development tasks. Just as importantly, we paired a lot! I remember pairing with former SDETs to build a feature. I was decent at thinking about how to make something work, and SDETs were really good at pointing out edge cases I hadn’t considered. When it came to debugging, turns out that the testing mindset is super helpful, and I learned plenty of tricks this way!

Web teams across Microsoft started to quietly remove the SDET role. Back in 2014, our web team in the London Skype office felt ‘special,’ as the only other teams to merge the SDET function were web-based teams, of which there were not many. On every other team, SDETs kept working the way they always had.

And in the middle of 2014, Microsoft formally retired the SDET role and introduced the unified SE (software engineer) role. Which was, basically, what our team - and many other web teams - were already doing, and it worked pretty well for us.

For more details, check out this week's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue:  https://lnkd.in/eFjW67Jd

I've not worked with dedicated SDETs since the Skype/Microsoft days. In some ways, this is a bit sad: as I learned a ton about the 'testing mindset' from devs specializing in testing. A shoutout to the awesome Skype for Xbox One and Skype for Web teams, which were both great groups, and challenging, as well as fun projects!

text:
Talking with devs at AWS & AWS customers, one thing that AWS does better than probably any vendor:

Talking to customers.

Ask yourself: which other vendor called you, to want to meet and have *their PM* directly talk to you? AWS does this all the time! For most of their products. PMs reaching out directly to customers.

And it's not just that they reach out to customers: but an account manager at AWS told me that PMs on internal teams ping them all the time, asking if they can introduce this or that customer to them. Why? Because the eng/product team noticed this customer started using their product, and wanted to get their feedback on it. Again, it was the PM who would direct this outreach, not some customer support person.

Hard to believe but true. Here is Steve Yegge saying the same thing: when Grab was a very small AWS customer (earlier days), they got the same "royalty" treatment, and even Steve was confused as to why!

One thing that is amusing: AWS being obsessive about customers is an open secret. So why are so few other vendors doing this, and why are so few PMs not hopping to talk with their customers, directly?

The below screenshot is from an interview with Steve Yegge in The Pragmatic Engineer. Read the full piece (full of interesting stories) here: https://lnkd.in/egtnAkbj

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Are Google Cloud's availability zones sometimes running out of the same building? For its Paris region, the answer seems to be: maybe!

A fire in a data center caused a Google Cloud region to go offline for some time in Paris, France, back in mid-April. This should have not been possible, as every Google Cloud region consists of three or more zones, so one zone going offline should not stop the region from operating. 

But when Google Cloud powered down the building, it was not just one zone that went offline: but a second one, partially, as well.

So what is a zone within Google Cloud? It is a deployment area, which should be both physically and logically distinct from other zones. This is how Google defines this unit:

“A zone is a deployment area for Google Cloud resources within a region. Zones should be considered a single failure domain within a region. (...) Google Cloud intends to offer a minimum of three availability zones (physically and logically distinct zones) in every general-purpose region.”

Google published a pre-postmortem of this outage, where they made it crystal clear that powering down the one building "required all of europe-west9-a and a portion of europe-west9-c to be temporarily powered down.”

In what is curious: once the full postmortem was published, this reference on a part of the zone powered down was gone, replaced with acknowledging that Google's Spanner quorum was incorrectly distributed:

"Regional impact occurred because regional Spanner no longer had quorum, due to a misconfiguration. It had two of its quorum in the building powered down, instead of being correctly spread across the three buildings in the europe-west9 region"

I asked Google if europe-west9-a and europe-west9-c are operating at least partially from the same building. This is a straightforward question, as was Google’s response: they have nothing to add beyond the final incident report.

So, we don’t know for sure and Google won’t say. Throw in one more question mark around GCP, especially if you plan to use the Paris region!

Compare this with AWS, where the question on whether or not zones are in separate locations: this is crystal clear:

“Availability Zones are multiple, isolated locations within each Region.”

So no, AWS would not have two zones in the same building. As far as I know, most zones are ~20-30 miles away from each other, to ensure resilience for local events like floods, earthquakes, and so on. A fire in one zone should not bring down a full region: not for AWS, but also not for any mature cloud provider.

I covered more on this incident and my investigation in today's The Pragmatic Engineer at https://lnkd.in/dwaM3KcG

#googlecloud #resillience

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Three engineering challenges when building Uber's observability system, as shared by Martin Mao, one of the first engineers working in this area:

(As context, Martin was one of the early engineers on Uber's observability team in 2015, and eventually became the manager of this team. He left Uber in 2019 to co-found observability platform Chronosphere)

1. The scale. In 2018, these numbers were:

- Ingesting 600 million metrics per second (!!), requiring ~130 Gbits/sec bandwidth

- Storing 20 million resulting metrics per second, requiring ~50 Gbits/sec bandwidth 

- Getting 2,000 queries/second

- Powering 9,000 Grafana dashboards and 150,000 realtime alerts

- Fetching ~30B data points per second, requiring ~20Gbits/sec bandwidth

(All of these numbers are massive: and they are from 5 years ago!)

2. High cardinality

Cardinality refers to the uniqueness of data values in a label or tag. High cardinality means a label has a large number of unique values, which can be challenging for data processing and analysis.

3. Uber’s dynamic business growth

There aren’t many systems around that are designed to handle 10x year-on-year data growth! But it's how Uber was growing 2015-2017. Two years of consecutive growth means a 100x increase in data stored, which means you need to redesign the system. With this level of growth, horizontal scaling of a system went out out the window.

To make things more challenging, at Halloween and on New Year’s Eve, Uber would have about 4x the average daily load, but the team never got a 4x budget to put 4x as many machines in place for those days. 

Martin shares more details on how Uber's observability system - based on M3 - was built, and why the team decided to build a system from the ground up, instead of keeping using a Cassandra-based one. Full subscribers can read it in today's The Pragmatic Engineer here: https://lnkd.in/eRNwEHS9

#observability #scale #softwareengineering

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Meta built and launched the Threads app in 5 months. This would be an impressive timeline from a startup: even more so from a large company. How did they do it?

Here is what the Threads engineering team shared:

1. Evoking one of Meta’s early mantras, “code wins arguments.” 

Even before the team received the official greenlight, engineers were already prototyping the idea of a text feed, to prove feasibility.

2. Working in secrecy.

Pre-launch, the core engineering team was isolated from the rest of Instagram’s engineering org. Working in secrecy, we added our code to the shared codebase discreetly and only looped in partner teams when we faced issues which required their expertise.

3. A small team of senior engineers.

Some of the most senior engineers across the Instagram org were pulled into this project. This kept the team small and focused.

For a lot more details on how the project was built, and approaches the team followed, see this week’s The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/e2WqygQS

#meta #threads #softwarengineering

text:
Talked with a founder who shared a low during their career: when they were a TLM with 20 reports. Still, they were expected to produce the output of a senior engineer, plus lead the team at the same time!

This diagram doesn't get old:

Unfortunately, the Tech Lead Manager role is often in the smack middle of all of this: meaning that either you do a poor job at some (or all) of these; or you burn out; but most frequently: it's both.

There are exceptions to this, of course. Healthy TLM roles I've seen all shared how the TLM was experienced - and usually was a manager in the past - their team was small (usually well below 10 people; frequently around 5); the mission clear; and the team pretty senior, with not much career growth ambition for most people (which meant the TLM had to spend less time on career growth support; and could spend more on hands-on work).

The image is from this The Pragmatic Engineer article: https://lnkd.in/eNKxDuJj

article.title: Measuring developer productivity? A response to McKinsey, Part 2
article.subtitle: newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Who would have thought that it would take a McKinsey article to get me and Kent Beck talking - about why both of us got worked up by what we felt is a misguided take. And so we have published our response, for the benefit of software engineers:

As background, McKinsey came up with a framework that they now market to C-level folks as the solution to measure developer productivity. The company has introduced it at nearly 20 companies already, and now feels ready to onboard more companies to this system.

Both me and Kent Beck feel companies onboarding to such a framework will find that the damage caused to the software engineering function could take years to undo. But criticizing would only go so far.

The reality is that engineering leaders who are seen as wanting to avoid the question "how do you measure developer productivity" will eventually find that the frustrated CEO hires McKinsey - or another consultant - who claims they have a framework.

So, as software professionals we *need* to answer this question. We present an approach we strong believe works better, with the interest of maintaining a healthy engineering culture, and is far more in-line with how e.g. Sales or Recruitment teams create the kind of accountability that many CEOs expect from Engineering.

Read this article in today's free The Pragmatic Engineer here: https://lnkd.in/ejivCJ-N or on Kent's newsletter here: https://lnkd.in/edPpek4M

Also, Kent Beck has an excellent newsletter I recommend subscribing to: Software Design Tidy First? You can find it here: https://lnkd.in/e3eVz3WN

#softwareengineering #developerproductivity 

—-

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Which software engineering practices were ones coupled to the decade-long zero interest rate environment when investment was plenty and growth was more important than profitability?

These will surely be ones that make little business sense when you need to think about ROI on investment (in terms of engineering effort).

A few that will likely fall in this bucket:

- Not paying attention to cloud / SaaS per-unit costs and how it will scale with usage going up

- Not starting as a “microservices by default” architecture. Microserves as a default makes sense when you predict hypergrowth in hiring: which is no longer the case.

- Building an in-house, complex, developer focused tool over adopting an exiting open source solution or considering buying one (taking costs into account)

-Immediately reaching for hiring X% more engineers as a way to want to increase the engineering team’s output by X% 

What are practices you think thrived ~2010-2022 and will increasingly disappear going forward? And new ones that will gain popularity?

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
One thing I wish I realized much, much earlier: company internal trainings are an ok place to learn - but you probably can learn the same stuff by reading the slides. What this setting is so good for is this:


To meet people you have some common work interests/challenges with.

I avoided company internal trainings like the plague for a very long time... until I realized this. And then suddenly it was a lot less time wasted, especially eg the manager trainings: meeting managers from totally different disciplines, and understanding what they deal with.
