text:
How useful is it to know 'traditional' software engineering if wanting to get into games development? Here's what Tommy Thompson, director at AI and Games says:

(As a bit of context, me and Tommy met around 2010, our desks overlooking each other. Back then, both of us built mobile apps. Since then, Tommy went into games - both building them, consulting, an teaching how to build them - , and I went into more 'traditional' software engineering)

"Being a competent, technically proficient software engineer is a skillset that easily transfers to games development. All you need is time and practice to better understand the minutiae of game engines!

Having a background in “traditional” software engineering is a huge benefit for a game developer. Writing in a game engine requires writing code that doesn’t conform to ‘traditional’ approaches. Sure, you use object-oriented languages, but often in ways that butcher the principles of the object-oriented paradigm in order to service the needs of the engine. 

The game engine pushes away from ‘good’ programming practices and operates in its own unique way. You have to rethink how to approach implementing a feature to fit the game engine’s environment. Building games frequently means breaking good programming practices because you learn how to write the language in service of the game engine."

This quote is from this week's The Pragmatic Engineer, where Tommy did a walkthrough of building a simple enough game using Unity - and contrasts 'traditional' software engineering principles with how it's done with game development.

Full subscribers can read it here: https://lnkd.in/eHhnJ58u

And if you are interested how games typically get built, see this free The Pragmatic Engineer article, also by Tommy: https://lnkd.in/e2Vsx32J

#softwareengineering #gamesdevelopment

text:
I am a paying Google Docs customer. Imagine my surprise when I learned that one of my documents was blocked - but not from Google, but from a customer trying to access it.

The document in question is an email template people can send to their manager to expense The Pragmatic Engineer. Here's the link to it: https://lnkd.in/eQsZY3ZG

And the below image is what people saw. Google claims that document violates their terms of service, with definite language. It doesn't say this document "could be in violation" of the ToS, but that it "is."

But how is it that Google doesn't notify the main stakeholder in this case: me? The customer who both pays for the service, and who authored this document that supposedly violated the ToS?

The document doesn't violate anything, and here are its contents: https://lnkd.in/eVTBc3MT

I am speculating that when multiple people report a document, Google automatically blocks it. This is not unreasonable: given that Google needs to ensure its ToS is upheld: and letting people report violations - and then automatically blocking these documents until a manual review completes - can be a sensible approach.

What is not reasonable is not notifying paying customers when this happens, and putting up a clearly false statement that states that this document violates a ToS. It does not.

I'd understand this attitude if I was a free user - as we know that when you don't pay for a product: then you are the product. But I pay for all of this! And I pay because I expect stuff to work: and when a shared document is blocked, at least Google to notify me.

A reminder that this can happen to anyone using Google Workspace - and Google will likely not notify you either.

#googleworkspace

text:
Zoom told it's employees that those living within 50 miles of an office should return to the office at least 2 days a week. Is this a U-turn, given how Zoom and the tool for remote work have been synonymous the past years?

My gut reaction was to say: "Yes! Zoom doing an RTO feels like it's going against the company's DNA."

However, as I looked a bit closer: Zoom never had full-remote neither as it's main business use case, nor as it's company culture! The company's #1 use case since its founding in 2011 had always been "hybrid" work: teams in an office jumping on Zoom calls with teams in other locations; and people working from home; and on the go. And Zoom's leadership was clear that they are planning to return partially to the office as soon as lockdowns end.

As I looked through the company's website: everything about Zoom's marketing is about hybrid work. All the images are a mix of people working from the office: and some others being on a call - likely working from home. A typical images from Zoom's website is below: and I could find no images that did not have at least some people in an office on their product pages.

Zoom can undoubtedly be used - and is used! - for full-remote teams. But I'd wager that this segment of companies is not the majority of revenue for Zoom: and I'd expect that companies working as hybrid are the majority of their customers.

So is Zoom expecting staff to work from the office 2 days a week a deviation from their company's culture (and business interest?) I'd say it's not: and that this makes perfect sense for the company, and it's in-line with what the company's leadership had been saying for years: that the majority of Zoom employees will work both from the office, and from home.

I went into more analysis on Zoom's RTO a week ago in The Pragmatic Engineer. Full subscribers can read this piece here: https://lnkd.in/eS__uWrf

#zoom #remotework

text:
What is the job market like? No better person to answer than someone who has been out there, navigating it full-time. Davidson Fellipe was kind enough to share his observations in depth:

His job search took 3 months, starting from April this year.

In April and May, he observed a lot of hiring freezes, and processes cancelled.

Focus: initially, he applied to lots of places. He then changed his approach:

"Instead of applying broadly, I focused on engineering manager positions for frontend, product engineering, and developer experience (front-end.) Over time, I gradually opened myself up to individual contributor roles, but only for frontend positions (senior, lead, staff, and founding engineer roles.)"

And as to what tools helped him during the search, and how useful referrals were, and advice he has for job seekers? He shared all this in today's free issue of The Pragmatic Engineer which you can read here: https://lnkd.in/eufuJygA

One thing I'd like to highlight is how, even as he was searching for a job, Davidson still paused to help people on the way:

"I helped others impacted by layoffs, and the other way around. I shared opportunities or redirected recruiters to others I knew were let go, and people did the same for me. We were all experiencing the same challenges, after all!" 🙌

Thanks Davidson Fellipe for sharing, and congrats on getting through a tougher than usual market, and finding a great place to join! (You can also follow Davidson him on LinkedIn if you're interested to see his thoughts in your feed every now and then!)

text:
I updated my blog's robots.txt to opt out of ChatGPT training its next model(s) on my writing. Why? Because letting OpenAI train on my blog is a one-way relationship that benefits OpenAI, OpenAI's customers: and no one else.

OpenAI crawls the public internet, builds models that they then monetize: but the sites that were trained on get no citation, no links: nothing. And the public sees no benefit either: as OpenAI does not release models in the open. It's only made available for paying customers OpenAI approves.

At the same time, the more ChatGPT trains, the less reason anyone would have to visit my site to read e.g. my article on leading a project as a software engineer (https://lnkd.in/dzsSFaJ), my thoughts on readable code (https://lnkd.in/eWJD5eF6) or the concept of the "seniority rollercoaster" (https://lnkd.in/eYRwXjdY). ChatGPT can summarize all of these without hinting at where it learned all the details!

OpenAI is also a for-profit company: and has made a full 180 since its founding in 2015: and is the opposite of everything the company stood for back then.

OpenAI was founded as a non-profit company in 2015, with the mission to  "advance digital intelligence in the way that is most likely to benefit humanity as a whole, unconstrained by a need to generate financial return." However, the OpenAI of today has transformed into a for-profit entity, and employees are given Profit Participation Unit (PPUs): which give a share of employees for the future profit of the company (more details by Levels.fyi here: https://lnkd.in/eRRZMq7S). This aligns employees with generating profits - else their PPUs would be worth nothing, which goes against their financial interest.

Finally, when OpenAI builds its models: these models are closed. They are not released to the public to be used: instead, they are monetized by OpenAI via strategic partnerships (like the one with Microsoft) and customers who pay to access these models.

There are companies that build LLMs with the intent of opening them up. For example, Meta's Llama 2 model is available for free for research and commercial use. If Meta crawls my blog and uses it to train Llama 2: at least there is a sense of contributing to more of a "common good," which is not the case with ChatGPT.

So I've banned GPTBot.

Credit where it is due: OpenAI is the only AI company that allows to opt out from their crawler. No other AI vendor allows anything similar. Of course, OpenAI has a head start of having trained for years on public sites: and so the contents of my blog are likely part of ChatGPT 3; 3.5; and 4, and they will not (can not!) be removed. This change only impacts future models and my future posts to come.

Official docs on how to opt out are published by OpenAI here: https://lnkd.in/eJJbmbZd

text:
Is programming closer to ‘classical’ engineering or to novel writing?

This is an interesting question: and in The Art of Science and Engineering, Richard W Hamming (inventor of the Hamming distance, among others) suggested that, in his experience it’s closer to novel writing:

Reflecting on this question: I am also leaning to say that programming is closer to writing than it is to a 'hard' science.

Learning a programming language has similarities to learning a new language: a mix of understanding basic rules; repetition; feedback. Writing a program is expressing your thinking with that programming language. And you can get pretty creative in how you structure your code; create new structures; make very different design choices than what your peers would do; go against 'conventional' wisdom: and still get it to work!

And one more observation on the similarities: according to my wife, from her point of view there’s also no difference between me coding something - estimating that I’ll be done by dinner but then regularly running over by 30-60 minutes - or writing an article and doing exactly the same.

What is your take? Is programming closer to a 'hard' science or to writing a novel?

text:
An interesting find: Wise publicizes their software engineering salary ranges on their website, for all regions they hire in. Here they are:

Two things that tend to surprise people:

1. That all these numbers are public. And that the numbers are only base salary: equity allocation comes on top of these (which all engineers get).

As for cash bonuses: Wise doesn't do them: they issue equity instead with (which equity is liquid, as they are a public company, and equity allocations make employees longer-term shareholders and owners).

The transparency is very nice to see. While most companies have similar ranges internally: they are often not even shared with all staff. Publicizing these ranges also helps ensure fair pay, as every employee understands where the ranges start; where they end; and where they are within this range.

2. The wide gap between the US and other countries.

In my experience, most companies have a similar ranges thanks to the cost of labor differing across markets: that is, how much it costs to hire someone for the skillset in a given region.

The CTO of Wise, Harsh Sinha added additional context to these numbers:

- The numbers reflect what the company has found it takes to close great engineering talent in the respective markets.

- Wise's US footprint is much smaller, engineering-wise, and the company has scaled their engineering team heavily in the EU and UK.

The image comes from the The Pragmatic Engineer article "What is a senior software engineer at Wise and Amazon?", which goes into more details on what's expected at the senior level at Wise. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/exrbS2ps

(If you're wondering how these ranges are much higher than the usual numbers, here's an explanation on the trimodal nature of software engineering compensation: https://lnkd.in/de6Bt3r )

text:
A few months ago, I parntered with VC firm CREANDUM and their Head of Talent Michelle C. to try and answer: what are tech leaders at startups paid? A deepdive with results is out, and some surprises:

(As context, we ran this survey for about two months in April-May this month, and have been analyzing the results since. With more than 1,000 responses, there's plenty to uncover with the data.)

What the data shows, and was somewhat surprising:

1. Female tech leaders tended to make more on the median, than their male counterparts. This is both surprising but also nice to hear, given the opposite tends to be true in most industries.

2. Full-remote companies tended to pay more on the median, than hybrid, or even remote from a certain country. Again, one that is a bit unexpected given how full-remote results in more freedom of organizing the workday, thanks the lack of commute.

And some findings that are perhaps not that surprising, but reinforce market trends:

1. The US pays the most - by a lot - followed by the UK. Rest of Europe, Middle East, Asia all follow behind.

2. FinTech pays well: from all the sectors, packages were a bit higher than in other ones.

3. Equity is murky territory in early-stage startups and it's tricky on how to value it. The median packages don't have an equity component spelled out exactly because of this: but equity is the part that is especially important for leadership roles at startups.

See more details and analysis in this report created by CREANDUM and Michelle C.: https://lnkd.in/eTnC3Yfu

I'll also follow up with my own analysis of the data, and will send it out to all The Pragmatic Engineer subscribers in a month or two (you can subscribe here to not miss it: https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw)

Data like this is tricky to get hold of, especially in bulk, so I hope these details on the median packages are helpful! And thanks to CREANDUM and Michelle for the idea to do this research and report.

text:
How different is it to hire tech talent for a startup, versus to hire for a top academic institution? Well, Jean Yang was a professor at CMU and hired there: then founded her startup. Her take:

(As context, I sat down to talk with Jean earlier this week, right before she publicly announced how Postman had acquired her observability startup, Akita Software, and Jean shared her story from interning at Google/Facebook/Microsoft, through being a professor at CMU, and then the 5 years of Akita software)

Her take on the difference between hiring for academia, and then at a startup: that she had to "unlearn" a lot of things from academic hiring!

Apparently, at academia, the outcome teams optimize for is to get to a couple of "top" papers. So you can tolerate high variance.

At startups, you optimize for working as a team: and so teamwork and consistency is a lot more important.

Read more about what Jean learned in founding, running and selling her startup in today's free issue of The Pragmatic Engineer here: https://lnkd.in/eaPmn4GN

And, of course, congrats to Jean Yang and the Akita team on the sale, and their continued work to innovate in the observability space!

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
A teaching lesson that stays with me to this day: at college, we had a course where teams built an app over several weeks. Controlling a logistics chain simulation.

On evaluation, the prof went around and entered invalid inputs to every user input field. He broke half the apps immediately.

The first reaction to everyone was: "Hey! That's unfair! You didn't even check all this other stuff we built?"

His response was along the lines of "come back later today after the app *actually works* and I'll take another look."

To this day, I think what this prof did was, indeed unfair and harsh: in the specification we got, there was no mention on working with invalid inputs! But looking back, I appreciate what was clearly a deliberately harsh lesson, and one that was closer to the real world than most lectures.

What the prof did is exactly how customers behave in the real world: when you agree on the specification of building an app, customers will specify functionality they want. I have yet to see a customer spell out they don't want the app to crash when they enter random inputs. Of course they don't want this!

This class really both home the concept of input validation/sanitization, and how a specification won't capture all parts of "it should just work."

#softwareengineering #lifelesson #qa

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Here are 6 of the more "practical" software architect archetypes I came up with - as a follow-up of the 6 more "theoretical" ones from yesterday:

1. The Coding Machine

An experienced software engineer who has their head down coding for much of the time. As a note, I took this archetype from Facebook, where it is an actual archetype for promotions/performance reviews, and Michael Novati was the engineer it was first created for. (Shared more details about this one in https://lnkd.in/e6xTiTFj)

2. The Integrator

An experienced engineer who understands most systems a company has, how they work, what they do, and can extend or modify them with ease.

3. The Approachable one

An experienced software engineer who’s unexpectedly approachable and available.

4. The Detailed Documenter

An engineer who prolifically generates documents, helping other engineers to understand architecture, common concepts and terms.

5. The New and Shiny Chaser

An engineer who loves to jump to using the “latest and greatest” frameworks or approaches they can find.

6.  The Old Schooler

The opposite of the New and Shiny Chaser, this is an experienced engineer who prefers the same toolset that’s worked for decades, thanks very much, even though colleagues see these tools or approaches as dated and belonging in a museum.

So which archetypes are "better?"

I *do* have a bias towards more practical archetypes, as I’ve repeatedly observed that engineers who stay close to the code tend to consistently make better decisions than engineers who are more distant from this core part of engineering. 

However, there is no “best” or “worst” approach when it comes to architecture. More theoretical archetypes can be very useful: for example, in projects with a major blast radius. Also, when there are hard-to-revert decisions to make, more theoretical archetypes can offer organized approaches to planning and conduct detailed planning, which is exactly what the situation needs.

Read about 6 more theoretical archetypes I collected: https://lnkd.in/eWyWxXBP

I hope you had as much fun reading these as well, as I had fun coming up with them!

Full subscribers can read more thoughts on these archetypes, and the benefits of different archetypes and personalities working together in The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/eawJRgpq

What other archetypes have you come across?

#softwareengineering #softwarearchitecture

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Here are 6 software architect archetypes I identified when thinking about typical software architect traits and behaviours:

As context, as I was writing a software architecture section for The Software Engineer's Guidebook (https://lnkd.in/gq2722ZF), I was thinking about what architect archetypes could look like, and started making a list, as a fun activity.

As a note, these archetypes or traits are just ways of describing behavior patterns. As we know, behaviors can change over time and also quickly.

Here are ones I identified on the more theoretical side:

1. The Ivory Tower Architect. 

A software engineer who’s distant from day-to-day work, with few interactions with the software engineers responsible for implementing their architecture ideas

2. The Painfully Precise. 

This type of engineer pays very close attention to the exact details of what others say, and simply cannot overlook any error, however small. 

3. The Theory Addict. 

This engineer is an avid reader of books, papers and case studies. They’ll suggest a pattern or approach they read about, and cite classic or trending books, articles and case studies to justify their decisions.

4. The Philosopher. 

Similar to the theory addict, the philosopher engineer can bring a lot of value to an architecture debate by rigorously examining an approach and poisiting alternatives, or opposing viewpoints.

5. The Superior Linguist. 

This engineer has memorized the technical language, knows all the jargon, and won’t miss an opportunity to deploy the Correct Architecture Language.

6. The Walk-Away Advisor. 

An experienced engineer who provides advice during the early phases of a project, but then walks away and isn’t involved in the implementation phase. 

(I hope you had as much fun reading these as I had fun coming up with them!)

I go into more  a bit more detail on each of these archetypes in the full issue of The Pragmatic Engineer today. Subscribers can read more here: https://lnkd.in/eawJRgpq

And tomorrow I'll share 6 more "practical" archetypes to balance out the theoretical side, and not pretend that software architects are hands-off (most I know are not!) 

#softwareengineering #softwarearchitecture

There is a risk of treating employees poorly - because you know that they cannot leave to a competitor, as no competitors are hiring, at least not locally.

The risk becomes real when a competitor with a reputation for treating staff better - or valuing them more - suddenly starts to hire locally.

The market in tech has not been nearly as hot as before, and so if there has ever been temptation to treat tech staff not as nice as before, this was it. But as the market cooled down quickly, it can also turn around quick enough.

text:
What are domain registrars that *software engineers* recommend? I asked people over a Twitter thread, and of the more than 250 responses, here's the split. Some surprises:

1. The largest domain registrar was not in the top responses

GoDaddy is the absolute leader in global domain registrations: about 11% of all domains, globally are registered here, and GoDaddy is close to 10x larger in this sense than the 4th largest domain registrar, Google Domains used to be (until Google announced shutting it down and selling all domains to Squarespace two weeks ago; I covered details here: https://lnkd.in/eEwm9JUm )

Month-on-month, GoDaddy is the most favored domain registrar across all domain registrations. But, surprisingly very few developers mentioned using - or recommending - GoDaddy. (In all fairness, even as a current customer I would hesitate to recommend it to fellow engineers, as it's a pain to remember how to get to DNS settings page across confusing navigation; the upsells are annoying, and I did not appreciate parking my newly purchased domain parked on a GoDaddy adverts page without asking me).

2. Cloudflare being the top choice.

Cloudflare manages 1M domains globally - compared to e.g. GoDaddy with 80M, Namecheap with 18M, or Google Domains - until the shutdown - 10M.

Based on the current market size, it's surprising to see a "relatively smaller" registrar get the most mentions. Reasons people said they prefer Cloudflare included at-price domains (selling at wholesale cost, not putting any markup on domains), free WHOIS, DNSSEC, 2FA, free email routing, and trust in the brand to not "rug pull" and raise prices later.

3. Porkbun.

I'll be honest: never heard about Porkbun until this survey. It's a goofy site with a piglet as their image. But there were *so* many positive comments from devs! Here is one:

“They are just very good and affordable 🐷Plus all settings at 1 place 👐 And bulk options!”

They are cheap, seemingly straightforward on why (they make their margin on custom domains and hosting) and the cheeky brand seems to be a hit.

4. Google Domains *will* be missed.

I had a sense that a lot of developers really liked Google Domains: the simple interface, transparent and simple pricing, solid security, and no upsells or price raises since it has launched. It's pretty clear to me a large "domain migration" will start as Google Domains will be shut down: likely sometime around the end of the year, although Google is yet to communicate the exact timeline.

I went into more details for all of these providers in last week's free issue of The Pragmatic Engineer. Read it here, together with pointers to other domain registrars: 

https://lnkd.in/eRfWRG-Z

#googledomains #domains #softwareengineering

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
There's a quiet revolution happening across most large tech companies and several smaller ones, and I hear little about: engineers no longer developing locally but using cloud developer environments.

If you’re a software engineer who moves from a startup to a larger tech company like Uber, Slack, Shopify, Meta and similar companies: you'd notice how the codebase you work with gets much larger: there’s more lines of code, more static analysis rules, and more unit, integration and other automated tests. 

And yet, when you start developing and make that first change, the build and the running of tests are surprisingly fast. Even more curious, your laptop’s CPU utilization doesn’t spike like usually happens when building a project; no fan whirs at high speed (for Intel-based laptops) because your laptop is heating up under the load.

This is because a growing number of engineers at these companies use cloud-base developer environments, so the build doesn’t happen on your own machine, but on a much more powerful and remote one. For example, in the case of Uber, some of these machines are 48-core, 96GB memory 'beasts' that can build the very large Android codebase several times faster than if you did it locally.

The idea behind cloud development environments (CDEs) is this:

In a "normal" development setup, the workflow is something along the lines of 
1. Set up the local development environment
2. Check out the codebase
3. Make changes
4. Build the code
5. Run tests locally, or deploy the code locally
6. Debug

All these steps happen on a local developer machine, traditionally. With CDEs, you move the CPU and memory-intensive parts of the development workflow, like building the code, running tests, and deploying, to a machine in the cloud which is more powerful and faster. And you plug this into your IDE, so it feels like you're developing locally: but it's faster!

Of course, developers can still do everything locally if they want to - but why would they, when local is a lot slower? For example, at Slack, about 90% of developers voluntarily used a remote development environment, only 6 months after its rollout (!)

I dived into more details on how various companies go about building their own, custom CDE implementations in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue. Full subscribers can read it here: https://lnkd.in/enEw96G7

We talk a lot about how AI will make development more productive. Well, cloud-based development already brings a lot of gains: to the companies that invested in these setups.

In a follow-up article, I'll look at the CDE vendor landscape, which is also buzzing with players like GitPod, GitHub Codespaces, Coder, DevZero, Jetbtains Spaces, CodeSandbox and many others.

#softwareengineering #trends #cloud

Discussing business growth plans *as a the basis* of architecture decisions to be made.

text:
Be careful what you wish for: because you might just get it. It started a year ago with an article on The Pragmatic Engineer about odd things happening at events tech company Pollen. 

The CEO of Pollen, Callum Negus-Fancey, back then commented on my article "It's not like [BBC] Panorama where things are done properly." Well, he got his wish: tonight, at 9pm, the BBC is airing a movie about Pollen on BBC 3. Watch it here: https://lnkd.in/ekT_V8G5

For a bit more context: the CEO made this comment on 22 June 2022, on an engineering town hall, in response to the question on what he thought of this The Pragmatic Engineer article, which article shared details on what looked like not well executed layoffs: https://lnkd.in/eJCzYZFJ . Referring to The Pragmatic Engineer, the CEO said to engineers at Pollen:

"I do find like there's a real lack of accountability, no checks, and balances with this kind of investigative journalism. You know, it's not like [BBC] Panorama and things that are done properly and where it's broadcast. It's a very small organization. They chose to take a very one-sided view."

I will be honest, this comment did bug me. Why talk down to smaller publications that are publishing correct details? Is there something the CEO is hiding?

As the company went bankrupt and employees were not paid: I had a sense something was off. So I investigated more and published the article "Inside Pollen’s Collapse: “$200M Raised” but Staff Unpaid - Exclusive" https://lnkd.in/eTBUKz9W

During this investigation I became aware of alarming details: but ones I could not publish just yet. It would be fate that this article gathered more inbound interest from larger publications, several who reached out to work together in making a movie about what really happened at Pollen. One of them was the BBC. For the past months, I've been working with the BBC cast, sharing additional, unpublished details from my months-long investigation into the company.

It's only fitting that Pollen's CEO is getting his wish in the end. It's no longer The Pragmatic Engineer covering his former company, but it's broadcast on the BBC. Where things - by his words! - are done properly. 

Although it won't be visible from the outside: this Pollen article - and then, working with the BBC - has been far more draining, time-consuming and stressful than any other articles I've written before. I have a newfound respect for investigative journalists and publications that take on to publish such stories that the public deserves to know details about: and stories that these companies - and their founders - desperately want to squash.

And, for the record, everything reported in The Pragmatic Engineer articles turned out to be factually correct.

#documentary #investigativejournalism

text:
Agoda is one of the companies which operates its own private cloud, and does not utilize any public cloud provider heavily. Here are some stats not shared before on what this means:

7.5M queries per second: the peak load the company sees, spread across its managed database-as-a-service (DBAAS.)

80,000/second: the number of searches per second at peak. Interesting enough, the majority of this is not people typing in searches: it's by partners, affiliates and search engines!

600,000: virtual cores (vCores) and 300,000 physical cores.

6,500: the number of servers operated

6,600: the number of people at Agoda

1,600: the size of the engineering team

~10-50: the size of the hardware team, managing those 6,500 servers

~30PB: the company's largest data cluster (petabytes: 1PB is 1,000 terabytes or 1M gigabytes). As context, 10 years ago, this data cluster was 300GB as a Hadoop cluster; that’s around a 100,000-fold increase in data stored!

4: the number of data centers Agoda uses. One in the US and Europe, and two in Asia.

1MW: the power consumed by the largest data center for Agoda's workload. As context, a moderately sized data center would consume 12-20MW, and a large data center (hyperscale ones) 20-50MW.

I got all these details after talking with Agoda's CTO Idan Zalzberg. In today's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue, I went into a lot more details on Agoda's hardware setup, and why the company is not moving to public cloud providers (or at least not just yet) and what other companies could take from their approach. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/eKxmAyRQ

text:
Stuff few people tell you: being good at debugging at large tech companies is a good part this:

Knowing where to find the right dashboards (and know how to use them + filter/search in them) which make debugging prod issues *so* much easier.

This is especially true for both Big Tech and at the tech companies where all of this is custom (both the dashboards; and the internal systems; and the variety of what dashboards/logs other systems use that relate to your team), and many people figure out the importance of all this months in, or later.

Also, getting access to the logging service (e.g. Sentry, Datadog, Splunk, Sumo Logic or which ever vendor your company uses, or the custom internal one) and knowing how to filter and search; what the data retention period is; and how to access logs outside the retention period is similarly powerful.

And then the thing that even fewer people will tell you:

For some edge cases, it's a superpower for an engineer to get access the data warehouse where the logs and events are recorded, and learn how to query the stuff that relates to your product.

At the very least get to know a data analyst/scientist who can help figure out this.

#softwarenginering #debugging

text:
I came across the most "real-world" side project so far: a ridesharing simulation from software engineer Juraj Majerik. But it's a lot more than most side projects:

1. Simulation

The app is a web simulation of riders requesting a car, and drivers picking them up. The app is built using Node.js, Go and PostgreSQL on the backend and React/TypeScript on the frontend. The infrastructure runs on DigitalOcean VMs.

2. Observability and monitoring

But it goes a lot further. Like any real-world production application: this app has monitoring built-in for the servers: tracking uptime, system load, the number of concurrent connections, system load, CPU usage based on processes, and memory usage. And to make all of this realistic: the app is running on the smallest possible DigitalOcean virtual machine, and thus this load increases visibly as more visitors arrive to the site. Prometheus and Grafana are used for monitoring and visualising this.

3. Documentation and source code

The app comes with a system diagram, and an overview of what each component does. It's open sourced, so the code can be inspected.

4. Documenting how it was built

One thing I have not seen before: Juraj documented all the major steps in building the app. This is a series of 34 mini-blog posts, written over 7 months, while he built the app from scratch.

I talked with Juraj on why he built the app, what he learned, and how he reflects on this project. Read all this in today's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/e-irpG4Z

Or check out the app, the monitoring, the systems design diagram and the blog series right here: https://lnkd.in/eRmQsg89

Also, Juraj Majerik happens to be on the market - at least for now. If you are looking to hire a truly full-stack software engineer, you might want to consider him. And if you're an engineer looking for inspiration for a side project: this is it.

Truly outstanding work with this project!

#softwarengineering #sideproject

text:
What is a pizza box server? It's what it sounds like: a server shaped like a pizza box! Travel giant Agoda moved over to use these servers 10 years ago.  Some data center history:

(As context, the below is from a deepdive I did with Agoda CTO Idan Zalzberg on the evolution of data centers at Agoda; one of the few large tech companies still running their infrastructure on-premise, with no immediate plans to change)

1. Blade servers

Until 2012, Agoda used IBM and HP blade servers of two blade chassis per rack, with each of these “clusters” contained in a single rack. All servers ran Windows and used IIS (Internet Information Services) as the web server. 

2. Pizza boxes

In 2013, the company moved to “pizza box servers,” after experiencing too many issues with the chassis setup.

3. Non-Windows stack

In 2013, Agoda started to introduce non-Microsoft systems like Couchbase for caching, Cassandra as a NoSQL data store, and Elasticsearch for search and analytics.

4. OpenStack

Then in 2014, the company began rolling out OpenStack-based virtual machines. The OpenStack setup initially went live with 40 nodes, and grew from there. OpenStack is an open-source platform for deploying and managing large networks of virtual machines to provide Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS.)

5. Containerization & Kubernetes

In 2018-2019, containerized applications started to become common. Agoda used containers mostly as a packaging mechanism to solve deployment issues.
2020-2021 was when Kubernetes became the orchestration engine for containers. These were deployed on virtual machines in production and Agoda started testing the water with Kubernetes in test environments.

6. Fleet

In 2022-2023 an in-house orchestrator development called Fleet was created and Agoda rebuilt its deployment system for federated Kubernetes clusters.

For much more details on how Agoda built its data centers; the hardware it runs, and for a tour from the inside, subscribers can read more in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/etiJKRGs

#agoda #datacenter #pizzabox #softwareengineering

text:
What are ways to improve a Glassdoor score for a company? Encouraging employees to write reviews is one way. But some companies follow other approaches:

I got a message from a software engineer working at a company which laid off 30% of staff in December 2022. It’s a late-stage startup valued at around $3B which had around 1,000 employees before the layoffs. The engineer wrote:

“My company is removing Glassdoor reviews because their rating has gotten so low. The company’s score went to 2.3 and they started doing this. I don’t think my company is alone in this practice to protect themselves from bad press, but lots of my colleagues have had their reviews deleted. Effectively, we’ve been silenced.”

This message made me curious if we are only talking about one company, or a trend of companies removing unfavorable reviews, and perhaps doing other things to improve their Glassdoor ratings.

Well, turns out, that this is a trend, and it's an open secret on how to improve your company's Glassdoor reviews.

The details won't fit in a LinkedIn post, but I go through a detailed case study with cybersecurity company Trustwave - which company's Glassdoor review page has reached an all-time high Glassdoor rating: although it's less flattering that this is done with duplicate and generic 5-star reviews displayed below:

text:
We should talk about one of the longest-running and likely most profitable dark pricing patterns employed by Adobe. I also fell for it, completely. Question: how much does this Creative Cloud subscription cost? This one:

If you are like me, you probably think that it costs $54.99 per month, which is pricey, but worth it. So you probably - like me - get your credit card out, sign up for the 7-day trial on the monthly plan ($54.99/month!), and use the product for a while.

Then, one day, you decide to cancel this monthly plan. Upon canceling, you are told that you have to pay something in the range of ~$300 to cancel, as an early termination fee! What happened?

Well, in my case, I got tricked into Adobe's "Annual plan, paid monthly" package! This is a series of masterful deceitful pricing pages, which lead on customers like me to believe they are signing up for a monthly plan they can cancel anytime. But they are not! The details are hidden in the fine print, which are very, very, very hard to find, unless you're the "read the 12-page T&C" type of person.

This dark pattern is not new at Adobe: they've been running it for years. Here's a thread from more than year ago describing exactly how it works: and nothing has changed, even today: https://lnkd.in/dF9_7fPQ

This pricing dark pattern is still live, today, despite all the complaints from customers. The screenshot I took is from their website, today, and that $54.99/month is *not* the $54.99/month that people like me would assume it is.

I learned my lesson, the expensive way: never buy anything from Adobe while it employs the same deceit. I also do not and will not recommend anything from a company that tricks customers this way. It was thanks to this dark pattern I was forced to switch and find alternatives to Adobe products, and found the Affinity Design suite (https://lnkd.in/d5APTGNs) which is just as capable as Adobe software, but has clear, one-off, upfront pricing. I'm a happy customer since, and use Affinity both for their PDF editing software, as well as an alternative to Photoshop. I also use Canva  and Excalidraw for online editing, both which are monthly subscriptions, where “monthly” means what I expect it means. (The usual disclaimer: none of these are promotions, I'm not paid by any of these companies to mention them, as is the case with all my posts. It's the other way round: I pay for them, just like I used to pay for Adobe. My ethics policy: https://lnkd.in/eG4G6svW )

Adobe: congratulations on making a one-off profit on me that one time, and also on losing me for life. Sad that the company degraded to employing these such patterns, not caring about the long-term impact to the brand, and increasingly alienating its own paying, and formerly loyal customers.

#adobe #darkpatterns

text:
When I talked with Steve Yegge, he told me about this company he worked at that had the *BEST* developer tooling, to date. Guess what year this was in? It was 1992!

The company was called GeoWorks, and it was a company building the GEOS 16-bit operating system. This system was built in 8086 Assembly language, and the company was writing and maintaining millions of lines of Assembly code. Everything was Assembly: the operating system, the apps - you name it.

And, to make development more productive, the company built its own debugger that Steve said is the best debugger he's used, to date, with some more powerful features than the debuggers of today.

It's interesting how, 30 years later, Steve is back to building better developer productivity tools, and perhaps to surpass the tools at GeoWorks.

This was just a small bit from my interview with Steve. Read the full piece in this free issue of The Pragmatic Engineer here: https://lnkd.in/egtnAkbj

#developerexperience #debugging #softwareengineering

Why would I go ahead to publish a postmortem on behalf of the company? Well, ~2 weeks ago, their CEO said, on earnings:

text:
"Why are there no real alternatives to PagerDuty?" someone asked me. But there are!! Except, barely anyone knows of them, and they are hard to find even when searching. Let's change this:

I collected a list of 20 alternatives for incident management and alerting platforms. Here they are:

1. Zenduty
2. incident.io (disclaimer: I am an investor)
3. Jeli.io
4. Blameless
5. FireHydrant
6. Rootly
7. Spike.sh
8. Moogsoft
9. ilert
10. Squadcast
11. Transposit
12. Datadog incident management
13. Grafana Labs OnCall
14. Splunk on-call (formerly: VictorOps)
15. AWS Systems Manager Incident Manager (yes, this is really the name! But it works.)
16. OnPage®
17. AlertOps
18. xMatters, inc
19. Better Uptime
19. OpsGenie from Atlassian

Read more details about how each of these describe themselves and get a link to each on this list: https://lnkd.in/ert583VJ

And why even consider alternatives? Is there anything "wrong" with PagerDuty?

Well, PagerDuty is excellent at alerting. It is not as sophisticated at incident management and follow-up though, than many of the alternatives are.

Also: competition, pricing, features. PagerDuty is the market-leader in this area. Several of the alternatives listed offer different features, different SLAs, different pricing. Pricing is always a key point with PagerDuty, given the $47/user/month, paid monthly plan that offers SSO being on the higher end in the space (of course, large enterprises tend to negotiate custom deals). 

PagerDuty is the incumbent with a bit less need to innovate: and these alternatives tend to be the ones innovating, and aiming to out-execute what PagerDuty can offer, at least in a few dimensions.

The incident management and on-call space is buzzing, and it's wonderful to see all the innovation and energy here. I hope this list helps if you are considering improving oncall and incident management at your team or company!

What other alternatives does your team use, which should be on this list, but is not?

---

text:
In 2022, an unnamed crypto company spent $65M on Datadog, and churned in this spending for next year. But who was this? I tracked down that it was Coinbase, and here are details:

Datadog is a leading observability tooling provider which went public in 2019, with a current market cap of $28B. The company made $1.67B revenue in 2022, circa $140M per month. On an earnings call a week ago, on 4 May, the CFO mentioned a “large upfront bill that did not recur,” saying:

“Billings were $511 million, up 15% year-over-year. We had a large upfront bill for a client in Q1 2022 that did not recur at the same level or timing in Q1 2023. Pro forma for this client, billings growth was in the low 30s percent year-over-year.”

If you’re like me, you’d probably skim over this detail, as it’s 15% here, 30% there. However, analysts attend these calls whose bread and butter is crunching the numbers and figuring out what a company might be trying to hide. A JP Morgan stock analyst did just this, quickly crunching numbers and asking the question:

“David, looking at the math on this large upfront bill that did not recur, it seems to be about $65 million, if I'm running that correctly. Can you possibly shed a little more light?“

Datadog’s CFO, David Obstler gave more details:

“That was a crypto company which continues to be a customer of ours. But that was an early optimizer. We had always talked about some of the industries that were most affected and optimized.“

So, who is this mysterious crypto company? Investor Turner Novak speculated that it’s Coinbase: 

He added he doesn’t know for certain that it is Coinbase, as other crypto companies have also raised silly amounts of money in the past several years.

So, did Coinbase spend $65M on Datadog in 2023? Online there’s no shortage of theories, or people pretending to be Coinbase employees. I wanted to find the truth, so I tracked down software engineers at the company. And I got my answer:

Yes. Coinbase spent $65M with Datadog in 2021, and this was their due bill for that year. I can confirm this having talked with Coinbase insiders. 

Here’s what actually happened, as I understand from talking with engineers at Coinbase, as covered in today's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/eAfDpyKp

#softwareengineering #optimisation

text:
A list of CEOs at tech companies who were paid less than 10 times the median employee's total compensation in 2022, and the median total compensation figures at their respective companies.

In 2015, the US Securities and Exchange Commission issued a rule which mandates publicly traded US companies to disclose their compensation details:

1. The median annual total compensation of all employees, except the CEO;

2. The annual total compensation of its CEO; 

3. The ratio of these two amounts.

I went through public filings of 100 public tech companies in the past few weeks to collect these details, and this is where the data is from.

Looking at the numbers: given how highly paid CEOs generally are - frequently making more than 100 times the median employee wage - how is this possible that these CEOs take a lower than usual compensation?

In all cases, the CEOs are either founders, or possess significant equity from previous allocations. This means that by doing their job well and increasing the value of the company, their equity also appreciates.

However, they may also be leading by example and exercising restraint in not paying themselves too handsomely, compared to some founder CEOs who accept a market rate compensation package worth tens of millions of dollars – as proposed by the company’s board – on top of their significant equity share.

And it is not a given that a CEO who already owns a large part of their company does not pay themselves a market rate salary. For example, in 2022, the cofounder (and then still) CEO of Netflix, or the founder and CEO of Twilio both took compensation that put them in the list of the 10 highest compensated tech CEOs in 2022.

To me, a CEO not opting for a high package - on top of a healthy equity stake - signals that they are in it for the long game, and believe in their company. Coupled with compensating employees well (as reflected in the median compensation packages): this can signal a more employee-centric workplace than usual, and a leader who has ongoing conviction that their business will keep growing.

For more context on median compensation, and the list of the 35 tech companies that compensate the most on the median, see yesterday's free The Pragmatic Engineer article: https://lnkd.in/eE5bXm4z

For more details on CEO pay ranges, and details on CEOs paid the most, or the most, with respect to their company's market cap, see the subscriber-only The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/edft3zBZ

#tech #compensation

Which companies pay the most, on average? I crunched numbers from 100 publicly traded tech companies and have the answer for 2022. It was not Netflix, not Meta, not Google, but:

Okta.

The median compensation package last year was $397,707. This means that about 50% of the company likely made more than this, in total compensation.

How is this possible, when the previous year this number was much lower - although also pretty high, at $258,000?

text:
How can you thrive as a software engineer who is working on a platform team / infra team? Here are my thoughts on this topic:

Platform teams are the building blocks that product teams use to ship business-facing functionality. Products are built on top of platforms, which platforms enable product teams to move faster. Platform teams are key in scaling up high-growth engineering organizations. 

But because platform teams work different to product teams - most notably: their customers are internal, and their impact is usually less directly connected to business metrics - it is a different type of environment to work in.

So what are ways to thrive here? Here are my thoughts below.

One important addition: if you lead a platform team, aim to have some of the goals or success criterias of the team be connected with metrics and outcomes the business cares about. Your team should be playing a key role in the company: so spell it out by making it clear how the team is supporting things like revenue, or customer acquisition: even if indirectly, by being the building block for product teams that generate that specific value.

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
A reminder on how you need to do your research for new positions, and not take statements at face value comes from BeReal:

7 months ago, in September 2022, a recruiter reached out to expolore a VP of Engineering position at BeReal. The social media app was topping all charts, just hit 15M DAU and seemed like it would only keep growing.

“50M DAU will be reached in 6 months” the recruiter wrote. “There is a lot to build.”

Now we know that BeReal peaked in October 2022, and it’s been downhill from there. As per The Verge, using Apptopia data, they estimate the app likely has 6M DAU and it’s usage shrinking: https://lnkd.in/e5BBQUUB BeReal responded to this article claiming they have 20M DAUs, which, in turn, suggest no growth the last 7 months: https://lnkd.in/eBbsG7wt . Either way, this number is nowhere near the prediction the recruiter presented me.

I’ve seen people blame recruiters after they joined scaleups where recruiters “promised” the company to get to a given valuation, to pay out certain amount of bonuses, or for the stock price to hit a certain value.

In all cases, these are hopeful statements. Do your own research and draw your conclusions. And don’t place all the blame recruiters or hiring managers: of course they hope for a great outcome for a company, and it’s why they are working for the organisation. But it is you who makes the decision, and takes a risk. No one can predict the future, and be very skeptical if someone makes it seem like they can.

Startups are high-risk, high-reward: even the “hottest” ones. There is a world where BeReal kept growing and hit those 50M DAUs: but it’s not this one. 

#career #engineeringmanagement #bereal

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The definition of what "region" in the cloud is different at AWS, and at Google Cloud and Azure. Why is this important? Well, one incident in one data center took a whole GCP *region* offline!

When I researched differences between cloud providers, something stood out: AWS treats their region definition much, much more strict than other cloud providers do.

At AWS, a region consist of 3 or more zones (one zone can be 1 or more data centers). Zones are at least 30 miles away from each other and usually no more than 60 miles, to mitigate against local disasters like earthquakes, floods, extreme weather etc. This means that if a data center has issues, or even if a whole zone goes offline, the region continues to operate. So customers who operate in two or more zones within a region can keep operating in this region.

On the other hand, Google Cloud - as well as Azure - is a lot more vague on what a region is. Sure, they both say how a region consists of multiple availability zones: but neither is clear on how "separate" a zone is. Neither guarantee geographic separation, or even building separation. Both make it clear that zones *are* independent of one another for e.g. power supply, networking etc. But no more specifics than this are given.

Normally, the difference is subtle, as zones have some level of separation. Except when a local disaster strikes!

Google Cloud has water intrusion in one of their data centers, impacting one zone, in eu-west-9, which is in Paris. What this would normally mean is one zone is impacted.

However, in this case, the whole region - with several zones - went offline.

How could this happen?

Is the whole region in one building, without geographic separation and so could have "water intrusion" hit the whole building? Or was it a critical service operating in the zone impacted, took down the whole region? If so, why were zones inter-connected so tightly?

We don’t have answers to either of these questions just yet.

One thing is certain: "region" means something different in AWS than in other cloud providers. Incidentally, both Google Cloud and Azure claim to have more regions and zones than AWS, globally. But the comparison is not entirely apples to apples, once you realize how some cloud providers locate a region in one location (or building), while others spread them out, to allow for much more reilliency for disasters that can happen in a data center.

Google will have to provide a detailed postmortem to restore trust, and explain how a problem in one data center can take a whole region down, and what improvements they will do to avoid this in the future. More details on the GCP outage: https://lnkd.in/emyBJke2

If you're a decision maker on choosing cloud providers, pay attention to the details on how they define a "region" or a "zone!"

---

text:
A curious thing I noticed: it's so rare for an engineering manager or Staff+ engineer to have a *direct* chat with a business stakeholder. Most rely on PMs to do this, and do the "translation."

And yet, talking with the business directly is such a high-leverage activity for engineering.

I have yet to see pushback from the business: almost always it's the opposite, as they usually *want* to get more attention from engineering, and not less.

And I'm not saying that as an engineer/manager you want to start doing this all the time - this is a good part of the PM responsibility, for good reason.

But build that connection for the key business folks you build for, or with. It's such a useful thing to do.

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
How poorly executed migrations have customers cancel: my story. This month I stopped being both a MediaTemple and a Mailjet by Sinch customer after their migrations broke my stuff.

For MediaTemple: I had been a MediaTemple customer for 15 years (!), using their shared hosting service. GoDaddy bought MediaTemple 10 years ago, but nothing changed: things kept working as before. Hooray! This month, they migrated my MediaTemple hosting to GoDaddy hosting, and broke the last site I was operating on MediaTemple. I was sent an email on how I need to log on to an SSH host and then fix it myself.

The truth was, I was planning to move off of MediaTemple for ~8 years. But it always worked, and why mess with something that operates well enough? So I ended up paying ~$300/year to keep this one site alive that had less than 100 visitors per month. The migration breaking it sealed the deal: I migrated my site off of MediaTemple (now: GoDaddy hosting).

To the credit of MediaTemple and GoDaddy: I asked for a partial refund as I was paying annually, but they broke my site. The company acknlowledged this and did the refund.

As for Mailjet: I have been a customer for ~3 years, integrating a widget to The Software Engineer's Guidebook landing page. I originally contemplated building the widget myself, but figured going with an email provider will be more useful.

Yesterday, this widget broke.

I reached out to Mailjet, and turns out that they did a migration where they deprecated all old forms, and also deleted all existing form definitions and these "unfortunately cannot be restored."

Mailjet claimed they sent out emails warning customers they need to migrate: I somehow missed these. Migration meant using a brand new form builder they put in place, and creating new workflows, then testing those new workflows work: only to create something similar to how the existing signup forms worked. In the end, my functionality was broken, and with a sour taste in my mouth I asked for a partial refund, as I am an annual subscriber. Mailjet declined, writing:

"In regards to your refund inquiry, I am afraid that we cannot accept it. As mentioned above, we gave enough time to all of our customers to take the necessary actions so their processes are not interrupted."

My suspicion is that both in the case of MediaTemple, and at Mailjet, the engineering team prioritized their convenience over customers and did not care too much about customers they broke churning. This can make sense, especially when it's time-consuming to build migration tools, or the goal *is* for smaller customers like myself to churn.

Still, both of these examples are good reminders that migrations that break customers - and when customers need to do work to "unbreak" themselves are likely to lead to customers leaving, having a bad taste in their mouth, or looking to leave later.

My view is both these migrations were done poorly. Here's advice on doing them better: https://lnkd.in/gXSYPry7

text:
Figma captured ~90% of the UI design tooling market in a few years, so they must do something right. I sat down with CTO Kris Rasmussen to learn more about how Figma works, from an engineering point of view.

A few things that caught my attention:

- Intentional growth. Figma has also limited its pace of growth to not more than 100% increase in headcount, year-on-year, and they have not exceeded this, even during their fastest growth periods.

- Figma has no "design docs" culture across the company.

- Milestones for projects. Although not many things are mandated at the company, all projects needing to have milestones are one such thing.

- Quality weeks. The engineering team regularly has these, to pay down quality debt, which can range from bugs, to limitations of features at launch.

And one more thing: engineering teams prioritise fixing every "fresh" bug that they recently introduced, even if those are low impact ones, and would normally not be considered ones to fix.

Full subscribers can read the full issue in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/eVMCGp39

#softwareengineering #engineeringmanagement #figma

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Lyft raised a total of $7.2B in capital yet is valued at half of this today. The company raised $4.9B during venture funding rounds and $2.3B during its IPO, and, still, the company is worth $3.3B right now. But this is not the biggest challenge for the company.

On Tuesday, the company appointed a new CEO: David Risher, who was Amazon’s SVP for US Retail between 1997-2002 is taking over the chief executive position. 

The cofounders abruptly stepping down - and away from day-to-day management - and bringing in a former Amazon executive to attempt to turn the ship around, can be seen as a sensible business decision. It could also be an act of surrender by them and throwing in the towel, unable or unwilling to make the tough changes that are surely ahead for the company.

Could we be seeing a reckoning for VC-funded companies that struggle to build a profitable business? 

I think this is what is happening at Lyft, and similar other cases will surely play out. It was only in 2020 that Lyft was paying top of the market entry-level software engineers than any other company in the US. But with the company being in a financially difficult situation, it’s valid to ask: does Lyft have the business model to compete with Big Tech compensation packages, and also turn a profit?

I cover more of my thoughts on the situation in this week's free The Pragmatic Engineer article: https://lnkd.in/e7Y67W7E

text:
Is there a drop in software engineering job openings, globally?

There’s plenty of news and anecdotal evidence suggesting the jobs market for software engineers is cooling. But what does the data say?

I dug into job stats from Hacker News and Indeed, and here's something interesting I found, coming from Indeed.

The Indeed Job Postings Index is a data source made public thanks to Indeed’s Hiring Lab. The data is a “Job Postings Index” segmented by country and category. 100 is the reference data point, representing the number of job postings on 1 February 2022. I’ve visualized the data for “Software Developer” job listings on Indeed, and here’s the three “hottest” markets - see the image below:

The “peak” of the market was between mid-2021 and mid-2022 in most markets. It’s interesting that Australia was the “hottest” market of all, as benchmarked by Indeed. Also interesting to see is that while the number of software developer job postings this month in the US and Canada is about equal to February 2020. In Australia, it’s almost double.

Not all markets were as “hot” as the US, Canada and Australia. The next two “hottest” markets were Germany and the UK - where "peaks" were smaller. In France, the market seemed flat, and in Japan, it - unexplainably - increased significantly.

What can we say based on all of these data points? 

The second half of 2021 saw a spike in software engineer job listings - and it coincided with my article on A Perfect Storm Causing an Insane Hiring Market (an un-paywalled article: https://lnkd.in/dxeVn8sN). Job listings are today roughly at the level they were in February 2020 in the US, Canada and UK, and for the Hacker News startup and scaleup job opportunities.

Read the full, free The Pragmatic Engineer article here with more data points and further conclusions: https://lnkd.in/d47XE_Ga

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Wow. Newsletter platform Substack ran an experiment to see if prices ending with $X.99 would see a higher conversion. The result? Significantly fewer people bought e.g. the $9.99 subscription vs the $10 one!

This was a surprising outcome, given it's conventional wisdom that anything ending with .99 sells better. It's why you see this pricing on so many things: in the shop, and online.

However, it's also a good reminder that "conventional wisdom" will not mean it's universally applicable. Clearly, the people who buy newsletter subscriptions have developed some suspicion for this kind of pricing - or, perhaps, they have been used to seeing round prices to the point of a .99 price looking cheap or suspicious. Or it could be that Substack's customers are well aware of the .99 pricing "trick" and are saying "no thank you" when seeing it.

This is a good reminder that it is helpful to validate assumptions, especially when you have millions of users, where "conventional wisdom" might not apply.

As a disclaimer, The Pragmatic Engineer is a Substack customer - though it's not Substack paying me, but I'm paying 10% of my earnings every month to the platform. A reason I do this is that Substack re-invests this fee into the platform with improvements that they then validate - and sometimes throw away, like with this pricing experiment. (The other reason is that every month, Substack generates more than 10% of my paid subscriptions through their discovery and platform features, making this an easy business decision.)

It's interesting to think about what other "conventional" strategies might not apply to groups like software engineers, Gen Z, or niche groups online.

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
One thing that surprises so many companies and vendors: how they cannot pay me to mention them in any form. I made a conscious decision in saying no to "easy money."

Here's an email exchange from today where a vendor kept insisting they want to pay me to mention their product somehow, and it took me three responses to make it clear there is no such option.

The reality is that anyone with a "following" or "audience" in tech will be approached by companies to promote their offering simply because this is a cost-efficient advertising channel. So why don't I do any of this? Would it not help those brands and vendors get their offering out?

I don't do ads for 3 reasons:

1. When relying on advertising, you are incentivized to maximise your reach.

If I made advertising and sponsorship a part of my revenue channels, my incentive would be to increase my reach - with e.g. views, followers etc - and charge advertisers more, in return. This would be an incentive to start to share more "populist" things that are inherently more shallow.

We've all seen what I'm talking about. Contrversial talkes to, generic wisdom, lists with X things, juming on the bandwagon for popular memes: all of these are good for business - if your business depends on reach, that is!

2. It's becoming hard to trust when an "influencer" recommends something.

I observe more people with large reach start to recommend things: often not disclosing they are paid to do so. If I was to sometimes recommend things as paid, I can see how it would eventually become confusing: was I paid to mention something?

Right now: it's very clear. I am not paid for any mentions, and if I have any affiliation (eg investor) I always disclose it.

3. Because I have the luxury to not need to rely on ads.

I'm grateful that I have a business that is subscriber supported: selling newsletter subscriptions and books accounts for the vast majority of my income, and - at least for the time being - it allows me to say no to any form of sponsorships.

For any and all sponsorship requests, my response is this "Sponsorship page" where I make it clear why I don't do this, and newsletters/websites I can recommend: https://lnkd.in/ePhTaK3B

And my ethics policy on how I stay independent and minimize - and disclose - biases: https://lnkd.in/eG4G6svW

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
About half of US and UK VC-funded startups banked with Silicon Valley Bank. Here's how deposits at the bank grew for 32 years. The bank collapsed on Friday. What happened and what does this mean for tech?

Here's the timeline of how things played out:

Thursday, 9 March, Around 9am in the morning (PST / California time), founders were tipped off by venture capitalists that SVB looked in trouble. The investors suggested companies immediately withdraw their funds for safekeeping, while still possible.

11am: Bloomberg reported that Founders Fund, a VC fund co-founded by Peter Thiel, advised all its portfolio companies “to pull money from Silicon Valley Bank.” This news is widely shared on social media and in private messages.

11:30am: SVB CEO, Gregory Becker, holds a 10-minute conference call with top VCs, saying the bank has “ample liquidity to support our clients with one exception: If everyone is telling each other SVB is in trouble, that would be a challenge.”

12am: Details of the call leak, and focus shifts onto the CEO’s quote: “If everyone is telling each other SVB is in trouble, that would be a challenge.” On social media, founders share that they are observing a bank run to be unfolding. This news naturally fuels the run.

From 12am: VCs tell portfolio companies to pull money they have in Silicon Valley Bank. The bank’s systems start to be overloaded to the point of customers not being able to log on and transfer.

We were witnessing a full-on bank run unfold in a matter of hours - the fastest in history - bank customers transferring out all their holdings.

In the aftermath, we would learn that Silicon Valley Bank processed $42B in deposit outflows on Thursday: which is nearly a quarter of all deposits they held!

On Friday morning, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC,) announced it was closing the bank. The FDIC is a government agency whose goal is to maintain stability and public confidence in the US financial system.

On Sunday afternoon, at around 5pm PST, the US Department of Treasury, the Federal Reserve and FDIC issued a joint statement, stating all depositors were fully protected, and Silicon Valley Bank customers will have access to all of their money starting Monday.

The statement went on to state that no losses associated with the sale of SVB assets would be borne by taxpayers. Only shareholders and certain unsecured debt holders were not protected from this.

So what does this collapse mean for the tech industry, given that depositors are made whole? Quite a bit:

1. VC-funded startups will rethink their banking approach
2. Startups building up buffers are likely to proceed with more cost-cutting
3. More startups are likely to start fundraising earlier.
4. Distrust in VCs amongst founders and employees could grow.

Read more on the events that led to SVB going down in this week's free issue of The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/e2kpZPQV 

#svbcollapse #startups #tech

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Where are Uber's data centers located, how many does the company have, and what's the story behind building their own data centers?

Uber was founded in 2010 and started to grow quickly around 2013. In 2014, the company not only did a Program/Platform structure change (https://lnkd.in/duxvwJeq), but it was the year when Uber began on the path of building its own data center. Talking with a tenured engineer, it was then that Uber’s first data center in San Jose – called SJC, internally – was built and teams migrated services over to it.

Following SJC, a second data center was built near Washington DC, known as DCA. For each data center, additional zones - isolated areas - were added, which were named by a number at the end of the data center’s acronym; SJC1 was Zone 1 within SJC, DCA1 was Zone 1 within DCA, etc.

Back then, building a private data center made a lot of sense for Uber. Cloud computing services were not nearly as sophisticated as today, and Uber was growing rapidly and raising funding equally fast. The company was also expanding into new verticals like UberEats, UberRush, UberAir and more. Owning and operating its own data centers was as much a strategic choice as that which Google or Facebook made for their businesses. Critically, Uber had the capital to invest in the upfront costs of its new data centers.

Around 2019, Uber started to build a third data center in Phoenix, Arizona, called PHX. The reason for building this data center? SJC was too small for Uber’s requirements, could not be extended and also had reliability issues. Infra leadership decided the best approach was to develop a new data center and to decommission SJC when it went online.

I covered a lot more details on the history of Uber's data centers, challenges in operating them - e.g. OEM vs ODC hardware - and reasons Uber's engineering team felt a push to consider moving - at least partially - to the Cloud.

Read it in this week's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/eQkEt3nv

text:
Keki was one of my favorite recruiters to work with at Uber. After working at Flow Traders, Disney+, Reddit, Uber, she is starting her  career consultation business. I met up with her on the launch day of her company.

When I became an engineering manager at Uber in 2016, Keki was the 'sourcer' I worked with: the recruiter who reviewed all inbound resumes for positions I hired for. So when I'd post a position, for e.g. a Software Engineer, Senior Software Engineer, and get hundreds of applications and resumes: it would be Keki who would go through 98% of these - the 2% being ones she'd share with me, or ones that ended up on my desk via warm referrals.

The recruitment processes at large tech companies like Uber can be confusing. What does an inbound sourcer do, and why? How is a sourcer different to an inbound sourcer? What about the recruiter - what do they do? Why is the hiring manager not doing resume screening? Why do sourcers care about different things than hiring managers? Why are recruiters on 'the candidate's side' after they passed on resume screening and how can you, as a candidate, take advantage of this?

Keki worked her way through all these roles the past decade: doing inbound sourcing, sourcing, recruitment, and all at companies which have been notoriously to get hired at.

Until now, the only way to get feedback, tips, advice and 'the real deal' on the recruitment funnel from Keki was if you applied to the company she was recruiting for. And I've observed Keki being amazing at helping those candidates succeed: because as an in-house recruiter, this is also part of the job! And Keki was great at this job.

Until now, I had no person to recommend for e.g. career conversations, tech resume reviews, but now, with Keki going full-time on her business, I do:

Hit up Keki at Expat Career Development. https://lnkd.in/e8Hgqsdy

Finally, a photo from us this morning, on day one of Expat Career Development, as I shared some of my learnings from the release of The Tech Resume Inside Out book (https://thetechresume.com/) which Keki also helped write! - and as Keki shared her excitement to help guide people to build CVs that do justice for them, and prepare them for the often confusing big tech interview processes.

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Something very unusual is happening at a Big Tech that announced return to office: employees are not having any of it, and are pushing back to reverse this decision. This company is Amazon. What happened?

I talked with current Amazon software engineers and managers, and here are the details I got:

One week ago, on Friday, a post appeared on Amazon's intranet site, which announced Amazon requiring all employees to spend 3 days/week in the office from 1 May.

The reaction was unlike I've seen at most Big Tech announcing the same - e.g. Apple, Google. At Amazon, employees seemed far more upset.

Amazon did a few things different to Google and Apple:

1. A "sneaky" announcement.

While Amazon had a town hall the same week, there was no mention of the policy change. And when it was announced, it was not done with an email sent out to people: just an intranet post.

2. The quiet understanding that remote was here to stay, indefinite.

Many accepted jobs at Amazon in 2021-22 on the understanding their role would be mostly remote, indefinitely, regardless of what the contract said. Many people did not live within commuting distance of offices, when they signed.

3. The sudden realization that "virtual" teams had different types of contracts.

There are teams in which everyone was hired on the premise they’d be fully remote: but only some employees have a “virtual” contract, while others are discovering they have an office assigned.

4. Until now, the autonomy was at directors to decide how their orgs work.

From 2021, directors can decide if they want their teams to work remote, hybrid, or in the office. Most engineering directors put remote in place and it was assumed by most staff that this setup of director-level decisions on remote policy would stay indefinitely. However, in Amazon India, some directors already mandated a return to office from the end of last year.

Amazon employees have started a petition, which I've seen, and had more than 10,400 signatures yesterday. They are asking for Amazon to reinstate the policy empowering teams to adopt that the work style works for them.

There's an internal Slack channel with more than 20,000 members, where people are also protesting.

Amazon's executive team - the S-Team - has been unusually silent so far. Given the pushback that is unlike at any major tech company, it's an open question in my view if Amazon will stick to this sudden policy change, or meet employees somewhere in the middle.

The irony in all of this is in the summer of 2021, when Amazon announced how directors have autonomy to define how their teams work, they also announced a new leadership principle: Strive to be Earth’s Best Employer.

I covered a bit more details on what I heard in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue sent out yesterday: https://lnkd.in/e38jxnGT

---
