text:
Tech companies are aggressively cutting back on vendor spending, I've confirmed, after researching this topic for a few weeks. Here are the areas most companies are optimizing:

As background, I spend the last few weeks talking with about two dozen founders, software engineers, engineering managers, and running survey with more than 60 participants about how they are reducing (or not reducing) vendor spend.

90% of people participating in the survey said their team or their company has current goals to reduce vendor spend. I collected the specific details on what vendors they are targeting to reduce usage - or move off - and categorized these answers. Here is the most frequently targeted ones.

What should not be surprising is how Cloud - IaaS, PaaS - is on the top of the list. After all, for many tech companies, Cloud spend is the second largest spend after people.

What was surprising to me is how observability and logging comes as the second biggest group, by a margin. The leading vendor in this area was mentioned countless times, as engineers told me the optimizations they are doing to reduce cost, and alternatives they are looking for to tame their pretty significant bill.

Notable mention goes to digramming tools being targeted surprisingly frequently in cost reductions in my sample size, compared to e.g. developer tools or chat solutions.

For a lot more details, and for access to the raw data, I went deep in this topic in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue. Full subscribers can read it here: https://lnkd.in/eyQvStKv

That issue contains actionable advice on how to go about reducing vendor spend, as well as observations on what vendors could do in this coming period, which will surely be a challenging one for many of them.

#softwareengineering #startups #bigtech

text:
"What if we took 2-3 months to rewrite the whole thing? Start from scratch, fix all the mistakes we know about, and we will have a clean state, all our problems solved."

There's a divide between engineers who thought about doing this and those who did it. The divide is this:

Those who had the opportunity to do what looked like a straightforward rewrite usually stumbled on the brutal reality on how rewrites introduce a host of issues you never thought of - while solving some of the problems you wanted to solve.

This kind of hard-earned learning is hard to appreciate unless you experience it yourself. Some things, you can "learn" by reading books, but this type of learning feels to me is not as sticky unless it comes through having done it.

text:
“Today we’re going back, way back. Back to the beginning of React. 10 years in the rear view mirror, it may feel like React was always going to succeed. That a framework by Facebook was too big to fail."

This is how React.js, the documentary starts, narrated by Chantastic, the former host of the React Podcast. Chantastic goes on:

"But this isn’t that kind of story. In fact, there were many times in React’s history when it seemed like it wasn’t going to succeed at all. 

So, what happened? How did React go in 10 years from looking dead on arrival, to becoming the dominant front-end JavaScript framework that it is today? 

It starts at Facebook, in 2011. JQuery and Backbone are dominating front-end JavaScript. But more opinionated tools like Ember, Angular and Knockout are already on the scene.

In the outside world, “Gangnam Style” has just been uploaded to YouTube, “Call Me Maybe” is topping the charts, and the freshest meme is “Overly Attached Girlfriend.” In internet years, it’s basically been an eternity.

So, strap in, enable that do-not-disturb, because this is quite the underdog story.”

The start of the movie got me hooked, and I watched the story of how React went form a project that most people could assume would not work, to the most popular frontend framework of today.

After watching the movie, I sat down with the creator of the movie, Ida Lærke Bechtle to get some "behind the scenes" details of how the documentary was made, and she shared some interesting things.

Read these details in today's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/e8pwesAF . And thanks Ida for sharing these!

text:
Why do some fast-growing companies not hire software engineers for a specific programming language? Here's one reason I've seen: because there is no "one" language used.

Below is a slide deck from a talk I gave in 2018, sharing the various systems my team at Uber was modifying. Note that we did not own most of these services, save for the PSP Gateway: but Uber had an "internal open source" model where any team could raise a PR for any codebase, and the owner of the codebase would review and approve/reject it.

Back in 2018, Uber's internal systems were all over the place, in terms of languages used. Up to 2016, teams could choose what technologies they used, so we had Node, Python at first, then more Go and some Java services. Around 2016-17, engineering leadership decided that only Go and Java would be supported, and teams slowly started to build new systems in these languages, and migrate existing ones over.

Still, the reality of my team was that in any given week, we'd often push code using Python, Go, Java and sometimes Node. So when we hired engineers, we didn't ask "are you an expert in either of these?", but we asked "can you code using an object-oriented language, and are you okay picking up new languages if you've not used them before?". We probed for coding skills, and for backend architecture experience during the interviews.

Although it's not as common to have such an exotic backend stack as Uber did in 2018, this flexibility of hiring people who are open to picking up languages they've not used before: it's a big bonus for companies where the tech stack is still more fluid.

PS: if you're interested in the presentation where I used this slide, it's here, it's the story of how we built the Google Pay integration, and why a seemingly simple project became challenging, behind the scenes: https://lnkd.in/ewS6DycX

text:
Meta will be reducing the number of managers it has, in an effort to increase efficiency. I talked with a few engineers and managers at the company for their take, and here's what they said:

1. Some organizations within engineering are, indeed, bloated, levels-wise. 

Within leaner organizations, a typical reporting chain of a software engineer looks like (image on the left): a software engineer is around 5-6 levels beneath Zuckerberg.

However, there are organizations where individual contributors are as much as 10 or 11 levels away from Zuck. Here’s the actual, anonymized reporting chain of both a software engineer and a data engineer with Meta. Both examples are outliers in how deep they are, but they showcase how deep hierarchies can get (images on the right).

2. The goal of this exercise does not seem to be to get managers out. 

I’m already seeing comments on social media suggesting that Meta is “firing” managers. Having talked with someone at director-level in Meta, this does not seem to be the case within engineering.

The goal of this exercise is to flatten the organization. While this will reduce the number of managers, at least for engineering managers, everyone expects the IC route to be an option.

3. The tech lead manager (TLM) role looks set to regain popularity at Meta, following the org flattening.

Meta has always had the TLM role, an engineer who’s hands-on and writes code, while managing a small team of 3-5 people.
The problem with this role was that when the company grew fast, TLMs were pushed to manage more people and become EMs. The consensus within Meta was that one should be either a great IC or a great manager.

However, with the engineering organization not growing as fast now, and with a focus on creating a flatter org, the TLM role will undoubtedly make a comeback at the company.

4. My take

I was surprised to learn about more than 8-deep reporting chains at Meta, known to be a company which has a culture of moving fast and staying nimble. As I learned, very long reporting chains within software engineering are more the outliers, than the norm and is more common in heavy profit centers (eg Ads): but still. When I was at JP Morgan, I was 9 levels from the CEO and it made me feel insignificant - and moving to tech companies it was pretty refreshing to know that I'm close enough to people making the "big" decisions in my organization.

In the end, flat orgs have lots of benefits but also drawbacks (eg ability for managers to support, career growth options etc) so there is no one "best" approach.

I covered more insights and predictions from engineering managers at Meta in the latest, subscriber-only The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/eJuHkz5K

#softwareengineering #engineeringmanagement #meta

---

text:
How does a migration of 1 million lines of code, from a Python monolith, to Go services look like? In the case of Khan Academy it looked like this, spanning 3.5 years:

I talked with Brian Genisio and Kevin Dangoor - both of who played key roles in this migration - to learn how the company went through this exercise of not only changing languages (Python -> Go), but changing APIs (REST -> GraphQL) and architecture (monolith -> GraphQL federation with smaller services).

The team started by agreeing on the architecture approach, and then migrating the smallest and simplest possible service to this new architecture, and sending production traffic onto it. This was a service that answered the question of what was the oldest version of the mobile client that could be supported, and accounted for probably less than 0.01% of website traffic.

The team then diligently migrated field after field, endpoint after endpoint.

At the halfway of the project, about 95% of traffic was migrated over. However, this was the point when the existing tools needed to be rebuilt - or re-fitted - and the services needed new capabilities, and some new features to be added.

Still, 3.5 years is a long time, and, as Brian shared:

"By the end of the project, all of us: engineering, product, design and the rest of the business, just wanted the migration to be over so we could move into the post-migration world."

Read more details on this migration on this week's free The Pragmatic Engineer issue here: https://lnkd.in/egsKTWzJ

And thanks to Brian and Kevin for sharing details and learnings!

text:
A software engineer at Facebook asked if I can review their resume versions. I sent this person the same answer I send to all such requests:

Advice I most commonly give, are:

1. Instead of choosing a "master" format, tailor for each job application. Highlight achievements, strengths, activites that are relevant for *that specific job* that you apply to.

2. Don't forget the goal of a CV: to get you past screening! It's *not* about giving your life story, or representing your career the most accurate way. Take this into account when tailoring your resume.

3. Your resume is not the most important thing for all job applications. A warm referral counts far more. So can being local, not needing a visa or relocation, for most roles.

4. The more relevant industry expereince you have, the easier it is to write your resume, and the less the format matters. This is why, for e.g. industry switchers or new grads, there's little actionable advice to give, because there is not (yet) too many speicifics to add your resume. Usually simple templates that are easy to screen for hiring managers work well. Here's a simple enough one I created, free with a GDoc or a Word formats: https://lnkd.in/dXz4Zdy

I used to do resume reviews - I did well over 100 in 2020. I found myself giving very similar advice, after a while, so I ended up sending out a PDF later on. First, this was 10 pages. Then, 30. After this, I spent more time talking with other recruiters and hiring managers and turned it into a book: https://thetechresume.com/ 

The book is free for software engineers without a job - including those who have yet to get their first job. My only request is if you get a job, with the help of the book, pay it forward and help someone else in the industry: https://lnkd.in/gfBqxMKu

And here's a blog post that summarizes my advice in more detail than this post, covering many key topics from the book as well: https://lnkd.in/eTweUb6X

text:
A trend I've started to observe, across many companies: more aggressive performance reviews happening. Examples include HelloFresh, Delivery Hero, Uber, FREE NOW.

HelloFresh is a company where I got more details, and one where after a top-down mandated process to identify more underperformers, those underperformers were presented with a "PIP or mutual exit termination" choice. 

In the case of HelloFresh, the problem is the countries where the company tries to take this approach: in countries like Germany - with employees protected more, at a regulatory level - this approach doesn't work well.

At Delivery Hero, around 10% of the staff were to be put in the "underperforming" bucket. At FREE NOW, promotion criteria was more strict, and I'm hearing that at Uber, there is a stronger push to fill the 5% "bottom" bucket, than in previous years.

So what is there to take away from these events?

Mentally preparing for performance reviews to be harsher this year, could be a good strategy. 

My ‘scoop’ covers a handful of companies – HelloFresh, Delivery Hero, Uber, and FREE NOW – which all face pressure on their business and for whom 2023 is unlikely to be an easy year. But I predict more strict performance calibrations to be the norm at places where business is not booming – and doubly so where business is bad. At companies where belts need tightening, calibrations may realistically serve as a way to identify lowest performers. The bottom few percent could then be moved on to performance improvement plans, with some exiting the company.

Getting a “meets” could be harder this time around. So if you get this feedback, take into account whether your company has stricter criteria before feeling disappointed. Similarly, being rejected for a promotion might be because there’s not enough budget to promote everyone who is ready, not that you aren’t ready.

If you’re a manager preparing for calibrations, try to feel out what the situation is and prepare for a potentially more cutthroat meeting than usual. I covered more on performance calibrations: https://lnkd.in/eaavg4h6

The above insights were from this week's The Scoop issue from The Pragmatic Engineer. Subscribers can read the full issue here: https://lnkd.in/eaavg4h6

#softwareengineering #career #performancereview

text:
What do different "Staff+" engineering levels typically mean? With the caveat that these definitions can (and do!) differ between companies, here's the typical scopes of the role:

The below observations come from ✨ Nicky Wrightson, who was a principal software engineer for several years, before moving into a Head of Engineering position recently.

Staff Engineer: Often embedded in a team, but often in a more complex space with a high degree of inter-team dependencies.

Senior Staff: Operates across several teams and will embed where needed.

Principal Engineer: Operates across many teams, or an entire domain.

Senior Principal: Operates across more complex domains, or is brought into business domains to help set direction.

Distinguished Engineer: Operating across several business domains.

Fellow Engineer: Working across the entire organisation.

I'll add my two cents on how the size of the organization very much matters in the scope of these roles. For example, the "measured" scope of a Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft will, naturally, be wider - given that Microsoft has well above 100,000 employees - than that of at a Series B scaleup with 200 people. Still, it's likely that a Distinguished Engineer would have a scope that touches on one or more, company-wide business domains.

Nicky went into a lot more observations this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue on Staff+ roles in the What is a Staff+ Engineer? article. Subscribers can read it here: https://lnkd.in/ereshjHD

Thanks a lot, ✨ Nicky, for your insights!

#softwareengineering #career

text:
Important message for anyone being hit by redundancies, based in a country with strong employee protections (e.g Germany, France, the Netherlands): DO talk to an employment lawyer before signing anything.

Sadly, some tech companies are conducting both mass layoffs, and performance-based layoffs. I am getting details that especially in the case of mass layoffs, many of these companies either don't know, or don't follow local regulation - that is usually very strict on such separation. I am also getting details that this is also happening for several performance-based layoffs.

When workers are heavily protected from layoffs, the standard approach for companies is not to lay off, but offer an agreement which is you accepting to resign. If you sign this, you get a nice amount paid, and leave on your own accord. However, in countries like Germany this will also mean, for example, that you're ineligible for unemployment benefits - that you would get, if laid off.

If the company wants to terminate you and offers an agreement, do yourself a favor and get an employment lawyer to review this document. Many employment lawyers do a first consultation for free, and e.g. in Germany, if you have employment law insurance, their fee is covered. Even if it's not covered, spending e.g €150-200 for an hour-long consultation can have a handsome return in severance outcome, and knowing your rights.

Be aware that signing a separation agreement that you've not reviewed with a professional might mean signing away rights you would normally have.

To find lawyers: google them, ask colleagues in the same boat, and for Germany, here are a few suggestions in this thread: https://lnkd.in/eQQ4duRe

There are plenty of countries where, unfortunately, there are no great employment protections. So if you live and work in one where these protections are strong, use them, and seek help from people who specialize in enforcing these regulations.

Strong employment protections are a tradeoff that tech companies should take into consideration both when hiring, and when laying off. Given we've seen a long bull market, many companies only realize just how strong these employment protections are, now: especially when their staff push them to follow the regulation.

Focusing on the layoffs that happened doesn't tell the real story of the past 12 months. Here's what happens when we take into the hiring that happened, *together* with the job cuts:

Big Tech companies that let people go still hired more, net, than e.g. Apple which hired slower.

While I don't have hiring data for all companies that announced layoffs, I would expect a lot more companies to fit this trend.

Take SAP, a company that announced layoffs just today: to let go 3,000 people (about 3% of staff). The company hired more than 11,000 employees in 2022 (11%) and even after taking this cut into account, their 2022 growth rate will have been 8%: ahead of e.g. Apple's 6% headcount growth.

To be clear, the job cuts by Microsoft - which posted it's best-ever full-year results in 2022, profits of $72.9B - and Google - which is expected to have it's second-best year in 2022, with profits of around $66B -  were not a "must" for these companies, in that they could not afford to have them. They were a "want" in order to serve their shareholders.

text:
What is the difference between a code freeze, a code chill and a code slush?

A code (deployment) freeze is when you pause all deployments to production.

A code (deployment) chill:

At some companies, code freezes are not enforced and teams are asked to only deploy changes that cannot wait. Teams have leverage to follow this guidance on their own. Both Square and Uber use this approach, referring to it as a “code chill.” An engineering director at Square shared why they like it:

“I like a chill vs a freeze differentiation. A code chill says that you should use your judgment. Know that the impact can be high, if you mess up your deployment.”

A code chill can be a good step in moving away from a hard code freeze, as it allows teams to deploy important changes more freely and gives them the autonomy – and responsibility – to make the call on whether a deployment is important enough to risk deploying. 

A code (deployment) slush:

 Etsy was one of the first companies to share that they call their code deploy slowdown a “slush,” not a freeze. Slush not only means melted snow or ice, it’s also the name of a popular drink.

In a 2016 blog post, Etsy’s engineering team detailed their approach in an engineering blog post, writing:

"Since at Etsy, we still push code during the holidays, just more carefully, it’s not a true code freeze, but a cold, melty mixture of water and ice. Hence, Slush. (...)

In the early days, Slush was far more strict, in part because Etsy’s infrastructure was not as robust and mature as it is today. We operated off of a federated database model, which in theory was meant to prevent one database crash from affecting another, but in practice, it was hard to keep clusters from affecting one another and site stability suffered as a result. This technical approach also made it hard to understand what went wrong and how the team could fix it. (...)

Today, Slush means that major buyer and seller facing feature changes are put on hold, or pushed “dark,” where they are hidden behind config flags and not shown publicly."

At companies where each team can decide whether to freeze or not, the result is a slush. This is especially true at places where teams own services which depend on one another, and some are frozen and others not.

I personally find the Slush concept more confusing than the code chill approach. I also noticed very few people mention the concept of “code slush”, suggesting this term hasn’t really caught on.

Have you done code chills or "slushes" in the past? How did you find them?

I cover more on code freeze practices across the industry in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue. Subscribers can read it here: https://lnkd.in/ehKg_biJ

#codefreeze #codeslush #softwareengineering

Today was a sad day with lots of large layoffs announced. But there's also good news, and I'd like to share this instead: 93,000 employees at Mercedes-Benz - including SWEs - awoke to this:

The company announced paying out a €7,300 bonus to all employees in Germany, as a profit sharing bonus. This bonus coms on top of the "regular" bonuses people have (e.g. the 13 and 14th months' salary). It's the largest bonus employees received in the history of the company, and above the previous "top limit" for bonuses.

It's an interesting contrast on how Mercedes - that is traditionally not a tech company - is rewarding employees and sharing it's profits, companies like Google or Microsoft - both which are massively profitable, Microsoft closing their most profitable year in 2022 - have announced large layoffs the same week. Google announced today letting go 12,000 people (8% of staff) and Microsoft on Wednesday eliminating 10,000 positions (5% of staff).

I talked with software engineers at Mercedes, in Germany, and it feels like the tide might be changing: perhaps "traditional" companies like Mercedes that take care of employees, and share profits, are not as "boring" as they were perceived, and the Big Tech companies now laying off are not as generous or amazing as they seemed the past several years?

And yes, the bonus amounts Mercedes pays are not at the level of what most Big Tech companies award to e.g. senior engineers and above: but at a time when many employees are disappointed in their employer, people at Mercedes are very pleasantly - and unexpectedly - surprised at the outcome.

text:
How have Big Tech companies changed how they are hiring, whether they laid off, and their performance reviews in just a year? Dramatically:

In July 2022, I created a first version of these images in The Pragmatic Engineer, writing about whether we are seeing the start of a tech winter, writing:

“But what is making Big Tech react much faster than other companies? I’ll suggest this is because they are more nimble in how they both gather and analyze data, and in how they operate, compared to some more traditional companies.

This means Big Tech predicts a slowdown in business, based on data they have, and is implementing a slowdown immediately. Traditional companies either don’t detect changes in the market with such sensitivity, or they might have learned to take a longer-term outlook, and not worry about changing direction.

Don’t forget, most of these traditional companies have been around longer than Big Tech firms, and the fact that they are not as “jumpy” in reacting quickly is a feature for them, not a bug.”

This was back when Big Tech was “merely” freezing hiring; Microsoft, Google, Meta all froze at that time, and Apple was rumored to be reducing its pace of hiring.

Since then, we've seen more companies freeze hiring or lay off - Microsoft being the latest Big Tech, announcing layoffs impacting 10,000 positions - about 5% of staff - yesterday.

It’s certain we’ll see a correction of 2021-22’s hiring frenzy and it’s a given that Big Tech will hire much less this year than in 2022, while the question remains whether other large tech companies will follow suit and announce layoffs in the coming months.

Read more on what these layoffs mean for the industry, and why I'm especially worried seeing Microsoft and Amazon lay people off: https://lnkd.in/eyuMxHKp

text:
What raises can software engineers expect to get this year, at a time when inflation is at a decades-old high in Europe and the US? I gathered details from 100 companies on their plans:

As context, the majority of people who shared details about their companies' compensation change plans for 2023 were CTOs, engineering directors, engineering managers and HR folks. A few respondents are tech leads and software engineers to whom the increases have been communicated. Two CEOs also shared details. 

Of the 100 companies which shared details 6% are raising above inflation, 36% in-line with inflation, 39% below inflation, 9% well below inflation, and 10% are not raising at all.

Even at companies offering below-inflation raises, top performers are given bumps that match inflation or better. Several budgeting decision makers shared that they are planning to raise average salaries well below inflation. The reasons for this ranged from being worried about a recession in 2023, the business not being as close to profitability as expected, to wanting to preserve their runway. 

More determined managing out of low performers definitely feels like a trend. Some respondents noted that low performers are expected to be pushed out more aggressively than in previous years. I’m hearing rumors some Big Tech companies are also issuing stricter guidance and expanding the ‘below expectations’ bracket in performance calibration sessions.

Region-wise, there’s surprisingly few differences across regions, in terms of the salary raises one can expect relative to inflation. It’s clear that this year it’ll be exceedingly rare for companies to raise salaries above inflation for all employees, on average.

Subscribers can read more insights on these data points in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue, including some surprising findings on raises and the funding stage of companies: https://lnkd.in/ehtyAFMg

In summary, budgets for salary raises have decreased from last year at many companies. In 2021, the market for tech was very hot - detailed in the now un-paywalled article https://lnkd.in/dT7j3x-F - , and annual rises at the beginning of 2022 reflected the increased competition. Throughout 2022, we saw the tech market cool off, and decreasing budgets in 2023 reflect a new reality that competition for software engineers – and the risk of attrition – is decreasing across most companies and markets.

Thanks to everyone who shared details on pay raise plans!

#softwareengineering #compensation #europe #us

---

text:
"My team/company does not use a modern tech stack. How important is it for me to keep up with the modern stack?" An important question. Some thoughts:

1. It's a ticky question with no one answer.

This is because it greatly depends on what career stage you're at, where you are and where you want to be (e.g. if you work in Oil and Gas and want to stay there, it's not bad if you keep using older versions of Java)

2. Early career

In general, early in your career, keeping learning new things could be more important. 

But: mastering one thing at a time in my view is more important than keeping on jumping on new things. Once you get good at e.g. the main language/framework your team uses: consider mastering it. This has lots of benefits. Once you’ve mastered it, and are the expert, consider picking up something new.

3. Later in your career.

Later in your career, with plenty of technologies under your belt, the specific technologies might be less important.
BUT your willingness and ability to learn will not.

ALso, the more technologies you work with, the more nuanced your understanding of the tradeoffs between them. And you keep your learning fresh!

What is your take on the importance of keeping up with new technologies?

#softwareengineering #advice

--

text:
A lot of engineers whose company uses CircleCI today had a bad day. They got an email this morning telling them to *immediately* rotate any and all secrets stored in CircleCI. What happened?

Details are sparse, as CircleCI is tight-lipped in sharing any details with customers or with me while they conduct the investigation. They instructed customers to not just rotate all keys, but review internal logs for unauthorized access between 21 Dec - 4 Jan.

Second-guessing the message, CircleCI customers could have had their secrets accessed by third parties, and this went undetected for two weeks - during the holiday period. Now, it’s all on them to work out if malicious actors could have accessed their systems via leaked CircleCI credentials.

Why did CircleCI take so long to detect a security incident?

I can only speculate, but it’s fair to assume that starting on 21 December, people at CircleCI, just like at most companies, were already starting to take holidays, and no one expected anything important to happen. If an attacker was to identify the best time to breach a system and go undetected for as long as possible, then the longest annual period of downtime would be it.

So, how much can you trust your CI provider or your infrastructure provider to not leak secrets? 

This is a very good question. Over the past 12 months, Travis CI, Heroku and CircleCI have all failed at keeping customer’s secrets – and therefore their codebases – safe.

I expect that platforms which design themselves according to a security-first approach will start to gain popularity. It is becoming increasingly risky to give a CI system full access to your code repository, in the way CircleCI or TravisCI expect.

Alternatives that don't open up access to source code include approaches like Buildkite or GitHub's self-hosted runners: both running CI inside your own infrastructure, having no source code or secrets access. It's more work to set up, but a far more secure approach.

I'm hearing many CircleCI customers are opting for some of these, more secure approaches.

Now is a great time to do a pre-mortem on what could happen if your CI provider gets hacked, and your secrets leaked. Even if you’re not a CircleCI, TravisCI or Heroku customer, don’t assume your provider won’t suffer a security breach. What sensitive data could be leaked if this happened, and how badly would it affect your business? Do you have measures in place like secret rotation to minimize the damage?

I wrote more about this incident and learnings engineers can take: https://lnkd.in/ew9NVFwA

---

text:
"How do you mandate a code deployment freeze? How do most companies do this?" Here are the most common approaches to do this:

First, why have a code deploy freeze at all? Well, you'll need it. Sooner or later, all tech companies get to the point of needing to mandate a deployment freeze. It could be an end-of-year holiday code freeze put in place. 

More likely, it will be because of a very high priority outage that needs resolving and no new code should be pushed. Or it could be to freeze deployments before an important company event - for startups, a critical demo. For larger companies, it could be an annual event like the re:Invent event at Amazon.

Common approaches:

1. Ask people not to deploy. 

Tell people that deploys can only be done with a signoff, from a given date. This is a bit more formal, but still has no automation. It could be asking to put in the signed off person’s name into the merge message. It’s asking people to follow new guidance sent over chat, email and in-person communication.

2. Tell people that deploys can only be done with a signoff, from a given date. 

This is a bit more formal, but still has no automation. 

3. Stop the deployment pipeline. 

Messages will go unread, and there could be automated changes that kick off deploys. To endforce a code freeze, the clearest thing is to suspend automated deploys. For example, if a change merged to the main branch kicks off a deploy to production, suspend that deploy job. 

4. Disallow merging to the deploy branch. 

The clearest way to do #3 is not to stop deploy jobs - and have the deploy branch get merges - but to disallow merges to that branch, to start with. Again, this is more involved, but it’s a more secure approach. It also allows for cherry picking changes for emergency deploys.

5. More involved tooling changes. 

At companies with staging environments, it could be more complicated to do a deployment freeze. For example, you might allow merges and deployments to staging, but not to the production environment. How do you do this? It could be #3, or #4, or some more complicated approach, based on your current tooling. You could have a hotfix process that bypasses some tooling that you use to ship emergency fixes to production.

What other approaches have you seen for mandating a code deploy freeze?

---

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Code deploy freezes are these things that almost everyone does, but we don't talk about that much. Here are 3 good, and 3 bad things about them:

The good:

1. Higher reliability and fewer outages. 

This is the most obvious upside. If you look at the root causes of most outages, they’re overwhelmingly due to a code change gone wrong. By not having any code changes, you reduce the most common cause of outages.

2. People can properly recharge and disconnect. 

There are plenty of companies at which people are able to go on holiday and to disconnect because there are colleagues in the office standing in for them. For example, on a team with three backend engineers, if one goes on holiday, the remaining pair can take care of any issues or outages which arise.

3. Breathing space, thinking space and developer tools innovation.

many professionals perform better within alternating intensity periods. I’ve seen lots of tech debt work cleaned up during code freezes and tooling improvements made – none of them planned. You can argue about how useful such work is, but I’ve always observed that engineers’ motivation and happiness increases and some unexpectedly useful productivity gains are made.


And 3 downsides:

1. The rush to get all changes in before the code freeze begins.

The longer a code freeze lasts, the more likely it is there will be a mad dash to get the final few changes shipped a day or two before it starts.

2. Large and risky refactorings happening during the freeze

Large code freezes are an invitation to do the large refactorings that have been deemed too risky, too lengthy, or not high enough priority. This is when the upgrading of major library versions tends to happen; major upgrades involving sweeping changes across the codebase. It’s when new patterns can be – and are – rolled out across the codebase, when deprecated code is removed, and when other complex, under-the-hood, changes are made.

3. The day after a code freeze ends: merge issues and rebase headaches. 

At companies which use trunk-based development – where the main branch is deployed to production – a code freeze means no merging into the main branch occurs. With a code freeze lifted, pull requests make it into the main branch. Some of these pull requests tend to be larger than usual; for example, the massive refactor ones. This means that if your change is bound to merge after such a large change, then you’ll have to rebase and resolve any conflicts before being good to go.

What other benefits and drawbacks have you observed about code deploy freezes?

---

Read more about code freeze approaches at various Big Tech companies - like Meta, Amazon, Apple or Spotify, and a lot more details about the benefits and the tradeoffs of this approach in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue for subscribers: https://lnkd.in/enrK47iM

#codefreeze #softwarengineering

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I talked with more than 30 hiring managers to answer the question: is it easier to hire software engineers and engineering managers? Here's what I saw:

1. In almost all regions, it’s easier to hire. 

The only exceptions to this were a hiring manager in western Europe struggling to fill a staff role, and in India, where there seems to be trouble in hiring senior engineers.

2. The US West and East Coasts seem to be cooling down the most, after the previous hiring frenzy. Hiring managers I talked to have been the most delighted by how much easier it is to hire in these regions. Note, many hiring managers had generous budgets to work with, and were at companies that paid closer to the top of the market already.

3. Compensation seems to be dropping, and remote companies can capitalize on this. 

Especially in Europe, several hiring managers mentioned that they can now hire for less in several other regions, where the market is not as competitive. This was especially true for companies either hiring for remote, or hiring for flexible work where the office is within commuting distance.

4. Local companies are still struggling to hire in some regions. 

In places where local companies compete with overseas rivals with deeper pockets, it’s still hard to hire. This especially seems to be the case in Latin America, especially when wanting to hire software engineers who have decent English language skills.

5. Overall, we're seeing a market correction, and this will be good for companies, worse for employees.

Companies find it easier to hire software engineers, for the most part, across almost all regions. Compensation expectations seem to be not increasing, and going down in some regions. This is good news for hiring managers with headcount, founders, and companies.

It's less good news for those on the job market, especially compared to where the job market was a year ago (see this summary on where the market was: https://lnkd.in/dT7j3x-F). I still hear that experienced software engineers and hands-on engineering managers are very much in-demand, but it's less of an employee's market than even a few months ago.

For a lot more details, see the latest issue of The Pragmatic Engineer for subscribers: https://lnkd.in/ez9ZY9A3

#hiring #softwareengineering

text:
Two perspectives of the same thing, and how it can relate to software engineering.

Most users and customers of any system only interact with the frontend: may this be a web app or a mobile app. In most cases, lots of complexity for the app to work lies in the backend: invisible to most users - and sometimes, even to executives!

And then, there's the infrastructure that the backend runs on. Invisible to frontend engineers, non-technical people, and - naturally - to customers. Infrastructure could be outsourced or be kept in-house, and the complexity can be surprising even for software engineers.

Some of the most interesting engineering challenges happen at the infrastructure level: just like how in this photo some of the most complex pieces of machinery are in the infra (the specialized trash truck).

If you are not seeing a large backend and infrastructure component for a frontend you're looking at: take a step back and look again.

#softwareengineering #backend #frontend #infrastructure

text:
Yesterday, the New York Times ran the article "Computer Science Students Face a Shrinking Big Tech Job Market". Reading it, there was nothing surprising about it, beyond confirming what I've been seeing for months.

In The Pragmatic Engineer, I've been covering how the tech hiring market changes, and how these changes hit new grads especially hard. In October, I analyzed the state of the job market and predicted what the New York Times now confirmed is happening: new grads struggling to get jobs in Big Tech, because - predictably - hiring is down, and will stay down.

Here is the New York Times article: https://lnkd.in/eDyfuzAu

Here is my analysis from October, on the Big Tech hiring slowdown (a subscriber-only piece): https://lnkd.in/eAwYJ9za

In what is interesting, in 2021 I also analyzed the then *very* hot job market, and the New York Times did an in-depth report on the same topic, confirming my analysis and predictions on what would happen. This is the original article, open to read: https://lnkd.in/dT7j3x-F

Finally, here is advice for new grads I have for this very tough market: https://lnkd.in/es4ZT35S

It's a very tough market for new grads and those with little professional experience, and it will continue to be so. If you're in this position, you'll most likely have to lower expectations, apply to places with less competition, and spend time and energy to stand out from a growing pool of similarly qualified applicants. The last time the job market was so tough for CS graduates was around 2008-2009, and the current situation is, sadly, not looking better than it did back then.

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Can software engineers at Twitter not get a break, not even for Thanksgiving? The night before Thanksgiving, Twitter fired more developers and put dozens more on performance warnings:

I talked with software engineers inside the company, both who were fired because their recent code review exercise determined "that your code is not satisfactory." In slap in the face, these people are given 4 weeks severance. This is an insult, because a week earlier, anyone who opted to leave the company and not sign up for long hours was given 3 months of severance.

Those who stuck it out with Elon Musk's Twitter had to put up with all of this, just in 4 weeks:

Week 1: 50% of their colleagues laid off, and several of these software engineers as well. Covered more here: https://lnkd.in/e2MqFq_m

Week 2: Several laid off were called back after the company realized they let go too many key people. Some did come back. The same week, remote work was cancelled with a days' notice. More here: https://lnkd.in/ewAa_N6E

Week 3: People were given 2 days to click 'yes' to confirm they will work at the new, "hardcore", Twitter 2.0 with longer hours, and higher performance expectations, or get 3 months severance if they don't choose this. The majority of remaining software engineers chose the 3 months.

Week 4: all remaining perks cut, including childcare allowances, despite having to return the office to work. Many of those who committed to the longer hours were fired, or given performance warnings. The performance warning email closes writing:

"Please use this opportunity to restore our confidence and demonstrate your contributions to the team and company."

I have lost words at the actions that Musk is doing to his employees. Is this some test to have the most loyal ones stay, despite knowing they could be let go any time, e.g. because their code will not be good enough? Is this what an abusive relationship looks like?

If you're a manager, I strongly suggest you take a hard look at how not to run a team or a company. If you're a software engineer, now you know what it's like to work for Elon Musk.

I'll call this what it is: unnecessary cruelty and a toxic environment.

#twitter #softwareengineering

text:
Yesterday, Musk fired about 20 software engineers at Twitter who had criticised him in one way or the other. He then proceeded to mock them on their way out.

The people let go were not told the reason of their dismissal, beyond a generic email said that “your recent behaviour has violated corporate policy.” Neither the recent behaviour, nor the corporate policy was specified. What is common with all of the software engineers is that they have been critical about leadership either publicly - on Twitter - or internally, on Slack. The New York Times reported that Musk instructed a team to "comb through messages in Twitter’s internal chat platform and make a list of employees who were insubordinate": https://lnkd.in/ewBgHxNb

While Musk can decide who he works with - he is the owner! - I find it appalling that he then proceeds to mock these software engineers on their way out. These are not faceless people: most of them have built up lots of goodwill within the tech community.

While Musk only had snarky remarks for people he just fired, their peers working in tech offered praise and support.

Just look at replies, for example for Yao Yue, who had been at Twitter for 12 years, to see the contrast: https://lnkd.in/ev6MaBVi

I have no doubt that those let go - who have been building bridges with the engineering community - will have little problem finding teams that want to work with them. 

I do foresee that Elon Musk will be able to hire from a smaller pool of software engineers, as devs all observe how he treats their peers, and how he can’t stop himself kicking them one more time on the way out. He is the world’s richest man, after all. He can. Right?

#twitter #softwareengineering #firing

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
You've been fired with ~50% of the company on Friday. On Monday, you get a call, telling you you can get your job back BUT you have an hour to decide. This is not made up: it's what's happening at Twitter.

I talked with a software engineer who got laid off on Friday, and on Monday got this phone call with a deadline of thirty minutes to decide. This person said "no", and to their knowledge, so did other people. As people learned about the calls, several people stopped answering unknown numbers to avoid this conversation.

A week after Elon Musk took over Twitter a massive ~50% layoff was done. I wrote in-depth on what the turmoil of this first week was like in The Pragmatic Engineer, and how people were treated that week: told to print out their code, given a Monday deadline to hit on Saturday, and some fired while working on the weekend. Read the full issue here: https://lnkd.in/e2MqFq_m

I have yet to see any responsible tech company conduct so large, 50% layoffs, with so little planning. This is because there's a "second layoff" effect after every layoff.

As a rule of thumb, after you lay off X% of people, you get about an additional half attrition. Lay off 10%: expect another 5% to quit. Lay off 50%... not unreasonable to expect another 25% to quit.

In the case of Twitter, it seems that the first layoff went poorly: firing people whose expertise the company does need, and realizing this within less than a day of the firing. Tech journalist Casey Newton was the first to report on this on Twitter (https://lnkd.in/eh_euH2g) and in Platformer: https://lnkd.in/eN2NjbHk

I'm told that managers within Twitter are getting desperate, trying to call back more people. People are saying "no", and more senior, staff and principal engineers are quitting.

The most experienced people leaving - who were not fired - will likely be a big problem for Twitter. Twitter has a complex architecture *for a reason.* For example, they have on prem data centers and advanced infra (eg multi-level feature flags, advanced infra-level incremental rollouts). Much of these sophisticated systems are in place to reduce the likelihood of outages, and to allow for safe changes and experimentation.

These systems need some level of institutional knowledge to maintain. This institutional knowledge both got fired, and is walking out the door.

For Twitter's sake, I hope management can slow down people leaving.

Read more about week one of the turmoil at Twitter: https://lnkd.in/e2MqFq_m

And I'll cover more of what's happening at the company at The Pragmatic Engineer. I've not seen anything nearly as of a wild company takeover in tech as what is happening at Twitter. Subscribe here: https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw

#twitter #layoffs #softwareengineering 

—

text:
I don't know what is happening today and this week, but we're seeing layoffs upon layoffs at tech companies. Just today: Lyft, Stripe, Pleo, CloudKitchens, Delivery Hero.

This week: OpenDoor, Chime, Daper Labs, Gem, Secret Escapes, MessageBird, Kry, EquityBee, EquityZen.

What is the reason behind this fresh spike in layoffs? These layoffs and Big Tech freezing hiring are connected: the market looks gloomy, and the economy is expected to weaken as we head into 2023. Demand for both consumer spend and business spend will be down.

This means that no matter what space a company operates in - may that be B2C or B2B - they should expect that their business growth will slow, and potentially even turn negative.

To add to the problem of venture-funded startups: venture funding has all but dried up. The companies that can reasonably expect to raise funding are either in very early stages, or ones that don’t need the funding, because they are already profitable.

More details and analysis on why this could be happening in The Pragmatic Engineer (subscriber-only issue): https://lnkd.in/egNeaceR

#layoffs #tech #startups #growth

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What are approaches - tactics, strategies - that help make software engineering teams effective? 

I'd love to hear your thoughts. In return, will share a summary of both what other people see, and a mental model to think about software engineering team productivity.

A few raw thoughts:

1. Tactics.

There are so many on this. I've seen teams get more productive by adding in eg standups, some got better by *removing* them. Short iterations, showing progress, avoiding throwing things 'over the fence', knowing 'why', kickoff meetings... a long list!

2. Strategy. 

When the strategy of what a team, and an organization does, was clear, I almost always saw more focus. Which led to saying not to stuff that was unimportant.

With a lack of strategy, "productivity" might have felt higher, but output was  a lot worse.

3. Key people playing outsized roles.

A great engineering manager who can help make the focus clear, makes people included and protects the team makes a *huge* difference.

Same goes for product managers. And not to mention up the leadership chain.

4. The easiest way to destroy productivity.

This happens at the top. Eg the CEO or CTO changes direction, out of the blue. Forcing teams to stop work midway, and start something new, confused...

What other thoughts do you have? Feel free to share here as a comment, or take five minutes to share more details in this form, and I'll share the draft of my writeup with you: https://lnkd.in/eAaT_d2G

#productivity #softwareengineering #engineeringmanagement #people

text:
Dollar Shave Club - the company that was bought for $1B by Unilever in 2016 - is down for the 7th day. This is what their website looks like. Here's what I know:

1. This is NOT a ransomware attack.

When a site is down for this long with no communications, it's often because the company is blackmailed by ransomware, all systems locked, and restoring from backups take for a long time.

2. The company is having trouble to fix the problem with their existing software engineering team. So they have called in external folks who can hopefully help with bringing their flagship site back online.

3. I'm gathering more details.

If you work or worked at Dollar Shave Club as a software engineer and have details or want to trade notes on information I can't yet share in public, please contact me: https://lnkd.in/eTp2P_h7. 

I treat all details as anonymous.

I also reached out to the company to get more details.

I'll share more as I gather information on this increasingly embarrassing outage. There must be things that the broader community can learn once the causes to what happened are uncovered.

Don't forget: anyone could see things go wrong with their own flagship product. The question is: how long will it take to fix it or restore it, and how to do better next time?

At least experienced native engineers.

I talked with two native engineers on the US West Coast. Both have ~10 years of experience working on native apps and impressive backgrounds and portfolios. Both have previously led mobile teams.

One shared the number of reach outs they receive via LinkedIn InMails, about native positions. They list their title as “Tech Lead” at a current unicorn – a company valued at $1B or more – and has extensive iOS experience. This is the number of reach-outs they’ve gotten per month, this year.

According to them, the distribution of these reach-outs is evenly split between:

1. Small places: Developer agencies with poor reputations, or small US-based regional contractor firms.

2. Non-tech companies: grocery chains, banks, telcos and other companies where tech is a cost center.

3. Big Tech: like Meta, Microsoft, Uber, Pinterest.

And it’s not just the Bay Area where developers have such numbers of reach outs. I talked with a native iOS engineer based in eastern Europe who’s seen a similar trend in reach outs to them.

text:
What a super nice compliment for The Pragmatic Engineer! I'm framing this one. A motivation to start the newsletter is how I didn't have anything "useful" to subscribe to when I became an eng manager:

When I transitioned to the engineering management track at Uber, my manager at the time suggested I expense a professional publication that will help me become a better EM

So I looked around. At Uber, we already had an O'Reilly Learning subscription: but that subscription was more about accessing long-form things like books or videos. While it comes with so many good resources and I read lots of books, utilizing this subscription: it didn't feel like it would be something "regular" I'd keep up with. I looked at weekly and monthly publications, and in the end, bought Harvard Business Review.

However, while HBR was ok, none of the articles were for *software engineers turned managers*. At one point I emailed the HBR staff offering to write an article about the unique challenges on engineering management, but I didn't hear back from the editorial staff.

So when I started The Pragmatic Engineer, my vision was to write the publication I wish I had when working at a tech company. Something I'd look forward to read, something that is a mix of deep and interesting, and something that is unmistakably written for software engineers working in Big Tech or high-growth places: by people who are deeply familiar with how these places work.

I don't think The Pragmatic Engineer ever can or will compete with the depth and breadth that a subscription like the O'Reilly Learning Platform can have. But I sure hope that people will find themselves doing what they could never do with O'Reilly: read - or at least, skim - all the articles that come out. Because they're timely, interesting, and fresh.

And if you have a learning and development budget at work, you can expense The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw

#business #softwareengineering #engineeringmanagement  #learning

text:
My first print mention in a major Dutch newspaper: NRC, in the article about how Booking.com rescinded offers from 11 tech workers in Amsterdam, written by Stijn Bronzwaer. The real problem in my view is this:

As background, starting 30 August, Booking published a strictly confidential internal guideline to ban hiring people residing in 6 regions - including Iran, parts of Ukraine, Syria and a few other countries. Based on this guidance, the company started to rescind offers for these people, many of them a few days away from starting, and people who signed these offers months before. These people quit jobs, sold houses and cars, booked tickets... then got an email telling them "sorry, you no longer have an offer."

A month ago time, I covered this story in The Pragmatic Engineer (read the in-depth coverage here: https://lnkd.in/ess68hMH) and reached out to Booking. Booking confirmed the rescinding of offers, but also told me in a statement:

"We have been and will continue to be in touch with the individuals impacted as we work to understand what we can do."

Well, this statement turned out to be untrue. The people whose offers were rescinded were not getting responses from Booking, and the company was not in touch with them, despite what the spokesperson told me. It was only following a major social media outrage thanks to the post from Maede Rajabi in October that gathered 25,000 likes and hundreds of comments, that the company actually reached out these people (see the full post here: https://lnkd.in/e8vQwZnG)

Booking.com has acted shamefully: rescinding offers is one thing - especially if there are business reasons behind it, even if not all those reasons can be shared to the public - but lying to the press, not compensating people whose lives are heavily impacted, and ignoring the messages of these people they took back offers the 11th hour from is not something any decent company would do.

I reached out to Booking 3 days ago, asking them why they misled me on 15 September with their statement, saying they are in touch with impacted people when they were not. I have yet to receive a response.

Booking.com: you need to do better with current and future candidates, and with regards to telling the truth when making statements to the press. "No comment" is much preferrable to stating things that turn out to be false like it has been the case.

Read the full NRC article here. Excellent reporting by Stijn Bronzwaer.: https://lnkd.in/eFT5R-YY

#booking #hiring #shame

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The news on Booking .com resinding Amsterdam offers for people based in Iran is starting to spread more on social media. Here are exclusive details on what exactly happened and why:

1. Early September 2022, Booking changed its guidance on hiring practices.

The company put a ban in place for employees located in 6 countries: Iran, Syria, Crimea (region), Donetsk or Luhansk (region), Cuba or North Korea. According to an internal document I reviewed which was marked as "strictly confidential", this guidance is effective from August 30, 2022. 

The rationale Booking makes in this document on this change is how the EU, the UK and the US maintain economic sanctions on specific countries, governments and individuals. The document makes the case that some sanctions are comprehensive - like the US one on Iran, while some are selective - like EU sanctions related to Russia. The document makes the case that hiring a particular employee could create a sanctions risk for the company.

2. Iranian employees within Booking in Amsterdam started speaking up internally.

Of all the regions to no longer hire from, the most sensitive one for Booking is Iran. This is because the company has historically hired and relocated plenty of employees - mostly software engineers - from this country.
These employees are especially upset at two things: 1. How signed offers have been rescinded, having a significant impact on people uprooting their lives; 2. How the policy is not communicated in public. Some recruiters and hiring managers at Booking are not even aware of this policy.
 
3. Booking can - and does - still hire citizens of US-sanctioned countries who do NOT reside in those countries.

I confirmed with a hiring manager that the company has recently gone ahead and hired a software engineer with an Iranian passport but residing in the Netherlands. 

4. My take.

Rescinding offers is a terrible move, and one that is hurting a large number of people who sold off properties, and were ready to move from countries like Iran.

I suspect the move was not taken lightly by the company, and it feels like Booking chose to not risk operating illegally (breaking sanctions) over doing the right thing for these people: but risking legal action, fines, and being marked as a company sabotaging sanctions imposed by governments in several countries and regions where the company operates.

I also see this example of Booking showing how increasingly challenging it is for global companies to navigate a growing number of sanctions.

I covered this story a month ago in The Pragmatic Engineer, and just un-paywalled this section. Read it all here: https://lnkd.in/ess68hMH

If you work in tech, consider signing up to the newsletter to get exclusive stories like this in your inbox, earlier: https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw

#booking #hiring #sanctions

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Will Facebook do layoffs? Business Insider writes "12,000 Facebook employees, 15% of its workforce, may lose jobs amid quiet layoff." I came to a very different conclusion, talking with Meta insiders. 

What I found & my analysis on the situation:

1. The reported 15% "needs improvement" target doesn't apply across all of tech - in fact, I could not confirm it applying to tech *at all*.

Business Insider bases their article on how directors were told to put 15% of their teams on "needs improvement". I talked with engineering managers and directors within engineering where there is no such target - according to insiders.

2. There IS a new performance review process happening. 

As I covered before in The Pragmatic Engineer for subscribers, Meta is moving to an annual performance review process this year. Changes to the process are happening this year, and some things are being worked out on the fly.

3. There has always been a “needs support” target for engineering organizations and this hasn’t changed. 

Talking with a tenured senior manager, they shared there has always been an organization-wide target for “needs support,” of around 10%. They said the target has not been moved. In their words, “performance review is business as usual.” Other engineering managers also shared that the 10% target has not changed.

4. "Needs support" does not mean letting people go.

"Needs support" does not automatically mean a PIP (performance improvement plan). A PIP does not mean people will be let go. PIPs at Facebook have been far more considerate than eg at Amazon, in the past. So it's not true that all "needs review" rated people might be let go. Never has been.

5. Does Meta even NEED to do layoffs?

Instead of relying on gut feels of people, I crunched the numbers, and did the maths on the financials of the company, focusing on whether engineers would need to be let go.

My conclusion is that Meta likely doesn’t need to do layoffs, because attrition will take care of itself. 

Read more of my analysis in with un-pawyalled sections of The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/e2Xnebmp

I decided to un-paywall these parts, seeing information I see as creating fear, uncertainty, and doubt without proper analysis spread in the media.

#facebook #meta #layoffs

text:
Lots of people ask for advice on how to write a newsletter that will be successful; looking at The Pragmatic Engineer which passed 180,000 subscribers.

I'm hesitant to give advice for a few reasons:

1. Survivor bias.

Any advice I would give would be less applicable, given the advice would reflect my own beliefs of why it was successful, conveniently leaving out failures or context that helped this growth. Not necessarily doing this deliberately: but simply because we like to forget our failures in the past.

2. Because even I don't know.

Honestly, I have no clue why the newsletter (and blog) became widely-known. There are articles which I spend less time on that are a big hit, and ones I toil away on for weeks that resonate less. I keep changing what I write about based on my interests, and sometimes this works out, and other times it's a flop.

One thing is for sure: I get to spend most of my time writing - and talking with people and researching related to what I'll write about - and I'm lucky not to have to spend time on marketing/advertising (save for a reflection post every now and then like this), thanks to a good chunk of people discovering the newsletter from The Pragmatic Engineer Blog. This leads me to the next point.

3. Because the success was not an overnight one.

Although I started The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter in August 2021 - and it did very well from the start - this newsletter was the follow-on of The Pragmatic Engineer Blog (https://lnkd.in/eBgdGpVa)

I started this blog in 2015 and below are the traffic stats for the first 6 years. It was around 2020 - year 5 - when traffic started to pick up. Before that, one or two articles were a hit, and that was about it.

4. Because success is personal.

If you had asked me in 2017 if I thought my blog was successful, I would have said it was, because paying $30/month for hosting reminded me every month that I should keep writing, at the very least for my own benefit. So I did! I liked doing it, and it slowly helped me build up this habit of expressing my thoughts in a blog post or an article.

Check out the blog here: https://lnkd.in/eBgdGpVa

#writing #softwareengineering 

---

text:
I’m excited to share that I’m coming on as an advisor at TravelPerk, one of the leading B2B travel management companies.

Why did I come on as an advisor?

1. Unfinished business.

When working at Skyscanner, I was the founding mobile engineer on a new product called TravelPro, headed up by Ross McNairn. We were building a “Skyscanner for businesses”: a tool that made booking business travel a breeze, and ahead of what any company was offering. We called it TravelPro and were off to a fantastic start, with bold plans.

Sadly, for various reasons TravelPro was ultimately shut down: I left Skyscanner when the signs were clear that we did not have the support and funding the project needed. But I always felt this idea was ripe for someone to grab and run with it.

Ross never let this rest, and TravelPerk reminds me what would have happened if we kept working on TravelPro for another several years. It’s the project I never got to finish, but always wanted to do so.

2. The engineering team.

Last year, I attended the annual TravelPerk tech offsite, and I loved the energy coming from the team. This year, I returned to the same event - called BuildCon - for the second time. The team kept growing and leveling up. On this offsite, I shared ideas on utilizing lightweight written planning processes similar to RFCs - something TravelPerk is in the middle of adopting.

I'm looking forward to being more involved with TravelPerk as they grow and balance delighting customers, executing rapidly, and building with reliability and maintainability in mind.

Here’s to another great engineering hub growing - and all based in Europe! (for now, at least)

#travelperk #softwareengineering #tech #growth

article.title: The Scoop #26: Formerly high-flying companies now struggling
article.subtitle: newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
There have been so many layoffs happening at various tech companies. However, I've observed far fewer layoffs done in a considerate way.

Assuming the decision to let people go is done and final - and the business has little choice, there's a big difference in how companies approach breaking this news, and supporting those impacted.

In my book, considerate layoffs mean:

1. Generous severance for those impacted, paid well above what is required by law. Removing equity vesting cliffs, and extending options exercise windows.

2. Clear communications. The announcement makes it clear why the layoffs happened and are necessary. The reasoning is much more specific than throwing around terms like 'the macroeconomic confitions' or 'the current market'. Leaders explain what bets they made, and what of their mistakes led to hiring of too many people.

3. Promptness in clarifying those impacted, and those safe.

4. Transparency: being clear that the company is letting staff go, clarity on how many people are impacted, in what regions, and what the next steps are.

5. Supporting those let go in finding their next role through e.g. an alumni site - where external recruiters can contact those let go - the recruitment team supporting those let go, outplacement services, and so on.

6. Not making unreasonable promises that sound good in the moment but are promises that cannot be kept. See e.g. how Klarna's CEO said to all staff in May how layoffs are finished and this was the final round and the company is already doing layoffs, again.

An example of companies that executed layoffs better, recently, have been Twilio and Patreon.

Twilio issued at least 12 weeks of pay - plus allow people to stay on payroll this time, thus get insurance - removed equity cliffs and expedited vesting, and was very clear to employees, and the public, that layoffs are happening. Patreon did the same, with details below.

It's still surprising for me how few companies execute what I would consider consierate layoffs, and how Twilio and Patreon are standout examples between e.g. companies like Oracle or Tesla doing layoffs in absolute secret.

I covered how to execute layoffs humanely if there’s no other choice in The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/e9NKSMP3

The screenshot below on how Patreon executed the cuts is from The Scoop #25 in The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/ess68hMH 

#layoffs #twilio #patreon

What is Snowflake, Databricks, Apache Iceberg, Trino, Spark, Airflow, Perfect or Dagster?

Snowflake, Databricks (Delta Lake) and Apache Iceberg are various data storage tools*. (*the truth is tools like Snowflake and Databricks are both far more than storage. But they also tend to be the location we store data)

Presto (now: Trino), Apache Spark are data processing engines.

And Airflow, Prefect and Dagster are data orchestration tools.

In the latest The Pragmatic Engineer issue, Benjamin Rogojan guides us through all of these tools, in an overview of the data engineering field, and some of the more common data engineering tools. text:
My attempt to summarize how Uber's engineering organization worked in a humorous way:

The only two thing that all teams shared at Uber was:

1. Teams build and maintain microservices. It used to be fairly common for teams to have more microservices than engineers on the team.

2. RFCs (request for comment) / ERDs (engineering requirements documents). From the early days, engineering teams at Uber would write RFCs on how they plan to build things, and circulate these documents. Now days, these documents are called ERDs and there's a more formal process. Still all teams do this for any non-trivial piece of work. More on RFCs: https://lnkd.in/dgBEsuz

Both the microservices talk with lots of other microservices - spread across many teams - and teams heavily interact with one another via RFCs / ERDs.

This diagram was inspired by the very spot on organizational charts of Amazon, Google, Facebook, Microsoft, Apple and Oracle by Manu Cornet: https://lnkd.in/dfXXRhS2

text:
A tech company raised more than $200M, hired techies who worked at the likes of Twitter, Apple, Deliveroo... and then could not pay wages of these people. This company was called Pollen.

In recent years, I have not heard about a company which raised large amounts from respected VCs in Europe shut down in such a terrible way: employees not paid, many vendors not paid, some employee's pension contributions missing.

So I decided to investigate what actually happened. A month of research, and after talking to more than 20 - now former - employees, I uncovered unusual details, such as:

1. Decent European investors investing more than $200M into the company over six years, and delegating partners to join the company’s board of directors.

2. Unknown to most employees, all external investors quietly resigning from their seats on the board of directors.

3. A partner at a well-known VC firm referring to themselves as a current board member at a company-wide town hall, when documents show that the previous day they resigned from their board seat.

4. Charging thousands of customers for payments not yet due, then not finishing a postmortem or doing an incident review in the following 10 weeks.

5. Shutting down Slack for the whole company after US employees took to the channel to complain about not being paid.

Read the whole story here: https://lnkd.in/eTBUKz9W

#pollen #bankruptcy #startups #scaleups

text:
Amazon's build system is called Brazil, and one of their deployment systems are Apollo (and Apollo replaced Houston). Here are details about Brazil and Apollo as shared by former Amazon software engineer Carl Myers:

(Note that this writeup refers to the situation close to 10 years ago. However, both Brazil and Apollo are still around: Brazil still popular amongst Amazon engineers I talked with; Apollo becoming a bit of a pain to use as I gathered as teams are moving over to AWS)

From the article:

"Amazon's build system is called Brazil. The main build driver is a mess 'o Perl scripts that generate makefiles. The build system is bootstrapped by a minimal Perl script which assumes only base Perl deps and GCC are available, and downloads all other dependencies.

The build system is "data driven", meaning there are configuration files which explain what to build. Code is broken up into units called "packages", each has a configuration file which says what to build, what artifacts are produced, what the package depends on, and frequently details about how it is deployed as well."

What is interesting to read is Carl's take on just how great Amazon's internal tools were even 10 years ago, and well ahead of the likes of Google or Facebook at the time. I can't attest to whether this is still the case: but what is certainly true is how Amazon keeps investing in building internal systems to solve their own problems: and sometimes open up those tools and platforms to customers on AWS, if they are applicable.

Read the full writeup here: https://lnkd.in/dX_GxQnU

#amazon #tooling

text:
Scoop: Uber is closing its engineering office in Vilnius, Lithuania. The decision impacts about 60 software engineers. The decision was communicated in what is a considerate manner, giving ample heads up.

I knew some of the engineers from Vilnius from my time working at Uber, and meeting them on events. The site always had a heavy infra and developer tooling focus. Teams on this site own or co-own infrastructure offerings like Cassandra, Elastisearch, container deployment/management, OS configuration, oncall tooling. A project called Devpods - a remote developer environment similar to Gitpod - has been built in Vilnius and is fully owned here (originally started in Amsterdam in 2020, thanks for the correction Chris Laffra!)

On Wednesday, 7th of September, Uber’s leadership announced the Vilnius site would be permanently closed. The directors of their respective teams came to the office to share the sad news that Vilnius, as an engineering site, will be closed down. The reason? Lack of local, senior leadership.

Having talked to software engineers at Uber, in Vilnius, the reasoning did have merit. Hiring managers was challenging in this location, and there have been influential managers in the past who moved back to being an individual contributor.

Uber is handling the closing of the office in a considerate way. There are no layoffs on the spot, and nothing is rushed. The site will close in June 2023 - almost a year away. Uber will support people who want to relocate and move to other offices of their choice. The performance review process will run its course in early 2023, and bonuses will be paid around March 2023.

While closing an office is never good news, it's nice to hear that Uber leadership is not rushing this process, and all directors made the effort - and the trip - to share this difficult news in-person.

If you are hiring for outstanding engineers, I'm sure some of the folks will be open to new opportunities: either now, or in a few months. I had nothing but great experiences with the people I worked with.

Sending support to Uber Engineering in Vilnius and a special thanks to those who built - or worked with - the in-house oncall tooling that I opened almost every day when I still worked at Uber. A great team 🔥

I covered more about this event in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/g9ngZmBD

#uber #lithuania #softwareengineering
