text:
Three things I learned about the newsletter business - after a year of writing my own paid newsletter:

1. The paid newsletter model is a viable model for publications which mainstream media would never fund.

I don’t have a background in writing or journalism. The topics in The Pragmatic Engineer are narrowed down to those that software engineers or engineering managers care about. When tech journalist Casey Newton – author of Platformer – and VC reporter Eric Newcomer – author of Newcomer – sat down to talk about newsletters, Casey said this about The Pragmatic Engineer:

“If you were to pitch [The Pragmatic Engineer] column to any mainstream publication they’d say, ‘that's way too niche, Hey, get out of here, our readers would hate this guy.’”

2. Nothing beats interesting, in-depth writing.

The biggest spikes in subscribers – both free and paid – were when I published interesting, in-depth articles about a topic you couldn’t read about elsewhere. If it was a free article, it often gained popularity on social media. If it was a paid article, it got forwarded more than usual.

I have not engaged in any marketing opportunities or “growth hacking,” no bartering or other exchanges. I wrote no guest posts on other publications, except for cross-posting two articles to Newcomer – a tech VC focused publication – and Eric Newcomer also shared two relevant articles in return. My focus during the first year was to write about an interesting topic when I came across it.

This is how I decided to go deep into why JIRA went down for ten days for thousands of users without meaningful updates (https://lnkd.in/dPqRy3SP), or go broad and deep on the typical strategies of shipping to production (https://lnkd.in/dsQzhQ7S).

3. My engineering blog which I wrote for six years has been what’s helped the newsletter the most. 

Much of my credibility when starting the newsletter – and still the #1 source of paying subscribers – is my engineering blog. This is curious, as most Substack authors consistently mention Twitter as their main subscriber source – but it’s not true for me.

When I started writing my engineering blog in 2015, my goal was to write for myself, and I did not plan on monetizing it in any way. It would be four years later, in 2019, when I added a small ad unit to the blog to cover hosting costs, and in 2020 – just as I was leaving Uber – I added affiliate links.

I did not think my blog would play any role in any future venture. However, this blog was the playground where I got to practice writing, and also where many current newsletter subscribers first came across The Pragmatic Engineer. 

Read more of my learnings - and numbers on the publication - in this article: https://lnkd.in/duJ9YMNZ

#enterpreneurship #learnings

text:
On Friday, The Pragmatic Engineer crossed the incredible 150,000 subscribers after a year of launching it. I took the day off from writing to chill - and celebrate! Thank you to everyone who is a reader.

I thought it might be interesting to share how the newsletter has grown and the inflection points. The below numbers are the total number of subscribers - this means both free and paid subscribers. The majority, of course, are free subscribers.

Conventional wisdom is that most newsletters grow about the same rate over the months and years - unless there's something major happening, like a big marketing event, or a post going viral.

When starting the newsletter, I imported ~9,000 emails which were from my "old" The Pragmatic Engineer Blog newsletter, which grew to that size in two years.

A few of the popular posts have resulted in significant bumps - you can see these marked as "popular article". These were articles usually either picked up by social media, or by sites like Reddit or Hacker News.

Interesting enough, these "viral" posts did not make visible bends when zooming out. However, something else did.

Substack launched the "Recommendations" feature in April 2022. This made it easy for Substack newsletters to recommend each other. After someone signs up to The Pragmatic Engineer, they are offered to subscribe to publications I recommend - and read! - with a single click.

This feature has made a significant difference in people discovering - and subscribing to the newsletter. It's also a feature no other newsletter platform has today - I do wonder until how long, given it's a powerful way to spread awareness between newsletters. The past 30 days, ~70% of free subscriptions come from the Recommendations feature and ~10% of paid subscriptions from Substack platform features. So far I'm very happy with how my vendor helped the newsletter grow the past year.

(Note: I have no affiliation with Substack beyond being a customer, and have not been paid by them in any form. It's the other way around: they charge a percentage-based fee to use their platform every month! See also my ethics statement https://lnkd.in/eG4G6svW)

#enterpreneurship #newsletter #milestone

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Three weeks ago, Netflix introduced levels for software engineers after 25 years of having a single level ("Senior Software Engineer"): Why did the change happen, and what is the vibe among software engineers?

Netflix really had an incredible run with this single level. They scaled it up the use of a single software engineering level to ~2,000 engineers (!!). This makes them the single biggest tech company to grow so much with just one level. While there are companies which have one *external* level - like Facebook, for software engineers - those companies always had internal levels.

On Wednesday, 10th August, the ‘great reveal’ happened. 

This was the day when the corporate directory was updated, and everyone was able to see the levels of everyone else. Before this, people were told what their level was, and they shared this information with close colleagues.

The levelling process has had its tensions over the past few months. I talked with an engineering manager who wanted to get all their reports into the E6 position, as all these folks are long-tenured engineers with the work impact to justify it. Their director was not supportive in the least. In the end, this manager was able to get only 20% of their team to the E6 level. The rest stayed as E5.

Lots of tenured software engineers left Netflix during the past few months, as the re-levelling project moved ahead. In many cases, the reason for leaving was they were not happy with how they were to be levelled as E5 in the new levelling system, and not the higher level they felt was appropriate.

The biggest worry software engineers have in getting levelled as E5 level, has to do with compensation. 

Right now in the US, those levelled as E5 make between $400,000 to $800,000 in total compensation. Clearly, the higher end of this range will be E6 or E7 levels. So, what will happen to those earning on the higher end?

Netflix employees will find out soon, because the annual compensation review has started. The annual compensation review runs from 22nd August to 21st October. This is when the compensation numbers – and the raises – are decided. Between 24th October and 11th November, managers will communicate the new numbers to all employees.

So why did this change happen? And what were the benefits and the drawbacks of a single level? Read all of this in this week's free, bonus issue of The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/dTeYijBi

This article was part of The Scoop. A lot of what I share in The Scoop is exclusive to The Pragmatic Engineer, meaning it’s not been covered in any other media outlet before and you’re the first to read about it. It's also the case with this topic. Subscribe to my newsletter if you don't want to miss such exclusives: https://lnkd.in/grXSBkVw

#netflix #bigtech #career #softwareengineering

text:
This is just so incredibly nice. It's a video of Maestro, an open source UI automation tool built & released us at mobile.dev (I’m an advisor). This is how simple it is to build mobile automation for an app like Twitter:

It's a few lines of code:
- launchApp
- tapOn: Search and Expore
- tapOn: Search Twitter
- inputText: "@mobile__dev"
- tapOn: mobile .dev
- tapOn: Follow
- assertVisible: Following

How clear was that to read?? And it takes a minute or two to write the whole test, and seconds to change it.

Why is this such a big deal? It's because if you've built UI tests on iOS or Android, you know HOW difficult it is to build these tests, and how fragile they are. For years, I would battle with E2E and UI tests. Maestro - or something similar - is a solution I wish I would have had back then.

Maestro supports both iOS and Android. It was built in a way that embraces how UI tests are flakey, and it tries to counter this flakyness internally as much as possible. The target group is engineers who either don't have UI tests - yet! - or have given up on writing UI tests with hard-to-work-with approaches like Espresso. And for anyone wanting to spend minutes writing UI tests and seconds tweaking them.

Oh, and at mobile.dev we've just launched User Flows, which is the next evolution of the mobile .dev platform: a fully managed platform to run Maestro User Flows.

Once you set up User Flows, mobile .dev detecs regressions on pull requests - may this be functional or things like app size increases or memory leaks -, allows to immediately debug these and track trends.

You can sign up to use User Flows here: https://www.mobile.dev/ And read more about Maestro - and try it out - here: https://lnkd.in/d8j7y87x

#ios #android #mobiledevelopment

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Just in: Snap is laying off 20% of staff AND shutting down Zenly. Zenly is a location-based social app with more than 40M MAUs, growing fast, ran by a team of less than 100 people.

Snap bought Zenly for an estimated $250-350M in 2017 when the app has around 4M downloads. Since that acquisition, Snap invested in the company, and Zenly has been on fire, growth-wise.

So why is Snap shutting it down? Zenly could easily be sold for multiple times of what Snap paid for it, given the high growth, and how it penetrated markets like Japan, Asia, Eastern Europe and the Nordics: markets where most social media apps have found hard to set foot on? 

A long-time software engineer I talked to speculated that it's about not wanting to create a competitor.

"If Snap sold off Zenly, or spun out the company, Zenly would be a direct competitor to Snap. Perhaps it would become just as successful as Snap, given our growth rate. I don't think Snap would want this. So they're killing the app, and laying off the team instead."

This news of shutting down an acquisition that did extremely well after acquired is a sad reminder that once a company is sold, the parent company is fully in charge of it. Snap's interests is not the same as Zenly's interest. And Zenly's 40M monthly users will have to find a new home to keep track of where their friends are, and some other way to replicate the "Zenly experience".

PS: if you're hiring, you should contact the folks working at Zenly. Some amazing and world-class software engineers and tech professionals in Europe, most of them based in Paris, France.

#zenly #snap #layoffs #strategy

text:
I removed all affiliate links from my blog and YouTube channel. Here's how much revenue these generated over the past two years:

Affiliate links have been a stable source of revenue - in no small part thanks to a lot of people visiting and reading my blog, and occasionally clicking to buy books mentioned there. So why remove these links, and cut off this income channel? It's for a few reasons:

1. Affiliates create a financial pull to recommend things that people will buy.

With affiliates, anyone could rightfully question if I'm making truly independent recommendations. Would I recommend something that does not have an affiliate program? While my answer would be "yes", there's always a financial pull, to at least prioritize affiliates.

2. The newsletter never had any affiliates.

In The Pragmatic Engineer I never placed affiliate links. It would have not been ethical to do so for paying subscribers. And if I didn't place it for paying subscribers, would have it been ethical to do this for free subscribers? It didn't feel like that for me.

As the newsletter is my main business, it made sense to extend the policies I have in my newsletter also to my blog and other places I write at.

3. Disclosing affiliates is cumbersome, but not disclosing them is not ethical. 

When a link is an affiliate one - meaning you get a kickback from it - it's only fair to mention this fact. My site had small text on the side informing people that links can be affiliates, and some of the articles with more affiliate links had this text on the bottom as well.

In my case, I'd like to not have to disclose that links are affiliates: because none of them are as of now, or going forward.

4. Because I'm lucky enough to be able to say to no to affiliates and advertising. 

When I was writing this blog on the side, the additional income from ads and affiliates covered things like my hosting-related costs, and the rest was a nice bonus. Now that I'm writing the newsletter full-time, I'm lucky enough to be able to cut out these revenue streams from my business model (thank you to everyone who is a reader or a customer!)

5. Link directly to publishers, not to Amazon.

An interesting change is how, as I was replacing affiliate links, I started to directly link books to their own landing pages, or to the pages of the publisher. While I was an Amazon affiliate I would have not considered doing so: it comes to show the pull that Amazon's affiliate program has.

I shared a lot more numbers and some learnings on affiliate links here. I hope this is useful for anyone thinking of monetizing a blog or website: https://lnkd.in/dVWyCZcH

Going forward, I am no longer an Amazon affiliate - or any affiliate, for that matter.

#affiliates #transparency #enterpreneurship #money

text:
There's an assumption that India is a place to hire world-class software engineers cheaply. Well, here's some data on how much Microsoft pays: $80-100K for senior engineers (L63-64) as a median (that's €80-100K!)

Because these are median numbers, it means that 50% these senior engineers make more: there could be engineers making $120, or even $140K at these levels (and the same in Euros). For such an outlier data point, see eg how in the comments Tanay Pratap shared how he made ~$130K/year as an L63 (the lower level within senior engineer), mostly due to stock awards.

And we're talking about Microsoft: a company known to have excellent employee benefits on top of their compensation, and a pretty employee-centric culture. It also means that, compared to other Big Tech companies, Microsoft is not necessarily paying the absolute top of the local markets - expect other international Big Techs to offer somewhat more for engineers - although also fewer benefits, and possibly a somewhat more stressful working environment.

To me, this data illustrates how it's misguided to think of India as a place to hire "cheap" engineers at the top of the market. You can find world-class engineers with skills and expertise to build complex systems at scale, but be prepared to pay a price that is not that much lower that what we're used to in places like Europe.

Note that this is one data point at the top of the market: and does not represent all of India - it’s just one data point with one company (Microsoft: a company employing ~8,000 people in India, of which I estimate at least 2,000 are in tech). As others noted in the comments, Microsoft pays serval times what service sector companies offer in the country. But to hire software engineers from Microsoft in India - or direct competitors -: this is how much you’ll need to likely pay.

The data comes from more than 2,000 data points analyzed for Microsoft's compensation structures and raises, published in The Pragmatic Engineer. Subscribers can read the full article with more details here: https://lnkd.in/d_vfqryK

#microsoft #compensation #india

In the spring of 2019 I was at a barbeque, and one of my articles just got picked up on Hacker News. "So how much money does that make you?" - someone at this bbq asked. "You could make a lot with ads."

Until then, I didn't think of "monetizing" my blog in any way. I was paying ~$300/year for hosting via Ghost, and that was it. But this comment got me thinking: could I make enough money just from blog traffic to cover my hosting costs? That could be neat.

In June 2019 I added a small advertising block on my blog, in a way that I hoped would not be intrusive, lacking any dark patterns, and one that was practically invisible on mobile - at the very bottom of the page.

In March of 2022 I removed this piece of advertising for good, alongside any other third-party sponsorships or ads. Still, over 3 years it indeed paid for hosting, and then some more, making ~$3,500 during this time.

I wrote a summary with detailed statistics for anyone interested on how much a 130x100px image can make on a blog or a website. When I was debating whether to insert an ad onto my blog, I would have appreciated a summary like this. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/gKjmS8Zi

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Travel, training and morale spend seems to be frozen across Microsoft, several current employees I talked with confirmed.

I received messages from current Microsoft software engineers sharing how their managers told teams that travel is frozen for the quarter. 1st of July to 30th September counts as Q1 for Microsoft, so the freeze is for at least this duration.

As I poked around more, engineers and managers across different organizations shared that they have the same freeze: from Azure, through GitHub, to Microsoft Research.

All business-critical expenses are frozen effective immediately, across all of Microsoft. 

This means not just travel, but also training and morale budgets, as I confirmed with an engineering manager who received this guidance from their management chain.

This engineering manager shared how the morale budget is a cut their team can feel, as they were just preparing to get together, as things return to “normal.” Still, this person was understanding of the necessity of the freeze, given the uncertain economic environment.

Travel spend freezes include cancelling previously booked conferences. 

I talked with an engineer at Microsoft Research who was told to cancel their conference attendance and save the money on the flight and the hotel, despite losing the non-refundable conference ticket.
A full-remote engineer I talked with was unhappy about the travel spend freeze. This person had been working full-remote for several years, and the occasional travel is how they have connected with teammates. However, their travel request for Q1 was denied. This engineer shared how they think Microsoft wouldn’t have frozen travel before Covid-19, when travel was deemed more necessary for good teamwork.

The aggressive pace of travel spend cutting surprised long-time Microsofties I talked with. One person told me how the pre-2020 Microsoft surely would have not clamped down this much on reducing travel spend.

Microsoft is still hiring, though. I need to add that the company has not frozen hiring across all organizations. As per the Microsoft earnings call on 26th July, when CFO Amy Hood shared how the company expects to hire 11,000 hires globally – not just in tech – and called out Cloud Engineering and LinkedIn as two organizations that will get some of these hires.

Some organizations are no longer allowed to hire for backfills, as I confirmed with engineering managers in two different organizations, both of them product groups. Not allowing for backfills is a strategy to downsize an organization which will be getting significantly less investment, at companies like Microsoft. While it can also be one of the final steps before layoffs, I would hesitate to say that this step presages layoffs.

I cover more on this topic - and other happenings in the tech world - in this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue. Subscribers can read it here: https://lnkd.in/enduVgT5

#microsoft #softwareengineering

text:
I get increasingly more inquiries to mention products in exchange for payments. I do not do this and now have an ethics statement to make this stance clear.

Although I dislike the term 'influencer' - as my view is people should strive for critical thinking, and pay attention to people based on merit, not based on artificial numbers like number of followers, number of likes etc - it's the case that when I post something, there are enough people paying attention that advertisers are starting to take notice.

I personally don't think it's fair game to pretend you recommend something when you get paid for this mention - but not mention that you got paid. Making it clear that you're paid to mention a product is fair. However, it's not a business I want to be in as it's a slippery slope. If I get paid to recommend a product and I do so on LinkedIn or Twitter, would I be just as critical of this product if I write about it in my newsletter? My gut tells me it would create bias.

My main business is The Pragmatic Engineer which is a newsletter that people pay for, and one where those customers expect full independence in exchange for paying. I want to keep independence not just in the newsletter, but outside of it.

So if you see me talk about any company or product, know that I am not paid to do so, links are not affiliates, and - unless I indicate it - I don't have an affiliation with the company. (I am in the process of removing all affiliate links across my blog and YouTube videos)

Read my full ethics statement, including disclosing biases I have and what I'm doing to counter these: https://lnkd.in/eG4G6svW

If you're looking to sponsor or collaborate with people like me, know that I don't do this, but I can recommend publications that do: https://lnkd.in/ePhTaK3B

posted_at.relative: 2 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
"I'm a new grad software engineer and it's hard to get responses to my applications. What advice would you have to land that first job?"

I usually stay away from advice on this field as there are plenty of people and companies specializing in this area. However, I noticed few people are telling new grads the reality of the 2022 tech market: it's the worst one it's been for new grads wanting to enter the software industry in at least a decade.

There are fewer companies hiring for entry-level positions and more people competing for those spots - thanks to a chilly 2020 and 2021 for entry-level positions, but universities and bootcamps graduating students at the same pace.

So here's advice I have to maximise chances of hearing back from applications:

1. Familiarize yourself with the reality of this current market.

VC-funded companies are cutting back hiring - especially new grad hiring - and Big Tech is slowing down. Meta is not extending intern return offers - for the first time in forever - and entry-level positions are the first ones to go in hiring slowdowns. "Traditional" and "boring" companies are still hiring, though!

2. Forget about only applying to 'amazing' companies.

Aim wide when applying. Apply to less competitive companies, including 'unsexy' ones. Apply to local companies, not just remote ones. Apply to consultancies/developer agencies as well. Know that almost no company will sponsor visas for entry-level positions.

3. Improve your resume while you keep applying

Build your experience while you are job hunting. Contribute to open source in non-trivial ways. For more ideas to improve your profile and chances, see unfiltered advice from Raymond Gan (a bootcamp engineer who broke into the industry and is now a senior engineer) and see his featured posts on his LinkedIn profile.

4. Don't be picky with offers

If you only have one offer: take it. It's more important that you get started over getting a perfect start. 

5. A note to hiring managers

Now is a great time to hire new grads. So do this: if you have more than one or two headcount to fill, make space for a new grad hire. Your team and your budget will both thank you: and you will likely make a large difference in the career of the new grad you hire.

Read more advice in a longer post I wrote here: https://lnkd.in/es4ZT35S

#career #newgrad #jobsearchtips #softwareengineering

text:
"Which companies pay for oncall, and how much do they compensate?" After weeks of gathering data, I'm happy to publish information on more than 80 companies. Top "paying" places for oncall:

My thinking on compensating oncall has shifted over the years. At Microsoft/Skype, Skyscanner and Uber, oncall was never paid: it was just part of the job. I always assumed this is how it is. (Note that these days Skyscanner pays for oncall)

However, by making it part of the job, it's easy to institutionalize a poor oncall culture - like eg the case right now at Twilio or Amazon - and it shows that there's no respect for the outside working hours.

By paying for oncall, companies now have to budget for this time spent, and the managers handling the budgets are incentivized to minimise the number of people oncall, with the same reliability.

When not paying for oncall, there is no such incentive. And most of Big Tech does not pay for oncall - save for Google. In what might not be a surprise, Google seems to have the healthiest oncall setup across all of Big Tech.

Read the monthly, free issue of The Pragmatic Engineer for a much longer list on how much companies pay, and which ones don't compensate at all, and why. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/eHgJt8GM

#oncall #softwareengineering #engineeringmanagement #compensation

text:
How would you respond if you learned that despite your VC-funded company doing poorly, five recently joined execs to sell $32M in stocks, while long-standing employees who built the business were not given such an option? It's exactly what happened at DataRobot:

The company was not doing well, and employees were disappointed with promises from management that never came true - such as being close to an IPO, or the valuation of the company to further increase.

Then, in what would be a damning article, The Information published details on how recently joined executives were allowed to sell stocks in the tens of millions.

What happened was not what these executives likely expected, and led to the DataRobot CEO having to step down a few days ago.

So, what can founders hoping to cash out via secondary stock sales learn from this event? It's that if you do sales like this, but you don't offer it for emloyees, you will see similar backlash, people resigning, and will have a real hard time hiring.

Also, my view is that such secret sales are immoral, so do it at your own risk.

Read the full story with more details in this issue of The Pragmatic Engineer open to everyone: https://lnkd.in/e4pCu4FT

#equity #datarobot #stock #advice

text:
I was grudgingly on the phone to cancel my online newspaper subscription for Boston Globe Media as there's no other way to do so. The agent then says: "Sir, you cannot cancel the first 24 hours. Your account is not synced to the customer support system."

I had signed up a few hours earlier to The Boston Globe to read a news article. I make a point to pay for all my online subscriptions, and then cancel ones where I don't find further value in them.

The article in question wasn't what I was looking for, so I looked for how to cancel my now recurring monthly subscription from next month. Well, the only way to do so is by calling a US phone number over US business hours.

Ok. I'm not happy to waste time on this, but I do it.

I wait through the usual "all our agents are busy" and then annoying music, and get through to the agent.

Then, the agent tells me they cannot cancel my subscription in the first 24 hours, and says I need to call back another day, wait my turn and then maybe, just maybe, they can cancel.

You know what? I'm not calling back because you deliberately make it impossible to cancel a monthly transaction. If I get another charge for next month, I'm doing a chargeback on my credit card. I emailed this as my subscription cancellation. How many hoops does one need to jump to cancel a subscription they don't want from next month?

I really want to support local newspapers and media, but I will not support ones that employ dark pattern upon dark pattern, making it impossible for customers to cancel the subscription the same way that they signed up.

On the flip side, for my publication, I aim to give exactly this experience: cancel anytime, with a click. I go further, and offer a 30-days retroactive full refund, with a no-questions-asked policy. So far, three people have asked for such a refund, and my philosophy is that if a customer is not happy, I want to make it easy for them to get their money back, even if they have consumed my publication.

It's the difference on how I put paying customers and their experiences first, and traditional newspapers like The Boston Globe put paying subscribers as last.

Remind me why traditional newspapers are dying.

#media #bostonglobe #cancellation

text:
How much money can you make with Amazon affiliate links? As I'm retiring all such links from my blog and YouTube, here's some data. This year - in ~6.5 months - I made $5,225. And some details:

The $5,225 was a result of generating ~$120-130K in purchases on Amazon, the vast majority of these software engineering book purchases. The affiliate revenue came from at least 57,800 clicks (as per GeniusLink). ~75% of these clicks came from my blog (about 44,000 clicks), ~10% from my YouTube channel, and the rest was not attirbuted.

For Amazon affiliate links, I use Geniuslink which takes care of mapping a link to the right regional Amazon store. Amazon has about ~20 countries they operate stores in (like amazon .com, .de, .nl, .jp). As an affiliate, you need to register separate for each of these stores - I'm registered with around 14. 

Then, you need to direct visitors to the "right" link. Amazon has their own service called OneLink which supports 5 regions and I've observed it was very flakey for outside US visitors when I tested it over a VPN. GeniusLink costs $5/month and works seamlessly with all regions, and gives me some nice stats like number of clicks, alerts on broken links or out of stock Amazon products.

Those 44,000 clicks from my blog resulted from ~893K visitors and 1.4M page views this year. Important to note that most of my pages don't have book recommendations. My book recommendations page saw ~29K clicks this year (https://lnkd.in/dft_m-n)

Of the clicks, 31% came from the US, but those clicks accounted for 53% of all revenue ($2,800 out of the $5,225).

Why am I retiring a pretty respectable revenue source, and on track to bring in potentially ~$10K of revenue in 2022?

It's the same reason I don't do sponsorships, and why I retired ads. As I write The Pragmatic Engineer I want to stay an independent voice in the tech world. While affiliates are mostly harmless - I only recommended things I liked - they can create an incentive for me to recommend things that will pay more in affiliate fees; to not recommend things with no affiliates; and to prefer linking to Amazon, instead of directly to the publisher.

I also observe many " influencers " sharing affiliate links without ever disclosing that these are affiliates, making them money. I want to remove any guesswork from any of my recommendations: they are never motivated by financial gains. Hence, all affiliates are going.

Read more about my ethics statement here: https://lnkd.in/eG4G6svW

I do think affiliates are a good earning channel for many people, and I hope sharing these numbers helps those people with more data.

#affiliates #business #enterpreneurship

text:
A major reason I am anti "web3", crypto, DeFi and NFTs is because so much of the money in the space comes from everyday people who are non-accredited investors (aka not rich)... and money they cannot afford to lose. And then they lose it.

These people fall for false advertising, and even if they do deep research, they are unable to notice what are unregulated offerings - one which is often a ponzi scheme.

Close to $5B has been put into Celsius, a scheme that promised up to 18% yields for crypto deposited with them. They advertised this as a completely safe and legit setup.

Some of my own friends put in a large part of their savings into this scheme. It didn't matter that I told them that if something sounds too good to be true - like 18% yield farming - then it probably is.

Well, it was, indeed too good to be true, and Celsius is turning out to be yet another unsustainable scheme. A scheme that has now collapsed, leaving all investors with their investments inaccessible.

Most of "web3" and DeFi riches come from new entrants putting in money, and providing liquidity for existing members. All of this is unregulated.

If you, a friend, or a family member thinks it's safe to put your money in yet another crypto or DeFi-based scheme promising guaranteed returns to beat inflation: you're likely the sucker and have a good chance of losing your money.

Here are letters that Celsius Network investors are sending to this case's judge. Read more of these heartbreaking letters collected by Molly White at https://lnkd.in/eShpuRUE. 

And show them to any of your friends investing in similar unregulated - and most likely fraudulent - schemes. They could be the next ones to write similar letters.

I also vote with my feet in not promoting "web3": I do not take any form of money from crypto companies: not sponsorships, not job adverts, and not in my talent collective. I turned down a sizeable amount of revenue this way already.

#crypto #celsius #bankruptcy #defi

The cooling systems could not keep up with the heat in the London locations and these data centers needed to be taken offline. There is no ETA neither for Oracle, nor for Google on when they'll be back online.

1. Reduce my own and my family's carbon footprint as we can. Use public transport and bikes where we can over cars.

3. Support businesses that are carbon neutral or carbon negative, voting with my wallet.

text:
If you are a startup and are copying Big Tech in how you do hiring or on-call practices: chances are, you are doing it wrong.

Big Tech is a place that has some of the most unhealthy on-call practices and can get away with it. 

I speak from experience: while at Uber, although, as a manager I wanted to prioritize better on-call, for years we had terrible on-calls. The incentives were always about shipping impact, and, frankly, we paid too well for people to quit over on-call, and our team did not mind the high on-call load. No single person on my team quit because of on-call load, despite the intensity of it.

If you asked me back then: I also did not mind, and I felt I was fairly compensated, overall. But try to do what we did at any other place that does not pay as well as Big Tech or doesn't have the brand: and you'd see huge attrition.

My advice is: don't copy Big Tech on-call practices. Aim for better.

Linked some such better examples in the comments.

#oncall #softwareengineering #engineeringmanagement

text:
Bloomberg reported today "Apple to Slow Hiring and Spending for Some Teams Next Year". More than two weeks ago, I shared the same scoop with The Pragmatic Engineer subscribers:

Bloomberg wrote: "Apple Inc. plans to slow hiring and spending growth next year in some divisions to cope with a potential economic downturn." in this article: https://lnkd.in/ew4cWECm

I wrote along the same lines two weeks ago in https://lnkd.in/epMzPeyF (see the image below).

However, my sources tell me the slowdown comes not next year, but from the fall, because Apple's financial year ends in September.

What does this change mean?

Only Amazon is hiring as "normal" across Big Tech. And personally, I am unsure if even this will last. As I wrote in the latest The Pragmatic Engineer issue on Thursday:

"Where in Big Tech are there no hiring freezes? The two notable companies where hiring is continuing as normal are Amazon and Apple. However, Apple might be slowing down hiring from September, though I confirmed this to be the case in only a few organizations. Still, it would be a surprise if, in the face of high inflation and economic uncertainty, Apple does not take a more conservative view with hiring, across the board.

This past week, Google announced a hiring slowdown, Oracle a plan to cut back on costs – and is laying off heavily – Microsoft did make cuts, and Facebook employees are worried the company will push up attrition.

We’re now in the situation where many Big Tech companies have slowed down hiring: Google, Facebook, Microsoft, Twitter, Netflix, Tesla are in this group, and Apple will likely join them in September. The only major company where hiring is as before is Amazon.

If you are planning to move into Big Tech or switch jobs, now might be the time to make that move. I would not be surprised if all of Big Tech slows hiring by the end of the year, out of caution. This will mean more competition for fewer openings, and likely only jobs for more experienced software engineers, engineering leaders and other tech professionals."

#hiring #bigtech #apple #amazon

reshared_post.text:
The biggest story in tech right now is the depth and breadth of layoffs.

And the best coverage is coming from a former engineer named Gergely Orosz who writes a newsletter called The The Pragmatic Engineer Engineer.

It’s a reminder of how much the media landscape has transformed. People waiting for traditional news organizations to keep them informed are missing out.

Orosz started his newsletter 10 months ago focusing on practical — though in-the-weeds — stories such as best practices for shipping code or why Big Tech doesn’t use Agile.

But as the layoffs piled up in April he was quick to recognize their significance and capitalize on sources he’d developed who were on the front lines. What’s striking is that he breaks news and provides context in a way people usually expect from journalists.

You can see the impact in the accompanying chart of subscriber growth.

The layoff story is huge because it is the first time engineers are being targeted since tech went on a hiring tear a decade ago.

Orosz is emblematic of a new phenomenon on the web: someone who isn’t a trained journalist but has news judgment, sources, deep knowledge and perspective.

In the past, reporters called people like Orosz to get the story. Now he publishes directly.

A great example was his story this week about Bitpanda, an Austrian based crypto exchange backed by Peter Thiel, which fired 30% of its staff two weeks ago.

He noticed a LinkedIn post by the company’s former social media manager complaining that Bitpanda was threatening to sue him for a social media rant.

Given the slump in Bitcoin, it’s not surprising a crypto company is laying off people. What is surprising is that they are suing former employees for social media posts.

This is the type of story the Wall Street Journal or Bloomberg or the Financial Times will publish as a “cautionary tale” feature in a week or two.

Orosz already has it covered. He talked to the manager and labeled the move a SLAPP (strategic lawsuit against public participation). He anticipated the Streisand Effect would prompt Bitpanda to cave.

Bitpanda noticed Orosz’s coverage and reached out to explain that they had to threaten the suit. Orosz countered telling Bitpanda he would cover the manager’s legal costs.

Bitpanda backed down. Game. Set. Match.

This isn’t journalism exactly, but it is a huge story.

Tech firms everywhere are muddling through layoffs and often doing a bad job. You can be sure the lawyers and HR departments in tech companies have taken note.

No one wants to be the next Bitpanda.

As a postscript, Bitpanda apologized to the manager they threatened to sue and invited him to provide feedback to the company.

The manager’s takeaway: “This shows you never have to fear companies. You have the world at your back, always. If ever in a situation like this, come back to these posts and remember we came out on top.”

Read The Pragmatic Engineer post about Bitpanda here: https://lnkd.in/g7vBdFBM

text:
One US dollar is worth 1 EUR. The last time this was the case was 20 years ago, after the Dotcom bust ended. What impact will this have on tech? One important one for sure:

US companies hiring software engineers as remote will find hiring in Europe - and especially in Western Europe - far more affordable. Imagine hiring an experienced software engineer who worked at well-known tech companies or high-growth startups and can work at scale, for ~$90-130K/year. Well, if offering €90-130K*, you'll have plenty of candidates, and if you go higher you can find staff-level folks as well. Just a year ago, €90-130K meant $112-$162K/year - quote the difference in cost versus now!

This also means that companies that offer location-independent salaries in US dollars will see more interest from Europe, thanks to the EUR weakened. And companies that raised their funding in USD will see their hiring budgets go further in EUR.

Basically, with remote work and a weaker EUR, we could well see even more US companies growing teams in Europe.

On the other hand, European companies with EUR funding will find it more expensive to hire in the US and expanding their market and engineering teams there will likely slow down.

*as a caveat, don't forget about the employer-only costs that vary based on countries in Europe, and are comparable to, but often higher than the 401K costs. More details here:  https://lnkd.in/gxue8Cz7

What are your predictions on how the weaker EUR will impact the tech market?

#eur #usd #currency #hiring

text:
Here's what a company trapped in a downward spiral looks like. UK-based events startup Pollen raised $150M in April, laid off a third of staff in May, and missed payroll for all of staff in June. Now they turned off the company Slack.

The justification is how leadership is sad about how some employees have been talking with the press - after the company has failed to pay them their salary! When the company explained why they are not paying, they explained that they are in the final round of negotiating a deal or acquisition with a partner they cannot name, and have no money in the bank for salaries until this deal is signed.

People laid off in May have still not received their severance or pension contributions. The company is not paying its vendors, who are now also publicly complaining.

What is clear if if this deal is delayed any longer, the company will go bankrupt, despite having raised money barely two months ago.

Sadly, we might see more companies making massive losses and fully dependent on raising more funding go bust, or get bought on fire sale - one of which will definitely happen to Pollen.

Also, advice to founders and C-level: if you break your side of the contract - like not paying a salary, or laying people off out of the blue - you should expect that these people, without any other options, talk with the press, in the hopes that writing about this publicly will warn others of the company, instead of keeping all of this private.

#startups #economy

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
A week ago, Mark Zuckerberg said that Meta would "turn up the heat" on perf reviews. This quote was picked up by the media. But is there anything to it? I talked with current Facebook engineers to find out:

After talking with close to a dozen engineers, an engineering managers, a few things are clear:

1. "The media has over-blown this one sentence fragment."

Everyone I talked with told me that they feel the media is doing what is common: take a sentence out of context, and zoom in on it. In this case, that sentence was “turning up the heat”.

2. “I like that Zuck is straightforward.” 

I heard this several times. Many engineers appreciate that Zuck did not resort to corporate-style fluffy talk, but spoke his mind and didn’t dilute what he had to say.

3. "Important context is missing on the Adjusted Expectations Program"

Meta put in place an Adjusted Expectations starting in 2020, which allowed people to keep their jobs while performing below what was expected of them. This allows people to opt out of the normal performance review program, and set goals instead that they meet. The catch is that they cannot get above "Meets Expectations" if they choose this path. Zuck referred to this program, according to several people I talked with (see also the screenshot)

4. My take:

Zuck said out loud what many CEOs keep to themselves. We have rapidly gone from the most-heated job market less than a year ago, to the tech boom being over. Many people are still in denial about how radically the tech job market has changed. 

Facebook is a pretty stable place to be in, in the middle of this market turmoil. They are not laying off, even as profits drop, and Zuck is likely a key reason why FB never did layoffs in its history.

The market has changed. A lot. Now is not the time to ask for more money, and/or less work; not even at Facebook.

Read more about this in the latest The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/gYgfsswQ

#facebook #meta #bigtech #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What happens when you try to measure developer productivity that comes with out-of-the-box metrics like Pluralsight Flow? Laura Tacho rolled out GitPrime - the predecessor of this tool - to her company and observed this:

In this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue she wrote about her journey on measuring developer productivity, and a step on this was rolling out GitPrime. In her words:

"I fell into the trap of relying on other metrics in the tool; either things I hadn’t thought to measure yet, or proprietary calculated metrics like “impact,” to give me signals about my team’s performance. But when I did this, I let a group of people who didn’t know anything about my team’s goals make a judgment call about how these developers should work. I felt bad that our “weekly impact” stat wasn’t going up week after week. 

Some of the out-of-the-box metrics directly contradicted our culture. We spent a lot of time prototyping and used feature flags heavily. This meant our code churn was high, which negatively influenced some metrics like “impact.” And since we started staffing projects with 2 or more engineers whenever possible, it was true – and intentional – that the same groups of people were reviewing each other’s PRs. That often showed up as a red flag in these tools.

Knowing what your metrics incentivize is so important, and I learned the hard way not to let someone else make that decision"

Moving on from DORA and artificial metrics like "impact" in GitPrime, Laura came across the SPACE framework:

"By learning about SPACE, I began to shift my focus from developer productivity to developer effectiveness. A team which is highly motivated, satisfied, works with tooling which enables them to ship quickly, and consistently delivers quality software is a highly effective team. The flow from code to cloud is fairly frictionless. Developer tooling is an important aspect of this environment, but it’s not the only one. Project management processes, interpersonal relationships, company policies, and work environments add a fair amount of weight."

If you're also looking into measuring developer productivity, I also strongly suggest reading the SPACE paper, as it opens a more realistic - and also more nuanced - view on developer productivity. https://lnkd.in/gURhMMti

If you're hoping to have one single metric that will tell you how productive your team is: you'll be disappointed. But if you want to get better at getting a pulse on how productive your team is amongst various dimensions that matter: you'll want to dive deeper.

Subscribers also have access to the full article on Measuring software engineering productivity at https://lnkd.in/gsnrVhqF

#productivity #engineeringmanagement #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Why do most "digital transformation" projects fail in making companies "tech-first"? It's because they don't change how engineering is viewed as a cost center and not a profit center. Take Visa as an example.

Is Visa a "tech-first" company? They *do* make most of their profits thanks to technology - hardware and software - that powers the card network. However, tech-first companies treat engineering as a profit center. Is this the case with Visa?

I talked with several current and former tech employees and they all shared that while leadership is vocal about wanting to change the mindset to becoming a “tech-first” company, engineering is treated like a cost center across most of the organization.

To be fair, there are pockets of organizations within Visa that operate more as profit centers. Ones employees mentioned are Visa Direct, Visa Installments Services, Verified by Visa, Visa Risk Manager and Visa Direct. However, the company still overwhelmingly treats engineering as a service function: engineering autonomy is relatively low, and although the company has a product management function, project managers still frequently direct engineers.

And can we blame Visa for not changing its mindset? They are ridiculously profitable. They have been successful for decades. If it’s not broken, why fix it?

text:
Profit centers vs cost centers in tech: which one are you working in? Here are some of my thoughts on what "cost centers" look like and signs this might be the case.

A time I wish I had known about the differences was when I joined JP Morgan in 2011. The offer was good, and the company boasted about how they are becoming a tech-first company, which I understood as "tech is the most important thing here".

How wrong I was. Tech stayed a cost center.

The "profit center" for JP Morgan was the traders and everything to do with the front office, where the company made money. Tech was part of IT, and I could not even get approvals to install a tool that would help refactor my code. Our role was execution based on what the business told us to do. I can't complain about compensation, but I noticed that it was only at ED or MD (Executive Director or Managing Director) where people would be awarded stock as part of their compensation - these were rumors.

I say I wish I had known about these terms as it made sense why I never felt "home" at the company, missing the autonomy and having a say in how we build things. Overnight, things changed with a snap as soon as I joined Skype where it felt that tech was "running the show". We had so much more autonomy, and got involved with product, user research, and building the roadmap. I was now working at a company where engineering was treated as a profit center. And yes, every engineer got equity - though this was post-Microsoft acquisition - and even though small-time owners, but we were owners in the company.

If you've worked (or work) at a place where tech, engineering or your team is more of a cost center, what other signs have you seen that this is the case?

#costcenter #softwareengineering #career #bigtech #startups

---

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Instead of building systems - similar to how buildings are constructed, following a well-defined plan+phases - we should grow them, having a working system evolving every day. It’s how modern teams operate today.

Incredibly, this was written ~30 years ago in the Mythical Man-Month, in the 20th, Anniversary Edition published in 1995.

As proof to it truth to this thought, CI/CD and shipping multiple times a day result in overall faster shipping than anything else even today. The book Accelerate confirmed that "elite" teams operate like this. My experience through the last decade was also in-line with this: engineering teams that opted for the "well-thought out building" approach ended up executing slower than those "growing" software with modern tools.

I am surprised how parts of The Mythical Man-Month that are deducted off first principles - like the No Silver Bullet chapter - held up exceptionally well.

I started reading this book as I was doubtful there's much relevant to learn from a book originally published in 1975. While several parts are out of date, it's striking to see ones that hold just as true today as they did when the book was written, in the age of mainframes, no internet, no personal computers, no version control and no CI/CD.

#book #softwareengineering #softwaredevelopment #practices

text:
What would you do if your company did large layoffs last month, and this month they couldn't pay your salary? This is not a theoretical question: it's what is happening to hundreds of employees at Pollen right now.

UK-based travel and entertainment technology company that brands itself as a tech-first company laid off a third of its staff on the 10th of May. For those remaining, they told them to expect to receive salaries on the last day of the month, going forward, not on the 25th as it's common in the UK.

On June 30th, those salaries did no arrive. The same day the company sent a Slack message to employees that read:

"We are now on a the cusp of closing a deal (with a new partner) that will replace Sienna as our main investor and shareholder. (...)

We anticipated finishing earlier in the week but (...) shareholders are only just releasing the next funding installment which means that payroll is delayed and everyone will receive their paychecks tomorrow rather than today."

The CEO later wrote to all staff:

"We are closing a transaction with a large, well known entertainment company. (...) I am working hard to finalize negotiations with our shareholders as fast as possible and get them to wire funds. I am really sorry about the inconvenience and anxiety has caused all of you. I have expressed this to my shareholders but ultimately their priority remains ensuring their position is protected and maximising their own return. (...)

This is a very a-typical situation and (...) I assure you this will never happen again and apologize again that you have been impacted by this negotiation, I did all I could void that being the case."

I am told laid off employees have also not been paid their last months' salary, and several months of pension contributions.

Employees I talked with tell me they are deeply disappointed with leadership both not being transparent, and how it feels that it all hangs on this supposed deal and how their salaries paid out come as a second priority to shareholders maximising their own return.

My take is that, sadly, we will likely see more situations like this going ahead as some companies either struggle to secure new funding - and might have to close shop, like Fast has (wrote an in-depth overview of what happened at Fast here: https://lnkd.in/gcz4Rqeu - or are at the mercy of the new investors transferring funds, those investors moving much slower - and being far more picky in investing and in how much - in the current market environment.

Update: salaries also won't arrive on 1st July. The company is said to be organizing emergency payments so people can cover expenses over the weekend.

#pollen #startups #salary #economy

---

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What is "great", "normal" and "bad" attrition at various companies for tech teams? I asked ~30 engineering leaders and Dominic Jacquesson. Here's what I learned:

1. Above 20-25% attrition is bad.

Every manager working at any company categorized such attrition as "bad", from seed-stage startups, through scaleups to developer agencies.

2. The stage of the company matters.

At the early stage of the company, attrition is typically lower. Peak attrition usually comes around Series B and "hypergrowth". Mature companies tend to have lower attrition.

3. What about developer agencies?

I expected developer agencies to have higher attrition than most other companies, but this turned out to not be the case. Attrition numbers for "great", "normal" and "bad" are not to different than e.g. Series B companies.

4. Adjustment factors

There are plenty of things that can increase attrition - and some will decrease it. Economic cycles (e.g. the hot market of 2021 or the cooldown right now), annual cycles, regional differences, the "tier" of the company and many others make a difference.

5. Events that impact attrition

Lots of events can increase attrition! Like forced return to the office, key leaders leaving, negative events, poor public image and others.

6. Remote work and attrition

Companies that go remote typically see a drop in attrition, according to Marissa Goldberg, founder at Remote Work Prep.

However, remote work is no longer the "silver bullet" as it was before 2020. Back in 2016-2019, companies offering full-remote work like Doist, Zapier or Buffer saw extremely low attrition. This changed starting from 2020, as full-remote work is more common.

I wrote a lot more about attrition, and advice on how to get ahead of attrition in this week's issue of The Pragmatic Engineer. Read it here: 🔒 https://lnkd.in/gHNmtjWu

#hiring #attrition #bigtech #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
We've heard about a lot of layoffs in tech. However, no mass layoff was done in secrecy this year... until now, that is. Tesla fired 10% of staff and is trying to cover it up.

I talked with Tesla software engineers in California who were let go, to understand what happened. Here's the short of it:

Tesla terminated ~10% of its workforce and made it look like these are performance-related layoffs. But it was actually a mass layoff happening in one go. I talked with three software engineers who all exited on Wednesday, 14 June. The setup was the same in all cases:

- The manager of the employee read from a script when terminating the employment, saying they were terminating based on performance.
- These meetings were done in secret, in the sense only the affected employee and their manager knew of the layoff.
- Employees had five business days to accept the severance package, which is one week only. Compare this with both Klarna and Coinbase’s offerings of at least 14 weeks-worth of severance.
- Managers were not allowed to say the layoffs were en masse; to do so risked their own position.

Tesla seems to be trying to bypass the WARN act in California in order to avoid paying severance. This act requires the employer to pay 60 days of salary and benefits if it does not give at least 60 days notice before a mass layoff. Tesla failed to give notice, yet is laying off in bulk.

Employees being laid off now are in a difficult situation. Officially, they were let go because of performance issues. In reality, they were let go because Tesla decided to fire 10% of employees. But their reference check will not confirm this. As a result, Tesla employees have taken the sharing of facts into their own hands. Just search for "Tesla 10%" on LinkedIn and you'll see the dozens of posts from laid off Tesla employees and see what I mean.

The software engineers I talked with were in disbelief, but also very worried because Tesla is providing little to no benefit. One week’s severance is paltry; it’s practically nothing. Those I talked with are in touch with employment lawyers to see if they can legally challenge the decision. 

A lawsuit was filed on Sunday, 19th June, by several former employees, alleging Tesla violated law by not providing advance notice of the layoffs. Employees are referring to the WARN act in this lawsuit.

I'm awarding the worst mass layoff award of 2022 to Tesla both for the 1-week severance, and for trying to do mass layoffs under the disguise of performance-related layoffs.

For anyone interested in more details, I wrote more about this topic in this week's The Scoop issue published in The Pragmatic Engineer.

#tesla #layoffs #bigtech #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What companies use an RFC-like planning process (request for comments) and why? Here are just a few companies that follow this approach:

Many Big Tech companies and high-growth startups have ended up with a process similar to Uber’s, and settle on a form of the Design Document or RFC process.

Here’s how it usually works:

1. Product Requirement Documents (PRDs)

These are commonly run side by side with an engineering design document. This is because if product managers don’t specify what they’d like the team to build, it’s likely the company doesn’t have a writing culture.

2. RFCs or Design Docs are written by engineers for non-trivial projects, before they start meaningful work. There are usually no hard rules around this. These documents are written to share context, the suggested approach, tradeoffs, and to invite feedback.

The goal of writing and sending out such a document is to decrease the time taken to complete a project, by getting key feedback early. It’s down to the engineers working on the project to decide how an RFC fits into their workflow. Here are a few different approaches I’ve seen, depending on the project:

- Prototype, then RFC, then build. This is typical for projects with lots of unknowns – like building on a new framework – which can be quickly answered with prototyping. The RFC shares a more complete plan, perhaps with some gaps. The team gets feedback, then builds.

- RFC, wait for feedback, then build. For projects that have lots of dependencies – or many other teams are dependent on what’s being built – it might result in faster progress overall to wait for all the feedback from other teams.

- RFC, then start building while awaiting feedback. For projects with some complexity, but when the team is pretty certain there won’t be many questions, it’s pragmatic to start building right as they send out the RFC. The team can easily incorporate feedback, and even if they need to make changes, not much time is wasted.

- Build, then write an RFC. In my observation, this pattern is fairly common. Engineers build what they need to. Then they decide to write an RFC, retroactively documenting the decisions made. This might be because the project got more complex than planned, or to comply with RFC “rules.” It’s an awkward situation, as the team rarely wants to get feedback, they just do a tick box exercise. But it can be useful to have a document for historic reasons, and getting feedback is still better than the alternative.

See examples of public RFC documents here: https://lnkd.in/gcXuNwVR

And read more about good practices and tradeoffs with RFC processes in this week's subscriber-only The Pragmatic Engineer post: https://lnkd.in/gAe66q65

#softwareengineering #planning #rfc #bigtech #startups

---

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
At the risk of stating the obvious: A competent software engineer can pick up a new framework rapidly - when familiar w the language - and learn to use a new programming language quickly.

If you hire for framework or language knowledge, you are missing out on a *lot* of people who would be great addition to the team, and would bring valuable experience from another domain.

Let me give an example: at Uber, we hired software engineers who were familiar with any object oriented language on the backend. Backend engineers in my team would write code in Node.JS, Python, Go and Java the same week. We hired a person who mostly did C# before, and they picked up all of this quickly.

A new grad engineer whom I hired for an iOS position had zero iOS experience and never used a Mac before - they only developed in Java before. In a month, they shipped iOS apps using Objective C and Swift - with support from the team.

I remember how, for a long time, I thought that picking up a new language at work was a "big deal". This was until we had a manager calibration meeting where we had to answer the question:

"A Software Engineer 2 (a level above the entry-level, but a level below the Senior level) moved teams and had never done Go before. On their new team, they picked up Go in 2 months, and are now shipping at the speed of everyone else. What performance review would they get? Below average, average or above average?"

The answer was "average". This situation was extremely common - of someone picking up a new language when they needed to - and also expected from this level upwards.

I always preferred both working at places that looked to software engineers as people who can learn new languages quickly, and also ones that encouraged this and gave support and time to do so. E.g. when someone switched languages on my team, I'd make it clear that of course, this takes time, and they will be slower initially and can get whatever resources they'd like, and learn during working hours.

An important caveat: of course, you *need* specialists - people with e.g. deep language knowledge and experience - to give feedback, and help people picking these up. It helps if you have strong tooling in place, platform teams, and other support systems. But you most likely don't need every single hire to a specialist!

#softwareengineering #bigtech #startups

---

text:
The past week, several crypto exchanges have done large layoffs. But why? Here's a visual explanation that can be applied outside crypto as well:

OpenSea is the largest NFT (non-fungible token) marketplace where transactions are mostly settled in ETH (Ethereum). OpenSea is seeing a stable number of NFTs sold on the platform; around 1.4-1.6M per month. However, the fees OpenSea realizes on these transactions are down from about $200M a month, to about $80M forecast for June, thanks to the drop in ETH prices: (see image)

Such a drop in revenue would be bad news for OpenSea, which was valued at $13.3B at its last fundraise in January. We can also assume that similar drops are happening revenue-wise to most crypto trading platforms.

Other crypto trading platforms have announced layoffs. This is most likely due to revenue drops similar to what OpenSea is seeing, plus the gloomy outlook on the crypto market. Companies announcing layoffs include:

- Gemini, a crypto trading platform, announced it was laying off 10% of staff on Thursday, 2nd June.
- Crypto .com, a crypto trading platform announced it was letting go about 5% of its workforce (~260 people) on Friday, 10th of June. 
- BlockFi, another cryptocurrency trading platform, announced it was laying off 20% of its workforce (~200 people) on Monday, 13 June.
- Coinbase, a crypto trading platform announced it is laying off 18% of staff on Tuesday, 14th June
- Bitso, a crypto exchange in Mexico, let go about 80 employees (10% of staff) at the end of May
- BitMex, another crypto exchange focused on derivatives, laid off 25% of its staff (~75 people) in early April this year.

There is a lesson to be taken here: while a volatile market can be good for exchanges the short term - more trading, thus more revenue - a bear market is bad news - even with the same trading, they collect less revenues due to prices going down.

Read more about the Crypto bust, struggles at Coinbase and how Twitter is rolling out Agile in the latest, subscriber-only issue of The Scoop in The Pragmatic Engineer: 🔒https://lnkd.in/gte8VJqe

#bigtech #startups

text:
Just how bad is the tech market? LinkedIn has no shortage of posts reciting every layoff happening. But what about the companies not laying off, but hiring? I dug into this, and here's the data:

1. Data from layoffs .fyi.

The site layoffs .fyi offers a handy comparison, starting with data from 2020. Looking at this, it seems like we’re seeing fewer layoffs than in Spring 2020, when COVID-19 struck, global lockdowns kicked off, and many businesses decided to take this drastic measure. Looking at this chart, it feels we’re seeing a similar layoffs trend as at the beginning of the pandemic.

2. The number of jobs posted on the monthly Hacker News “who is hiring”.

This data source does not suggest a worrying trend. Hacker News is one of the most popular sites among software engineers looking for interesting, tech-related things to read. The site is operated by startup accelerator Y Combinator and has a monthly open thread called “Who is Hiring.” 

Job posts on this thread overwhelmingly from startups and venture-funded companies, and some are from publicly traded tech companies. Using this script, I grabbed the data and visualized how the number of postings on the site has changed over the years.

In May 2020, the number of jobs posted hit a bottom of 540 vacancies. In June 2021, it hit a peak of 1,041 jobs posted. In May 2022, the number of jobs posted was almost identical to in January 2021.
Based on this data source, the jobs being posted now are indeed down, the decline does not look dramatic.

3. The number of tech jobs posted last months, globally.

This source shows no visible drop over the past month. I got in touch with DQ Market, a matchmaking platform connecting developers with fast-scaling engineering teams. Their team started a project to track the number of jobs posted for software engineers, worldwide. They do this by scraping the major job boards and aggregators like LinkedIn, Indeed and other job boards. I got access to some early results.

Looking at these numbers, there might be a slowdown in hiring, but it's not nearly as dramatic as all the layoff posts imply. And in the latest, subscriber issue of The Pragmatic Engineer, I went into detail on what types of companies are more likely to lay off and why. Read it here: 🔒 https://lnkd.in/gYuQCNhB

#layoffs #tech #data #hiring

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What are some good engineering planning documents (e.g. design docs, RFCs, ERDs, ADRs) that you have seen?

Engineering planning and design documents are this interesting area with software engineering where I've observed two extremes with people:

1. Those who worked at a place with a "design docs" culture and got to see the upsides - like more transparency, and feedback from more people - alongside the tradeoffs - like when not to write such a doc, or how focusing too much on the docs can slow down some projects.

2. Those who never worked at such a place, and assume that writing a doc before writing a single line of code is a waste of time.

I've also observed that above a certain size - say, 100-200 engineers - companies that have no culture of writing down planning decisions start to have problems in growing the organization while keeping the quality bar. This is the point where a "chief architect" can no longer oversee what all teams do, and efforts for e.g. architecture committees - in-person, of course, as there are no docs! - and similar ones start to introduce an uncomfortable amount of bureaucracy.

What are good examples of these documents you've seen? What structure did they follow? I am sharing some in the comments.

#planning #softwareengineering #rfc

text:
I’m starting a new position as Advisor at DX. DX is building the world’s first developer experience management platform and I'm excited to work with Abi Noda, Greyson Junggren, Brook Perry, and the rest of the team.

Developer productivity has been an illusive problem for decades - it's never been a better time to figure out how to make this space easier to work with.

text:
Companies, where engineers write & send project updates to internal audiences, are places software engineers get to learn to communicate on the job. Uber was a place like this and it's something I really appreciated.

An example of such an update - similar to ones people on my teams sent out.

At Uber, most team-level projects were led by software engineers, and each team had full autonomy to decide how they get things done.

On my team, while not mandatory, I recommended project leads to put a weekly, short report together on how things are going. I noticed that projects which did this ended up being more focused, caught issues and misunderstandings earlier (often thanks to the report), and generally, shipped more frequently and overall faster.

The dirty secret I rarely told people was how *writing* this report helped *the team* more than anyone else. Writing these reports would bring up risks not everyone was aware of, and force team members to be honest on how things really are going.

Others reading it was just a bonus - but contrary to what many people would assume, it was not really the main goal.

Here is an example of such a project report. Feel free to use it for inspiration.

Have you done a similar approach of summarizing how a project is going, and sharing it out to those involved in the project, directly or indirectly?

text:
An unpleasant thing about running your business is how some customers will be unhappy with what they buy and - understandably - complain. My approach is to give a full refund to anyone voicing disappointment.

I do this when we're talking about digital services or goods where there are no material or unrecoverable costs involved. It makes for a "clean" business practice.

Nine months into my newsletter - The Pragmatic Engineer - I had the first unhappy customer who bought an annual subscription, but two months in, they said the content is different to what they expected and they don't get the value they hoped they would. I refunded the full amount as, putting myself in their place, that's the only thing I would deem acceptable. 

I listen to feedback, sometimes act on it - when it makes sense - but my principle is to not argue with the customer.

Having a "direct" link with customers makes it so much easier to do "clean business" in the sense of I have full flexibility of doing refunds. If my business went through a third party - like a publisher, or like Amazon - then I would be at the mercy of the customer support of the platform, which customer support might decide to argue with customers, and not give up on revenue that easily.

#learning #business #smallbusiness

text:
I spoke with more than 30 Klarna employees during and after the layoffs, where the company let ~700 people / ~10% of the staff go. A few interesting things:

1. Monday morning, a meeting invite for all hands appeared, then disappeared from everyone's calendar. This made people nervous.

2. Employees in Europe did not know for a whole night if they will be laid off or not. I talked with people as late as 3am who were up and worried.

3. Promotions finished a few weeks before layoffs. Some people promoted were laid off, causing disbelief amongst employees. Why did leadership not postpone promotions if they knew they wanted to lay off. Or perhaps they did not know a few weeks ahead?

4. The severance packages issued to US employees caused an uproar, as they were much lower than similar tech companies issued them. Klarna increased the package significantly yesterday, on 1st June, benchmarking themselves to Airbnb's 2020 severance packages.

5. The idea of setting up an union gained more popularity after Klarna's CEO bashed the idea of the union on the Q&A following the layoffs. Unions can offer layoff protection which employees now very much want, but leadership does not want it.

Read more about how the layoffs played out, the current mood within Klarna, and why I would caution any leader to be very, very careful in using layoffs as a tool to cut costs, given the high price to pay in morale, negative press, and attrition:

https://lnkd.in/gRtWBQ2V

text:
Now that more companies are doing (and will be doing) layoffs: it's a good reminder that many leaders underestimate the *real* impact of layoffs. Consistently.

Speaking myself, Uber's layoffs in the spring/summer of 2020 was the direct trigger for me to decide it was time to leave.

Few people will admit they quit because of a layoff when they were not the ones laid off. For me, the layoffs made me realize I'm expendable and could be fired any day. A team of ~30 were let go who used to sit next to me. Including people with more tenure & experience than me.

Interesting how being close to layoffs end up pushing some people to be entrepreneurs. Talking with a well-known entrepreneur he admitted he is working for himself so no one can ever fire him (again).

Observing those layoffs definitely helped me decide to try going at it alone, and not join another corporate - where, if things go downhill, I could have found myself in a similar situation as at Uber.

In an interesting “what if”, had Uber been able to avoid layoffs - and I never experienced what it's like to go through all the uncertainty - The Pragmatic Engineer might not exist in its current form. It’s safe to say that this publication began as a ripple effect of the Uber layoffs.

And the latest The Pragmatic Engineer issue is exactly about layoffs - which, sadly, will keep happening at companies that urgently need to cut costs - and advice on how to do them humanely.

Have you observed layoffs change your, or someone else's relationship to companies, or thinking about work or career?

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Companies where software engineers were laid off this year: Peloton, Hopin, Cameo, Robinhood, Carvana, Zwift, Pollen. Companies slowing or freezing hiring: Meta, Twitter, Coinbase, Uber, Doordash, Unity. Just how bad are things?

My take is that they're not as bad as the above news makes it sound.

Yes: it's true that there are layoffs with companies cutting costs, thanks to the slowdown in growth, and a challenging economic environment.

It's also true that raising money for companies making a loss became 10x more difficult overnight. Many of the layoffs are coming from companies needing to extend their runway.

However, looking closer at the layoffs: software engineers and tech professionals are let go as a low % of people, if any. For example Pollen let go ~200 people, about ~20 of them were tech related, and perhaps half of them (~10) software engineers. Netflix laid off 150 people, but no one in tech that I could confirm.

On the other side: the majority of companies are doing fine. Many more are hiring aggressively than those laying off. Some are a bit more cautious with hiring and more cost conscious, but they are hiring till - like how Uber is still hiring software engineers in many locations.

My take is that the incredibly heated tech hiring market that was typical for 2021, and early 2022: this is over. And yes, sadly, we will see more layoffs from mostly VC-funded companies unable to raise new rounds. We might even see companies shut down, like it happened with one-click checkout company Fast.

But I don't see a "tech bubble bust" like around 2001 happening. All I see is the hottest tech market of all time has ended - and it's becoming a "normal" tech market.

#compensation #layoffs #hiring #softwareengineering
