posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
How do different types of companies typically ship code into production? Here's my attempt at visualizing the differences:

A few notes:

1. Startups typically do fewer quality checks than any other company

These companies tend to prioritize moving fast and iterating quickly, and often do so without much safety net. This makes perfect sense when they don't (yet) have customers. As the company gets users, these teams need to start to find ways to not cause regressions or ship bugs. They then have choices to go down two paths: hire QAs or invest in automation.

2. Traditional companies tend to rely heavier on QAs

While automation is sometimes present in more traditional companies: it's very typical that these places rely on large QA teams to verify what they build. Working on branches is also typical: it's rare to have trunk-based development in these environments.

Code gets pushed into production typically on a schedule (e.g. weekly or even less frequent) after the QA team has verified functionality.

3. Large tech companies invest heavily in infrastructure and automation related to shipping with confidence.

These investment include automated tests running quickly and delivering rapid feedback, canarying, feature flags and staged rollouts.

These companies aim to keep a high quality bar, but also ship immediately after quality checks have finished, working on trunk. Tooling to deal with merge conflicts becomes important, given some of these companies can see over 100 changes on trunk every day.

4. Facebook has an approach few other companies have.

Facebook's core product is an interesting one: it has less automated tests than many would assume, but, in return, it has an exceptional automated canarying functionality, where the code is rolled out through 4 environments: from a testing environment with automation, through one that all employees use, through a test market of a smaller region, to all users. In every stage, if metrics are off, the rollout automatically halts.

Over to you: what is your experience in shipping to production? What has your company done similar and differently, and how well did it work?

Read a longer article about practices in shipping to production: https://lnkd.in/gM9wJHXz

text:
"What are things engineers and engineering managers should know about Facebook's engineering culture?" Months of research later, I have an answer, but it's not short.

I just published the second and final part of "Inside Facebook's Engineering Culture" on The Pragmatic Engineer. The series is ~90 pages (~22,000 words) combined, and covers a wide range of topics, going in-depth in most areas.

Here are a few fun facts I learned about the company:

1. An engineering-centric culture. 

Facebook has a very engineer-centric culture: more so than any other Big Tech company I know of. Engineers have autonomy in choosing their team (more flexibility than all companies), deciding what to work on, and are compensated above most tech functions.

2. Internal mobility. 

This very high, and movements between teams are easier than other, similar companies. It's a system that few companies would dare experiment with, fearing that key teams would be left unstaffed. It is daring that Facebook allows this level of movement.

3. Engineering competencies.

Facebook / Meta calls these "axes". They are: impact, direction, people and engineering excellence. The latter two were introduced later on (~2015 and 2016/17) to help shape the culture of the company.

4. Engineers leading projects and rollout tooling.

Teams operate autonomously, with engineers leading many projects. Systems to roll out changes, and to do experimentation and analysis are excellent: better than at most companies.

5. Internal systems.

Safe to say that Facebook has built pretty much any engineering system you can think of, in-house and they don't buy from vendors. GitHub? Check. PagerDuty? Check. Slack? Check. JIRA? Check. You won't see your "usual suspects" in tooling here.

Subscribers can read the full article here: 🔒 https://lnkd.in/dWxGhdrE

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Fast raised a $102M Series B, and just over a year later, laid off all of its ~500 employees. Five pieces of advice to avoid joining the "next Fast": a company that could suffer a similar fate on short notice:

1. Do your research on the company and founders. Do your homework on the history of the company, and past red flags. 

I did this when I got a reachout to potentially interview for Fast in the second half of 2020. I found some unsettling information about the founder of Fast, Dominic Holland and decided to stay away from a company ran by someone with a shady past.

2. Ask for numbers. 

How much runway does the company have left? What is the burn per month? What is the revenue of the company, and what is the spending?

Especially when joining at senior levels - Staff engineer or above, Director or above - it should be a red flag if people don’t share these numbers with you, at least verbally. If you get pushback, you can always use the Fast story as reasoning on why you want to know these numbers.

3. Ask if there is a clear set of key business metrics are and if engineers have access to these close to real-time. Is it the number of users? Revenue? Signups? Percentage of returning daily users?

If the answer is “no” to either of the questions, treat it as a red flag. In the case of Fast, these metrics were likely not defined, and the data was not shared with all engineers.

4. Reverse interview your future manager and a founder. Once you have an offer, ask to talk with your future manager and a founder. Collect all your doubts and questions, and ask them on the spot.
Ask to talk with lead investors. Investors are almost always happy to jump on a call to talk with potential candidates. Expect that this will mostly be a sell call, where the investors share why they invested in the company. Still, you might find out information.

5. Talk with people who left. Try to find people who left the startup, and who were in a similar position to what you are applying for. Message them on LinkedIn, and ask if they can share why they left. A surprisingly large number of them tend to reply.

For more advice on what to look for, and for a summary of just how quickly Fast imploded - and to find out why I wore my Uber T-shirt when recording - see my latest video on YouTube: https://lnkd.in/esadkpGs

#startups #fast #advice #research

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
One of the biggest "shockers" to any software engineer who starts to work at Meta / Facebook: there's practically no documentation or inline code comments!! How is this possible?

Most software engineers have been drilled to document, document, document. The conventional wisdom of scaling companies is to write things down, so that knowledge scales.

How on Earth did Meta / Facebook then scale to over 30,000 people working in tech, and to ~3.5B people using their products, monthly (the second largest company by reach, after Google)?

Context is important. Facebook can - and does - get away with this unconventional practice because:

1. They push for readable and clear code. The lack of comments and documentation forces the code to "explain itself", as it's now the single source of truth.

2. They have a culture where it's not just OK, but expected to ping people, which people respond close to immediately. Don't understand what the code does? Just ping the person who wrote it or last modified it.

3. Have a pretty long tenure with engineers. The previous point doesn't work if engineers leave every 6 months. However, at Facebook, they tend to stay for pretty long: so there is someone to ask.

4. Have ways to compensate for lack of documentation. What is the worst that happens if you misunderstand the code? You make a bad change. Code reviews, automated canarying, and monitoring+alerting help catch issues quickly.

The upside of this approach? A few things that companies going all-in on documentation don't have:

1. No outdated documentation, and only one source of truth.

2. Fewer decisions. There's no need to decide what to document, what not to document. Whether to change the existing documentation or not.

3. More personal connections. A LOT more than places that are documents driven.

Teams have full autonomy, so they can always add more documentation or comments, if they decide this helps the team/project; and some teams do exactly this.

As food for thought, another company that follows this "low documentation" approach is Apple, for similar reasons.

Have you worked at a company that deliberately did little documentation? How did it go?

#meta #facebook #documentation #code

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
After months of researching Facebook / Meta, I published part 1 of a 65-page (17K word) deepdive into their engineering culture, revealing more details than I've seen anywhere.

As I talked with more than a dozen current and former Facebook engineers, managers and directors and here are 6 interesting things to know about Facebook's engineering culture, which are standout versus other Big Tech:

1. A lack of rigid processes. 

Facebook seems to have the least amount of processes or standardization across all of Big Tech. Don’t even try to compare it to Amazon’s engineering culture and the countless formal processes there. But even compared to companies like Google, Microsoft or Uber, Facebook’s processes are much looser. Most of this comes from the engineering-centric nature of the company and engineers disliking processes.

2. A quirky tech stack. 

Everything within Facebook is custom, much more so than at almost all of Big Tech. Google comes closest to a “non-standard tech stack”, but Facebook takes it a step further. This quirky tech stack is a good reason why they have — and need! — Bootcamp for onboarding.

3. Surprisingly little emphasis on testing or documentation. 

You’ll find shockingly little automated testing and documentation at Facebook, compared to the rest of Big Tech. Originally, this was a conscious decision in order to foster more collaboration and personal connection. Instead of reading documentation that is possibly stale, you have to look at the code, find out who wrote it — or modified it — and connect with them. This approach means building personal relationships, and possibly learning more than you could just from documentation.

4. A founder-engineer driven company. Facebook is now the only Big Tech firm whose founder is an engineer, and still is the CEO. Amazon was the other example of this until recently, but it’s not the case at Google, Apple, Netflix and others. There are good examples of smaller companies like CloudFlare, but they’re all younger than Facebook.

5. Engineers are product-oriented. 

They are expected — and are rewarded for — collaborating well with design, product and data science. Engineers tend to represent the product-minded engineer very closely. They do far more than just deliver on requirements. They frequently define requirements themselves and deliver on those they think will have the highest impact. This is true for the product organizations: Infra orgs are still very engineering oriented, focused on scaling, and engineering metrics.

6. Levels are hidden across the company. 

Every software engineer’s title is SWE, and every product manager is called Product Manager. For managers, you see Manager (but not if it’s M1 or M2), Director (but not if it’s D1 or D2) or VP. 

To read more about the engineering culture, see this article as published in The Pragmatic Engineer: 🔒 https://lnkd.in/eePCuTjt

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Why are software engineers paid more compared to other engineering fields? Some people believe it's because this field of work is more complex than most positions. This is false:

Mechanical engineering, civil engineering, and chemical engineering are all just as complex - and, in many cases, arguably even more complex - as software engineering.

Some then say it's because of how tech has more leverage. But hardware engineers and embedded engineers are usually paid less, even though they also produce similar leverage.

So why is it?

It's the demand growing faster than supply can keep up. Demand for chemical engineers, mechanical engineers, and even hardware engineers is stable, and there is enough supply to cover this. To get these positions, people almost always need to have university degrees: it's all but impossible to get those positions any other way than through college.

Yet, demand is so high for software engineers that there's a whole industry of bootcamps offering training of a few months, and many success stories of these people getting jobs - jobs that frequently pay well above what new grad hardware engineers or other engineering fields get after completing years of university studies.

It truly is a "golden age" of software engineers. But don't forget the root cause of this: an ongoing increase in demand, which increase has steadily grown globally, over the past decades.

Once this demand cools off, eventually, the salaries will follow in cooling off, as well. What is hard to predict is when, and by how much this demand will cool.

#softwareengineering #career #dynamics

---

text:
There is a blanket hiring freeze across Meta, and almost all interviews were canceled this week. There are also rumors of rescinding offers and pending layoffs. Digging into these rumors, this is what I found:

Meta is NOT rescinding offers: not in any way that I can confirm. This is after following up with both people claiming they had offers rescinded, and talked with Meta engineering directors in locations where those offers were supposedly rescinded.

Interviews ARE canceled for up to E6 and M1 positions, and most people in the hiring pipeline got an email about this between Wednesday or Friday.

Details don't check out from sources who claimed their offers were rescinded. In 3 cases out of 4 where I followed up in more detail, it was not offers rescinded, but people who went through the interview loop and were expecting an offer - but not given one -  not getting that offer thanks to the freeze. I talked with one person claiming to have their UK offer rescinded. This one source went quiet after I offered to connect them with an engineering director in the company to sort things out.

Meta is honoring all signed contracts is the information I got from people within the company. I have no confirmed rescinded offers as signed contracts for any software engineers. I have confirmed two outstanding, but not signed offers being withdrawn due to the freeze though.

For a popular LinkedIn post where a new grad software engineer got their offer rescinded because they did not get an H1B visa for the US: Meta is now moving them to Canada. For a popular post with a recruiter's offer being rescinded: that offer was through an agency, not directly with Meta.

As for layoffs: I see zero chance of this for software engineers or engineering managers in the coming 6 months, and I am putting this on the record. Here is what I wrote in The Pragmatic Engineer a week ago, on 28th April, and it's my belief as well. Layoffs would make no sense for Meta - they can, and do, already control expenses with a hiring freeze.

In the same way, rescinding signed offers make just as little sense, given the bad PR this would create, and the negligible cost savings.

We will see more layoffs across the industry, mostly at scaleups that raised their last funding round in 2020/21, hired aggressively, are burning lots of cash, and need more money. Most companies laying off will avoid laying off software engineers as much as they can - given how challenging this skillset is to hire for. 

Meta is not the place where engineering layoffs will come is my prediction.

#metalayoffs #bigtech #hiring

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What are some of the "typical" transition paths from software engineer to engineering manager?

In this week's newsletter on The Pragmatic Engineer, senior EM Diego Ballona summarized his observations and advice on moving from an engineer to an engineering manager. Talking about the most common transitions, he wrote:

"While there are many paths to engineering management, some seem to be more common than others:

1. Tech lead to tech lead manager. You may be operating as a "tech lead" (TL) after you have performed the senior engineer role for some time and have a track record of supporting engineers less senior than you. "Tech lead" is a situational role and less frequently a title, and its responsibilities may overlap with EM responsibilities. This overlap makes TLs strong candidates for EM roles. Commonly, TLs will step into the EM role and still do some of their individual contribution work as a tech lead manager (TLM).

2. Move to a smaller company and become a first-time manager. Another common path into management is moving from a larger company as a senior engineer doing the aforementioned TL role, to join a smaller company as an EM. Sometimes this means leaving as a senior engineer, being hired as a staff engineer, and then moving to management.

3. Staff+ Engineer to EM: In many companies, the engineering career ladder framework has corresponding IC / EM levels, whereby you can do a sideways career move. In most cases, a staff engineer would move to an experienced EM role, right above the TLM / first-time manager level. For some, a more natural progression is to go through the staff engineer path first and then transition into management.

There are many other transition paths, but the underlying commonalities are that a prospective IC transitioning to the EM role needs to have had some experience of being a senior or staff engineer and has showcased strong leadership traits. 

A last missing bit is the need for a new EM to step up. It can come through your manager quitting, a restructuring in the organisation, your team growing to a point where your manager has more reports than they can manage, and many other situations. As the stars align – or you align them yourself by convincing your manager you can manage a part of the team – you'll be on your way."

For more advice on the first year in the role, and growing as an engineering manager, read the full article here: 🔒 https://lnkd.in/eKFqfpNs

#engineeringmanagement #advice #softwareengineering #career

text:
Software engineering teams usually take twice as long to get something done as estimated. This is my observation, in general. Turns out, not much has changed in 50 years:

The book The Mythical Man Month was published in 1975 and in Chapter 8, it covers the topic on estimating. Based on research in the 60s and 70s, and gathering insights from industry professionals, the book comes to a similar conclusion:

How delivering software usually takes twice as long as most engineers estimate*. And how engineers are overly optimistic and when asked what were things that caused delays, they mention machine failures, higher priority unrelated jobs, meetings, paperwork, company business, sickness, personal time etc came up.

Incredible how the observation of delivering software taking roughly twice as long seems to have remained constant over 50 years. From the book:

*Except when writing compilers, or OSes. The author suggests shipping compilers take another 3x, and OSes another 3x than compilers (so 9x).

#estimation #softwareengineering

text:
With Airbnb announcing they will pay the same salary for anyone in the US, it's a good time to remind why "equal pay across countries" is a tricky question. Equal pay - but from what perspective?

This is roughly how much paying €100K in gross salary costs in various European countries. The total cost for the company is around 13-42% higher and is to do usually with company social security-related contributions per employee.

So when people ask "why are gross salaries significantly higher in the UK than, say, France" there's a chance that the company spends the same amount on employees: but the gross salaries end up lower in France, higher in the UK.

Of course, it’s not all apples to apples. In some countries the higher taxes result in more social security benefits - a good example is the UK vs France, where much of these taxes fund social security (and count as future credits) vs the UK where private pensions are the norm, and are encouraged by the government.

Regardless, companies employing across several countries will care about their total expenditure per employee, everything g included - not just the compensation, but adding those additional items like pension, healthcare, office space costs per employee and so on.

For more details, see the "A CTO’s Guide to Remote Compensation Strategies" issue in The Pragmatic Engineer.

#compensation #taxes #europe #salary

text:
Apple has started mandated return to the office, 3 days a week: Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday, across all offices. Here is how it's going:

I talked with a couple of current software engineers and engineering managers at Apple, to get more details. They are the only Big Tech where return to the office is "forced" in the sense that it's expected that people show up and there are very few, if any, exceptions. Exceptions are the very few full-remote roles that Apple has in place for engineers.

Apple's strict return to the office is different to how e.g. Meta or Google engineering teams still are not expected to be in on certain days, and managers are not enforcing people coming in.

The feeling seems to be mixed among engineers on how they feel about the policy. Some are happy to go back to see people, some less so. Most people would have loved more flexibility, and not 3 days that are not connected in one go.

Several long-standing engineers have left Apple prior to the return to office mandate. I cannot confirm that these departures were definitely because of the return to the office, but I have reports of more than usual notices coming shortly before April.

Apple seems to be hiring more than usual, according to several engineers and managers I have talked with. Teams usually have few headcounts open: but I heard of several teams with of 12-14 headcount where 4-6 headcounts are to be backfilled.

Headcount is being allocated without much resistance, is what managers I’ve talked with confirmed. Securing headcount used to be much more difficult in the past, than it is now. Most teams that need people and can hire are given these headcounts.

I want to call out that the increase in hiring might not just be because of possibly higher-than-normal attrition, one that might have been caused by mandating the return to the office. Among Big Tech, Apple is one of the very few companies where valuation has kept increasing in a bear tech market. Out of all of Big Tech, right now Apple seems to be the one most likely to keep growing its headcount, and the topic of hiring freezes not coming up for the next period. Few other companies are in this position.

I covered this, and other news in the industry - like Netflix introducing levels, layoffs at Robinhood, and companies where hiring freezes or layoffs might be expected next - in this week's The Scoop column in The Pragmatic Engineer. Read it here: 🔒 https://lnkd.in/eHH74pwj

text:
“I am not a tech person but have an offer to join this tech company that claim they are on the cutting edge of tech. What is your take?”. My take is: run, and run fast.

Here is an excerpt on how this company describes their technology. It's buzzword bingo with zero meaning at best. I read through the full website. In an incredible achievement, the website manages to say absolutely nothing over tens of thousands of words like ("we will continue to develop to empower the future of everything"), and full of contradicting facts (like "we use blockhain technology" and "ultra low carbon footprint infrastructure")

This is no tech company: it's likely a marketing page generated by GPT-3 (and AI creating text from inputs, e.g. by being told: "GPT-3, write 100 pages about a web3 company").

My advice to anyone considering joining any company: do your research and understand what they do. If you do not understand what they do, do not join.

If you join a company where you don't understand what they do, you have no one but yourself to blame.

Oh, and this company that likely does nothing beyond generate a webpage using GPT-3 is EyA. Link to their incomprehensible parody website in the first comment if you want to torture yourself.

I'm still puzzled why anyone would go through the effort to put together a website like this. With GPT-3 becoming easier to access, we should likely see more sites like this, acting as a facade for fake businesses - similar to how we already have fake profiles on LinkedIn.

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Something anyone working in tech - and getting stocks - or investing in tech stocks needs to understand: how tech company valuations work.

Most tech stock is *growth stock*. This means that the valuation for the present reflects the expectation of growth. If growth slows: valuation drops, as do stocks.

Why is this important to understand? It's especially key if you are about to join a startup, which, say, just raised funding at a $10B valuation. Let's assume they make ~$100-300M in annual revenue.

Without understanding how growth stocks work, you might think "oh wow, I am getting a sweet deal, joining a company already worth $10B, and next year they could be worth $20B or $30B and my stocks double or triple."

In reality, unless the company - and you! - can show 50-100% growth on the metrics that matter - like revenue, profits, customers - *for the next several years* the valuation will be well below $10B in no time.

And growing 50-100% at a company that is already making ~$100M in revenue is much harder than most realize! That's like building a whole new startup similar to the size of the current one: just in a year! Many don't realize how the company still needs to "earn" that valuation which it was given during the latest fundraising round: and most of the focus of new hires is exactly this.

Growth stocks have massive upsides. They can also crash in value if the growth slows down. The same is true for private startups valued as growth tech companies.

text:
What would happen if JIRA went down at your company for 3 weeks? Several companies paying for JIRA are finding it out as we speak and I talked with them about their opinion of this kind of outage.

JIRA has had a global outage for 6 days impacting many customers, and they estimate some customers will be down for another 2 weeks.

I talked with a few companies that are impacted by this outage.

"We cannot do anything" - an engineering manager shared. "Any time we try to access JIRA we get the 'page unavailable' status. JIRA told us our site was so complex that they need another 2 weeks to rebuild it. In the meantime, we'll use... I don't know, Google Sheets?"

"This outage is having a have a huge impact across the company. We use it for both dev, operational issues, product planning, business processes, etc. We had no backup." - another engineering manager.

"We've been hit. We're deeply integrated w Atlassian and it's been nothing but misery the whole week. Can't believe we pay for this." - a tech lead.

"Last we heard (yesterday) about our instance is that they 'started restoration', whatever that means. Nothing seems up yet. It's been a pretty hard lesson in just how dependent we are on Atlassian - We have almost every team, including marketing and finance, on JIRA " - from a tech company heavily dependent on Atlassian.

Atlassian has an uptime SLA of 99.9% or 99.95% for paying customers. Clearly, for many of their customers this has been breached and some customers could see an uptime of as little as 94% this year (!!)

The root cause, as per an Atlassian tweet is how a maintenance script disabled several sites, doing so unintentionally. Somehow, bringing back those sites seems to be a manual effort now. In 6 days, they have only restored one third of the affected sites.

On one end, outages can, and do happen, and SLA budgets are broken. On the other, weeks-long outages signal very big problems in the background.

We can only hope Atlassian will publish a public postmortem. Unlike other service providers like Slack, Honeycomb.io, Cloudflare which publish all major outage reports in the open, Atlassian has no history of doing so.

Finally, let me give a shoutout to Linear, which is a scaleup founded by Uber alumni, and is the tool most fast-growing tech startups I know - and invest in - use. It's faster than JIRA in response times, built by engineers, for software engineers and has yet to see a similar outage as JIRA has. (I have no affiliation with them and have not been paid to say this: outages from the market leader are a reminder that there are alternatives ahead in reliability and customer experience than the incumbent). 

Good luck to engineers at JIRA over the next week, and I really hope we see a public postmortem. If we won't see any postmortem: I would hesitate to recommend Atlassian as a tool to rely on, as who knows if the root cause is fixed or not.

#outage #jira #incidentmanagement #bigtech

text:
My career turned out different to what I imagined only 18 months ago. I'm lucky to make a living by writing online. In an effort to give back, here is what I've learned about monetizing what you create, as a software engineer:

1. I don't think "creator" is a real thing. If anyone, I would be considered a successful creator. Still, I don't think anyone should aim for this category. I don't think of myself as "a creator" at all and to have a sustainable "creator career", I would discourage others to think this way.

2. There are only so many ways to make a living NOT as an employee. Freelancing, contracting, bootstrapped business, VC-funded business, or living off investments. Every creator is a variation of these.

3. Instead of "creator", think "one-person business". 

4. There are many ways you can prepare to run a one-person business, even on the job! I write about examples here: https://lnkd.in/gtWcnf2p

5. Understand the models of making money. You can make money selling either to consumers (B2C) or to businesses (B2B). There's a wide range of things you can sell as a software engineer, though some things are more common to sell to one of the groups.

6. Experiment with multiple revenue streams. You can get started even while working full-time with some of them.

7. Prepare to take a risk. If you never take the leap, you'll never know what it's like.

I hope this helps. I suggest not chasing the "creator dream" which might turn out to be disappointing, but chasing the "one-person business" dream instead which is a lot easier path to follow.

Read all the above advice with more details in my article: https://lnkd.in/gtWcnf2p

#advice #creatoreconomy #bigtech #startups #softwareengineer

text:
"I joined a company which brands itself as a tech-first company. I was super excited. As I was setting up my new Macbook, I noticed I have no local admin rights. Turned out I needed to request permission from IT to install *anything*. And my request for Visual Studio Code was rejected."

This is a real quote from a software engineer who told me this was the first realization that they got "tricked" and felt handcuffed at their job, and it was the main reason they quit later.

They couldn't install small utilities they preferred to use and used on all other jobs - all rejected by IT! - nor could they use the IDE of their choice. VS Code was on a ban list because apparently, IT feared some extensions can be malicious so decided a blanket ban was the safest way to go.

This engineer tried moving teams to see if things would get better. They were not. So they left. They shared how it's much better now:

“It really started with the overly restrictive environment. It felt like I was handcuffed to do my job. I now work at a scaleup in the same space for practically the same compensation. It just feels like a breath of fresh air.”

A reminder that the environment engineers work at matters. And overly restrictive IT policies will result in engineers leaving who feel handcuffed and not heard when it comes to using their preferred tools to be productive.

#advice #career #softwareengineering #bigtech 

---

text:
What does "location-independent salary" mean? It means paying equal salaries independent of where you are based. But equal based on WHAT exactly?

The total cost for the company? Or the gross salary the employee sees? Or the takehome each employee gets?

While location-independent salaries sound equitable in theory, it's hard to implement it in a way that it's truly equal. If a company benchmarks to the same cost to the company, in certain countries with high empower contribution taxes, employees see lower gross salaries. 

At the same time, if every employee gets the same gross salary - as with below - the cost for the company can be a LOT higher in some companies, even as employees see a lower take-home after taxes!

In this week's The Pragmatic Engineer issue with Sergio Pereira we mapped out everything worth knowing about choosing a strategy for remote compensation. Read it here: https://lnkd.in/epXs96ue

text:
If you've been seeing lots of people announcing their last day at companies like Robinhood, Pinterest, Zoom and other companies where the stock has not had a good year: this is no surprise.

High-growth tech companies which offer similar total compensation packages to new hires have seen wildly different stock trajectories. And if someone joined Tesla or Apple with a $350K/year compensation package is already seeing $500K more in gains versus if they worked at Robinhood or Pinterest.

This means that it makes less sense for recent Robinhood or Pinterest employees to stick around with their compensation effectively down 25% or more - not even taking the 7% or more inflation in account. So apart from key employees who are getting major refreshers or retainers in the hundreds of thousands of dollars, many of them won't.

I go into more details and more calculations in the latest issue of The Pragmatic Engineer on this topic, and list even bigger winners and losers regarding stock compensation.

#compensation #equity #bigtech #startups

text:
"I'm completing my CS Bachelors. I have a job offer to work as a software engineer, but I'm hesitating whether to go on with a Masters." Here is how most hiring managers think of these two options:

Having been a hiring manager reviewing hundreds of these resumes, and also talking with dozens of more hiring managers, when a hiring manager sees two profiles:

1. Candidate A: Bachelors degree and 2 years of work experience as a software engineer

2. Candidate B: Bachelors degree and 2 years of Masters, but no work experience as a software engineer

The overwhelming majority of hiring managers will prioritize Candiate A because of the work experience which is exactly 2 years ahead of Candidate B. Industry work experience >> academia in the context of applying for jobs in the software engineering industry.

In practice, it's a bit more nuanced:
- Candidate A is typically considered for a more experienced role, one level above the entry-level software engineer role. So they interview for e.g. Engineer 2 or SDE 2 roles.
- Candidate B is typically considered for only entry-level roles like Eng1, SDE1 or Graduate Software Engineer.

There are a select few exceptions to the above:

1. For Data Science, ML and AI-related fields and few other niche areas, a Masters is still and advantage - but only because there are relatively few openings for a swarm of qualified applicants who all have Masters or even PhDs.

2. A Masters from different, and more prestigious university vs the Bachelors can have strong signalling and can help Candiate B stand out.

3. If you'll want to move abroad for work and will need a visa, a Masters might make all the difference for visa purposes. Many countries have points systems and the Masters might be the thing that allows you to get that visa. Don't forget that you can always do a Masters later, even part-time, while working as a software engineer, though.

For generic software engineers, my honest view is a Masters makes no difference for your career as a software engineer.

If you have an offer to start working: you'll be years ahead by taking this over doing a Masters. You can also do a Masters later, on the side, to see what you missed, and without slowing down your career trajectory.

#advice #career #bachelorsdegree #mastersdegree #softwareengineering 

---

During these 12 years has not only built up an ever stronger friendship with Putin but is now the only leader of an EU country to openly support Russia's war on Ukraine and sabotage the EU according to the interests of Russia. 

In 1956, Russian tanks roamed Budapest, ending a Hungarian freedom fight against Russian influence with bloodshed. For the past 12 years, on the anniversary of this event, Orban has repeatedly said freedom in Europe means freedom from Russian oppression. He then turned to Hungary, and told Orban he needs to decide who he is with: the democratic world or with Russia?

Over the past 12 years Orban has built up an even stronger admiration for the Putin way of running a country and has been following Putin's footsteps in limiting the free press severely, shutting down independent media, and chipping away the foundations of modern democracy.

text:
The latest issue of The Pragmatic Engineer is about how to retain engineers. Here's advice I have on retaining early-career engineers: something more teams should pay attention to.

---

Retaining early-career engineers is exceptionally challenging at many companies. I’ve talked with hiring managers at agencies and startups which hire more entry-level engineers than most, invest in them and help them grow. And yet, they see these engineers “head hunted” as soon as they’ve spent 6-12 months at their company.

Companies that do a great job growing entry-level engineers are often targeted by Big Tech firms, which take their early to mid-career colleagues. As a CTO at a digital agency shared:

“We have a reputation of being an agency with a very strong engineering culture. We are also good at hiring people with close to zero experience, and help them become great engineers."

"The past few years, we’ve noticed recruiters from Amazon and other large tech companies targeting anyone who has been with us for at least 6 months. We cannot compete with these offers, and have been losing lots of great employees.”

So, is there anything you can do to retain early-career colleagues, especially when you cannot compete financially with some players on the market? Turns out there is plenty.

1. Money & career

- Career path. Many early-career engineers leave for other companies because they don’t know a career path exists for them, beyond a months-long onboarding process. So create a multi-year career path with clear promotion points, and communicate what people can expect. A multi-year plan that the company delivers on, has been the single best retention tool for all agencies and startups I’ve talked with.

- Promotions. Be clear when these engineers can expect promotions and what they need to do for one. Early-career people are almost always ambitious and want to get to that next level. If the next promotion level takes most people more than two years to reach, consider introducing interim levels to help with formal career progression.

2. Growth

- Make it clear what you invest in and how. If engineers don’t know what professional investment they are getting, they might not even realize how much you are helping them. Outline the resources they are getting; be it training, mentorship, events, stretch projects or others.

- Mentorship. Having regular access to experienced mentors is something most entry-level engineers value highly. However, few get this, and even fewer realize they are getting mentorship when it’s called something else. Consider introducing a more formalized mentorship program to bring clarity for everyone: both the entry-level engineers who benefit and the mentors, so they know what support they are expected to provide.

Read more here: 🔒https://lnkd.in/g2BQpG3N

#career #retainingtalent #bigtech #startups

text:
Are we seeing the end of The Great Resignation in tech and the start of The Great Tech Reshuffle?

I'm observing far more people switching jobs starting from the beginning of this year than what has typically been "normal".

There seem to be alseveral reasons for this:

1. The pandemic becoming more tame, and people being able to plan ahead.

2. Many people have avoided switching jobs amid the uncertainty the pandemic has brought; this was especially true for those with families.

3. The tech job market is incredibly hot, and compensation bands have moved up by 30%+ just the past 12 months: such increases are hard to resist.

4. Remote work changes. Companies which are starting to introduce return to the office or cutting compensation for remote workers are seeing these same people look around on the market.

5. Having served the "tour of duty" and some people have overstayed due to #1 or #2.

If you're seeing more people than usual leave around you: it's not just you. And if you're a hiring manager, now might be a great time to reach out to people who have not changed jobs for 2 or more years.

They might just be ready.

(I wrote more about this in the latest issue of The Pragmatic Engineer).

#career #bigtech #startups

My way of thinking about the four key areas you should make sure people feel good about. If they do: unlikely they will leave. If 2 or more are missing: they are likely to go.

I'm writing more on this topic in Tuesday's issue of The Pragmatic Engineer. I'll be covering:

- Retaining early-career / "junior" engineers. There's a lot of low-hanging fruit I've observed relatively few companies do. Those that do these end up with talented early-career folks growth within the company over leaving somewhere else.

- Retaining software engineers. Unless you've been living under a rock, you'll know how challenging this is.

- Retaining engineering managers. SO many people forget about this one, assuming managers will magically stay. While managers are usually ones that take their time to decide to leave: losing a great manager is a loss that is much harder to backfill than most other roles.

text:
Let's talk about an uncomfortable truth about algorithmic coding interviews: they are biased, get in the way of hiring diverse teams, and turn away perfectly good candidates. And a growing number of startups no longer uses this approach.

So why does Big Tech use them? There's a host of reasons, but the reasons it works for them - and they can still hire a somewhat diverse team - is this:

1. Big Tech gets 10-100x as many applicants as they have headcount. They will have diverse candidates apply and get through, even as most are rejected.

2. Most of Big Tech throws money at the problem and has dedicated recruiters only reaching out to minorities to get them to apply. Some companies do dedicated preparation sessions to help these people more. Through sheer volume, they'll still hire more diverse candidates.

3. Big Tech will advertise how they care about diversity, but they care even more about hiring people who will stick around. This means hiring people who can put up with a stressful and somewhat unpredictable workplace just as much - and rewarding people who do so with top-of-the-market compensation it's close to impossible to get anywhere else.

The problem with algorithmic interviews is not that Big Tech does these. It works for them, for the most part.

The problem is when small companies and startups copy the same approach, without realizing how they are turning away perfectly good candidates, struggling to hire a diverse team - heck, they struggle to hire at all.

I've talked with close to a dozen startup founders the past month about how they hire. All of those who are not struggling to hire do not have algorithmic coding interview questions.

Be careful on copying Big Tech hiring approaches when you are not Big Tech.

#hiring #bigtech #startups #advice

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
In what is eye-opening: if you start working at a consumer-focused tech company (e.g. Google, Meta, Spotify) you'll likely soon get reachouts from friends of friends to help them. They have a problem and you're the only human they can reach at the company.

As you attempt to help, you start to see how broken customer complaints handling at scale is - and the more time you work here, the more you understand this is not by accident, but by design.

The first time you get such a request, you try to help. As you do, you start to realize how the focus on revenue and impact disincentivises fixing one-off issues.

Most  tickets you open will be closed by teams owning the issue as “cannot repro”, “on the backlog already w low priority” or stay idle.

The irony is that two years into working at the company, you also “get it”.

A ticket from a new joiner comes along how their aunt is locked out of their account. You are the team owning this problem. You’re the one now closing out the ticket as “know issue, low priority.”

Why does this attitude happen?

Carrots and sticks.

Very few if any people and team get rewarded for fixing low impact issues impacting 0.002% of users. Also few get punished by not doing so.

The only exception is when it’s the CEO’s aunt. Then you fix this ASAP.

At any Big Tech, incentives that drive promotions and performance reviews end up driving a surprisingly large amount of decisions.

Most of these incentives are impact, revenue generation at scale & savings at scale.

Fixing small issues fits none of these.

In fact, fixing one-off issues is one of the most expensive things to do. It would - and does! - pull profits down. This is bad for business.

It’s why Google & most tech companies have notoriously few customer support staff and are more profitable than any company that has it.

To sum it up: fixing individual issues doesn't scale, and this is why large companies focused on profits don't do it.

You know who does things that doesn't scale and is not obsessed with profits?

Startups and small businesses. So if you, as a consumer want your issues fixed: vote with your wallet.

Don't use Gmail as email but pay for Fastmail, Protonmail or similar where humans do respond to you. Book your stay with the hotel or a local travel agent who directly benefit from giving you proper support, not Booking .com where customer support costs are being cut down year after year to increase profits. Or give startups a chance - which startups will bend backwards to make you happy: until they become Big Tech, and cut down on one-off fixes and customer support, that is.

#bigtech #startups 

---

text:
"How could anyone in India order free UberEats for almost a whole weekend?". 3 years later, it's time to finally share what happed. Storytime about breaking changes, error handling, idempotency and free food:

1. What happened? One morning someone in India tried to order food via UberEats in India, using PayTM as a payment method. But they didn't have enough balance.

Got an error. Tapped "try again".

The order went through!! Without having to pay!

News spread quick and soon all college students in India started ordering free food.

2. This was a payments-related bug. The problem with these is how the bug was in the reconciliation flow. And Uber reconciled with PayTM maybe once a week.

How Uber discovered this: restaurants started going offline thanks to huge order quantities in very short times.

3. After it was clear something was up, Uber suspended PayTM as a payment method and started the investigation.

My team owned the PayTM payment method at the time, so this was my team.

We naturally looked at what code changes we've made in the timeframe. None.

4. So if we made zero changes on our end, what happened?

Turns out the PayTM team did a change late on a Friday that looked innocent enough.

It silently changed an API endpoint from behaving idempotent to non-idempotent.

Why does idempotency matter?

5. Idempotency means that you can safely repeat requests as you get the same response every time.

I remember the endpoint was charge-related. 

Before, it always returned the same error when trying to charge a wallet without enough credits. With the change, not anymore:

6. Before:
1. "Try to charge wallet X without funds" -> Error1
2. "Try to charge wallet X without funds again" -> Error1

After:
1. "Try to charge wallet X without funds" -> Error1
2. "Try to charge wallet X without funds again" -> A Status not seen before (not an error, but not success either)

7. Now this might look like a small change, but on Uber's side, the assumption was the endpoint was idempotent, so there was no testing on getting anything else back. The new response was unknown and not mapped to anything.

Long story short it was interpreted as "ok to proceed" and failed open.

8. As a note, all PayTM responses at the time came through 200 OK HTTP status codes, with messages in the body to be parsed. So we could not use HTTP status codes to differentiate between "ok" and "not ok" responses: it was down to the message parser.

9. So PayTM slightly changed the behavior of the API without communicating. Some partners assumed idempotency changes are breaking API changes to be communicated: but they were not. Uber was one of these partners.

The result? Free food until discovered!

10. Being in the middle of this outage, a few things I learned:

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
How do you put together a hiring process for software engineers that works for *your* team? Should you copy what Google and other well-known companies do?

I would suggest not.

Instead, follow these steps:

1. Define the role. What do you expect of it and why? What does success look like? Write it down.

2. Write the job description. Write it based on the role description you have. Take inspiration from the structure of other JDs.

3. Define the interview process. Instead of starting from interviews, start with what signals you need to gather, based on #1. Figure out what interviews you absolutely need.

4. Be clear on how you decide. What is the debrief process? Does the hiring manager decide by themselves? Is it a consensus? Are there roles of those who can veto?

5. Calibrating the process. How can you increase consistency and reduce bias in interviews? Use tools like shadowing, reverse shadowing or virtual shadowing. Look into interview intelligence tools like Metaview.

6. Feedback loops. How well is your process working? You won't know if you don't measure. Things you could measure:
- Hiring funnel & drops between stages
- Close %
- Time to offer accepted
- Candidate feedback & NPS
- Interviewer load
- Sourcing stats
- Interview metrics like candidates being talked over on interviews or interviews where suspiciously few questions are asked by the interviewer (tools like Metaview can report/alert on these).

For a lot more details and templates for documenting an interview process and debriefs, see this week's 🔒 subscriber-only The Pragmatic Engineer issue: https://lnkd.in/gStzz9Ya

#hiring #bigtech #startups

text:
Does Google still have a "20% time" - meaning you can spend a day a week working on a "sideproject"? The short answer is: yes, it's still alive!*

*In specific Google orgs, Google Engineering included. For example, I hear that in GBO and GTech there is often little to no 20% budget so if you work here, the answer is more likely "No". If you work outside of engineering, the answer might also be "No".

Starting from 2013 there have been reports how Google "effectively killed" 20% time as it tied it to manager approvals, and reports in 2013 said how managers were strongly discouraged to approve projects.

However, I talked with several current and former engineers and engineering managers Google, and they all said how while yes, approval is needed, they get or give approvals almost immediately. 

From Google engineering director Orion Espino:

"Nobody in either of the two organizations that I have ran in Google have ever had to ask for permission to do a 20% project. They just do it. Perhaps, like many things, your mileage may vary."

From former Google staff engineer Jaana Dogan:

"I had four 20% projects that turned into long-term initiatives when I was there. There is no official policy against 20%s. Google understands the risk of putting something experimental out there and killing it."

From an engineer currently at working at Google on their second 20% project (after the pandemic made the first project impractical):

"Manager approval was a 5-minute conversation with my manager during our regular 1:1, and I didn't even have to convince him - he was genuinely interested. Bottom line: 20% is alive - but as with everything in live you have to go and do it. Nobody will come and say "hey, I think it would be a great idea for you to work on this thing here". This is one of the things I learned early on at Google: don't wait for the cool things to come to you - go and get them yourself."

#bigtech #google #sideproject

text:
Luxoft - the 3rd largest tech company in Ukraine - sent an email to all staff fully condemning "Russia's full-scale invasion on Ukraine". As a response, their mother company is closing their Russia offices with 4,000 employees (!!).

Luxoft had about 3,500 employees in Ukraine and is a company with Russian roots, started in Moscow 22 years ago.

Details from the email sent to all staff:

"Russia has taken the irrevocable step of launching a full-scale invasion of Ukraine. This is war and a clear act of aggression. We fully condemn it and hope that common sense will prevail.

This war must stop.

We are extremely proud of our Ukrainian colleagues for their bravery and resilience in the face of danger. It is a pleasure and a privilege to have you with us at Luxoft. Seeing the way all of you have responded, we are reminded of just how impressive people, specifically our Luxofters, can be. (...)

We are pursuing every possible path to solve the current humanitarian crisis and help people return to their homes. As a company and as people, we are doing everything in our power to ensure the physical safety and job security of our Ukrainian Luxofters and their families. (...)

In the response to the aggression by Russia, DXC (the mother company of Luxoft) will be exiting this market. (...)

We have close to 4,000 employees in Russia who have given valued service to our company and clients over many years. We recognize and thank them for their service and are supporting them to consider different career opportunities in our global business. We continue to maintain rigorous compliance with sanctions levied against Russia.

I sincerely thank every one of you for your efforts. Remember: unity and mutual help make us strong: both as a company and human beings."

DXC is a US-based company and I expect many US and European companies to follow suit, large and small. Some will do so willingly, and some will not have any choice, having to follow sanctions imposed by the US and Europe on Russia. Any company in the US and Europe with offices in Russia needs to make contingency plans so they can comply with sanctions - if they have not made these plans already.

#StandWithUkraine

text:
If you are living in Europe, Putin's war on Ukraine is about you as well. Putin's open goal is to restore the USSR territory and influence, penetrating into central Europe over the next decade. If Ukraine falls, he will not stop: he will get even more bold.

I was born into the Eastern Block and my family had the chance to settle in the US in 1990, getting a green card. We chose not to, coming back to Hungary because my parents saw a future that did not involve being occupied by Russia and one without the Cold War and the threat of a nuclear standoff.

Putin now wants to bring back of what he thinks is the glory days of the USSR and the Eastern Block. When Hungary, Poland, Romania, Bulgaria, Czechia, Slovakia, Ukraine, Latvia, Estonia, Lithuania, Albania were all under Russian influence. Citizens lived in a world of quiet fear, one where teaching Russian was mandatory in schools, fleeing West close to impossible and where Russians could enter with tanks when they pleased - and they did! It was a time where the though of living freely in a democracy was but a dream.

I don't want this past to return. Right now, Ukraine is the only thing standing in Putin's way on this road.

I #standwithukraine, not just with words, but using the means that I have. Starting now, my family and I are redirecting what we have put aside as monthly savings to supporting refugees and the Ukranian army.

If you are European: the future of Europe is being decided now. I urge you to act, so this war is not just Russia vs Ukraine, but Russia vs Ukraine supported by all of Europe.

#europe #future

reshared_post.text:
Dear all,

Now that the war in Ukraine is fully ongoing I feel I can no longer hold my silence. What most might not know is that in my youth I've experienced war, first hand. A bloody war between Iraq and Iran emerged, resulting in more than 1 million casualties, and the humanitarian impact of this war can still be felt today. Lost limbs, psychological disorders, consequences of chemical attacks, etc. all still cause continuous suffering amongst many people. 

It breaks my heart to think that we might be at the start of similar suffering on a grand scale. And I cannot look the other way.

The one thing I've learned through my personal experience is that the world is far too complex to be divided in simple terms like good and bad. Things tend to be far more subtle than one might think from the outset. It's only once we see through each other’s eyes that we learn to appreciate the other person's position, often helping us see the commonalities rather than the differences. What I've also learned is that this process is painstaking and difficult; it's much easier to use big words that forego the precious value of human lives. 

War is always an easy way out for those who don't personally get affected by it.

It is very unfortunate that we have failed to resolve the underlying issues leading to this war cooperatively and peacefully. As a consequence - yet again - the ordinary people are suffering. That's why I'd like to state that bunq will do its utmost to get as many people to safety as possible - Ukrainians and Russians alike.

If you are affected and all you want to do is live in peace and harmony, please send your details to people@bunq.com and we’ll do everything in our power to get you over to the Netherlands under an HSM visa. If you can provide shelter, harbor people or have any other ideas on how we can help please contact us too.

I sincerely hope this unnecessary tragedy will soon come to an end.

Peace,

Ali

(The plan as it stands now: we want to provide shelter to people in Bulgaria or Georgia and from there on coordinate them getting an HSM visa through the Dutch embassy)

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Back to office announcements are happening and some are not going well. From a CEO: "The new way is the old way, and we will all go back to the office. (...) If software engineers think they can work from home, we can hire someone in Asia and pay them €300."

These quotes are from a Friday a few weeks ago, on an all-hands where the CEO of a more "traditional" company shared how the company made an all-time record net profit of €1B in 2021, up more than 300% from the previous year. The profit increase was thanks to transforming from an analogue to a fully digital business model, enabling them to deliver all services digitally and at digital cost levels, powered by software (and software engineers).

Right after this he also announced the post-COVID plans and felt it necessary to call out software engineers whom they heard are not fully on board with coming back to the office.

The all-hands recordings are always accessible right after the all-hands: however, this recording was absent, despite many software engineers eager to re-listen to what the CEO just said, making sure they didn't imagine those words, and the "or else we'll outsource for cheaper" threat.

The coming Monday the CEO fully backtracked on all his comments, and on the forced return to the office for software engineers. 

What happened? 

I assume someone in tech, like the CTO, told him the grim reality of the job market. How retaining software engineers is the smartest thing to do in this climate, and how no, you can no longer hire for "cheap" even in Asia right now.

The backtracking email this CEO sent out is below, with the company name redacted.

The company name is not important. What's important is companies where leadership is openly hostile to software engineers and not accepting how people in this field do have options: those companies will have attrition problems. And hiring engineers in this market is very, very hard.

Even for companies that want to get back to the office: I suggest a more tactful approach, and to listen to what people working in tech have to say about your plans and adjust based on feedback, before trying to force it down their throats.

#hiring #remotework #advice

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
"Software engineers should be paid based on value created, not where they live." Sounds fair, right? But following this model, marketing should probably be paid as much as software engineering. Here's why:

Plausible Analytics is a two-person company. Software engineer Uku Täht started the company and built it for two years. The product grew but had very few customers.

Two years in, marketer Marko Saric joined as cofounder to do marketing. Six months after joining, the number of customers started to steadily go up, and two years later the company has passed the $500K/year run rate.

Now let's go back to the point of paying people based on the value they create, not on their location. If this was a "traditional" company, who would the CEO assume created more value? The software engineer who built the product? Or the marketing person who made sure people knew about it and this reach ultimately resulted in paying customers?

Both were equally needed for the success. The first part of the graph shows what happens to a great product without any marketing. But second part of the graph is not possible without a great product.

It's only fair to conclude that both software engineering and marketing add comparable value dependent on one another, so software engineers and marketing folks should be compensated the same, following the "pay for value added" approach.

So why are marketing people, in reality, paid less than software engineers?

Because in practice, it's far cheaper to hire marketing people.

As software engineers, we're paid more than most professions because there is a shortage of experienced people who can build complex systems. We're not paid on the value we create but based on how much it costs to hire someone else with similar skills we have.

#career #hiring #bigtech #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Layoffs are nothing to be sensational about or take easy dabs at them. I removed a previous post on a layoff as it’s exactly what that post did: making a big deal of what was an innocent mistake.

Layoffs are devastating for those shown the door. But they are also very stressful for those staying at the company: people who are trying to help those laid off get a softer landing, while also trying to get the day-to-day work done.

When stressed, people make more mistakes, even as they mean well.

I should know this: when Uber had layoffs, even though I wasn’t let go, I sent out an email that I wish I didn’t do. It was an email I thought would help the group laid off, but was tone-deaf: something I would have also realised, had I not sent it out at the end of a long and stressful day.

Sadly, we are likely to see more companies forced to let people go, as their predictions of the future fail to match up with the current business reality. 

Learn from my mistake and avoid judging, and “told you so” takes. Instead, see how you can help both those let go of no fault of their own, and support the people still at the company, who also don’t have it easy.

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Google interviewing devs: "Design a system that can scale to billions of users, with a P99* of 30 seconds for data replication." Meanwhile, Google when changing your profile image:

It's ironic how a company with one of the most engineering-heavy cultures never prioritized getting customer-facing changes down to minutes, over days. 

The reality is that Google has a large number of systems owned by various orgs and teams, and spreading non-critical information like this is just not a priority. Some systems might use updates like use batching, or as Johnie Lee shared it's just... replication delays and cache invalidation (the classic “hardest problem in computer science”)

Still, what a contrast on expectation versus reality. Especially that most Google engineers have no problem solving these type of replication problems… in theory.

Turns out some replication problems are not worth solving, only covering up with a message like this, even at Google’s level.

*P99: the 99th latency percentile. It means that 99 out of 100 requests will be 30 seconds or less. In practice, this means "almost all users will have this experience". P95 and P99 are common ways to define latency targets.

#bigtech #google #softwareengineering

text:
Between PMs (product manager), EM (engineering manager) and TPM (technical program manager), who tends to own "what"? Here's my mental model:

The larger an organization, the more specializations. In large engineering organizations (typically 50-100 engineers), the TPM role starts to make lots of sense in scaling up the org without losing too much efficiency.

Of course, who owns what should be (and will be) different within every team and company. The important thing is to clarify this with each other, and be clear on the overlaps (e.g. on a team, the PM and EM might both get involved in the "who" question). And of course, engineers can - and should - be involved in giving their take wherever possible (this model is deliberately simple, focusing on three roles, assuming the EM involves their team and empowers them).

When you're small, one person or one team will own almost everything: they have to!

text:
Adyen is a fintech with very deep pockets, making €630M in EBIDTA on revenues of €1B in 2021. Yet their CEO is "greatly inconvenienced" by startups with "deep pockets", making it hard for Adyen to hire software engineers.

Here's why.

Adyen is making a profit of €314K per employee per year (after having paid wages etc) or a revenue of €500K per year, per employee. They have only 2,000 employees as a €56B company which is an incredibly lean company. For comparison, Stripe has more software engineers than Adyen has employees, and Uber has 10x as many employees with a similar market cap.

Other companies that can show this level of profitability or revenue per employee include Google, Netflix, and Meta. Adyen is in the group of the most profitable companies EBIDTA-margin wise, worldwide.

All those companies pay well above what Adyen is paying for software engineers in total compensation, including when they hire software engineers in the Netherlands. Heck, Uber has paid and continues to pay well above what Adyen offers in Amsterdam, despite Uber hitting their first-ever EBIDTA-profitability (of less than 1% on revenue) after ten years.

Adyen's complaints of how "inconvenient" it is to hire software engineers are, is true: this is exactly why similarly profitable companies have been "forced" to pay more for these people.

The "companies with deep pockets" are actually startups with VC funding who are trying to pay *as little* as possible for software engineers as they, unlike Adyen, are not profitable.

I would be empathetic for companies making losses complaining about how they're slowly going bankrupt, unable to pay for software engineers.

But for Adyen, all I can say is this: time to pay up. The market has moved up for experienced software engineers by about 30% the last 12 months, and Adyen have the profits to move with it and still close with record profits in 2022.

Adyen is so profitable that even if it tripled compensation for all 2,000 employees it would remain profitable. 

Their leadership knows this, and so do Adyen employees.

Update: I’ve been thinking and it feels there’s more under the surface of this statement. Why would an experienced and good CEO publicly mention hiring problems for software engineers in The Financial Times and blame the market? The C-level has little space to raise wages - they need to keep EBIDTA high, else the stock drops and the board can ultimately fire leadership.

But could this statement be a preparation for a large raise for software engineers within Adyen? It is a likely explanation: as well as this message is really for investors, preparing them for this unavoidable change - and also testing the waters with this tease.

#bigtech #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The "law of software project physics" that I have consistently observed to be true is this: 

Between people, scope and timeline, if one changes, another one also NEEDS to change.

If the scope increases, either the timeline needs to also increase, or the "people" part needs to increase.

What does "people" increasing mean? Either engineers need to spend more time working - overtime or working late - or you need to add more developers to the project. It can mean paying a contractor working 3 days/week on a project to do 5 days. It can also mean asking people to take vacations later, or to work over the weekend (I don't suggest either: but both count as "more people").

"Adding people to a project never works" - many will say, quoting Brook's Law from The Mythical Man Month (“Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later.”). And it's true that adding another engineer to the project includes onboarding, and more communications overhead. None of this is "extra" if you just push people to work more hours.

However, for projects where you can pull in people with domain context (==little onboarding), teams use the same tooling (==familiarity), places where "internal open source" and CI/CD is a given (consistent and resilient codebase) and for projects where there is work that can be parallelized and worked on in isolation (==less comms overhead), this can, and does work. 

How do I know? I did this exact approach, successfully. I also observed other people thoughtfully add more engineers with domain knowledge to keep a timeline, and ship an expanded scope.

The point is, if either the scope changes or the timeline does (gets moved forward) or people changes (e.g. people fall ill and are out for weeks), something will have to change. And working overtime is a change itself.

Know this "law", and you'll find it easier to reason on what to do when a new risk pops up on a project, adding more scope. Reduce the scope, increase the timeline, push people to work longer hours or - in rare cases - add more people to the project. I suggest sticking with the first two.

text:
"Why is Google, Apple, Amazon, or Microsoft not jumping on the full-remote work bandwagon already?" Because it makes zero sense for them. Let me explain, using the innovator's dilemma:

All of these companies are market leaders, have outsized profits, and are 4 out of the 5 most valuable companies in the world.

Full-remote work is a new trend that is taking on the tech world like a storm. It's a major shift from working in the office: a productive full-remote team needs to work very differently to one that is in-office.

This is the innovator's dilemma.

Google, Apple, Amazon, Microsoft have gotten to where they are by out-innovating their former competition (e.g. Amazon out-innovating Barnes and Noble, and Goolge out-innovating Altavista and Yahoo). These companies are now firm market, and a new trend comes along that could disrupt things, again. Are they the first to adopt it? Or do they wait and see?

The only sensible approach for these companies is to wait and see. Let smaller competitors switch to full-remote working first, and observe what happens. If it's a winning formula, they copy and throw money at the problem (they have plenty).

And you can see this happen real time. Amazon has close to 800K employees and is worth 14x as much as their competitor, Shopify. Shopify has 7,000 employees (Amazon likely has 10x more people just working in tech) and has gone full-remote.

Amazon will wait and see how this full-remote work trend works to, and likely do small, internal experiments. If it's a winning formula, they'll adopt it, as one of the last companies.

But if COVID is gone, and things go back to as they were before? Amazon now has the option to decide what will work better for their business, and what approach will help them keep their market-leading position.

This is the innovator's dilemma: those who won the market by innovating earlier now stop innovating, as innovating too fast could cause them to lose that same market. They instead wait, then copy, and this approach is usually the winning strategy to hold on to their leading position.

#business #strategy #remotework #bigtech

text:
"We've seen a hot tech job market just like this during the Dotcom Boom. Is it not exactly the same this time?" No. I see six major differences:

1. Root cause. The Dotcom Boom was driven by the hype of the growth of the internet, and how rapidly this growth would happen. Today's hot market is driven by several forces triggered by COVID, a minority of them based on hype. If anything, many of them are driven based on companies' fear of falling behind in the global tech race.

2. Impact. The "blast radius" of the Dotcom Boom was much smaller and mostly US-based. Tech was also several magnitudes smaller back then. This one is global. Companies are equally struggling in places like LatAm or Asia to hire as they are in the US.

3. Hiring juniors / people with little to no tech experience. In the Dotcom Boom, this was happening very much. Right now it is not. This one is a massive difference and shows how much more complex technology has become in twenty years.

4. Remote. This was not an option during the Dotcom Boom. Much of tech hiring on the other hand this time is remote.

5. Sustainability. The Dotcom Boom was followed by Dotcom Bust. The current heated tech market is unlikely to have a bust - save for a few pockets, like the crypto/web3 segment that could see a crypto/web3 winter - or even the bubble busting - at one point.

6. Cash compensation. During the Dotcom Boom, emphasis was mostly on stock upsides. During this hiring boom, cash is king - alongside stocks, but well-funded startups are already competing with big tech base salaries.

I wrote a longer article with advice on how to make the most of this tech market - link in the comments.

text:
Looked back on all the side-projects I built alongside my full-time or part-time jobs over the past 15 years. Though most of them are dead, I learned a lot from them. Some brought indirect career opportunities my way.

I quit my fulltime job at Uber late 2020, so the "side projects" became projects.

I never started any of my side projects for the money. I started some to learn something new - like build a game. Others I kicked off when I saw an opporunity to be early on a new platform: like social apps becoming big on social sites in 2009, or Windows Phone launching 2010. And some I did to scratch an itch I had - like not knowing what time my bus would arrive when commuting home in Edinburgh.

Looking back, many of these side projects helped me practice skills useful for entrepreneurs: investing money and time upfront in ideas, building things that many people want, trying out new approaches, and turning some of the promising ideas into businesses. Most importantly: killing projects that were going nowhere, and reflecting on why I was wrong about my assumptions.

text:
Companies that I personally refer to as *tech* startups / scaleups: ones where *every* engineer has equity from day one and technology is at their core.

There is no better way to align engineering interests and the company one, than by making engineers owners from day one. Not just verbally, but in practice.

Ideally, all employees would be allocated equity on joining with a vesting cliff (aka you need to meet expectations and still be with the company for eg a year to get this equity). And top performers get additional allocation. But for tech startups I refer to, it is a must to allocate for tech people the very least, and have it included in offers.

Most tech-focused startups in the US are tech startups/scaleups, and they offer equity to hires outside the US as well. There is a growing number of companies in Europe where equity is a given for engineers. Private companies that fit this definition in Europe include mobile.dev, Fonoa  Craft Docs, Linear, FindHotel, Catawiki, MessageBird, Miro, Relive, incident.io, TravelPerk, Dott, Bitrise, Shapr3D, TrueLayer, Bolt and  many other companies.

If you work as a software engineer at venture-funded company with no equity: know there are many tech startups where on top of a fair salary, you get a tangible owernship stake as part of offers handed out.

What other tech startups or scaleups do you know of in Europe?

Ones mentioned in the comments: Penta, Juni, Kiwi.com, Personio, Capmo, HelloFresh.
