posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Update from Checkout.com:

"It sounds like you spoke to only one engineer before writing your post. I can confirm that a significant proportion of Checkout.com employees (including engineers) do in fact have equity. As was pointed out, we are not a US-headquartered corporation, and in the US equity-based compensation is more widespread - but a significant proportion of our employees have equity nonetheless."

Original post:
----
I asked an early Checkout.com engineer what their equity stake was worth in the company. Even 0.01% is $4M in a $40B company. The answer was not what I expected:

“Zero. Early engineers and early employees got no equity: no stock, no options.”

“It’s a bitter feeling” - the person continued. “We built the foundations of the company and received none of the later fruits of the labour that is common in startups via equity.”

Early engineers can typically treceive 0.05-0.5% in VC-funded companies that allocate equity stakes in-line with industry standards. Even after dilution, this would have amounted for millions of dollars in gains - tens of millions at “regular” startups allocating what’s more common in the tech industry. 

So why did this employee stick around for so many years? They did not, after realising equity was off the table.

“I left years later, after it was clear equity is out of the question for employees. I joined a startup where I was allocated fair equity - getting ownership was what drove my search. I am happy I did so - as the startup grew, so have my gains, in-line with my contributions and the risk I took. I’m just sad I had to leave Checkout.com to be an owner in the startup I work at.”

My take is this: you can’t call a company a tech startup if it doesn’t issue equity to its engineers - and ideally to all employees.

Early employees at Uber owned stakes worth well above $10M by the time the company was valued at $40B and even later joiners got significant equity. Much of those equity gains flowed back to the startup ecosystem, with more than 200 Uber alumni startups started, a good part of them thanks to the equity gains these employees realized, and they now had a financial safety net to found a startup.

This fundraising is wonderful news to all people with meaningful equity at Checkout.com - very few who are current or former employees. The Checkout.com mafia will not be comparable to the PayPal mafia or Uber mafia despite similar company valuations: employees don’t have even comparable gains than those companies had. Sad for the ecosystem.

When joining a high-growth startup: remember it’s no startup if they don’t allocate equity.

#equity #startups #career

text:
"What are approaches to hire junior/entry-level software enginers?" The latest issue of my newsletter goes deep into this topic.

Before you decide on how to hire, start with team dynamics. Make sure that the newly-hired entry-level engineers will have sufficient support in the organization. Balance this with how much the output of the team will slow as they invest in helping the new joiners onboard and grow.

The three most-common models teams follow are:

1. The "inverted pyramid": a conservative and battle-tested approach where there are more seniors than entry-level people. This helps with plenty of mentorship and the team's output not slowing much. However, it’s the most expensive setup, and the hardest to hire for.

2. The "cooking pot". Roughly a 1:1 mapping between senior and entry-level engineers. This allows for either seniors to mentor early career folks, or mid-level engineers to step in and do this - also growing as they do.

3. Bottom-heavy approaches. The easiest to hire for, and the one with most risk of poor experience for entry-level engieers. This setup I've observed work well when there are cohorts of early-career engineers and there's dedicated support, plus the business acknowledges the team will have to slow down to speed up.

Read more on hiring tactics and advice, from running internship programs through partnering with bootcamps to tailoring the interview process, and the many benefits of hiring early-career engineers: https://lnkd.in/gfygFU5X (this is a 🔒 subscriber-only article)

#hiring #career #bigtech #startups

text:
You'd think that tech startups with $1B, $4B, $6B, $10B, $17B valuations operate pretty well. In reality, many of these places are held together by duct tape. When inside: you can barely believe it.

By duct tape, I mean key operations based on manually merging Google Sheets, outages across the place, the lack of basic engineering hygene in key systems, and the list goes on.

While it can be unexpected to see how "messy" things are in the inside: it's also an opportunity to fix things, and have an outsized impact by doing so.

It's why I see people thrive at these places who have previously worked at more "organized" tech companies. These people know where the scaleup needs to go, and the most important things that need fixing. 

It's why I suggest to people to work at a scrappy startup and a well-run tech company. Once you've done both, you have the experience to thrive and have an outsized impact in both environments.

#startups #reality #bigtech

text:
“I like my company, they treat me well, but I got an offers with a 50-200% pay increase as an experienced software engineer/manager. I'd feel guilty leaving. What is your take?”

I am getting more messages along the lines of these questions. My advice is this:

You now know your market value. 

If you really like your company, you can offer them to stay for XX% less than this value. Base this percentage on the mission, your career opportunities, the people, and other things important to you.

I know people who offered to take a 20% cut from their market value, some going as high as 30%. These discussions are negotiation grounds for not just higher salary, but getting more equity (or finally getting equity if you have none!), or even working fewer days per week.

Your company should know they are now below the market, and you are doing them a favor for staying. It’s a business: they should offer things in return.

No one benefits from keeping your market value to yourself.

#advice #career #salary #startups

"I'm a senior engineer who got promoted but only got a 10% raise. Given the hot market, I'm disappointed. What do you think?"

Your company clearly did not get the memo on how much the software engineering market has moved up. 10-15% raises early 2022 will be baseline for tech companies aware of market dynamics *without promotions*. Several companies have made larger one-off adjustments during 2021. Here's some data I collected on this: https://lnkd.in/efJeWG6t

I would tell your company you are disappointed, and that the market has moved much more.

And you can always vote with your feet. Look around on the market, and get feedback on your market value. If you like your team and company, offer your company that you'll stay on if they can match this value, or come close enough.

Companies who don't move with the market are already losing some of their best engineers. This is even more the case if they don't even issue equity.

I cover the software engineering compensation market in my newsletter: https://lnkd.in/dT7j3x-F (these are paywalled articles, as acting on this information is a strategic advantage - just as not being aware, or not acting on them can be costly in terms of attrition).

text:
“Do I choose Big Tech or a scaleup as my next career step?” an engineer asks. My take:

If you want to maximise career options, and you never worked at Big Tech, choose Big Tech / Google.

Having Google on your resume opens 10x more doors than having a scaleup… except if that scaleup becomes the next Google on the next few years.

Pedigree is very real in tech, especially at more senior positions. If you have proved you are good enough to get in, and thrive at companies considered the top of the market, doors open to:

1. All other Big Tech companies

2. Scaleups that want to hire expertise that people at Big Tech have

3. Opportunities to start a company with the experience you gathered

4. Charge more for your expertise thanks to having worked at the likes of Google if you decide to contract

5. Retire earlier if you decide to maximise your compensation - and it’s hard to beat Google and similar companies in this game.

 #career #advice #bigtech #startups

text:
"I've got an offer from an early-stage startup. The base salary is 15-20% more than my current total compensation as a senior software engineer at an established company. I'm hesitating because of the risky nature of startups though. What is your advice? Oh, I get equity on top of this very strong salary." 

Up to a few years ago, getting an offer from a startup meant taking a financial risk - basically, a 15-30% paycut from your current base salary. The fact that you have to take no such paycut and get more money at an early-stage startup is both incredible, but also reflects the very strong startup funding environment. Startup seed funding rounds are what Series A rounds used to be 4-5 years ago.

Before you make the decision, be sure to "reverse interview" the future team: the founder(s) and the longest tenured engineer(s). I wrote an article on this, and linked in the comments.

Assuming it looks good after the reverse interview and no red flags: if you want an adventure, learning - and more cash in your pocket - take it. If you want zero risk, stay where you are - and stop interviewing at startups. It will only make you think "what if" years later.

#advice #career #startups

text:
Advice for the new year for those with busy calendars: color code yours! Here's how I did it:

When I was an engineering manager at Uber, I had lots of meetings and teams towards the end. I felt overwhelmed.

Color coding my calendar helped differentiate between my types of meetings (recurring? One-off? 1:1? Very important? Hiring? Personal time?)

These color codes really helped me:

1. Cut down on recurring meetings. There should be very, very few of these - they are a huge drain! I could easily spot these, and figure out if they were needed, if I was needed, or if they could be replaced by an email or chat messages.

2. Balance 1:1s better throughout the week, as a manager. I wanted to do 1:1s when I had good energy, and not have to rush to and from them.

3. Record "not available for meetings" time. For me, leaving on time on certain days, and not taking evening meetings on certain was a priority. This was tricky, given I worked across several timezones: without making it a focus, it would have not happened.

4. Visualize interviews & important meetings that I could not be late for, and ones I had to often prepare for.

If you're not yet color coding your calendar: give it a go to define catgories that matter to you!

#productivity #advice #bigtech #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I decided to not publish the article "Debunking Bunq" and made a new year's resolution about (not) writing these types of articles.

After months' long of getting messages from current and former employees, I had lots of information on how the first - and only - challenger bank in the Netherlands works. 

However, as I edited, read, and re-read the article, it started to feel the article reads like a hit piece with no real takeways. People reading it would get entertainment, but it would not help with how to build better companies and organizations. Even worse, it would not help anyone currently or previously having worked at the company: in fact, it would alienate many of these people, and do this for no good reason.

I took a step back and decided I don't want to write about negatives about a specific startup, singling them out. Building any startup is hard. I can call out negatives for every single startup or scaleup, no matter how successful they are. It's too easy to then bunk on these negatives and the things that don't work well.

What is hard is the opposite: find what works in successful companies, *why* those approaches work, and offer ways other startups can try these approaches. This is what I have been writing about, and will continue to do so. This approach is more "boring", and will get less views: but it's one which is educational, not just dramatic.

And with that, I want to close with three things Bunq is doing, that very few, if any other companies do, and do this well:

1. Taking on an existing industry where there's little space for new companies. Banks have not had disruption for decades: yet Bunq is the only company in the Netherlands to even try to change this. By doing so, they are forcing the incubent banks to respond 10x faster than they normally would, which is a win for everyone.

2. Bunq hires juniors, *in bulk*. They take on people with zero to six months' professional experience. I don't know any other product company who does this. Many of these people leave a short amount of time (1-2 years): but they get that "foot in the door" and many are better for it (those who don't burn out).

3. They advertise what they are - at least a good part of it. You should expect not great pay, hard work, longer hours than most places and no upfront equity offered when you join, and to work the Bunq way. Read the reviews on e.g. Glassdoor and decide if it's for you (it will not be, for many, but it will be for others). They keep these promises, from what I have heard. Like many startups, it's not a place many people will enjoy, but some do thrive.

Don't expect a fixed roadmap and an easy ride or a focus on work-life-balance or boundaries. Do expect to have more access to what it takes to build a bank on a budget, and - perhaps - succeed against the odds with an unconventional approach. Don't expect most people to stay here for long: ask people who left in a similar role on more details on why.

"What are the most common Staff Engineer archetypes?" Will Larson wrote an excellent book on this topic (Staff Engineer), and here are the four:

1. The Tech Lead: guiding the execution of a particular team. They might or might not manage a few people.

2. The Architect: owning a critical area, responsible for the direction and approach. The are usually spans well beyond one team.

3. The Solver: digs deep into complex problems, often taking longer periods on these.

4. The Right Hand: extends the attention of an executive - like a CTO or a VP of Engineering. Especially common at large organizations.

In the article "Staff archetypes" (link in the comments), Will sketched what the calendar for the week for each of these Staff archetypes could look like. It's an interesting view of how these roles differ.

text:
This is what survivor bias looks like. I had a what I'd call a successful career. Still, I made £68k/year ($90K) at the age of 27 in London, and £26K/year ($34K) two years before, at 25, when I lived in Edinburgh.

I felt very lucky throughout. I got paid to do something I loved - writing software - worked with people whom I learned from every day and made enough not just to pay rent, but to eat out and have some left to travel.

Today, I know people working in Big Tech making above $500K/year at the age of 27. They are less than 0.05% of the software engineering population.

Both the employee path and the entrepreneur one is viable for software engineers. And both are rewarding in many ways, not just financially.

But stop comparing yourself with outlier numbers like this. Focus on your own progress.

Trust yourself, not a process.

#motivation

article.title: Working with Product Managers: Advice from PMs
article.subtitle: newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What are approaches for eng manager and engineers to work better with product managers? This week's subscriber-only article covers this topic in-depth.

Here's three pieces of advice you can take as an engineer, to work closer with product, especially applicable at big tech and high-growth startups:

1. Understand how the business works. 

How does the company make money? What is the role of your team in this? What is growing, what is profitable, what is not? Do your research, ask people on the team, including your manager and the product person.

2. Build a relationship with your PM. 

Request to have a 1:1 catchup with them. At this session, make it clear that you’d like to learn. Offer to help them, too. Ask about pieces of work from a product perspective that you can do, which would help the product manager or the team.

3. Offer to co-write a Product Requirements Document (PRD). 

This exercise is one of the best ways to build more empathy with what product work is like. I especially suggest this approach after you have a product idea that’s promising. Ask the product manager if they’d be okay with you writing a product spec and giving you guidance on it. Tell them this exercise will help you learn about the product, will also hopefully result in less work for the PM, and help the team.

At Uber, every time an engineer co-wrote a PRD with the product manager, they came out of this exercise with lots of learnings on product, and a newfound product sense.

The weekly longform article covers more advice for engineers, and lots of approaches for engineering managers to build a strong product relationship: https://lnkd.in/deSC_s-V (🔒subscriber-only post)

#productmanagement #softwareengineering #bigtech #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Crypto startups are starting to pay more cash compensation than any similar startup and are raising huge amounts if money.

We’ve seen this before, and I cannot shake how similar the rise of web3/crypto/NFT startups are to the Dotcom Boom.

1. Dotcom Boom companies paid a *lot* more cash and equity for employees versus other tech companies, with the expectation of becoming millionaires in 6-18 months.

Same thing is driving the hiring success for web3 companies who are burning cash like there’s no tomorrow, paying above market. Base salaries for senior engineers can be in the $200-500K range in the US, and not much lower when working remote.

2. Just like with the Dotcom Boom, it’s impossible for people without deep understanding separate the garbage and scam projects from the few really good ones.

It’s incredibly noisy. Some projects run off with money. Others have zero expertise, but know influencers etc.

3. During the Dotcom Boom,lots of technology innovation was a decade ahead of its time.

WebVan .com was Instacart / Ocado / Picnic and similar twenty years early, with not enough customers, not enough trust, and too difficult infra challenges.

DeFi startups face the same hurdle: lack of customers, trust, infra.

4. For the Dotcom Boom, there was the Dotcom Bust.

It’s impossible to predict if this will follow: but I do predict a crypto winter. All crypto startups need hypergrowth in users/revenue to survive & success depends on mainstream adoption.

I expect some adoption: not mainstream. This will drive many if these startups out of business. So if you do accept a web3 offer, make sure it’s not just for tokens, but hard cash.

#prediction #crypto #hiring #startups

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Hot take: stop holding on to the Agile Manifesto like some sort of holy grail of four sentences. Companies who ignored the Agile Manifesto and still succeeded:

Apple, Amazon, Microsoft, Facebook, Google.

Those companies are also the five most valuable companies in the world. They work how in ways that works for *them*, which might or might not fit into any of those four sentences, but they sure don’t care. And they are all different working styles and cultures!

Others notable mentions: Tesla, Twitter, Coinbase, Roblox, Robinhood, Uber, DoorDash, Lyft… and most of big tech.

Figure out what works for you, in your context. And don’t forget the context of the Agile Maifesto: it was written 20 years ago by a group that was (and is) overwhelmingly in consulting.

text:
How can product and engineering work better together? Here's an idea: map out who owns what part of the process, all the way until after shipping.

Sit down with the product manager and draw out all activities you both do in order to ship software. Start from a blank sheet and add areas. Once these are there, discuss which of you is ultimately responsible for getting that work done.

And don't forget to update regularly, as the team changes how they work, or the type of features and products they ship.

I'll be writing more about ways engineering can work well with product in the next issue of The Pragmatic Engineer.

As inspiration, here's a map similar to what a team working within a larger tech company came up with. 

The details are less important: every team will come up with vastly different definitions, maps, phases. It’s the process of talking through “what types of work do we do? Who owns that part? What are any “handover points” (like the PRD / product requirements document in this example), if any?

text:
A story I can now share: in ~2018, some figured out they can take free Uber rides thanks to a bug. They set up a Whatsapp group to profit from it. This was the first time you could use WhatsApp to take Uber rides.

If you logged into this group, you had to tell where you wanted to go, pay upfront (about 30% of the “regular”) fee and *BOOM* an Uber appeared and took you to your destination.

How do I know this? I was responsible for introducing this bug, when my team shipped Google Pay support in Uber and we missed an edge case. You could perform a few steps including a VPN connection faking location somewhere else, then a few more steps to get an account where in a country like India you could get free rides.

We realized there’s a bug when someone working at Uber got invited to this group and was wondering how is it possible to get cheap Uber rides on WhatsApp.

We fixed the bug and learned from the mistake (and how to prevent it). People got cheaper rides and some people pocketed money while the bug lasted. Drivers never noticed anything (they got paid irrespective of the bug) and it was Uber that took the loss.

I’m glad there’s now an official way to book an Uber ride through WhatsApp though :)

#uber #whatsapp #bug #story

This is my brother taking a call coming from Apple CEO Tim Cook. 

For many years, Balint would share his frustration on how there’s no writing app or documents solution that works *seamlessly* like you’d expect in 2016. None in 2017. Still none in 2018. All apps seemed to be designed “good enough” for everyone, often coming from a web-first approach. None of them “felt” seamless or native on the devices many tech professionals use every day like on Mac, iPad, iPhone and switching between them on the go.

“Why don’t you build one?” 

In 2019, he did. It’s called Craft Docs.

Apple just named it the Mac App of the Year for 2021 - this was why Tim Cook called.

I could not be more proud. I’ve been using the app since alpha and do most of my writing with it. If you’re not using it yet: search “Craft Docs” and start using it for free.

This is also a reminder that you shouldn’t settle for “good enough”. If you find an opportunity where the world seems to be happy enough with mediocre, but you keep being frustrated with what is available: do what my brother did.

Build the future.

(And massive congrats to the whole team behind Craft Docs. They’re just getting started and hiring. Now is the best time to get onboard.)

text:
If you told me in early 2021 that Facebook would recruit full-remote engineers during 2021, I would have not believed you. I'd assumed that with Google, Apple, Amazon they would want people back in offices.

Yet after a challenging year for Facebook: here we are.

I'm hearing the biggest reason for changing their stance and opening up full-remote hiring is how, in the US, on the West Coast (Menlo Park and Seattle), hiring is going very badly for Facebook. Candidate accepts are at historic lows. New York is at level, but slightly down. London is doing fine, but there's only so many people they can hire. And that was all 4 of the major Facebook engineering offices.

So they decided to double down hiring remote: something the Facebook/Meta recruitment team forecasted would allow them to hire senior engineers much-much quicker.

In this first phase of remote hiring, 7 European countries are opened - these are locations Facebook/Meta is setting up entities. These are: Germany, Italy, Ireland, France, Poland, Netherlands, Spain.

The first verbal offers are already going out, and in many countries like Poland, the start dates will be April 2022.

text:
Tech interviews that trigger performance anxiety - such as live coding - could exclude up to 40% of the candidate population, according to a study.

Quoting the study: "For example, a large population of people are impacted by performance anxiety (estimated 40 percent of all adults in the U.S. [23]). Collectively, otherwise qualified candidates—who happen to perform poorly due to performance anxiety–could be excluded from fair consideration for hiring. Furthermore, scientific evidence finds that women experience disproportionately more negative effects from test and performance anxiety [25, 46, 65], which could explain our observations."

Big Tech and well-known companies have the luxury to exclude such a larger engineering population: they'll get enough applicants from the remaining 60%.

The question is: does your team and company have the same luxury? If not, I suggest looking at ways to reduce or remove settings that trigger this anxiety.

Even if you do live coding, take suggestions from this study, such as doing a warmup interview, allowing for a free reset, not starting from a blank state (doing partial program sketches instead) and allowing candidates to work from their own environment/IDE over using yet another online coding sandbox.

Study: Does Stress Impact Technical Interview Performance? from NC State University and Microsoft.

#hiring #anxiety #tech #softwareengineering

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
There's more talk about hiring divese teams. Yet there's no talk on how live coding interviews are biased against competent software engineers who are women, from underrepresented groups, and those who have performance anxiety in these interview settings. 

The above is based on all evidence I am seeing - both hard data and anecdotal. I have not come across any evidence pointing to the opposite.

The past weeks I have talked with several women developers who all said the same: they are good at their job, but freeze on interviews, do very poor, and usually get rejected. Most of them have started to look for places that don't have live coding, and do more collaborative approaches like code reviews, or take-home tasks. Many of them move companies through referrals.

Former Apple engineer Cher Scarlett no longer engages with companies who do live coding or whiteboard exercises. She writes: 

"These interviews case me (and a lot of other women and under-represented people) severe anxiety and physiological damage. Despite that I obviously have navigated tech and climbed into great places in spite of these types of these interview processes, I am just exhausted with it and want it to change." https://lnkd.in/dsinKx2S

Netflix senior software engineer Jenn Creighton shared on the Single-threaded Podcast how she struggles in these unnatural settings:

"I also am not good being watched. I'm not good in that sort of like unnatural pressure setting. I'm not good without access to Google. I'm just not like, I will forget things. And I need to remember how they work. Because our field, you could never remember all of it in your head. But interviews kind of act as if you're supposed to." 

Listen to the full episode here, it's well worth it: https://lnkd.in/d7iqisHX

Before men jump in to say "but there's no research on this and anecdotal evidence doesn't count even if it comes from established women in the field", there actually is research. NC State University ran a research with 48 participants that confirmed that the in the "traditional" live coding interview format, participants did worse than working on their own, in a private setting. In a damning outcome, all women were rejected with the live coding setting, while all women working privately passed. 

Read the whole study: "Tech Sector Job Interviews Assess Anxiety, Not Software Skills" https://lnkd.in/dSsVGN5e

It's time to rethink the live coding interview for any company that wants to hire inclusively. 

#diversity #bias #hiring #softwareengineering

text:
When you think about it, tech hiring is optimised for zero-trust and verifying everything. Kind of like blockchain and most of crypto.

And this approach makes sense when you’re a large company with lots of resources, and have a lot to lose with a poor hire. This is big tech, and their hiring process needs to assume no trust.

But for smaller companies and startups, do you want to go with the same assumption? Can you even have people entertain the same heavyweight hiring process - where you start with a basic coding challenge that feels insulting to most experienced engineer? 

Most candidates humor the heavyweight interviews for Big Tech, knowing it's the "price of entry" to that elite club. But for a barely known company: what does it tell beyond they copy the big guys when they're not one?

The best startups I know build more lightweight interview processes and either gather additional signals from referrals or do more signal gathering during a trial period. 

This is a fancy way of saying the better startups take more chances on people, then verify. Because if they did not do this, they would fail to hire, and fail to grow.

An example of startups like this? Like when mobile.dev asked me about Dmitry Zaytsev who applied to work there and if he’s any good.

I hired Dmitry at Uber. And he wasn’t good. He was great.

(And yes, we’re still hiring for full-remote positions mobile.dev as we’re building the new standard for mobile development. All employees get strong salaries plus generous equity)

 #hiring #tech #bigtech

text:
Last week I shared how some companies have recently increased salaries by 15-30% for senior engineers. He's a message from an engineer that explains why these companies moved so early.

Big Tech is recruiting aggressively, and a senior engineer shared how they got a 50% increase in France, to work remotely for one of these companies (without having to move). This person is thrilled.

If you're an engineer, now is a great time to talk about raises for next year, asking for more equity (if you're in a startup) and testing out the market if you're feeling the company is not investing in people.

But where does this leave startups and scaleups, and hiring managers at these places?

1. Equity, equity, equity. If you're a growing company and not yet issuing equity to all engineers, explaining the value, and the growth potential, you're at high risk of losing these people. All the fast-growing startups I know are issuing generous equity: and not only are they hiring, but they're sometimes successfully hiring away from Big Tech (thanks to the equity upside plus strong base salary). mobile.dev (raised $3M, I'm an advisor: we are hiring some of the best mobile folks and yes, from Big Tech as well), Fonoa (just raised $25M, I'm an investor), Linear and many other "forward-thinking" startups all fit this trend.

2. Move with the market. The cost of senior engineers has moved up in the past 12 months, by about 30%. You'll need to either have to budget for this for next year, or...

3. Budget with more junior, fewer senior engineers. The market for less experienced engineers is one where there are far more applicants than positions and salaries have barely inched upwards. Most companies are unwilling to hire less experienced engineers, and don't want to invest in them. If you don't have the budget to move up another 30%: this is the queue where you *need* to become great at hiring, onboarding, and growing junior engineers.

4. Budget with fewer engineers. For next year's budget, if you assumed $X will allow you to hire 10 senior engineers, I suggest to revise to $X being sufficient for 7 engineers. Make plans accordingly on your product roadmap. Consider buying solutions over building, even if they cost $10K/year (which is still the fraction of a senior engineer's salary).

5. Review how you hire. If you're copying Big Tech hiring methods but you don't have the budget that Big Tech has... you'll lose when playing this game. Design a friendlier, faster and less stressful interview process. Take risks on people that Big Tech would not do (also goes back to #3 - hire engineers with less experience)

This market is fantastic for engineers, and challenging for employers. 

In both cases: the more information you have, the better you can prepare, and the more you can make of this market.

#salary #career #bigtech #startups

text:
Most engineers are, by default, terrible interviewers. Yet what do most startups do? "Your first interview will be with {developer} (who, by the way, never received any interview training or bias training)."

And then the same companies complain at how hard it is to hire engineers, and how they just can't seem to find diverse candidates who "meet their bar".

A conversation I had with such a startup:

"So where do most candidates drop off in the process?"

"It's during the technical screen."

"Who conducts this screen?"

"{Developer1} or {Developer2}."

"Have they gotten any interview training on what signals to gather, how to conduct interviews?"

"Uh... we talked about this once with them. But not a training, no."

"Any bias training on how to be aware of unconscious biases, similarity bias or similar ones?"

"I don't see why this is relevant."

"You should look into it yourself if you truly want to hire a diverse team. But let's move on. Have you, as the hiring manager shadowed them to observe how they interview, work with the candidate, or give feedback?"

"No, I never did this. I trust them."

"Okay, so I have good news, and I have bad news. The good news is there are plenty of people could hire and would be great employees. They are already in your pipeline. The bad news is that without investing in interviewers, training, feedback loops, and getting way more involved as a hiring manager, your current interview process and interviewers will keep rejecting these people."

#hiring #startup #biases

text:
As I'm building my business around The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter, I keep experimenting and iterating. Here's the latest change: no more job ads for paying subscribers (or any types of ads).

I started a job board, as an experiment, inspired by several paid newsletters that have a similar approach, with jobs embedded in the newsletter. It seemed like it could be a value-add for all newsletter subscribers, plus an additional revenue generator. For a few weeks, "Featured jobs" were listed in each newsletter.

However, there are two issues with this approach - that I could have predicted, but luckily subscribers directly told me:

1. It's still ads. And the value proposition I'd like to have is when you pay, you get no ads, no hidden ads, no sponsorship etc.

2. Many of my subscribers expense the newsletter with their employer, and more and more companies sign up to buy group subscriptions for all engineering managers. Job ads during such a hot job market are a strange thing to introduce to companies who are paying for their employees to get this newsletter to grow professionally (and, ideally, the company benefit from their growth).

So the experiment is killed for paying subscribers. I'll still experiment with the job board at the bottom of the free newsletter, and on the bottom of the articles in my blog. As with any experiment, after more data is in, it will be easier to decide to keep, to change or to retire this the experiment.

Although not deliberate, this process is pretty similar to how we ran experiments at Uber!

#experiment #business #learnings

text:
I’ve seen this take of “publish the damn salary” so many times, so let me give the other side of the story:

Companies who publish salary ranges see a LOT more applicants, assuming the salary range is *above* what people are currently making. 

Every hiring manager knows this. They also know they are getting fewer applicants than their competitors who publish the ranges. So why are companies not publishing them? 5 reasons:

1. They have enough candidates applying already. 

Notice how eg the corner shop will publish “employees wanted - $13/hr?”. They don’t have enough applicants. What about Google, who already has more than 100 applicants for each role and struggle to get back to everyone? Publishing the range would result in double the applicants only. Do they need more? Probably not.

2. Salary is not the whole story on compensation. 

In tech, at the top of the the market, salary is not the whole story: cash bonuses and equity can make up more than the salary portion for senior positions. I wrote more on this in The Trimodal Nature of Software Engineering Salaries (link in the comments). Publishing the salary range would "cheapen" the position, and candidates who otherwise would apply - and get large compensation packages - would pass.

3. Flexibility and negotiation. 

By publishing ranges upfront, the company leaves less negotiation space for themselves. The reality of the tech job market is that many companies do negotiate: and it’s much easier if they don’t say the first number.

4. Different salary based on leveling.

Salary will often be tied to the internal level. And the level often determined by the interview performance. So publishing this information could result in an overt large range.

5. Existing employees making less.

It’s common that a company needs to pay more to hire a new recruit than what existing employees get. This is even more so true in tech, right now, thanks to the hot job market. Publishing these ranges could let to existing employees quitting. This is one of the very real reasons many companies will never advertise salaries externally.

I’m the end, my take is that it comes down to supply and demand. When companies are lesser-known or want more applicants, they start to publish salary ranges.

Just like a lesser-known startup, incident.io is doing with their Product Engineer role in London, which is £70-130K in salary plus 0.2-0.8% in equity (apply link in the comments).

I’m an investor in incident.io: I was very impressed with the founding team, and how they are building one of the best incident response products, globally. And they publish their compensation range - at least for now (I can’t guarantee they’ll keep doing so when they’ll be well-known or have too many applicants!)

#salary #jobadvert #transparency

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
"I signed an offer, but I received a *lot* better one. What happens if I turn down my signed one?"

The same thing that would happen if the company rescinded your contract after signing (surprise: this also happens!), but in reverse. The hiring manager will get upset, but life will go on.

You'll likely permanently burn a bridge with this company and the hiring manager: the same way companies burn a bridge with a candidate when they rescind the offer before starting.

My two cents as a hiring manager: I'd rather people turn down (signed) offers than hand in their resignation on week one.

If you're someone in this situation and feel bad: know that it's happening a lot (thanks to the crazy hot market). People walk back on signed offers both for better offers they received later on and for counteroffers that their company has offered when they announced they will quit.

Hiring managers, also prepare for this. Keep candidates engaged right until they start. A piece of advice shared in my newsletter is this:

text:
Big news for software engineers in Europe: Facebook (Meta) is starting *remote* hiring from seven European countries to work with the London engineering office.

Why is this big news?

Because it will add to the already hot software engineering market, as Facebook is planning to hire thousands of engineers and dozens - if not hundreds - of engineering managers this way.

Facebook - like Google, Netflix and several other Big Tech - pays close to the top of the market, wherever they hire and they go head-to-head with most other compensation offers. This makes Facebook one of the biggest "big tech" who is hiring remote in more than one or two countries in Europe. I'm also expecting them to set the new remote rates in these new countries - and will likely move the existing bands upwards.

Twitter hires remote in Europe: though in fewer countries, as far as I know (UK, Germany, Spain, Ireland). Spotify hires remote in several European countries: but their compensation is a tier below Facebook. Stripe hires remote in a few countries and compensates similar to Facebook: but they do so in much smaller numbers and I've yet to hear Italy or Poland hires.

If you are a hiring manager in Germany, the Netherlands, Ireland, Spain, Poland, France, or Italy you'll likely feel the impact of this hiring spree soon enough.

Just a friendly heads up so you can get ready! You'll need to: both on offering remote-friendly options ("if Facebook can do it, why can't my company?"), and think about your strategy to reward and retain your best engineers.

For more advice on hiring managers on how to respond, see an article I wrote in the first comment.

I'll also send more updates with numbers on the most heated tech job market for all The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter subscribers later the week.

#hiring #career #remote #facebook

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
There’s no reason any startup should copy big tech hiring processes.

Startups have an upper hand by being more nimble with hiring - those startups, that is, that have moved beyond copying big tech approaches what won’t even work for them.

Big tech is hiring the way they do as their goals are different. They need a process that is scaleable enough so thousands of interviewers can more or less consistently evaluate candidates. They worry more about not hiring anyone who doesn't work out - false positives - over passing on people who could have worked out - false negatives.

Big tech will always win the compensation & brand recognition game if you go head-to-head with them, using the same approach and selection criteria. So play a different game.

Hiring faster, more personable and more inclusive - basically better - is the leverage every startup has.

Unless you're big tech, forget about repeating “we have a high hiring bar” and competing for the exact same profile that big tech is bringing in. Take risks on people that big tech would never take, have a high onboarding bar and have a “we make people better engineers quicker” approach.

#hiring #tech #startup #bigtech

text:
The difference in how Big Tech and traditional companies view internships is shocking. Especially in how traditional companies don't blink an eye to pay embarrassingly low wages for software engineer interns. A story.

A student got an offer for a summer internship at TomTom, in Amsterdam. The offer? €500/month. This is 1/3rd of the statutory minimum wage of €1,500/month in the Netherlands. 

Sadly, €500/month is the common amount most Dutch-founded tech companies and startups offer for internship compensation. They claim that software engineer interns are not doing regular work but are learning, taking advantage of a loophole in the regulation on work placement wages. They view interns both as cheap labor and also think that they are doing a favor for interns by giving them exposure to their work environment.

The problem was, this student did not have enough savings to supplement this monthly stipend to rent a room in Amsterdam. So although they wanted to work at TomTom, they had to decline due to finances. This person then applied to other internships, hoping some would pay enough for at least rent.

Big tech view internships as an investment. They aim to hire promising students, pay them well - on top of a salary, they usually allocate separately for housing. They involve them in production work throughout their internship and hope they will come back after graduation.

This person heard Uber pays above minimum wage for internships, and so they applied after TomTom would not increase their offer.

Software engineer interns at Uber are paid just below the Software Engineer compensation. They get around €4,500/month, housing included. This is important for two reasons. First, it sends the message that they are valued similarly to how a full-time employee is. Second, it allows people with no significant savings to take on internships without worrying about how they can pay for housing or food.

This intern turned out to be one of my best-ever engineering hires, contributed to an important production feature during their internship, and came back after graduation to have a great career and wide impact at Uber.

I sometimes think of the impact and career this person would have had at TomTom, if only they would have paid a minimum living wage for interns, not pushing them to look elsewhere. I also wonder how many other amazing future engineers traditional-thinking companies lose because they refuse to pay even minimum wage for internships.

If you work at a company that struggles to hire enough senior software engineers, ask the question: how much do you value your interns? How many future senior software engineers have you lost without knowing because you pay below minimum wage for internships?

#career #frugality #internship

text:
Some people suggest that developers do side projects, and these can help get them jobs. My experience was different. They did not help me get jobs, but they helped with something just as important.

I did lots of side projects during, and after university. When I was a developer at my first fulltime job, I built a web app that racked up 200K daily users on a competing social network to Facebook. At one point, the ad revenue from this sideproject was more than what I was making at my day job.

I built other websites, Windows Phone apps, and attended hackathons. However, when I looked for jobs, I always applied directly, and though I listed these side projects on my resume, barely anyone asked - or cared - about them.

To get my job at JP Morgan or Skype, I had to do well on those interviews, and that was pretty much all that mattered.

However, the side projects were not a waste of time. For one, they opened a lot of doors outside of work. I presented at meetups, got to meet many people from the Windows Phone community, and got invited to talk at conferences.

I also got lots of inbound opportunities that I could have taken advantage of, but did not. Nokia got in touch wanting to acquire one of my apps. Startup founders messaged asking if I'm interested to contract or work at their companies. I never took any of this on: but this was on me.

The biggest benefit, however, was how, after shipping my first few side projects, I stopped feeling confined by my job title at work.

At my day job, my role was initially limited to frontend engineering. However, on my sideprojects I owned everything, frontend to backend to database scaling.

I started to take this "ownership" mentality to work as well. I got involved with the work the backend team was doing and started to write backend code. I picked up new technologies like iOS on the job. I started to look at my day job similar to my side projects: in that I was not afraid to take ownership of the whole thing (when others could use help), not just the smaller parts allocated to me.

This is what I love about side projects: they teach you to own something you build end-to-end, even if you don't (yet) get to do it on your day job.

#sideproject #career

"How can I find a company and a position that is a step-up to where I am now, as an experienced tech professional?"

This week's newsletter answers this question, and I collected lesser-known resources than ever to help with this. Here are a few approaches:

1. For big tech roles, your best bet is to hit the career sites of well-known companies directly. Be aware that high-paying companies are competitive to get into: there's usually a tradeoff on compensation and competition.

2. For promising high-growth startups, an underrated approach is to look at the portfolios for better-known VCs. Many of them have job boards you can browse (all of which I've collected in this newsletter issue)

3. For engineering leadership positions, directly applying might be the least common way to land positions. Look to your network, and at back doors to start the application process.

Great jobs are rarely served on a plate - you often need to put in the work to find them. I've done some groundwork to help you get started easier, if you are planning to look around: https://lnkd.in/dqT3UXgp

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Uber and Shopify are effective immediately issuing the latest M1 MacBook Pros to engineers, Shopify letting people keep their Intel ones. Why and how are they doing it? The scoop:

They obsess about developer productivity, and the M1 is such a big, measurable step-up that it's an instant buy. Not just for them, but any other company who cares about engineers similar to how they do.

The longer version:

Both these companies have Developer Experience-like platform teams. People who obsess on how to make engineers more productive, between improving local dev elopement workflows, CI/CD, onboarding and other common tasks.

The most “idle” time engineers spend - especially in mobile development - is waiting on the build. These companies instrument machines so they know that on average, an engineer runs the build X times per day, waiting Y minutes or hours.

Whenever new hardware comes on true market, these teams benchmark build times, and do the math. How much time would a $4,000, top-of-the market machine like the M1 shave off from each build? How much total time over 2 years? How much “value” does this time mean per engineer?

With the M1, the answer is a no-brainier. The $4,000 per engineer spent will bring back *so* much more productivity versus top-of-the-market Intel ones, including the 2021 one.

And as a side note, the cost is not really $4,000 per engineer, but more $2,000 incremental to a more “basic” MacBook model. So it's more like $1,000/year, assuming a 2-year usage period.

If you can have engineers wait on their machine less, it’s worth every dollar to do so, considering how much cheaper hardware is than an engineer’s time. It's why these companies are switching.

The ones who care about engineers being efficient, that is.

#efficiency #bigtech #mac #uber #shopify

text:
Update: after 3 days, the outage is over. The root cause was an infra issue + a backend communications bug. Roblox promised a more detailed postmortem to come: https://lnkd.in/dSmSPHeY

A $50B company has been offline for 60 hours and counting. Here’s what we know of the Roblox outage impacting ~100M people**

Through the official channels: practically nothing. Which is unheard of in tech, for this size of an outage. Three generic updates over 2 days on their Twitter that have close to zero information (see the screenshot: this is all the updates).

The most official statement we’ve seen is Roblox confirming the issue is internal on The Verge: https://lnkd.in/dh2_26qQ

The issue started around Thursday midnight PST / Friday morning EU time.

Speculation I’ve heard (but not confirmed) ranged from internal systems issues all the way to ransomware. I’d be surprised if it was the latter though.

What we do know is Roblox builds its infra heavily on top of Portworx (storage) and the Hashicorp stack Nomad, Consul and Vault as shared in this video: https://lnkd.in/dD-MDJ-C But its unclear if the outage would have connection with any of this.

Here’s why this outage is unprecedented: I don’t know of any similar sized company being completely offline, with all 200M monthly users/players being locked out from using (and paying or earning).

It’s also unprecedented at how there is practically zero official communication on the nature of the outage. Which is a miss, as it’s a great way to engage with the engineering community (and not a bad thing for SRE recruitment!)

Sending #hugops to the Roblox team and I hope we’ll see a postmortem after the service is back up in either case.

** Roblox has ~50M daily, ~200M monthly users.

text:
An engineer created a fake CV with Instagram, Zillow, LinkedIn, Microsoft, and Berkeley on it, all details being nonsense. Got a 60% response rate. While most of the internet is in disbelief, here's what happened:

1. First, the responses came from popular tech companies like Notion, Airbnb, Reddit, Dropbox. These companies tend to get hundreds of inbound applications for each position listed.

2. Many of these companies employ so-called inbound sourcers. These are specialized recruiters whose job is to go through all inbound applications, and schedule a follow-up recruiter call with any promising ones. They usually need to go through several hundred applications per day, looking after most open roles.

3. Inbound sourcing is the least desirable recruiter position - within these companies they're often the entry-level recruiter position. These recruiters are typically the least technical among all of them. They look for signals that forecast someone being a good match. 

Years of experience paired with well-known companies are usually a good enough reason to move forward with a candidate and have an initial call with them. Did they read the bullet points? No. They didn't need to: someone with 10 YOE at these companies and Berkeley education is worth talking with.

4. Before you snicker on how a recruiter cannot spot how most of the resume is a joke, can you name the one obviously fake technology from the list of V, Brainfuck, Sleepy Puddy, VoldermortDB, Charizard, CockroachDB, Snort, John the Ripper, and Hadoop? The solution is in the first comment.

5. Before you conclude resumes like this are how you "fake it till you make it", consider the next round is with a recruiter - who talks through the resume, likely discovering things don't add up. If the candidate gets through them, on the onsite interview an engineer will call BS and likely reject the candidate.

6. Even if a person is able to fake most of their resume, most companies do due diligence and reference checks before extending an offer. Getting caught lying blacklists the person.

As a takeaway, this resume does showcase some harsh truths:

a) It's a lot easier to get an interview at a company like Notion or Airbnb once you worked at even better-known companies like Instagram or Microsoft.

b) The best way to make it through resume screens are to either have a strong pedigree (past companies, universities) or a referral. If you have either, it doesn't really matter what the details of your resume say for the first screen. However, later interviews go deeper.

Why am I writing about this? I spent years as a hiring manager at Uber, and afterward months researching and writing The Tech Resume Inside Out, an in-depth book on writing a fair software engineer resume. 

If you already work at big tech: likely don't need this book. The book is free for any software engineer who does not (yet) have a job. Get it here: https://thetechresume.com/

#resume #cv #career #bigtech

text:
How developer-friendly is your engineering culture? Answer these 12 yes-or-no questions to get a sense for it: more than 200 teams have done so.

30% of the teams who filled out the test scored a perfect 12/12, while 68% have gotten at least 10/12. The most common "missing" parts that companies or teams don't have is equity for all engineers (or generous profit sharing for non-VC-funded or public companies) and healthy oncall.

Take the test here, and check out the companies that seem like great engineering cultures, based on their results: https://lnkd.in/dbcx-wMP

A few companies where there are teams that score 12/12:

- Amazon, Apple, Facebook, Google, Microsoft, Netflix
- Atlassian, Cloudfare, GitHub, Robinhood, Robolox, Square, Twitter, Shopify, Spotify, Stripe, Uber
- Auth0, Expedia, Eventbrite, Freshbooks, HashiCorp, Intercom, Sumo Logic, Udemy, Yelp, Wise
- ClassPass, DocuSign, Gocardless, Glovo, Impala, KeepTruckin, Loom, NerdWallet, Miro, Monzo, Noom, Rippling, Scribd, Sonder, Zocdoc
- Birdie, Bud Financial, Butternut Box, CommonBond, Codat, Helge Scheil, Hubs, Indigo Ag, Kensho Technologies, Litmus, Pollen, Postscript, Orbit, Rev, Rows .com, Smartly .io, Quizlet, Northern .tech, ThoughtSpot

Note that for smaller companies, it's expected early on they won't yet score 12/12, as #9, #10 or #11 is something that often makes sense to put place above a certain size (20-30 engineers). These are the places where you should expect healthy equity allocations though.

text:
"How do you motivate yourself to regularly write, and where do you get your inspiration from?"

I decided to take longform writing seriously in 2015. For years, I published articles at least every few months - often more frequently. I did this mostly for myself: but it's proved to bring many benefits from improving my writing, to meeting new people, all the way to making a living off writing after I left Uber.

So what worked for me? These 12 principles and approaches:

1. Own your content
2. Start writing - regularly
3. Write for yourself
4. Copy writing styles you like
5. Capture ideas as they come
6. Freewrite
7. Draft
8. Edit
9. Publish
10. Feedback
11. Audience
12. Again, again again...

Here's a summary with examples on what my writing process looks like: https://lnkd.in/dSxm-Evs

Everyone finds their preferred approaches over time - that is often different to mine. But as the first step, you need to start writing, publishing what you write, and form a habit to do it regularly.

#writing #thinking #motivation

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
What advice would I give to senior engineers in how to think about career growth? This is what I wrote in this week's 🔒subscriber-only newsletter issue:

1. The industry is small, and you start to experience this 5-10 years into your career. This is the point when you start to come across former colleagues more frequently. Good impressions from years ago start to pay dividends. Bridges burnt can start to bite back.

2. Getting to the senior engineer level is relatively straightforward. Most people who spend 5-8 years in the industry get to the senior engineer level even if they make no focused effort to do so. Just by solving engineering problems for this long, you gain the experience, the toolset, and the perspective that most senior engineers need. 

3. It gets challenging after the senior engineering level. Going from the senior engineering level to the next one - typically Tech Lead or Staff engineer - is usually a clear target. However, it's not nearly as easy to make that jump as getting to the senior level. And beyond these levels, there is no playbook you can use.

4. Consider becoming an engineering manager - at least for a while, when there's an opening to do so. There is more demand and openings for engineering management in the industry than for staff and above engineers. Not only can it be easier to switch to engineering management for a while, but once you go back to being an engineer, you'll have the skills and experience to grow faster on the individual contributor track.

5. Your manager becomes increasingly important later on in your career. A manager who is on a growth path themselves and knows how to navigate the organization and influence others can help a fair bit with your growth: while a less experienced manager can significantly slow it down.

6. Joining highly regarded startups in their growth phase can strengthen your network - by a lot.

7. The more senior you become, the more your network is key for more varied and often better opportunities. Early on in your career, your interview performance tends to determine which roles you can get. As you hit the senior levels, referrals from people you know can help increase your chances of getting a role that is a step-up for you. Beyond this level, people referring you is a more common way of being considered - and getting - senior+ roles.

You'll find more advice observations on typical engineering career paths and big tech, leveling, and paths after hitting Staff engineer in the article: https://lnkd.in/dBiAx64b

#advice #career #softwareengineer #staff

text:
The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter recently crossed 1,000 paying subscribers: I'm now officially making a living directly from "1,000 true fans".

I read the article on 1,000 true fans years ago - https://lnkd.in/dk-nXg7V. A few quotes that stuck with me from this piece:

"To make a living as a craftsperson, photographer, musician, designer, author, animator, app maker, entrepreneur, or inventor you need only thousands of true fans. (...) Here’s how the math works. You need to meet two criteria. 

First, you have to create enough each year that you can earn, on average, $100 profit from each true fan. (...) Second, you must have a direct relationship with your fans. That is, they must pay you directly. (...) A thousand customers is a whole lot more feasible to aim for than a million fans. (...)

Now here’s the thing; the big corporations, the intermediates, the commercial producers, are all under-equipped and ill suited to connect with these thousand true fans. They are institutionally unable to find and deliver niche audiences and consumers. That means the long tail is wide open to you, the creator. (...)

The truth is that cultivating a thousand true fans is time consuming, sometimes nerve racking, and not for everyone. Done well (and why not do it well?) it can become another full-time job."

I remember thinking this approach sounds like it could work for musicians or artists (the article uses these mostly as examples). I never thought it could apply to me - yet as of a week ago, it does.

Thank you to everyone who is already a subscriber (paid or free) 🙌 

If you're interested in weekly, in-depth advice and observations for engineering managers and senior engineers at big tech and high-growth startups, sign up here: https://lnkd.in/dSqKPPpU

Recent topics included the out-of-control tech hiring market, platform teams, hiring EMs, down-levelling in tech and postmortems/incident reviews.

#thankyou #softwareengineering #engineeringmanagement

text:
You get a templated InMail from a recruiter that is completely irrelevant: it's clear they did zero research. Here's the one thing yo can do that sends the message that you don't appreciate this:

Don't reply.

That's right: don't tap on "Not interested..." or type out anything. Just archive the message which action hides it for good in your messages, and doesn't send a reply.

This is the single worst response anyone using InMails as reachouts can get, for two reasons:

1. Until you respond, they cannot message you further without spending another InMail credit from their tab. Respond in any way and they can keep messaging you for free.

2. InMails cost money to buy, and recrutier packages offer them in bulk, starting at around 30/month in the smallest LinkedIn packages (Recruiter Lite) and they go up with larger packages. Until someone replies to an InMail, that credit is "locked". If a recruiter sends out too many messages that get zero responses... they cannot send out any more for the rest of the month. This is by design: LinkedIn wants recruiters to think more carefully who they message, and not spam senselessly. Well, they can do it, but they need to buy a *lot* more credits for more $$$.

In the same spirit, if you get an InMail where the person clearly did the research, I do suggest sending a short "thanks but not right now" response to not "lock" those credits for recruiters who put in the work.

I wrote this up after I told this strategy to several people complaining about InMail "spam" who all responded to the recruiters to complain about them - not realizing this was an outcome that helped the same recruiters spam more.

#linkedin #recruiters #advice

text:
What are common incident handling practices engineering teams use? How do teams approach postmortems, following up, and who owns these processes?

I surveyed more than 60 teams at various organizations, and talked with engineers and researchers deep in this field like John Allspaw, Laura Maguire, PhD, Christopher Evans and Paul Osman.

A few interesting takeways:

1. "Postmortem" is the most common term teams refer to their incindet analysis. However, there are about 10 other terms, with more exotic ones like like ILD (Incident Learning Debrief) or ACI (Airplane Crash Investigation), Incident Report.

2. Most teams handle incidents in a similar way - that the article goes into.

3. Today's best practices include having a decompression period, having an enviornment where analysis is blameless, or separating the review and actions discussion.

4. Some teams have left many of what the majority considers "best practices" behind, and are better for them. They are moving off using templates, and stopped tracking action items.

5. Tech has a *lot* to learn from other industries on building resillient systems... but has huge advantages compared to industries like airlines or medicine.

Read the full article here, and access the survey results here:

#softwareengineering #devops #bestpractices

text:
I don’t think anyone has platform teams fully figured out. Which is why I was excited to sit down with Ganesh Srinivasan who has built numerous platform teams and organizations throughout his career at LinkedIn, Uber, and Confluent.

We discussed from when platform teams come to play in the growth curve of a company through getting funding from the business, all the way to what recurring challenges he's seen.

Here's my three takeaways from talking with Ganesh:

1. Convey the upside of a platform team to the business. 

Make it very clear how products will be shipped faster, with higher quality or better reliability following a platform investment.

2. Know that you might get funding later than you’d like to, however well you make your case. 

However, if you make a good case on the upsides to start with, that case will only get stronger over time. Once the upsides are impossible to ignore, you should get your funding. Both Ganesh and I had the same experience as we funded our teams.

3. Set the bar high while putting the use of platform use first. 

Ease of use is what will help scale the platform and what will encourage engineers to use it, instead of the platform team having to mandate it. In this sense, platforms are similar to customer-facing products. The only difference is that your customers are engineers.

For a deeper dive in this topic, including a business case example to fund a platform team, see the 🔒subscriber-only issue of my newsletter: https://lnkd.in/g8GtiNtt

#platform #softwareengineering #scaleup #bigtech

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
It's so easy to dunk on a company like Facebook when they go down. But have you noticed engineers at other large companies and SREs are refraining from doing so? Why?

It's because if you build or operate *any* large system, you know exactly how outages are not a matter of "if", but a matter of "when". You know that it doesn't matter what people you hire - or how you interview them -, how much effort you put into testing all parts of the system, there will always be blind spots.

And you *know* it can be you the next time.

These people are busy reading the postmortem Facebook is sharing and ones like Cloudfare's in-depth analysis. Several teams are already responding to BGP fragility that caused this outage like they were hit by it and strengthening tooling, processes and runbooks. They're looking at ways the mitigation was slowed thanks to building security tied to a the FB root domain with no override - and seeing if they have the same vulnerability.

They are doing this because they know they could be next, and it was dumb luck that it was not them going down globally and unable to get access to their own servers, for hours.

It's your choice to handwave and make fun of outages or use it to become a better software professional, even if the incident was not within your team or company.

text:
The biggest difference between places where engineers have autonomy to try new things, and those that have a Chief Architect to decide on these?

At the first type of places, as an engineer, you can try out new things and see if they work. Like an RFC process where you let other teams know what you're working on and invite relevant comments that can come from places you would not even expect.

At places with a central decision maker... your ideas often get shot down. Without ever trying.

Maybe the Chief Architect is right and having a way for other engineers see an RFC a team is working on would be "wildly inefficient" at that company. Maybe companies like Uber or Google just got lucky that this process worked up to thousands of engineers (after which it was tweaked to not send out to the whole company, but still keep designs open for anyone to find and view).

But I'd choose places to work where we get to try things, instead of being dependent on the Chief Architect's experience. Which Chief Architect probably never worked at one of the many tech companies where RFC-like processes work well. 

#architecture #softwaredesign #bigtech #startups

text:
How do you hire engineering managers?

I've found EMs are *so* much more challenging to hire than engineers, and that says a lot (hiring engineers is not easy to start with). 

Still, a poor EM hire can (and does!) impact a whole team. At the same time, a great hire can act like a multiplier, and grow people and the team faster, and help multiply the output of the team.

So what is so difficult about this process?

Most hiring managers start with the wrong part: they jump into defining the interviews and hiring process, often copying this from how another team, or company does it.

However, how can you hire for the role if you're not clear about what the role is? Start with defining, or re-defining what you're hiring for.

And hiring is not done when a candidate accepts an offer. Onboarding is especially critical for engineering managers - having even a basic onboarding process makes all the difference for a new EM accelerating, or succeeding at the company.

Read more details on a framework to define an EM hiring process, and how Facebook, Amazon and Stripe do this in my latest newsletter issue: https://lnkd.in/d5jTtP9K

#hiring #managers #engineeringmanagement

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Facebook, Instagram and Whatsapp are down for hours now. This brings an interesting question: how are Facebook's oncall/infra teams coordinating to get them back up?

Normally, these teams use Facebook's internal messaging system. I'd imagine Whatsapp is a fallback in case that messaging system is down.

However, all Facebook internal systems are down, and so is Whatsapp. What now?

Turns out the Facebook infra team had a plan for what to do when all infra is down. Which basically happened. What was this plan?

IRC.

IRC (Internet Relay Chat) is a protocol created in 1988, and one that peaked in popularity in 2003, used by niche communities these days.

Who would of thought IRC would play a role in restoring the biggest sites/services on the internet in 2021?

#facebook #outage #irc

text:
The best time to prepare for the end-of-year promotions is now: months before. Here's 7 pieces of advice on how to do this:

1. Be realistic. How was your last performance review? Did you exceed expectations? If not; there’s little chance you’d be promoted. Work towards exceeding expectations for the next review cycle: much of the advice below also applies to figuring out how to do this.

2. Understand how promotions work at the company. Who nominates? Who decides? What are the criteria? What process does your company follow?

3. Self-evaluate. Assuming there’s a clear set of expectations – like the career ladder with competencies – evaluate where you think you are, compared to the current, and the next level.

4. Talk with your manager. Bring up the topic of promotions. Ask them where they think you are, and what it would take them to support. Show them your self-review. Your manager usually has the final say on the projects you work on, and if you don’t have the opportunity to work on projects that prove that you’re ready for the next level, you will not have a strong promotion case.

5. Get feedback from peers. Ask for feedback on how you’re doing from your peers, especially from more senior colleagues. Come promotion time, you’ll likely need peers to support your case. Get ahead of this and ask the people who would give peer feedback.

6. Keep track of all your achievements. The most practical way to do this is to write down what you did, every week. Summarize key achievements every month, then every quarter. If you’re only starting now, write down your key achievements for the past year. You’ll want to have examples that show that you have continuously been delivering highly impactful work.

7. Don’t get overly obsessed with the promotion. You risk alienating your manager, and your peers if you become The Person Working Just For The Promotion. Have a plan, work towards it, but avoid the perception that this is the only thing you care about. And it’s not just about perception; avoid the promotion being your one and only goal. Find goals that are more in your control, and can give you more immediate satisfaction when you complete them.

+1: Think about the work you do among the "produce, publish, organize" dimensions, as detailed on this image. The more senior level you are at, the more expectations tend to push you to Produce less, Organize and Publish more.

I cover more promotions advice for engineering managers on how they can prepare for promotions in their teams, details on how promotions work in big tech, and more actionable points to get ready for the promotion in the latest issue of The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter. I also share my take on the common question: should I just interview for the next level, or stay and wait to get promoted?

#career #promotion #bigtech

text:
After a month into starting a paid newsletter, it is taking off faster than I ever hoped. Here are the numbers, and my learnings for anyone thinking of writing online:

~ 500 paid subscribers (thank you!)
~ 15,000 free subscribers
~ $62K ARR
- A top 10 Substack technology newsletter and the #1 for engineering managers and senior software engineers

What I learned (and advice I can share):

1. Start writing! I started The Pragmatic Engineer blog a long time ago. At first, no one noticed. 70+ long-form essays and many years later, 50-100K people read it monthly.

2. Write what *you* want to read. I always liked articles that shared observations on where tech is heading or explained important things with plain language. So I wrote about this. Like equity in tech, or product-minded engineers.

3. Before starting a newsletter, come up with a value proposition. When I became an engineering manager at Uber, my manager offered I could expense whatever publication I wanted, to grow professionally. I did not find anything for engineering managers or tech leads. So I am writing what I wish I had access to, back then.

4. Give, give, give. Help, help, help. Only then ask. On the day announced I'm starting a paid newsletter, 20 people signed up. Before the first issue, this was close to 100. Most of these people were ones I somehow helped in the past with one of my many articles, YouTube videos, or other advice. They trusted I'd deliver what they expected because they already got lots of value in the past.

5. Nothing beats regular original, and high quality. There is no shortage of people with original, interesting thoughts, and those who can write with high quality. Almost no one does this regularly. Not in engineering, not in other niches. If you do it, you will eventually stand out.

6. If you want to improve your writing - or are starting a paid newsletter - consider hiring an editor. Once you find someone you click with: stick with them. My editor is Best English Copy and has made me a better writer, and my writing more pleasant to read. Best money spent and 100% recommend.

7. Have a goal and stick to it. I want to make it easy for engineers and managers to grow, week after week, while keeping up with stuff that works in the industry, especially at high-growth companies.

Subscribe, if you've not already: https://lnkd.in/dSqKPPpU

PS: the annual price is $120/year as part of the launch discount (it will go to $150/year later). Most eng managers/engineers with a learning/training/development budget end up expensing this newsletter.

text:
New offers in tech are up 20-30% from 12 months ago, globally. What can you do, as a company, to compete?

Most companies do what everyone else does: raise new offers by 20-30%.

Smarter companies are adjusting the existing compensation for all current employees by 15-20% as well, to get ahead of them jumping ship.

One of the smartest companies did none of this. They did not raise compensation by a cent.

Instead, they announced a 4-day workweek. 

Budget-wise, this is roughly the same as what everyone else is doing (as compensation is not cut). However, as the first large company to make this move, they'll have no trouble hiring. And productivity-wise, I'm gonna bet that a 4-day work week will be well above 80% productivity of a 5-day week, and fewer people leaving balances even the remaining "productivity" loss the long-term.

Well played, Bolt.

#productivity #hiring #career #tech #bolt
