posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Job advert:
- Get shit done
- No waterfall
- Change the world of banking
- Flexible working
- We have fun and work hard

Reality:
- Mandatory to submit a written report to your manager of work you did, daily. And - of course! - a much more detailed weekly report.
- Submit the daily report before 6pm and expect to be shamed for wrapping up early.
- Flexible work? Ok, will sit outside for an hour to code a bit enjoying the sun... oh that's not allowed. Must work at your desk.
- What about a raise after a year of great work? "We* don't care about money and neither should you" (*the founder is already a millionaire)
- Low salary ("we're a startup")
- No equity for employees
(- The company raised hundreds of millions in cash and became a unicorn, but please ignore this as it conflicts with the above two points and on not caring about money)
- Unpaid non-compete they like to enforce when people decide it's time to leave to work for a bank for much better pay, or a startup for better pay and equity, and a sane culture.

Sadly, this is a true story. 

Many former employees at a startup shared they thought all the above practices were acceptable. This was because the startup has a strong brand and brands itself as "non-conventional". They make this clear on interviews: but don't share the details on how "non-conventional" means operating a close-to-zero trust environment with low freedom.

Also, most employees said they felt weird from day one: but everyone around them accepted and told them "This is how things work. Get with it and stop questioning things."

Know that none of the above are typical of any decent tech startup. Decent startups would issue equity and would not micromanage all aspects of your work. They're places you'd arrive to work excited, and not dreaded about being shamed for logging on one minute late.

How can you avoid places like this in the first place?

- Message employees who recently left on LinkedIn and ask them if they can share their experience. You'll be surprised at the candor. Many of my friends avoided places with poor culture like this.
- Read company reviews online... look for patterns and ignore the glaring reviews that say nothing, that many of these companies ask for employees to write.
- If you find yourself in a toxic environment like the above: there is no shame in leaving for somewhere sane. The job market is hotter than ever and startups and tech companies with solid work environments are hiring. It's never been a better time to leave poor work environments.

#career #startup #micromanaging

- Teams being allowed to choose their own way of working was more common at public tech companies and venture-funded scaleups.
- Team autonomy and high satisfaction seemed to be correlated.
- Teams struggling often had little to do with the methodologies.
- JIRA has been mentioned mostly with negative associations.

text:
The tech job market is hot and people with experience are getting solid raises when switching jobs. But does this mean you should start interviewing outside? Here's a quote from someone a year into their second job who is deliberately not doing so:

"I have a manager who is more supportive than I can ask for and a team where I feel like I keep learning new things every day.

Reflecting on where I am, I realize that I'm in the perfect environment for deep learning rather than broad learning - which is something I'm more familiar with.

This is why I'm hesitant to look outside and to leave my company unless something *really* amazing comes along: I see the potential to really build internal expertise. I can also see gaining the advantage of having a reputation for being not just senior in title, but senior in in-company depth."

I completely agree with this person. Especially in my earlier career, I always prioritized learning, going deep into areas, and working with the right people over a higher salary.

I suggest reflecting on how you prioritize other things outside of financials if and before you look around on the market.

#career

posted_at.relative: 3 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The tech hiring market has never been as hot as it is now: not even during the Dotcom Boom. Here's why this is happening, and what I advise people working in tech to make the most out of it.

The use of "never before" is not a hot take: but the result of weeks-long research. I gathered data, talked with dozens of engineering leaders across the globe, and chatted with swarms of software engineers and engineering managers switching jobs.

Root causes: there is no single one, but multiple hitting at the same time, causing a "perfect storm":

1. Companies going all-in digital after the Covid-19 wake-up call.

2. Capital markets, Big Tech, "New Big Tech" all investing very heavily, growing, hiring, and pushing the top of the market even further. No signs of slowing.

3. Pent-up demand from 2020 thanks to projects being canceled, headcount was frozen in a year that turned out to be a strong year for many businesses.

4. Remote work and the evening out of geographies. Most people assume this is the main reason: it's an important one, but in isolation it would have not moved the market to the extent where it is today.

5. Difference between non-tech and tech companies shrinking. The eye-watering investment sizes some non-tech companies are making, and the talent they're hiring are unlike before.

6. Fewer senior tech workers: a larger-than-usual part of this workforce taking months-long breaks with nothing lined up, or leaving the pool of potential employees (I'm personally an example of the latter)

What does this mean for tech workers?

1. If you have years of industry experience: massive opportunities both by looking around on the market, but also more leverage at your current workplace.

2. If you are someone with little to no experience: you will not feel the market being hot, because - in a strange twist - it has never been a more challenging market for junior tech workers. The last time the market was this hot - during the Dotcom Boom - it was a great time to start out. Today, the combination of remote work and companies investing to scale up - thus needing to hire seniors - has not increased the demand for juniors. In fact, some companies hire far less juniors than they did before Covid-19.

I wrote up more detailed advice both for experienced and junior tech workers on I'd make the most of this heated market, and how it's different to the Dotcom Boom: https://lnkd.in/diYmJw4j

If you're a manager hiring in tech, in my most detailed yet subscriber-only post I go into a lot more detail on the root causes of the market, and what companies from small startups to Big Tech are doing who are successfully growing - and retaining - their tech staff. Already several hiring managers shared how they are using this piece as an argument for an out-of-cycle compensation adjustment given the market. Read that piece here: https://lnkd.in/dT7j3x-F

#career #tech #jobmarket #bigtech

text:
What are Platform and Product teams, and why are Platform teams important in high-growth companies? At Uber, three years after the first engineers were be hired, everyone found out: this is when Uber executed the Program/Platform split in an aggressive timeframe, creating 11 Platform and 16 Program teams (most of which were not even fully staffed).

An analogy to these teams is akin to Lego bricks. You can think of Platforms as the specific types of bricks, and Programs/Products using these bricks to create something unique.

Platforms come in different shapes and sizes, but at Uber, the most common ones were Infra-like platforms (e.g. Storage, Compute, Mobile Platform) and Product Platforms (platforms serving several products, like payments platform components, a communications platform or rating service for various products).

At places like Uber - and similar companies - up to 30-40% of engineers would work on Platform teams. This might sound like a massive investment: and it is. But these companies do it because this investment allows the company to keep growing, quickly, and without hickups.

Uber had been one of the fastest growing companies around 2014-2017, and starting the first Platform teams was a key enabler to this growth. 

I've written an in-depth article about the process of the split, characteristics of platform teams, the aftermath, and my take on platform teams after years of working in this environment. This article is the first subscriber-only piece in The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter, and I recommend this piece to engineering leaders starting/having started platform teams or people onboarding to these teams.

Read it here: https://lnkd.in/duxvwJeq

The next issue of the newsletter - with similarly in-depth takes on topics related to high-growth startups and big tech - will go out to all readers, and you can subscribe here: https://lnkd.in/dSqKPPpU

text:
What does the future of mobile engineering look like? What engineering practices will it take to keep winning in mobile?

I've been thinking about this for a *long* time and we finally verbalized it with Leland Takamine.

The biggest problem mobile teams have: shipping with confidence. Why? Because it takes. So. Long (image #1).

What is the approach that decent teams/companies use today?
- Detect problems in prod via monitoring
- Identify regressions with alerting
- Resolve as realtime as possible: probably with feature flags
- Limit the blast radius via staged rollouts

The biggest problem in mobile is detecting problems in prod is *really* late. For issues behind feature flags, this works. But for non-functional issues like performance, binary size detecting is often done ad-hoc, and fixes take a long time.

Where the industry is heading - and some companies already building custom tooling - is the shifting left on all these quality checks: getting feedback before merging to main (image #2)

So where are we today?

There are little to no tools to "shift left" on mobile quality. Save for a few, massive companies, building this for themselves. Uber was one of these companies. 

This is why I joined mobile.dev Our mission is to set a new standard for mobile engineering. Read more about our vision for the industry, and how we plan to shape it: https://lnkd.in/dHb9VDzc

The team is insanely talented: Leland Takamine headed up mobile platform efforts at Uber and Jacob Krupski comes from Google, and we have incredible hires coming on.

We're hiring people who want to build world-class tooling with an incredible crew.

A recent hire shared why they're leaving Big Tech to join: "I can always go back - especially after I built the tools all Big Tech and startups will use."

We hire globally, remote. Needless to say that we issue equity for hires - those joining now would do incredibly well if the company does as well as we hope it will. See here for more details: https://lnkd.in/dvWBywWb

Finally, a confession.

I *always* felt the industry follows Apple and Google, waiting for solutions from them. I miss the boldness of building great dev tools ourselves.

This is why I got involved with mobile.dev. To jump ahead, not just follow. To build the tools we should have had.

#mobile #tooling #startup #equity

text:
Here's why I'll never be associated with anything crypto until it has the devastating energy waste footprint that it does. Instead of abstract things, here is a crypto "success story" from the front page of CNBC.

Kids decided to get into crypto mining to make money. Mining crypto is best done with powerful GPUs costing $2,500-3,000 each. The dad is an investment banker and buys a few.

The crypto mining generates too much heat and noise, so they move it to the garage. Father takes out a loan of $230-280K to buy 90 more GPUs.

The heat generated and noise are too much for the garage: they move the operation to an air-conditioned data center. (Did I mention this operation is happening in Dallas, where the climate is already noticeably changing thanks to global warming?)

After spending ~$300K on equipment, and $3K/month on the energy bill, the kids are making $30K/month.

This is what crypto mining, today is. Except it's not just these two kids and their father buying the equipment: but tens of thousands of people and organizations doing the same across the world: buying GPUs, using huge amounts of electricity, generating excess heat. How many?

Enough to generate a shortage on the GPU market (and another crypto is doing the same on the hard drive market, producing wasted hard drives in bulk also for no reason, driving a global SSD shortage).

Blockchain and crypto is an interesting area to study, but uses an insane amount of energy, globally - which energy generation is a complete waste. Nothing remotely useful was done with that energy wasted. Fossils are burnt to cover the energy costs of the new mining rigs (which the story half admits, when the kids share they use green energy for their home - but not the data center).

And yet, this story is demonstrated as an entrepreneurial success story. In a time that we know slowing global warning hangs on burning fewer fossils: not wasting energy senseless is something that would help.

Most of the tech industry is innovating on lower energy usage, but more efficient solutions - think ARM, M1, or quantum computing. Then - BAM - we have crypto, which has country-level energy footprints, in the middle of the critical decades to slow down global warming.

This is not something I can, or want to endorse. I will not buy or be associated with any crypto projects until the wasted energy footprint of this technology goes down to zero.

There is a gold rush happening with crypto; many people are getting rich, quickly, and even more are hoping to do so. But there is a bill that will be handed in, in a few decades in the form of how much hotter the planet has gotten thanks to all the fossils burnt to power these GPUs running nonstop.

I will do my part to not contribute to it.

#crypto #blockchain #climatechange

text:
I'm starting a weekly newsletter I wish I had access to when working at Uber: going deep in topics relevant for engineering managers and senior engineers.

When I moved to engineering management, my manager encouraged to invest in my education and get resources that will help me become a better engineering manager. I looked around, and Harvard Business Review was the closest I found. I subscribed for two years, but the generic management approaches did not feel relevant to working at a high-growth company. Most of the issues went unread.

Similarly, senior and staff engineers around me told me they found little relevant reading that could help them step up their game at work, or get inspiration to try new things.

I started writing articles on The Pragmatic Engineer blog covering my observations and advice working in environments like this: at big tech, and at fast-paced companies. I wrote about product-minded engineering, things that Silicon Valley "gets" that traditional companies do not, distributed systems learnings, and my take on software architecture being overrated at these places. And many other topics.

Despite barely publishing articles once a month, lots of people read and responded to these articles. Still, I hadn't even covered a tenth of the topics I would have wanted to.

The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter is going out weekly to paying subscribers, and up to twice a month to free subscribers. If you enjoy articles on The Pragmatic Engineer blog, you'll find this content just as - if not more - interesting.

If you're someone who works at a high-pace environment or in big tech, in a leadership position, you'll probably find these emails actionable, giving you tools and approaches you might have not considered. If you only take one action in a year that results your team, project or company being more efficient: the subscription will have already returned it's worth, and a few times over.

text:
If you ever come across Glassdoor company pages where the stars/reviews don't seem to match what the company is really like... don't forget you're *not* a Glassdoor customer.

Companies are - specifically the ones paying for Premium Employee Branding. They are the ones who pay Glassdoor's bills of 750+ employees, the segment that brings in an estimated $170M/year, and the primary customers Glassdoor caters for.

And the truth is that companies who pay for Glassdoor, and whose rating is permanently stuck under 4 stars are not good business: they churn and stop being customers.

Which is why you'll be hard-pressed to find any company with a less than 4 star rating, who is also a paying Glassdoor customer.

As soon as companies sign up for a paid plan, Glassdoor offers a variety of tools for them to manage (and improve) their branding and rating, such as ready-to-send-templates to request reviews from their current employees, ways for the company to respond to reviews, and ways for companies to request certain reviews to be removed. While Glassdoor could filer out bursts of unexpected positive reviews in a short timeframe coming from customers: it turns a blind eye. It would be bad for business if it did not.

Glassdoor started out as a neat idea: bring transparency to corporate culture and compensation. They soon discovered that employees paying for employer branding brings in far more money.

And the honest, but negative reviews? They're the sales tool that helps Glassdoor onboard companies not yet on the platform, turning them into customers, and giving them access to employer branding tools - that will eventually help them improve their ratings to the 4.x range.

This is why more and more of Glassdoor can feel "off". Look for who is the customer who Glassdoor prioritizes in keeping happy so they keep paying. 

Sorry, but that's not you.

#transparency #glassdoor #business

text:
I've been helping software engineers in Europe negotiate their big tech compensation offers, resulting in €20-50K/£30-50K per year increases. Here's the advice I gave them:

1. Do your research on what numbers are realistic if you can. Your network, Blind, levels FYI can be good sources (I'm also building a site to help with this in Europe - it's currently in alpha, just search for "TechPaysEU").

2. Know that at most of big tech, signing bonuses and RSUs are easier to negotiate than base salary. However, right now, many big tech companies move on base salary as well.

3. Be positive throughout the process. No one wants to up an offer for someone who acts like a jerk. Share things you're excited about working at the new company, things that you’d look forward from day one and so on.

4. Make a very clear and direct ask with numbers, ideally over email, or the phone (write down what you'll ask for before the phone conversation).

5. Use leverage where possible. If you have other interviews in progress, mention that. If you have other offers, also (you don't need to reveal the number). Same with counter-offers or willingness to walk away from the offer.

6. Be clear that if you get that number you ask for, you'll sign on the spot, stop other interviews/not accept a counteroffer. Drive a hard, but fair bargain.

7. Decide what you do if they don't or can't match what you ask for. Be professional and positive both if you still accept, but also if you'll ask for more time (e.g. to interview elsewhere) or if you reject, at least for now. I have seen companies come back with higher offers to those who rejected: but only if they did it in a pleasant and professional way.

8. It's an employee's market right now: and even the big guys are struggling to close candidates. I've not seen a better time to negotiate (or look around on the market). It won't always be this easy, so use it to your advantage.

9. Don't just look at the compensation. With a hot market, compensation has gone up. However, company culture and the nature of the work hasn't changed that much. A 10% higher offer might not make you as happy as a much better culture or a team that works for you. Make sure you join companies, teams and managers where you'll find what you're looking for, money aside. 

Don’t just have the company interview you: you should also interview (“reverse interview”) the company, your future team and future manager.

While most people/companies charge big bucks for salary negotiation, I offer my raw thoughts for free for big tech offers in Europe over Twitter DMs. These are the raw thoughts/suggestions that helped people get these amounts: most recently an increase of £50K/year for someone with an Apple offer. See the link to contact me on Twitter in the DMs.

#negotiation #softwareengineering #salary #compensation

text:
Google will cut pay by 5-25% for employees moving out of San Francisco to work remote. What does this mean for remote compensation?

It means the market is pricing in the cost of retaining remote workers. This is the perfect queue for *any* company: offer 100% of Google’s Bay Area total compensation for your employees to work remote, from anywhere and poach disgruntled Google employees: guaranteed.

If this doesn’t happen: then this is the top of the market rate for remote work. Working/living/commuting to SF pays 5-25% above working remote and not dealing with the commute or paying the SF housing premium.

In my view, no company will step up to benchmark the salary for all their staff to Google’s Bay Area compensation. I predict that remote work will move compensation upwards for the majority of people based *already outside* of the Bay Area: but not for those already based there.

Already, people taking remote job offers in Europe, India, LatAm and across the world are seeing major increases in their compensation - driven by remote.

The remote market rate for tech workers is calibrating itself on rates: but it won’t get close to the current Google SF total compensation levels. 

PS: if you know of any company who will step up and match this compensation for remote workers, please drop it in the comments.

#compensation #remote #softwareengineering

text:
A really good piece of career advice I agree with: make your boss afraid.

When I was a manager, I always knew the top performers on my team: they were the ones I was most afraid of leaving. The ones who had outsized impact and it would be unlikely I could hire someone who does what they do: because they were *so* good at their job.

Be this person: be so good at what you do, and how you help the whole team that your manager is afraid on what would happen if you left. These people I talk about picked up work others left behind, took on and completed hold new initiatives and were huge motivators for the team, raising morale and output when they were around.

Guess who I fought very hard for on performance review calibrations and promotions to be recognised where they should be? Names I put forward for retention grants and other financial rewards? People I talked about in detail with my manager and director as ones we need to invest in more? People I paid additional attention to their career progression?

Who are the people you think your manager is afraid of leaving? How can you be a person like this, if not already?

(The full article is linked in the comments)

text:
At Uber, there were no Project Managers - however, for every 50-100 engineers we had a TPM (Technical Program Manager). They led the most complex projects, like rewriting Uber's Rider app, implementing GDPR across the company, and all "hairy" projects that were way too big for any one team.

I'll be honest: I was initially skeptical about this role. "Sounds like a glorified project management role" - I thought to myself, as I heard about these "large-scale program managers".

I could have not been more wrong. TPMs I worked with made my job as an engineer - and engineering manager - far easier. The TPM folks were very technical, very product-minded, and ridiculously good at herding cats. They "got it" on how engineering worked but also challenged us where it made sense.

Still, outside of Uber, I've only heard a handful of companies that work with TPMs. 

How do you start from scratch to set up a TPM organization? What priorities should this role have? Should they report to the CEO or to engineering?

Sophia Vicent headed up the TPM function at Uber - building it out from scratch - and is now doing the same at Doordash.

Her article on the TPM function is the most in-depth read I've come across on this- rarely discussed - topic. If you're in engineering or in product, it's a very worthwhile read. I especially agree with the third principle on driving technical clarity by partnering with engineering. 

https://lnkd.in/dehbHyX

text:
An underrated way to go from a mid-level or senior engineer to the lead of an area, team or product at a high-growth company:

Keep an eye on startups announcing heathy seed, Series A and Series B funding. Then hit up the founders for a chat.

Here’s why this is underrated and works:

When a startup secures funding, they do it with business plan & hiring plan. Eg “$3M seed: hire first 6 engineers” / “$12M series A: grow engineering team from 6 to 20”. 

Startups then typically announce funding but don’t have these job descriptions out yet. Founders - naively - expect a bunch of inbound interest immediately.

For the first few people hired, the startup will be flexible with everything: title, compensation, etc.

However, once they hired eg an iOS lead… that’s it. They have one. They’ll go on to hire the rest of the team. Same for Android, infra, web, backend, {NewProduct} lead, and so on.

Here’s why pinging founders early means a large advantage:

After announcing the fundraising, there will actually be relatively few people reaching out. Most engineers who would be tempted to work at the company check the startup's site and see no jobs listed... sigh and move on.

A pity, as founders are the most likely to talk with people at this stage. So just reach out directly!

Most startups - irrespective of how well-funded they are - cannot hire people with the experience/seniority they really want. This makes it a reasonable shot for someone with some relevant experience - and lots of passion - to step up. Especially if this person reaches out before the job is posted on all the job sites, and a lot more people start applying.

Why will this work?

The startup has the funding to grow right after they raise the round *no matter what* (mostly). If you join early after this raise as a lead, you’ll all most certainly hire your team, and own the area.

Startups can boost your career - this is a great example how.

So where can you find fundraising announcements to look out for? I recommend checking AngelList, TechCrunch and searching Twitter and LinkedIn (see links in the comments).

PS: at mobile.dev we've raised our $3M seed round recently and are expanding to change how mobile engineering is done - and all the above applies.

text:
Some startups are using non-competes to force non-executive employees to stay. Here's an actual story shared with me:

A Dutch FinTech startup paying below the market adds noncompete clauses to all contracts. They regularly threaten individual contributors and frontline manager employees leaving, telling them they will enforce this non-compete, interpreting the non-compete reach very broadly.

One of their employees decided to leave anyway for a significantly better career prospect. We're talking about a person making less than €60K/year at this startup. The company decided to show who's in charge and went to court, suing the employee.

The court sided with the employee's interest, throwing out the non-compete.

What's changed since: not much. The same company still has noncompetes in their contracts, and still threatens employees leaving on enforcing it. I've not heard them go to court since this case though. 

I do hear employees not sharing with anyone where they're leaving to, and some starting to lie about not having anything lined up so the company does not bully them on their way out. A culture of fear is steadily growing, all thanks to non-competes.

Be *very* wary signing a contract with a non-compete, especially if you're also taking a paycut to join the company, like many of these startup employees are known to do.

Also know that you are not legally entitled to tell your current employer who your new employer will be. So don’t. What current employees are doing is sensible - especially that the company has a track record for harassing employees with a lawsuit they would lose again, but would drain the employee mentally and financially.

And founders: do better than using non-competes as a retainer tool for non-executive staff instead. What might seem like a helpful tool in the short-term against attrition will slowly but steadily poison the culture, like it's doing so for this company.

#noncompetes #career #startup

text:
Proof of the job market being hot: some companies that pay competitively across Europe now share the lower end of their salary band.

At Plaid, in Amsterdam, base salaries start at €119K ($140K) for senior software engineers, €98K ($116K) for mid-level (3+ years) sw engineers. Equity & bonus goes on top of this.

Plaid is a company I'd categorize as "Tier 3" - they have high expectations from hires, but also pay accordingly across Europe. The compensation is what I'd consider closer to the top of the market. Until now, however, none of these "Tier 3" companies would share this kind of salary information publicly.

What's changed?

The job market for software engineers is *very* hot. I've not seen anything like this for all the time I’ve been in the industry. Companies are having a much-much harder time grabbing the attention of software engineers with experience and skills they need to keep growing.

If you're thinking of exploring new opportunities: now it's an employee's market, more than ever. And I expect more companies sharing ranges upfront: it's a competitive advantage to do so - at least in Europe. (If you’re a hiring manager, consider giving it a go: you’ll be surprised at the difference in responses, and the increase in offer accepts when the range is known upfront)

Yet so much of the industry still sees tests as a waste of time - thanks to engineering leaders who never saw these befits first-hand.

There’s a reason tests are baseline at all of big tech: from Apple through Google, Uber, Microsoft, Facebook, and the list goes on and on. 

Not writing automated tests would result in far *more* time spent producing the same quality products and would not allow teams at these companies to ship multiple times a day.

If you’ve not yet worked at a place where you move faster because of tests: I hear your skepticism on how this can work. But it does. See this below tweet from a software engineer working at Apple for a year on how her approach to tests has changed.

text:
The single best career "hack" I know is this: reverse interview your future manager & team *after* you get an offer. Such a great way to "derisk" that big decision, yet so few people do this, from my experience as a hiring manager.

Here's how and why:

1. First, get an offer. Do your best on the interviews: be present, focused, positive, and bring your best self. Without an offer, you'll have nothing to negotiate or reverse interview.

2. Then, negotiate the offer. Get the numbers right and to the point that you'd be happy to accept. If the offer is not good enough for you, no point in reverse interviewing either.

3. Once you'd be ready to accept the offer: don't. Ask to talk 20-30 minutes with your future manager, a future peer (e.g. the most tenured person on the team), and other relevant folks (e.g. PMs, a director, or cofounder, based on the size of the company).

When I got a great offer from Uber, I asked to talk to my future manager and the longest-tenured engineer in Amsterdam. Hint: while managers are often great at sales, engineers give you the real deal on what the day-to-day is like.

You're about to make a leap to work for (hopefully) years for a company. They'll say yes to this request. If they don't: it's a major red flag. Why would they?

Come prepared with questions *you* want to know that will give you information on whether you'll enjoy working here. Ask about team dynamics. Career. Support. Stress/excitement. What people like and dislike about the place. And so on.

By reverse interviewing your future team, it's not only you who gets more information. Assuming you accept the role, you'll start with a small "network" of the people who reverse interviewed you, and who now have a positive impression of you. To this date, I still remember every person who asked to reverse interview when I was hiring at Uber.

Very few people reverse interview, and so many more will decide on taking an offer based on few percent higher cash compensation between competing offers or browsing Glassdoor / other online reviews that are a hit-or-miss.

In my case, reverse interviewing was the reason I accepted my Uber offer. Even with the good offer, I was skeptical of joining a satellite, young and small engineering office in Amsterdam. After talking to my future team in detail, I decided I *want* to work with these people and be part of the growth to come, and never looked back after.

#career #interviewing #softwareengineer

text:
Expectations vs reality about college as a software engineer. While at university studying computer science, I assumed these would be important for my future career:

1. The name of my school
2. My grades - especially relative to the rest of my class
3. Recommendations from my professors

I could have not been more wrong. Little to none of the above mattered. Instead, what really mattered in getting - and succeeding - at my first few jobs:

1. Building software (side projects, apps, websites, games etc)
2. Coding that I practiced with mostly #1 and languages/frameworks I taught myself, that were never part of the uni curriculum
3. Keeping learning on the job and outside of it

The “name” of my school ended up not mattering - I moved abroad early on and my locally known school did not ring a bell to anyone in the UK. Similarly, no one ever asked of my grades after I graduated or cared about prof recommendations. Hiring managers and recruiters were always more interested in things I’ve shipped, practices I used and my motivation.

While I don’t regret spending all the time I did studying for classes, I do wish I would have stressed less about my exam scores, and spent more time learning/practicing things that I was genuinely interested about, over retaking numerous exams to turn an “average” grade into a “good” or “excellent” one.

To make it full circle, when I became a hiring manager, I ended up caring about of none of the above. At Uber, in Amsterdam I hired interns and new grads from universities across Europe from Romania through France to the Netherlands: I don’t even know which universities as I skipped the institution name when reading resumes. I also didn’t know - or cared - if the students were top of their class or barely passing. What mattered is how they did on the interview, their attitude and their day-to-day contributions.

 #career #university #softwareengineering #computerscience

text:
A software engineer made £7-800K last week when Wise went public after they joined Transferwise years ago in London.

“After negotiating they offered £85K annual salary and £80K worth of options spread over 4 years. Not that the stock will be worth anything. I really tried to negotiate a higher salary but they only gave me more stock. Oh well.” - this was their take when they told me about their offer a few years ago.

I was pointing out that I’m betting that the stock value will grow 3-4x the next years, given Fintech valuations which would turn this stock into £250-300K on a future exit. Transferwise just crossed the $1B valuation back then and I argued if the stock is worth nothing a lot of investors will be losing a lot of money (and be wrong about their read of the market).

I was wrong by another 3-4x. Transferwise is worth $13B today. That £80K of stock turned out to be worth more than double than the salary this engineer received for 4 years.

This is the beauty of equity: if the company does very well, employees with equity can do just as well.

Wise is one of the few EU companies who allocated equity even early on to all employees / including all software engineers as well. Many of them did very well and gained years’ worth of salary after this exit.

In 2021, equity allocation is baseline for all US startups and it’s becoming the norm in all decent EU startups. We’ll see a series of EU exits that will result more employees seeing large gains like this - the ones who have equity, that is.

If you work at a startup as a software professional, negotiate for equity on top of a fair salary (not instead). The equity, of course, could turn out to be worth nothing, but you might also work at the next Wise.

Read more about equity and why it’s important here:

#equity #compensation #startup #salarynegotiation

text:
Non-competes in tech: did you know they are almost always non enforceable for non-executive tech workers, but still used by many companies for bullying employees into not taking better career prospects?

I have still to hear of a company successfully enforcing a non-compete via a court order for a software engineer / product manager / designer / data scientist non-director-level manager. (If you know of any, please, message me as per below!)

I’m writing a longer deep dive into non-compete agreements for non-executive tech workers (software engineers, PMs, designers etc). So far, I’ve received several direct messages - many anonymous.

There’s a clear pattern of hedge funds and certain FinTech companies threatening to enforce non-competes when non-executive staff leaves for better career prospects (significantly higher pay, more responsibility etc) to a company who could be considered competitors. This includes software engineers, product managers, data scientists, operations folks and EMs threatened.

In almost all cases, employees backed down from the threat from the legal case and decided to stay (you can imagine how motivated they were…). In some cases, they fought back, went to court: in all of these cases the employees winning after a long process. I have a report of a Dutch FinTech losing such a case in the Netherlands.

I’m writing a longer article on the *practical* uses of non-competes, and how much any non-executive tech worker has to “fear” from these. Please send me examples of these being enforced by companies either here, or on Twitter as a direct message, or to tips(at)pragmaticengineer(dot)com.

Be wary of signing contracts with such clauses. They limit competition, kill innovation and force you to stay in a job you don’t want to. The proof? Look at California and Silicon Valley. Non competes are illegal… and innovation is in full force.

If your company has non-compete clauses in place, know this will limit talented people you can hire: many are declining offers when this clause cannot be striked off. Rightfully so.

#career #softwareengineering #noncompete #tech

text:
One very important thing I learned during my year at JP Morgan is every few months you should ask yourself:

- Does my work give me energy?
- Do I feel challenged day-to-day?
- Am I learning?

If the answer is "no" to all of these, it's probably time to leave for a place that can turn those "no" answers into "yes" ones.

At JP Morgan I was making really good money, had a great team with super nice and supportive people. Still, the nature of the work and the culture drained me far more than any past workplace. Building my side projects after work felt like a recharge where I was finally free to do "real" software development with modern tools and frameworks which were still pending approval - or were already rejected - at work.

I spent far more time figuring out how to get approvals to build than building things. The regular emails from the CEO kept reminding me of the not-at-all-motivating vision and mission of the company: to generate value for our shareholders. Moving fast and building high-quality software was much less important than generating profits. New ideas were treated as risky and ones to avoid, especially when what we had was already working.

The summer of 2011 reflected this perfectly. Greece was about to default on its debt, sending the market panicking. JP Morgan customers started buying and selling assets at a frantic pace. In response, my group decided to freeze all software work for a month to not risk any systems breaking at a time of such market volatility. We sat doing nothing, watching our products generate 5-10x the profits than we'd normally do thanks to this increased activity. 

“Bonuses will be really high this year" - people around me would say, satisfied. "Why are we standing still, instead of building ahead, improving what we have, or doing new experiments?" - I was thinking, itching to do meaningful work over sitting idle, even though I was paid to do so.

It took me a reachout from a recruiter at Skype to realize this is not what I want to do, because I'm not excited about my work, and felt like I stopped growing professionally. I wanted to get back into a "tech first" culture and was ready to take a paycut to do so.

On a personal level, I really liked my team at JP Morgan and this made it tough to say goodbye. Still, it was the right call for my professional career. 

At Skype, I found myself surrounded by a mix of hackers, software craftsmen, and industry veterans who all just wanted to build, and were really good at doing so. It felt exactly where I wanted to be.

Since then, I reflect on these questions a few times a year and recommend others to do the same.

#career #softwareengineering #team #jobchange

text:
Both Uber and Doordash have removed the 1-year equity vesting cliff for employees: and this is a BIG deal.

Until now, a 1-year cliff was the norm in most of big tech**. If you left - or got let go - before a year, you saw nothing of your equity. This was especially an issue when being laid off, especially for no fault of your own, like it happened around COVID. Employees 7-9 months in saw none of the equity that was a meaningful part of their compensation, which was both unfair and frustrating.

I am hearing the change came after hiring - especially software engineers - is becoming increasingly heated and slowing down across most markets. Not having a 1-year cliff makes a whole lot of a difference as an employee - giving more compensation, earlier - while it basically costs the company little to nothing extra.

I am expecting we’ll see more of big tech follow. Kudos to Uber and Doordash for making this employee-friendly step!

** Update: people have let me know Google and Facebook removed the cliff 2 years ago, Pinterest 18 months ago and Spotify and Snap also no longer have it. All the more reason for other publicly traded big tech to follow.

text:
"What equity is good allocation as a software engineer if I'm joining an European startup?"

Here's what you can expect for *better* companies, where founders have read "A guide to stock options for European entrepreneurs" by Index Ventures, the best publicly available material on this topic. Know that all better startups allocate equity to all software engineers on top of a fair salary: even in Europe.

1. Seed funding stage (typically below 10 engineers, valuation of around $6M): around 0.4-1.1% for mid- or senior engineers (lower if the company valuation is higher, and can go up or down based on the salary you take). Don't forget that this will not mean you'll own this % of the company on a future exit, as each funding round will add some level of dilution. Still, if - beating most odds - the company becomes a unicorn and exits, you would do very well with such an allocation: much better than many later joiners at the same level. It’s a classical lottery ticket: very high risk, very high reward.

2. Series A: it's no longer about percentages, unless you're joining as an executive (CTO or VP Eng), and a billion-dollar exit would mean several millions in upside. At a $25M vauation, around 30% of your annual salary spread over 4 years is a more typical grant for mid- to senior-level engineers, up to 50% for above seniors (eg staff/principal). While this might seem low, at Series A, your salary should be market-level, and the company should have a *lot* of upside left. Uber was valued $50M at Series A in Feb 2011, 125x less than their IPO 9 years later. A $30K grant after this Series A might have been worth around $3M on exit.

3. Series B, C, D, E, F, H... the closer the company is to IPO, the more the stock grants would converge to similar, publicly traded companies. And the value of these grants tend to go up, as does the company valuation - and the likelihood of a 10x or 100x upside goes down. For example, companies like Databricks or Flexport hiring in Europe (IPO very likely the coming years) would issue similar stock packages to publicly traded tech companies. For the top of the market, this would mean anywhere from $20-120K/year in stock, on top of a solid base salary.

Similar to salaries, stock can be heavily negotiated. The earlier stage the company, and the more perceived value you bring to the table, and the more forward-thinking the founders are, the more you can do so. Founder Stephen Whitworth of incident.io says "If in doubt, be overly generous with your early employees" and I could not agree more.

For a more detailed read check out the free-to-read ebook from Index Ventures and other equity resources, linked in the comments.

 #startup #equity #softwareengineer #venturecapital #salarynegotiation

posted_at.relative: 4 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Would it not be nice to get rid of managers at your company? A growing number of startups is doing just this. But it's not as rosy as you'd think.

I talked with founders and employees working at companies that implemented a version of holacracy - no managers, management responsibilities being distributed to employees on some rotational basis.

Founders love it and think they've come up with a better way to run their company than other alternatives.

Employees tell a different story, with very real problem areas.

1. Hierarchies still exist, but they are now hidden, and typically tenure-based. Basically, some old-timers have a much bigger say in things, especially typical managerial areas (perf reviews/promotions). 

2. Performance reviews are a coin-toss, but also often highly political. They are being done by a (supposedly) randomly allocated peer, and you might also have to give rate another peer. It's dangerous to give an old-timer a poor review (see #1). And old-timers tend to game the system on the silent understanding of "you give me a good review, I give you a give review".

3. You'd think old-timers thrive in these places. They don't. They usually feel stuck, due to no real career and little professional growth. "I keep getting amazing performance reviews, and come out on the top of the group, whatever I do. Yet I don't feel I'm progressing" is what an early employee told me. He was getting all the benefits, but still was not happy.

4. Dealing with performance issues is a problem, as is low performers getting good reviews, and even promotions. Some people doing fine are given low performance reviews on their first few reviews and fired (thanks to #2 and getting an inexperienced reviewer or an old-timer securing their position). At the same time, low performers are rarely dealt with, especially if they are old-timers (also thanks to #2). Reviewers keep being rotated to ensure "fairness", and it's easy to coast along if you've been around for a while and know the drill.

5. It's very hard to hire senior engineering talent from the outside. Most people run from these places due to all the above issues. I’d do the same.

6. The - on paper - most senior engineers at these places are stuck in a "local maximum" where they feel they cannot learn anything new, yet are far less experienced than they would be at many other companies, thanks to not having more experienced people to learn from (see problem #5). This also traps them: they often stay as this is the only place they have a senior/principal title, other companies level them lower.

Founders were typically not managers before and put a system in place thinking "a bad manager can destroy a team. Let's make that not happen here." Now they *only* have bad/unaccountable managers in-place, and managed to put a hidden hierarchy in place that benefits few, outside the old-timers.

My advice: be careful before joining a place like this, and ensure these areas are recognised and being solved for.

posted_at.relative: 4 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Ever got an InMail to work for a company? It's not a job offer. Not even close. It's what recruiters would call a "sourcing outreach".

I hear many people refer to messages - like the one attached here -  as "Amazon wants me to work for them". But the reality is a lot less glamorous. Companies often don't get enough applications - or diverse enough ones - and so recruiters will reach out to people on various places, like LinkedIn, to get them interested, and start the application process. Large companies even have recruiters who do this most of the time, their role referred to as "sourcers".

You need to go through the exact same interviews as anyone who would apply externally, the first step being the recruiter screen. The only difference? You don't have to go through the resume screen.

Not having to go through the resume screen *is* a pretty good thing for large companies, though, as many of these companies get a very large number of applications for each role, and it can be a coin-toss if you hear back when applying directly, over immediately talking with a recruiter, following sourcing reach out.

Many of these sourcing messages - including this specific one - are generic templates, the only "customization" being swapping names on the message when reaching out to another candidate. This is because even without any customization, a message from a well-known company like Amazon results in a surprisingly good response rate, as I've learned, talking with many recruiters and sourcers. You don't get a message every day from WellKnownCompany, after all.

Michael R. Starkey sending the sourcing message to me probably did not read my profile - where I share I'm not looking for any employment - but if you are, and are interested in Amazon senior eng roles, I suggest you message him directly. Sounds like this role is hiring directly from Europe, and possibly other non-US locations as well.

text:
Q: "What are your thoughts about standup meetings and how can they be made effective?"

A: My view has changed dramatically on standups over the years, from "we must have them" to "well, we might be better without".

1. IF you have a standup meeting (usually, daily), make it SHORT. This means don't have more than the minimum necessary people, stick to the point, and take longer discussions for after the meeting. All of this made sense in the office, but with remote, things change a lot:

2. IF you have sync standup meetings as a remote team, the very least have some sort of agenda document that people can fill out ahead of time. It makes the meeting more focused, discussions easier to follow (or catch up on). Here's a template I created for inspiration: https://lnkd.in/dMEUX_6

3. IF you have a doc that goes with the standup... do you *really* need to have in-person standups with the team? Can you not just fill it out by a certain time, and everyone reviews it? Most of my teams at Uber ended up doing this, and meeting in sync few times per week (some only once!).

4. I've come to realize, as a manager how *I* think team standups should be run are not always the most efficient. I've started to encourage project leads to have full autonomy to decide what they want to do. Guess what - these templates and async standups came from them!

5. My philosophy on team leadership is to rotate project leadership among team members and let those team members run the project/team how they want to. Seriously*! I wrote this document with much more details: https://lnkd.in/dzsSFaJ

There you go. From someone who thought "standups are an absolute must" to observing how so many of my teams worked more efficient when they got to do things their way, with async status updates, they way it worked for them.

*except when someone has never run a project before. I would give them more opinionated suggestions as "do whatever you want" was too scary and not helpful, I found.

text:
Who is supporting the managers supporting their teams during this pandemic?

It's an increasingly difficult time to work in tech. Yes, engineers and tech workers still have jobs: but not seeing colleagues in-person, and the never ending stream of Zoom meetings is taking the toll on so many people. The people who are in the frontline of trying to keep teams as happy as possible are the frontline managers.

But who is supporting these managers? After all team members are done complaining to them, and after finishing the 30th Zoom meeting of the week with team members, stakeholders, customers: who do they have to "vent" to?

When a manager burns out, they often take their whole team with them. If your manager is visibly "checked out" day after day - how long will you be motivated? You won't. A team member quits. Another transfers out. One more finds a new opportunity... and eventually, the manager also quits, or takes a longer break to recover.

Some of the best organizations - where manager churn is low - have hired coaches for these managers (like Paulo André and similar coaches) to have a safe space to complain, vent, and turn frustration into action. I call this “manager therapy" and it does wonders to the manager, and through this, to the team.

The same companies also make sure managers also take vacations to recharge, have peer groups as "safe spaces", and managers of managers are spending a lot more time "just talking" and listening to their managers.

So who is supporting the managers at your workplace during these times?

author.headline: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 technology newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer's Guidebook.

posted_at.relative: 4 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Unlike Bunq becoming a unicorn, Mollie being valued at $6.5B is a lot better news for the Dutch startup ecosystem. Why?

It's because I am hearing that - unlike Bunq - Mollie *does* allocate meaningful equity (stock options) to some employees who negotiated this, including software engineers; and how all early engineers hold meaningful equity. With this latest valuation increase, those employees are already realizing very significant gains in their stock. On a future exit, these options would turn into real money, and fuel the startup ecosystem further. It’s not the majority of employees: but it’s a start.

Mollie is growing, they are hiring and I hear good things about the engineering culture. If you apply, make sure to negotiate for equity on top of a fair salary: I am hearing not all engineers are offered this, even today at Mollie, even when they are senior (you might want to fix this to keep hiring great talent and retain existing folks Marco dos Santos)

Equity for software engineers is a given in any high-growth startup wanting to hire world-class talent these days. I would hope becomes a baseline for *all* employees working at Mollie, the now 5th largest fintech company in the EU, who are successfully competing with Stripe.

Go Mollie!

text:
Today, Bunq became the latest unicorn in the Netherlands. You’d think this is something to celebrate. It’s not.

With the latest round of investment, Bunq is valued at $2 billion. At any “normal” startup, this would mean early employees are now paper millionaires. It meant this at Uber, at Hopin, at Deliveroo, and so on.

Not at Bunq. Here, no employees (software engineers or other) are allocated stock. There is one shareholder: the founder, who has close to 100% ownership.

And it’s not just that employees don’t get equity. Bunq has frugality at its core and prides itself for paying under the market. On my salary survey, the lowest compensation numbers for software engineers across the Netherlands have consistently been at Bunq. The company asked people to take paycuts to work there citing their mission and that they're a "startup". Many did.

I should be happy for the founder. With his charisma, he inspired employees to work long hours (employees tell me you were expected to be in the office before the CEO was in, not have longer than 30-minute lunches that were sometimes timed, and stay in the office until after he left), and for far less compensation than they would have earned anywhere else. He turned his $100M investment into a $2B valuation. Smart guy.

But as an employee who has benefitted from equity at successful startups, I’m sad for everyone else at Bunq. Bunq could have had dozens of paper millionaires who were the ones building the company. Instead, it has one paper billionaire.

If you work at a startup, as a software engineer, with below-the-market salary and no equity: know that it's not a startup. With a startup, you benefit from the success of the company, financially as well.

And please, stop treating Bunq as a Dutch startup success story. It’s a story of a charismatic founder who shares the upside with no one. The massive success of Bunq and a future billion-dollar exit will not contribute to the Dutch startup ecosystem: it won’t make employees wealthy enough to spin-off new ventures similar to the PayPal, Uber, or Skype alumni who all benefitted from employee equity. 

PS: to those hiring at "normal" startups and companies allocating equity: you know which company to reach out for great people who are paid well below market, and don't know it (yet).

text:
A motivated software engineer ships 2-3-N times as much value as someone who is “whatever”. So many engineering leaders/managers fail to recognise this.

If you want to deliver standout results, stop talking about 10x engineers and start focusing on motivated engineers and motivated teams.

Here are things that I did to motivate people on the team when I was a manager:
- Give context (share, share, share everything you can).
- Ensure engineers have lots of maker time (kill meetings).
- Encourage people asking for forgiveness, not permission (and *always* celebrate people taking initiatives, never shoot anyone down. You know who makes no mistakes? The person who took no initiatives or risks.)
- Figure out what each person is motivated by, as an individual. Can you help them do more of it/get closer to those goals?
- Give trust, responsibility and autonomy to people, with enough support so they succeed.
- Always give credit to the people who did the work and always take the blame on behalf of the team.
- Make sure performance review/promotion results are *never* a surprise and people know what to expect and why. Few things kill motivation more than when you hear unexpected, negative news out of nowhere on these important discussions.

text:
I agree with how interviews for software engineers are becoming more difficult, but disagree on how it’s in the “leetcode” dimension.

I’m seeing soft skills - communicating clear and concise, describing your ideas, talking through tradeoffs - becoming a lot more important. Yes, knowing how to use data structures to solve a problem is becoming a baseline at most places. 

But it’s not just the coding skills that only count, and companies - at least in Europe - don’t ask you dynamic programming questions. If they do: they’re shooting themselves in the foot. And they’ll rarely tell you if it was the non coding skills that was the reason for them passing. 

Here’s this advice in more detail: https://lnkd.in/d7YubSt

A comment from this video, on the importance of interpersonal skills, especially at Big Tech:

“I realised I was doing a lot of the stuff in the video because I studied interpersonal skills for years. I got FAANG and other offers left and right after graduating. It’s a rare and valuable skill.”

text:
Plato is a mentoring service charging $300+/month to connect with experienced mentors who volunteer their time for free. None of it goes to charity.

It's an ingenious business model, where most customers assume they pay for the time of founders, executives, managers, staff+ engineers. Plato is a VC-funded company, who conveniently leaves out how mentors are not paid, and Plato does not donate the mentor rate to charity, either.

I confronted Plato after I learned my mentees all assumed I must be paid. I asked them to donate my take rate - how much they charge for my time - to charity.

They said they cannot do this, and promised to make their business model more clear to customers. A month later, I have not seen any clarifications coming from the company. Good luck finding this "small detail" anywhere on their site - it's not there.

Here is why I decided to stop donating my time to grow a for-profit company, and alternatives I suggest for mentorship, that are either free (really!), or paid, but with a transparent model.

Nonprofit / pro bono alternatives to Plato I suggest are Mentoring Club, Coding Coach and Mentors in Tech (MinT).

text:
Coinbase rewrote all of their apps using React Native and rolled it out to 56M customers. Hearing this news, many teams will probably think it's a good idea to follow their lead and move to React Native. Right? Wrong.

Coinbase solved their own, pressing problems with a large, two-year investment. They shared some of the publicly shareable reasons in this blog post: https://lnkd.in/dXhXwEZ

And it seems one of the very big reasons for their move was that they could not move fast enough on mobile: and their biggest bottleneck was hiring native mobile engineers fast enough.

They had a strong React team in place who were productive but were disappointed in how the native team seemed to not be as productive in comparison.

They had to do a *lot* of work to make RN work for them, but now feel the investment was worth it.

My suggestion when evaluating mobile, backend, web, or any other technologies: do what Coinbase did. Look at your biggest pain points. At your constraints. At how long of a time you're willing to invest.

Don't blindly copy Coinbase, Uber, or any other tech company that blogs about their decision. But take inspiration from the process on how they got to their solution.

text:
What are the economics of publishing a book? Here are my numbers for Building Mobile Apps at Scale: an ebook, Kindle, and print book - that is also free as a PDF until the end of the month.

$20 book sold on Gumroad - I make $19 (Gumroad takes 3.5% + $0.30)

$22.50 print book sold on Amazon: $9.50 (Amazon takes a 40% cut + charges a $4 KDP print-on-demand fee)

$22.50 Kindle book sold on Amazon: $7.70 (Amazon takes a 65% cut from Kindle books priced above $10)

$22.50 ebook sold on iBook: $9 (I get a 40% cut: I'm using Ingram Spark to distribute here and take care of admin)

$22.50 book sold in a bookshop in the US: $5.86 (I sell through Ingram Spark, who charge for print-on-demand print, and wholesalers take an 55% distribution fee)

If I would have gone through a publisher, based on contract numbers:

$22.50 book published by a "traditional" publisher (e.g. Manning, O'Reilly): $1.50-2.00 (10-12.5% royalty from the net price)

$22.50 book published by a smaller publisher (e.g. Packt, who pay higher royalties, but are less well known): $2.00-$4.00

$22.50 book published by The Pragmatic Bookshelf: $8-10 (they pay the highest, 50% royalty in the tech publishing industry on the net price)

text:
Twelve years ago I was offered a job on Friday and was told I need to make a decision by Monday - else the offer is withdrawn. This was an exploding offer.

Exploding offers - offers that are void after a very short timeframe - are a tool that employers tend to use who have little leverage, and are afraid of competition and that a job seeker with another offer would not choose them. So they try to bully job seekers into stopping their job search.

I was given this exploding offer after I shared how I have multiple interviews happening the coming week. It was blackmail at its finest.

Ten years later, as a hiring manager, I've seen all too many exploding offers. My advice? Don't let yourself be bullied.

1. Stall signing of the contract as long as you can, and keep interviewing.
2. Insert a trial period in the contract - if there is none - of at least a month, where both parties can terminate the contract with no penalty.
3. Even after you signed, keep interviewing. If you get a better offer, hand in a notice in line with your original contract.

As for my situation? I wish I had this advice. I didn't stall: I told the company I NEED four more days to decide. The CEO called me, tried to persuade me to sign, then got angry and withdrew the contract. I probably dodged a bullet.

text:
"How did you learn to write so well?" I didn't. I just write a lot. And iterate even more.

After I'm done with rewriting, I check my writing with Grammarly, and tweak it some more. And I sometimes hire an editor for a second look and to give me more feedback.

These are the edits my editor is suggesting for what I consider a polished work on my end. It's on average an edit or more per sentence!

You rarely see how much work goes into writing something that reads well. I don't know anyone who can write out perfect sentences without lots of iteration - if there are people like this, it's certainly not me.

The book my editor is adding the final touches to is Building Mobile Apps at Scale (https://lnkd.in/dUz8Uxk) and my editor is a long-time journalist friend turned freelancer (https://lnkd.in/dV7TcUh).

If you're someone who is serious about your writing, I recommend hiring an editor every now and then - it's humbling for me to see where my writing can (and should) improve further.

text:

These are the books and resources I've used and read when applying for Facebook and Uber: and what I'd recommend for others.

There's a wealth of information out there: ironically, the challenge is how to reduce the noise and avoid low-quality resources.

If you take one piece of advice from my writeup: YOU should create your own study plan for the interviews you're preparing for. If you're planning to interview now or in the future, hope this helps get you started: https://lnkd.in/dZqDSzR

text:
When my son was born, Uber just just extended their 18-week paid leave for dads as well. I was hesitatant at first if I should take the whole thing.

I was a manager, after all. What about performance reviews? Promotions? Key projects? Perhaps I should just take a month or two? Who’s heard of a manager who is a new dad disappearing for 4 months?

A fellow manager’s thoughts convinced me. “You NEED to take all 4 months” - he told me. “When I was at Facebook, I only took 2 months, as I worried about going for longer. In hindsight, it would have made zero difference for work, but it would have made all the difference for my family. It’s one of my biggest regrets to date.”

I took all 18 weeks in one go, completely disconnecting from work. It was completely worth it. 

Work took care of itself - and that’s an understatement. Several people on my team stepped up far beyond what I would have expected of them. They all advanced their careers much faster than if I had not taken this time. 

#parentalleave #worklifebalance

posted_at.relative: 4 years ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

reshared_post.text:
When I was offered a job at Netflix, I was hesitant to accept. My wife was pregnant and I was concerned about the impact of joining a team and then immediately taking time off. I told my would-be manager that if I joined, I would need 2 to 3 weeks off for the birth. He clearly told me that wouldn't be enough, and suggested that I plan to take 4-6 months off.

Despite the clear support from my team, I was hesitant to take the time that had been granted to me. For men, there is a looming stigma. Fear of losing their good standing within the team, or even worse, their job. Telling a male employee that they’re less of a man for taking time to be with their family after their child’s birth is as stupid as it is outdated.

I'm writing this with my oldest son to my left, and my newborn to my right, as I begin my second Parental Leave. We're watching The Wiggles; I have all the words to all their songs memorized. There isn't anywhere I'd rather be. My Parental Leave made me a better man, a better husband, a better father, a better employee. At Netflix, we are constantly finding ways to increase joy. I'm grateful for a company that practices what it preaches internally with it's employees, as much as it does externally with it's paying members.

I had no idea software engineers could get stocks or options before I joined Microsoft/Skype. In 2012, this was still novel in Europe, but in 2021 it's thankfully more widespread.

I wrote up all the things that I wish I'd known about the - sometimes pretty confusing - equity concepts. And some of the success stories that not everyone has heard - engineers becoming millionaires thanks to stock - as well as the cautionary examples on making no money, or losing hundreds of thousands.

Equity for software engineers is becoming more common both at Big Tech and at tech startups and it's worth understanding its mechanics.

text:
Let's talk about software engineering salaries in Europe. The topic that rarely anyone brings up. And how there's a growing divide between what the same role in the same country typically pays.

Much of this is thanks to the rapid acceleration at the top of the market. This acceleration is happening for a few reasons: a booming software economy - and public offerings - or US companies opening and expanding EU offices.

I would know, having been on both sides: both applying for jobs and getting offers, as well as making offers and seeing which companies paid over or below my past workplaces.

I'm putting an open report together: by no means an "official" source of truth, but hopefully, another data point that can help evaluate if your offer - or salary - is closer to the top or the bottom of the market. Salary is only one of the many data points with any job: but one that we rarely have much data on.

I want to share numbers, and I have plenty of them already from various data sources: but I could use some more. If you work in Europe as a software engineer, consider sharing a salary you know of, so I can share not just one-off data points, but something that's a bit more holistic and speaks to trends. Fill out the survey to add in some data or watch the video in the first comment:

text:
"I have an offer from AmazingCompany1 and AmazingCompany2. Do you have advice on how to choose?"
"Easy:
.
.
.
Have you reverse interviewed both hiring managers?"
"No. What is that?"
"Well, you have an offer: they want you. Until you accept - or reject - you're in charge. You can now do a round of negotiation, but - just as importantly - you can, and should, ask all the questions that are important for you, directly from your future manager or future teammates."
"Hmm, do people really do this?"
"This is what I did before I accepted my Uber offer. But people actually rarely do it! As a hiring manager, I've seen about 1 out of every 20 people do it. And I always jumped on a call as soon as I could for those people. It signaled to me they are both serious and thoughtful."

The above is not a made-up conversation. A friend had an offer from Doordash and Amazon and asked me for advice. I shared my framework on evaluating offers - including reverse interviewing.

In the end, the reverse interview helped them decide which company to choose. You can see the video - and find out if they chose Doordash or Amazon - in the comment linked below:

I'm writing a book about building native mobile apps at scale - and you can grab it for free at http://mobileatscale.com.

While I was at Uber, I drafted an article on the 24 engineering challenges of building large mobile apps. I felt like few non-mobile engineers appreciated the complexity it takes to build *large* and *reliable* mobile apps, with so many challenges to solve. This article just kept growing - it was around 30 pages long already.

It still didn't feel ready, and I shared the table of contents online to see if there's any interest in this topic. There was - a lot of it! More than 20 mobile engineers and engineering leads building complex apps jumped in to give feedback, point out missing areas, or offer corrections. The updated version was 50 pages - and still not finished. I expect the final book to land at 80-100 pages, and there will be a paperback published as well.

The book is currently out in beta and is available for free, thanks to fantastic sponsors like Bitrise - the most mature iOS/Android CI/CD service on the market I know of -, Craft Docs (a seamless editor on iOS and MacOS) and Linear (the project management tool you'd love - especially if you hate how slow JIRA is). 

As it's still being written - and edited - feel free to send feedback my way.

text:
Do you know how the "recruiter side" of LinkedIn looks like and what recruiters see of your profile? Easier to show with an image and some explanation.

1. LinkedIn recruiter is the interface many recruiters will use for LinkedIn. It's both an expensive, but also a powerful recruiting tool.
2. At larger companies, they'd work with projects.
3. Each project has a "pipeline" to track: from people recruiters contact and inbound applications to those replied, declined screened... this is a form of an ATS (Application Tracking System)
4. Details recruiters see at a glance are your title and most recent experience: the current and 3 past experiences. Tip: set your title to what you want to be recruited for, even if it's not what your current title is! Your title can be long - you can use this.
5. Ever followed companies? This is shown. Following a company can slightly increase your chances of being sourced or noticed.
6. Ever got an InMain where you can select "Interested" or "Not now"? Hitting that button will automatically change your status to "Replied" / "Not interested".

Next time when you get an InMail - know you'll appear under "contacted" :)

(All personal details have been changed on the below screenshot).

text:
What would you say is more difficult: building large-scale distributed systems or building a mobile app?

Most people tend to assume that distributed systems are more challenging. I've done both: they're equally difficult when done at scale. But most people don't realize the depth native mobile demands when done right, and the unique challenges not present on the backend, with distributed systems, or on the web. In backend-heavy organizations, mobile engineers can feel underappreciated, despite solving hard problems, at scale.

I've been writing an article on-and-off for a year about this, heavily influenced through my learnings at Uber as a mobile engineer and engineering manager. This is a *very* meaty topic and I've finally published the first article in a series to follow.

If you're a mobile engineer or work with mobile engineers, you'll probably find this article interesting - and perhaps eye-opening:

https://lnkd.in/dUqH3j3

#mobiledevelopment #mobileapp #softwareengineering

text:
Book review: System Design Interview: an Insider's Guide by Alex Xu. I came across this book by accident, after I got a few questions from people on resources to prepare for the systems design/architecture interviews at tech companies and I explored a few different resources.

There are a lot of resources online that collect loads of information, but very few high-quality ones that take the reader step-by-step in building complex systems, starting small, and scaling up, addressing one bottleneck at a time. This book is solid recommend from me not just for interview prep, but for getting more familiar with designing and extending systems. I do see room for further improvement when it comes to alternative solutions and topics like caching and replication - and after posting this in the review, I heard back from the author, saying he is taking this feedback on board for the next version (wow!).

My longer book review and other systems design resources are here:

text:
Building a Career in Software by Daniel Heller arrived today and I’m already halfway through it. It’s every bit as good as I hoped it would be and then some.

If I have to give a tl;dr on it - the book also breaks down to anyone not familiar with the term - is this:

Get this book if you want to grow as a software professional and have not had a mentor like Dan. It’s a refreshingly opinionated book, the opinions and stories coming from someone who worked at Apple before the first iPhone was released, went to being a manager, then going back to development, and figured out the increasingly challenging problem of how to make more and more impact each step as you become more senior, while staying close to engineering. The stories and opinions make it worth reading, and these are what will make you think, pause, and rethink.

This book is the kind you should probably get as paperback, as you’ll keep going back and re-reading parts of. I know I will. Thanks Daniel for writing it. This one will age well.

Highly recommended. Get yours here: https://lnkd.in/d-yMddn

text:
I'm excited to publish The Tech Resume Inside Out: (probably) the most comprehensive guide on how to write a developer / engineering manager resume that represents you well. It's 215 pages of background, advice, examples, and real (but anonymized) resume case studies.

I say probably the most comprehensive, as I did not write this alone. More than 25 tech recruiters and engineering managers across five continents and several tech companies gave feedback, corrections, and new ideas. While there is no "definite guide" on how to put a dev or EM resume together, this one tries to get close.

The book is free for any dev without a job: just follow instructions in the website if you’re in this difficult position. COVID was what prompted me to write this book, my goal being to help those who were hit the hardest: people laid off.

The book has been in beta for some time, with all beta revenue going towards STEM nonprofits: $2,150 went to HackYourFuture and $850 to Anamaki STEM Academy - the latter a choice of the book's editor, Mike Reeves-McMillan.

You can grab your copy (or complimentary copy) here: https://thetechresume.com.

The list of people helping with this project is too long for the post: I'll add them in the comments. Thanks to everyone who helped make this a reality!
