It's been an incredibly tough day today. Layoffs at Uber, in Amsterdam, have been announced, impacting dozens of some of the best backend distributed systems, iOS and Android engineers, and payments PMs I have worked with.

People impacted include iOS and Android engineers who are product-minded, experts at shipping mobile apps to tens of millions of users, without regressions, and know RIBs better than anyone else.

It has engineers from the Production Engineering / SRE team, who built up the infrastructure that allows Uber to run at 99.99%, building systems, tools and putting best practices in place. Some are not even listed in the Google SRE book: we were writing our own, Uber SRE book on these.

There are engineers who built blazing fast build systems using speculation graphs on top of probabilistic models powered by logistic regression. Just google "keeping master green at scale".

And there are many others - PMs who have shipped next-generation payment experiences, and engineers architecting, building and shipping systems of all sorts and shapes at scale.

I know most of these people personally and would hire them back in heartbeat. If your company is hiring in Amsterdam, Europe, or remote, do send me a message so I can connect you with some of them.

text:
I could not be happier to congratulate five people on my team for their very well deserved promotions this cycle: Andreea Sicaru, Gabriel Garcia, Kiril Knysh, Oguz Babaoglu - congratulations! An even more special congrats to Robin van Dijke, with his second promotion since we've been working together, this time to Senior Engineer II.

Seeing people grow is what I enjoy most in my job. There are few clearer signs of people leveling up than a promotion.

At Uber, promotions are hard: packages need to be written, peers need to give clear feedback and committees decide if promotion candidates are ready for the next level, expectations at each level defined in our engineering ladder. Promotions are real teamwork: every one of the people promoted has been helped by mentors and by multiple people sticking their neck out for them, giving strong peer feedback on why they are ready for the next level. And the people who are promoted this time are ones who will be mentoring and "pulling up" others the next cycles.

If you're thinking about how to get promoted as a developer, here's the advice I've been giving to people on my team, on how to approach this ambitious goal. https://lnkd.in/d6C4azs

And now, 🍾🎉! #promotions #celebrate

text:
"How do I consistently deliver projects on the deadline the business is telling me to do so?"

"You don't. You just don't do Deadline Driven Development."

"But the business..."

"Look, you can either decide you're only serving the business, accept those deadlines without question and march like a good soilder, build up tech debt you won't have time to repay, lash the team and yourself exhaustion, and see them quit one by one as they burn out until you also throw in the towel in the end."

"Or you can decide you serve the team and business and do Tradeoff Based Development. Come up with estimates without deadline pressure, then negotiate with the business on what can reasonably and reliably be delivered, with a motivated team. Perhaps the internal deadline isn't that important. Perhaps it is and the scope can be cut for v1. Or if the deadline is non-negotiable, then perhaps we can add more people to the team - taking away from elsewhere - to get it done without scope cuts. While you're at it, you also educate the business on how software development - and project management, in general - works, and even the ground."

text:
"Is Uber a tech company?" I get this question from time to time from devs. I've started to tell a story instead of saying yes/no.

We have a developer platform team, who, for years, have been working on a program to reduce blockers on dev productivity. Examples of projects they shipped:

- Measured compile time performance differences on different hardware and made the business case to replace all mobile dev machines to 6-core Macbooks, as builds were about 30% faster vs the "old" 4-core ones. Productivity gain >> cost.

- Built SubmitQueue, which allows hundreds of pull requests per day - each needing to run 30-60 minutes worth of tests - to be merged to the same monorepo. This involves some parallelization and a speculation graph using a probabilistic model. It used to take 4-5 hours to land my change to prod, and I sometimes had merge issues. Now it takes around 20 minutes and I never have merge issues. https://lnkd.in/eA37KxC

- Sped up iOS builds about 10x by porting buck to Swift, working side-by-side with Facebook's mobile team. (https://lnkd.in/e2_qu7p) 

- A bunch of other projects on faster build times, fewer manual steps to deploy to prod, more/easier automated tests.

You see Uber in the physical world, but there's some advanced tech powering just our dev tools.

text:
Do companies hire new grads because they are cheaper than seniors, or do they do it so they can invest in them? At Uber Amsterdam, we’re in the second bucket. We’ve hired very few new grads the past three years, as we didn't feel we have the right ratio of seniors to support them in their growth.

So I couldn't be happier on just finishing our first-ever dedicated hiring event for new grads at Uber Amsterdam, extending offers to multiple talented, enthusiastic and full-of-energy engineers.

Each new grad will join a team in Uber, where they will have access to multiple mentors and several senior engineers. Their manager and their team are invested in helping them grow, and empower them to make an impact from day one - most of them will ship production code in their first few weeks that millions of people will interact with. And if the past is anything like the future, many of them will grow into senior roles, experts on distributed systems and some perhaps even into engineering management positions in the coming years.

Both the team and myself are excited to welcome them - here's to the new grad "class" of Uber Amsterdam 2020!

 #career #excited  #newgrads

text:
“How can I get into distributed systems?” - I get this question a lot, especially from people who read my distributed architecture concepts article ( https://lnkd.in/d6vhSyK).

Here is the advice I give to people - a shorter version of Twitter thread I shared (https://lnkd.in/dAaRJR8):
1. Do your homework on the theory of distributed systems
2. Look around and be (very) curious. You might already be working with distributed systems.
3. Befriend the devs who build the largest systems at work. If you're lucky, you'll find high-load systems. 
4. Go oncall for a system that needs to be reliable. You'll learn a lot.
5. If you don't have that many people to learn from at work, reach out to local devs and buy coffee/lunch for them.
6. Use these technologies as pet projects. Consider getting serious and contributing to one of the open source ones. Few people do - it's hard, but the best way to learn (without switching jobs, if you don't do that kind of work there).
7. If you have zero opportunities for this kind of work at your job, consider switching to somewhere where you do. Several startups and big companies have these kinds of opportunities.

text:
Fixit week #5 is on the way at Uber Amsterdam. A week, where all engineers within the company focus only on removing tech debt and shipping small fixes to delight our users.

For every Fixit week, we have themes. This week we're focusing on these 3 areas:
1. Cherish our customers - external and internal. Fix up systems with bugs we spotted, small or large.
2. Sharpen our axes: increase engineers' quality of delivery and dev velocity.
3. Nail the basics: eliminate duplications, decommission unused resources, clean up confusing code and dependencies.

We're working in small teams of 6-8 people, Fixit team leads wearing construction hats (ok, maybe not all day). And we have some special bounties to make this week extra fun, like:
- Back to the Future (automate a previously manual process)
- The Night Watch (adding/enhancing monitoring & alerting on a critical system)
- Foreign Affairs (fix a task in collaboration with a team from a different office & timezone)

You might ask, what do managers do on Fixit week? It's simple. We stay out of the way, canceling all meetings, clearing everyone's calendars for the week. And we look for things we can also fix.

This coming week, all engineers at Uber will contribute to "Fixit Week" and focus on removing technical debt across our code base. This yearly event empowers engineers to remove inefficiencies, short-cuts, and hacks their teams collected over time. It gives us the needed breathing space to add those missing tests, improves our docs, or clean up UX issues in our products.

Fixit weeks are great. Three important ground rules we apply at Uber:
- Everyone is included with no exempt teams or orgs.
- We remove technical debt and we fix things. 
- We do not add new features.

I am looking forward to cleaning up, refreshing, and improving the documentation for Screenflow, my product at Uber. I will also spend time at streamlining how we run unit tests.  

What will you be fixing this week? #uber #fixit

text:
We lost a really good candidate to another company and I knew I had to do something about it.

I was on the interview panel and after a long and thorough session, I was very impressed and a very strong "yes" for hire - something that is quite rare. The candidate followed up with a thank-you message on Linkedin, saying they also had a great experience. Later, the panel was equally enthusiastic and we extended an offer.

They let us know that although everything was great and they liked the people during the interview, they will be joining another, local company instead. It was a better match: they were looking for something small, and we were on the bigger side. Myself and the whole panel were gutted. So I did the only sensible thing left to do.

I wrote a message to them, over Linkedin, wishing them much success in this new role, at the other company, telling them I have no doubt they will do amazing there and have a fantastic time. I've only heard good things about this company, from my peers who worked there and am certain they will make a great impact.

It's far more important that people find that role and team that is the best fit for them than it is to join the company I work at.

text:
This is me and my son preparing to do something special over the usual daily routine: visit the office for lunch with my team for the first time since starting my parental leave.

It's already been two months since I've uninstalled all work stuff from my phone, setup out-of-office replies and put away the work laptop. Halfway through this generous paternity leave, it's been loads of fun with my little one, without any unexpected work interruptions, thanks to my fantastic and creative team and colleagues who have all stepped up. 

This time is also making me realize just how tough a job staying home alone with a baby is and how little I knew of how consuming it can be. I have a newfound empathy for stay-at-home moms and primary caregivers. It truly is a demanding full-time job! It's also one that's hard to appreciate until you do it yourself. 

Here's to paid parental leave becoming more common and longer, so others can have similar bonding and empathy-building experiences!

text:
Work laptop locked, out of office replies set, phone notifications turned off. On day one of my 4-month paternity leave, I could not be more ready to spend a lot of quality time with my recently arrived little one and my family.

While I'm grateful that I have the opportunity for such a nice bonding experience, I do wish this was more the exception than the norm. As surprising as it might sound, I will have a longer parental leave than my wife had - she went back to work after her 3-month paid maternity leave ended. Most dads in Europe, outside the Nordic countries, get no more than 2 weeks of paid paternity leave.

Luckily, more companies are rolling out family-friendly policies that go well beyond those defined by the law. While we wait for governments to catch up, we can - and should! - vote with our feet, asking for such policies at the workplace, as well as when switching companies, citing examples like these.

I'm thankful to Uber who introduced this new, 18-week global paid parental leave policy just in time as my little one arrived. I'm equally thankful for my team and peers at work, who have been very supportive ("Just go! We'll be fine! Why are you still here?") and are stepping in to fill the gap while I'm out.

posted_at.relative: 7 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
As much as companies are struggling with hiring the right talent, if you don't have a good and efficient recruitment process, your company will miss out on many great people.

One of the top engineers at Uber Amsterdam is relocating to another city in Europe, leaving Uber. They are someone I would (re-) hire in a heartbeat. While interviewing with many companies, a lot of great companies got crossed off their list - either because they fail to respond in a timely manner (in a month) or because of doing little to no digging - e.g. rejecting for not enough java experience, when this person wrote a large chunk of the some of the most mission-critical systems at Uber using a variety of languages (among others, java).

Next time, when your recruiting seems not as fast as it seems, go back and check the promising candidates you lost early on due to being too slow or for not doing enough digging on their background. Great companies are built on top of talented people - and hiring just as talented, if not even better people, as the company grows.

#recruiting #engineeringmanagement

posted_at.relative: 8 years ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Could not be more proud of my wife who decided to do a major career change to software engineering and has just come home from her first workday as a frontend developer.

She took an online Ruby course and signed up to a coding bootcamp learning coding basics with Ruby on Rails, HTML and Javascript. She attended the job fair organized by her bootcamp and really liked this mobile development agency who also build chatbots. For the interview she built her own Facebook chatbot that presented her CV, giving details to anyone interacting with it. She got the job. After her first day she told me her head has exploded with all the cool things she's learned and we talked Javascript package managers over dinner. (Here are more details: https://lnkd.in/gexJpi5 )

Having followed her journey I can see that it's not true that learning to code is easy - especially if you've not learned it in high school or university. It's hard. Probably harder than learning a new language like French. It gets a bit easier with a support network like peers at a coding school, a spouse or a close friend who also codes. Still, it's still hard work, long hours and many moments of self-doubt. But once you push through it, learn coding and land that first job, it opens up a wonderful world of opportunities.

text:
When working at high growth environment, surrounded by really smart people It's easy to think that best practices and advice from the outside don't go far. In an environment like this, unique solutions, sometimes radical ones often work better.

But I guarantee that there is someone like you, at another company, who is solving the same problems in very similar ways. I realized this when bumping into Wesley Gorman at a tech conference.  Wesley has seen the Zendesk Dublin office grow from 2 to hundreds of people in 4 years, at a similar pace - or even faster - to Uber in Amsterdam. He has also transitioned from engineer to engineering manager, similar to myself. I've already learned tons from him on solving key pain points we both share, like empowering people, scaling teams and creating a great hiring experience. Here's to more learning and sharing experiences with people who could be solving these (not-so)unique problems like yourself.

text:
Each week, more than 2 million people choose to drive with the Uber platform. I am proud to share we are kicking off 180 Days of Change for drivers. Starting today, every month we will release new features that transform the driving experience to a flexibe earning opportunity that drivers can depend on - worldwide.

Today, we launched tipping - initially in 3 US cities, which we are rolling out further. My team is are already busy working on even something even bigger, that I cannot wait until we can share with the rest of the world.

https://lnkd.in/gr5Ntjd
